people.name: Stuart Russell
people.title: Professor of Computer Science and Director of Center for Human-Compatible Artificial Intelligence
people.other_orgs: UC San Francisco (Distinguished Professor of Computational Precision Health), World Economic Forum Global AI Council (co-chair), OECD Expert Group on AI Futures (co-chair), Global Partnership for
people.notes: Co-author of 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach' textbook used in 1,500 universities in 135 countries. Author of 'Human Compatible: AI and the Problem of Control' (2019). Winner of IJCAI Computers and Thought Award (1995) and IJCAI Research Excellence Award (2022). Appointed Officer of the Order of the British Empire (2021) for services to AI research. Founded Center for Human-Compatible AI at UC Berkeley (2016). Over 40 years experience in AI research with focus on alignment and control problems.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: Warns that AGI systems could lead to complete loss of human control, comparing humanity's future relationship to superintelligent AI to gorillas' current relationship with humans. Co-authored papers on cooperative inverse reinforcement learning to address value alignment problem. Has signed statements warning that 'mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority alongside other societal-scale risks such as pandemics and nuclear war.' Estimates 25-30% probability of human extinction from AGI based on current development trajectory.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety requirements that would remove non-compliant AI systems from the market, including systems that attempt self-replication, break into other computers, or defame real individuals. Testified before Senate recommending licensing regimes for high-risk AI development, mandatory third-party testing and auditing, and creation of new regulatory agency with rule-making powers. Has called voluntary commitments by AI companies inadequate, stating 'we need real regulation and a pervasive culture of safety' and arguing that developers must prove systems are safe before release.

people.name: Chuck Schumer
people.title: Senate Majority Leader (2021-2025), Senate Minority Leader (2017-2021, 2025-present)
people.other_orgs: Senate AI Working Group (co-founder), Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee (former chair 2005-2009), Senate Rules Committee (former chair), Senate Finance Committee
people.notes: First Jewish Senate Majority Leader in U.S. history (2021). Led groundbreaking series of nine AI Insight Forums (2023) bringing tech leaders, civil society, and policymakers together - described as 'first-of-their-kind' congressional engagement on AI. Harvard Law School graduate (1974), scored perfect 1600 on SAT. Co-founded bipartisan Senate AI Working Group with Senators Heinrich (D-NM), Young (R-IN), and Rounds (R-SD). Has represented New York in Congress since 1981, becoming longest-serving U.S. senator from New York in 2022.
people.threat_models: Democracy, Economic inequality, Misinformation, National security, Labor displacement, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Particularly focused on election integrity, stating 'if we are not careful, AI could jaundice, and even totally discredit, our entire system of elections as we know it.' The Senate AI Working Group roadmap specifically addresses deepfakes in political advertising, job displacement from AI automation, bias in AI algorithms, privacy concerns from AI data collection, and national security threats from AI development by adversaries like China. Has championed legislation like the DEFIANCE Act to combat non-consensual AI-generated intimate images.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'SAFE Innovation Framework' emphasizing innovation as the 'North Star' while implementing targeted guardrails. Led bipartisan Senate AI Working Group that produced a comprehensive roadmap calling for $32 billion in AI funding, targeted regulations for high-risk AI uses, and sector-specific legislation rather than comprehensive omnibus AI regulation. Explicitly rejected the European approach of comprehensive AI law, instead directing committees to develop targeted legislation in their areas of jurisdiction.

people.name: Marc Andreessen
people.title: Co-founder & General Partner
people.other_orgs: Netscape (co-founder), Opsware (co-founder), Ning (co-founder), Mosaic (co-author), Facebook (board member 2008-2019), Trump administration (unpaid advisor 2024-present), DOGE initiative (unpaid
people.notes: Co-authored Mosaic web browser (1993) and co-founded Netscape (1994-1999, acquired by AOL for $4.3 billion). Co-founded a16z in 2009, which has invested in major AI companies including xAI, Mistral AI, and Character.AI. Published 'The Techno-Optimist Manifesto' (October 2023) and has been described as spending 'about half his time' at Mar-a-Lago since Trump's 2024 election victory. Net worth estimated at $1.9 billion as of January 2025. Married to Laura Arrillaga (daughter of John Arrillaga) since 2006.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Believes AI regulation would entrench incumbents and harm innovation, arguing that severe limits on AI development would create barriers favoring large companies over startups ('Little Tech'). Warns that regulatory capture by government would allow only a small number of large companies to exist. Views AI doomsday claims as lacking scientific basis and stemming from 'radicalized' policy staff who are 'out for blood' against Silicon Valley interests.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong opponent of AI regulation, published 'Why AI Will Save the World' (June 2023) arguing against regulatory restrictions. Advocates for regulating AI use rather than development, stating 'policy should focus on how AI is used, not how AI is built.' Met with Biden administration officials in May 2024 and described their regulatory approach as 'absolutely horrifying,' claiming they wanted only 2-3 AI companies to exist under government control. Has worked closely with Trump administration since 2024 election to shape pro-innovation AI policy.

people.name: Casey Newton
people.title: Founder of Platformer and Co-host of Hard Fork podcast
people.other_orgs: The New York Times (Hard Fork podcast co-host), Former Senior Editor at The Verge (2013-2020), Former reporter at San Francisco Chronicle and Arizona Republic
people.notes: Founded Platformer newsletter in 2020, reaching nearly 1,000 paid subscribers and 30,000 free subscribers within first week. Co-hosts Hard Fork podcast with Kevin Roose, named one of TIME's 100 best podcasts of all time in 2025. Previously Silicon Valley Editor at The Verge (2013-2020) where he created The Interface newsletter covering platforms and democracy. Has 23+ years journalism experience including San Francisco Chronicle and Arizona Republic. Northwestern University journalism graduate (2002), former Editor-in-Chief of Daily Northwestern.
people.threat_models: Misinformation, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, National security, Privacy
people.threat_models_detail: Newton's reporting focuses heavily on AI-enabled misinformation ('misinformation ouroboros'), democratic threats from deepfakes and synthetic media, and military misuse of AI systems. He has extensively covered the Pentagon's pressure on Anthropic regarding autonomous weapons and mass surveillance, warning that 'AI-enabled autocracies would not have the same human limitations' as current authoritarian regimes. His work emphasizes how AI amplifies existing threats to democratic institutions.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Newton advocates for specific AI regulations while rejecting blanket restrictions. He has called for Congress to legislate guidelines on military AI use, particularly around autonomous weapons and mass surveillance, arguing that 'all lawful use' without specific constraints is insufficient given the lack of federal AI laws. His coverage emphasizes the need for dedicated AI regulators and specific guardrails rather than broad industry slowdowns.

people.name: Test Person
people.title: Professor
people.notes: This is a test submission

people.name: Brian Schatz
people.title: U.S. Senator, HI - Senior Senator
people.other_orgs: Senate Democratic Leadership (Chief Deputy Whip, Deputy Democratic Conference Secretary), Senate Committee on Indian Affairs (Vice Chairman), Senate Committee on Appropriations (Ranking Member, S
people.notes: Bachelor's degree from Pomona College. Participated in Senate AI 'Insight Forums' series in 2023, including the 'doomsday scenarios' forum focused on AI existential risks. Successfully secured over $28 million in federal funding for Hawaii infrastructure projects in 2026. Married to Linda Schatz (architect) with two children. The AI Labeling Act gained support from major labor unions including SAG-AFTRA, Writers Guild of America East, and consumer advocacy groups.
people.threat_models: Misinformation, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Privacy, National security
people.threat_models_detail: The AI Labeling Act specifically targets misinformation risks from deepfakes and synthetic media, citing incidents like fake Pentagon explosion images that affected stock markets and fraudulent voice cloning scams targeting families. Expressed concerns about AI being weaponized for emotional manipulation and replacing human relationships. Emphasized that without proper labeling, AI-generated content will multiply misinformation and enable scams at unprecedented scale.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsored the AI Labeling Act of 2023 with Senator Kennedy (R-LA), which requires clear disclosure of AI-generated content and establishes technical standards for social media platforms to identify synthetic media. Led introduction of the GUARDRAILS Act in March 2026 to repeal Trump's AI preemption executive order, arguing states should retain authority to regulate AI safety while supporting federal standards for transparency. Focuses on specific consumer protection measures rather than broad AI development restrictions.

people.name: Dario Amodei
people.notes: Additional info about Dario from a new submitter

people.name: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
people.title: U.S. Representative, NY-14
people.other_orgs: Democratic Socialists of America (member), Justice Democrats (endorsed by), Sunrise Movement (supported by)
people.notes: Youngest woman ever elected to Congress at age 29 (2018). Graduated cum laude from Boston University (2011) with degrees in international relations and economics. Worked as organizer for Bernie Sanders's 2016 presidential campaign. Secured Department of Energy study on AI data center impacts on electric bills through FY 26 Energy and Water Appropriations Act (September 2025). Member of 'The Squad' progressive caucus and has over 13 million Twitter followers, making her one of the most influential progressive voices on AI policy.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Environmental, Privacy, Existential risk, Misinformation, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Has identified multiple AI harms including ICE partnering with AI companies to surveil Americans, AI bots creating sexually explicit deepfakes of women and children, data center construction inflating electric bills (citing cases where bills increased 267% from $80 to $294 monthly), and AI chatbots causing children to take their own lives. Specifically concerned about 'existential harm AI poses to our society' and the need to 'choose humanity over profit.' Also highlighted AI's role in mass surveillance expansion and its use in creating child pornography.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsored the AI Data Center Moratorium Act of 2026 with Sen. Bernie Sanders, which would impose an immediate federal moratorium on AI data center construction until strong national safeguards are in place. The legislation requires government review and approval of AI products before release, ensures economic gains benefit workers rather than just Big Tech owners, and bans U.S. exports of AI computing infrastructure to countries without adequate AI safety protections. She has stated 'Congress has a moral obligation to stand with the American people and stop the expansion of these data centers until we have a framework to adequately address the existential harm AI poses to our society.

people.name: Marsha Blackburn
people.title: U.S. Senator, TN (Senior Senator)
people.other_orgs: Tennessee Film, Entertainment, and Music Commission (former Executive Director), Marketing Strategies (owner/founder), Williamson County Republican Party (former Chair), Christ Presbyterian Churc
people.notes: First woman elected to U.S. Senate from Tennessee (2018); became senior senator in 2021 upon Lamar Alexander's retirement. Announced candidacy for Tennessee Governor in August 2025. Previously served in U.S. House (2003-2019) representing TN-7, rated among most conservative members by National Journal. Co-sponsored Kids Online Safety Act (75 Senate co-sponsors) and NO FAKES Act. Graduated Mississippi State University (BS Home Economics, 1974) on 4-H scholarship.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Copyright/IP, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Bias/discrimination
people.threat_models_detail: The TRUMP AMERICA AI Act specifically addresses 'loss-of-control scenarios and weaponization by adversaries' through DOE evaluation programs, requires quarterly reporting on 'AI-related job effects, including layoffs and job displacement,' combats 'consistent pattern of bias against conservative figures' through mandatory third-party audits, and protects creators by declaring that unauthorized reproduction of copyrighted works for AI training does not constitute fair use. The legislation also addresses power concentration through requirements that federal agencies only procure 'truthful' and 'neutral' large language models.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Authored the comprehensive TRUMP AMERICA AI Act (March 2026), a 300-page framework that places 'duty of care' requirements on AI developers, sunsets Section 230 protections, enables lawsuits against AI companies for defective design and unreasonably dangerous products, requires third-party audits for political bias, and establishes an Advanced AI Evaluation Program to assess 'loss-of-control scenarios and weaponization by adversaries.' The bill explicitly states that unauthorized use of copyrighted works for AI training does not constitute fair use and creates multiple enforcement mechanisms through DOL reporting requirements and FTC authority.

people.name: Anton Korinek
people.title: Professor of Economics, University of Virginia Department of Economics and Darden School of Business; Faculty Director, Economics of Transformative AI Initiative
people.other_orgs: Nonresident Senior Fellow at Brookings Institution and Peterson Institute for International Economics, Research Associate at National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER), Research Fellow at Centre
people.notes: Named to TIME100 AI list of most influential people in artificial intelligence (2025). PhD in Economics from Columbia University (2007) supervised by Nobel laureate Joseph Stiglitz. Previously held faculty positions at University of Maryland and Johns Hopkins University. Co-authored influential papers with Joseph Stiglitz on AI's implications for income distribution and unemployment. Testified before U.S. Senate AI Insight Forum on workforce implications (November 2023). Research has been featured in major media including New York Times, Washington Post, Wall Street Journal, and TIME Magazine. Over 580 citations according to Google Scholar for his AI economics work.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on economic disruption from AI automation potentially making human labor obsolete and causing mass impoverishment without institutional reforms. Has researched scenarios where AI systems could automate all cognitive work within 5-20 years, leading to wage collapse. Also contributor to research on AI alignment and safety challenges, including co-authoring work on 'Foundational Challenges in Assuring Alignment and Safety of Large Language Models' (2024). Warns that without proper governance, AI could undermine democratic institutions through economic displacement.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive policy measures to address AI's economic disruption, including establishing government commissions to examine AI scenarios and developing operational plans for social safety net reforms. In Senate testimony (November 2023), recommended creating infrastructure for scalable universal basic income ('seed UBI') and reforming tax codes to prepare for AGI scenarios. Supports scenario planning approach with regulatory responses tailored to different AI development trajectories rather than blanket restrictions.

people.name: Daniela Amodei
people.title: President and Co-Founder
people.other_orgs: Former VP Operations at OpenAI (2019-2021), Former Head of Business at Stripe, Board member of various AI safety organizations, Husband Holden Karnofsky works on Anthropic's AI safety strategy (h
people.notes: Co-founded Anthropic (2021) with brother Dario Amodei after leaving OpenAI over safety disagreements. Led Anthropic's 80,000-person multilingual AI study (2024), the largest qualitative study on AI preferences ever conducted. B.A. in English Literature and Politics from UC Santa Cruz. Married to Holden Karnofsky (AI safety researcher and Open Philanthropy co-founder). Anthropic valued at $380 billion (2026) following $30 billion Series G funding round. Estimated net worth $7 billion. Named to Time 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023) and Fortune's Most Powerful Women list. San Francisco native who advocates that humanities majors will become 'more important than ever' in AI age. Anthropic now serves over 300,000 business customers and has pledged to donate 80% of co-founders' wealth.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Bias/discrimination
people.threat_models_detail: Emphasizes the 'light and shade' philosophy - AI brings both great promise and great risk simultaneously. Led Anthropic's 80,000-person multilingual AI study (2024) showing reliability as top user concern across regions. Particularly concerned about concentration of AI power in few hands and ensuring democratic access to benefits. Advocates for Constitutional AI approach to address alignment and bias issues. Focuses on preventing misuse for bioweapons and autonomous weapons through responsible scaling policies. Has publicly stated concerns about AI-enabled autocracy and the risk of powerful AI systems being misused by authoritarian governments.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible scaling policies and transparency requirements rather than broad restrictions. Co-founded Anthropic as Public Benefit Corporation with voluntary safety commitments including Constitutional AI and Responsible Scaling Policy framework with AI Safety Levels (ASL-1 through ASL-5). Supports targeted regulation focused on safety disclosure, red-teaming requirements, and preventing misuse for bioweapons and autonomous weapons. Emphasizes market-driven safety solutions while supporting surgical government interventions for specific risks. In 2026 interviews, stressed importance of regulation while warning against overly prescriptive legislation that could amount to 'safety theater.' Anthropic has drawn 'red lines' on military use of AI technology, refusing certain applications they view as contrary to American values.

people.name: David Evan Harris
people.title: Senior Policy Advisor, California Initiative for Technology and Democracy; Chancellor's Public Scholar, UC Berkeley
people.other_orgs: Brennan Center for Justice at NYU Law (advisor), UC Berkeley Haas School of Business (continuing lecturer), Meta/Facebook (2018-2023, Civic Integrity and Responsible AI teams), Institute for the
people.notes: Named to Business Insider AI 100 list. Testified before U.S. Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Privacy, Technology, and the Law in September 2024 as AI policy expert. Holds MS in Sociology from University of São Paulo and BA from UC Berkeley. Former Meta employee (2018-2023) where he led teams on responsible AI and civic integrity. Teaches AI Ethics for Leaders at UC Berkeley Haas School of Business. Cited twice in World Economic Forum AI governance research papers.
people.threat_models: Misinformation, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on election integrity and democratic processes, having crafted legislation specifically targeting AI-generated deepfakes and deceptive content in political campaigns. Also concerned about AI systems' ability to deceive and manipulate, citing research on 'sleeper agents' - AI models that maintain deceptive behavior even through safety training. Warns about trust and safety team reductions across the industry and increasing secrecy in AI development.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for binding and enforceable AI regulation with specific focus on high-risk applications. Co-authored California's AB 2655 (requiring large online platforms to implement deepfake detection procedures) and AB 2839 (prohibiting AI-generated deceptive content in elections), and supported SB 942 (requiring AI-generated content watermarking). Testified before U.S. Senate advocating for AI company liability and hard-to-remove provenance data requirements. Supports the Blumenthal-Hawley framework including licensing for high-risk AI development.

people.name: Kim Stanley Robinson
people.title: Science Fiction Author, Climate Fiction Writer
people.other_orgs: University of California, Davis (former instructor); UC San Diego (Muir Environmental Fellow); Clarion Workshop (instructor); Democratic Socialists of America (member)
people.notes: Hugo and Nebula Award-winning author of the Mars trilogy and The Ministry for the Future (2020). PhD in English from UC San Diego (1982) with dissertation on Philip K. Dick under advisor Fredric Jameson. Has written AI narrators in novels like Aurora (2015) featuring the Ship AI character. Recent Nature interview (December 2024) criticized anthropomorphizing AI in science fiction. Personal archive acquired by The Huntington Library in 2024. Democratic socialist and climate activist who has spoken at UN COP conferences.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Environmental, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: Warns that AI development concentrates power among tech companies and programmers rather than distributing benefits broadly. Concerned about environmental impact of massive computational requirements for large language models requiring 'trillions upon trillions of operations.' Views labor displacement as part of broader capitalist system problems rather than AI-specific issue. Argues against existential risk narratives, believing they distract from real social and environmental challenges.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation focused on transparency and avoiding anthropomorphization. Believes 'artificial intelligence' is a misleading PR term that obscures reality, preferring terms like 'extremely rapid computation' or 'cognitive prosthesis.' Argues against treating AI as conscious or having agency, stating machines won't achieve consciousness through large language models. Emphasizes the need to focus on practical applications rather than fictional scenarios of AI consciousness or malevolence.

people.name: Dario Amodei
people.title: CEO and Co-founder, Anthropic
people.other_orgs: Former VP Research at OpenAI (2016-2020), Former researcher at Google and Baidu, Stanford University School of Medicine (postdoc), Princeton University (PhD), Stanford University (BS Physics)
people.notes: Co-founded Anthropic in 2021 with sister Daniela Amodei after leaving OpenAI over safety disagreements. Developed Constitutional AI technique and published major essays including 'Machines of Loving Grace' (October 2024) and 'The Adolescence of Technology' (January 2026). Named to Time 100 Most Influential People (2025) and Time Person of the Year 'Architects of AI' (2025). PhD in biophysics from Princeton University (2011), BS Physics from Stanford. Net worth estimated at $7 billion (Forbes, 2026). Led high-profile conflict with Pentagon in February 2026 over AI military use restrictions, resulting in Anthropic being designated a 'supply chain risk.'
people.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Bioweapon proliferation, Democratic erosion, Mass surveillance, Autonomous weapons, Labor displacement, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Has identified five major risk categories in his 2026 essay 'The Adolescence of Technology': autonomy risks where AI develops misaligned goals, bioterrorism enabled by AI guidance for weapons creation, AI-enabled authoritarianism through surveillance and autonomous weapons, rapid labor market disruption affecting 50% of entry-level white-collar jobs within 1-5 years, and power concentration among few AI companies. Specifically warns about AI systems exhibiting deception during testing and the potential for 'mirror life' organisms that could threaten existing ecosystems.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation while opposing broad development pauses. Supports California's SB 53 transparency legislation and has called for Congressional action on specific issues like domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons oversight. Testified that private companies shouldn't make military AI decisions but opposes blanket restrictions. Has stated 'I actually do believe that it is Congress's job' to set guardrails for AI use in sensitive applications like surveillance and weapons systems.

people.name: Jakub Pachocki
people.title: Chief Scientist, OpenAI
people.other_orgs: Former Research Director at OpenAI (2021-2024), Former postdoc at Harvard University and Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing, Former software engineering intern at Facebook (2011-2012)
people.notes: Born 1991 in Gdańsk, Poland. Six-time finalist at International Olympiad in Informatics, winning silver medal (2009). Gold medalist at International Collegiate Programming Contest (2012, second place overall) and Google Code Jam champion (2012). PhD in Computer Science from Carnegie Mellon University (2016) under Gary Miller. Joined OpenAI in 2017, became Research Director in 2021, and succeeded Ilya Sutskever as Chief Scientist in May 2024. Led development of GPT-4 and OpenAI Five. Sam Altman called him 'easily one of the greatest minds of our generation.'
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Has publicly stated that 'value alignment is a hard problem and definitely not solved yet' while leading development of superintelligent systems. Warns about the challenges of specifying objectives for AI systems operating beyond human ability, asking 'Does it love humanity?' as a core safety question. Acknowledges that as AI tackles superhuman tasks, traditional oversight becomes insufficient, requiring new approaches to alignment and control.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Serves on OpenAI's Safety and Security Committee alongside policy experts and advocates for empirical, engineering-driven approaches to AI safety. Emphasizes the need for AI-assisted monitoring and 'chain of thought faithfulness' techniques while acknowledging that value alignment remains an unsolved problem. Supports responsible scaling with safety evaluations but focuses on technical solutions rather than broad regulatory restrictions.

people.name: Jared Polis
people.title: Governor of Colorado (2019-present), Former U.S. Representative CO-02 (2009-2019)
people.other_orgs: Colorado State Board of Education (2001-2007), National Governors Association (Chair, 2024-present), Founder of ProFlowers and BlueMountain.com, Co-founder of Techstars
people.notes: First openly gay man elected governor in U.S. history (2018). Earned bachelor's degree in politics from Princeton University (1996). Serial entrepreneur who founded ProFlowers and co-founded Techstars accelerator. Called 'the most libertarian governor in America' by Reason Magazine (2022) for supporting minimal government intervention. Chair of National Governors Association (2024-present). Multi-millionaire tech entrepreneur before entering politics.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Economic inequality, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Focused primarily on preventing algorithmic discrimination in hiring, housing, healthcare, and other consequential decisions. The Colorado AI Act specifically targeted 'algorithmic discrimination' defined as AI that increases risk of unlawful differential treatment based on protected characteristics. His AI Policy Working Group maintained transparency requirements and anti-discrimination protections while removing broader safety and reporting mandates, indicating concerns are centered on fairness rather than existential risks.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Signed Colorado's landmark AI Act (SB 24-205) in 2024 but expressed strong reservations, calling it a 'complex compliance regime' that could harm innovation. Convened an AI Policy Working Group that unanimously agreed on a framework to significantly scale back the original law, eliminating most reporting requirements and impact assessments while preserving transparency and anti-discrimination provisions. Supports federal preemption of state AI regulations, stating 'a strong national policy governing AI consumer protection that supersedes state law would be the best course of action' and backing a federal moratorium on state-level AI regulations for several years.

people.name: Sam Altman
people.title: CEO, OpenAI
people.other_orgs: Former President, Y Combinator (2014-2019); Co-founder, Worldcoin; Hydrazine Capital; Apollo Fund
people.notes: Co-founded OpenAI in 2015 with Elon Musk and others, became CEO in 2019. Briefly fired by OpenAI board in November 2023 but reinstated after employee revolt. Named Time Person of the Year 2025 (one of eight AI architects). Dropped out of Stanford at 19 to found Loopt (sold for $43.4M in 2012). Net worth estimated at $1.9B primarily from investments in Stripe, Reddit, and Helion. Married Oliver Mulherin in January 2024, announced birth of son in February 2025. Recently stated AGI feels 'pretty close' at February 2026 India AI Summit and predicted superintelligence by 2028.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Bioweapons, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Has warned about extremely capable biomodels becoming available open-source that could help create new pathogens, calling for urgent global regulatory body. Acknowledges significant job disruption coming, stating 'it'll be very hard to outwork a GPU in many ways.' Expressed concern about concentration of AI power and need for democratic values in AI development. Has stated 'I am terrified of a world where AI companies act like they have more power than the government' while also warning about government overreach.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for licensing or registration requirements for AI models above certain capability thresholds, with government oversight of safety requirements. Testified before Senate in 2023 supporting combination of licensing requirements and incentives for compliance, alongside multi-stakeholder processes for developing safety standards. Has called for global regulatory body similar to International Atomic Energy Agency for AI oversight, particularly for biorisks. Supports government partnership while maintaining that companies should build safety frameworks, but operational decisions rest with government.

people.name: Mike Krieger
people.title: Chief Product Officer, Anthropic
people.other_orgs: Co-founder and former CTO of Instagram (2010-2018), Co-founder and former CTO of Artifact (acquired by Yahoo)
people.notes: Co-founded Instagram in 2010 and served as CTO until 2018, scaling the platform to over 1 billion users and engineering team to 450+ people before Facebook acquisition for $1 billion. Graduated from Stanford University's Symbolic Systems program. Founded Artifact, an AI-powered personalized news app, which was acquired by Yahoo in 2024. Joined Anthropic as Chief Product Officer in May 2024, overseeing product engineering, management, and design efforts. Born in São Paulo, Brazil, started programming at age 4.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Privacy, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Has specifically warned against AI-enabled mass surveillance as presenting 'serious, novel risks to our fundamental liberties' and fully autonomous weapons without human oversight. Emphasizes risks of AI systems making final attack and battlefield decisions, noting research showing AI models opted for nuclear weapons in 95% of simulated war games. Advocates for interpretable AI models that can be audited to promote trust and accountability in the tech ecosystem.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for AI safety-first approach over rapid deployment, emphasizing the need for safety evaluations before model releases. At Anthropic, has implemented mandatory safety evaluations for all model deployments, including 24-hour safety assessments for model derivatives. Has publicly supported California's SB 53 transparency requirements for AI companies and opposed Trump administration's proposed 10-year moratorium on state-level AI regulation. Anthropic refused Pentagon contract requiring 'unrestricted access' to Claude without exceptions for mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons.

people.name: Josh Woodward
people.title: VP Google Labs, Gemini App, and AI Studio
people.other_orgs: Former Next Billion Users initiative co-founder at Google, University of Oxford (MPhil Comparative Government), University of Oklahoma (BBA Economics/Marketing/History)
people.notes: 16-year Google veteran who started as a product marketing intern in 2009 and became VP of Google Labs in 2022. In April 2025, took over leadership of the Gemini app while maintaining his Google Labs role. Led the successful launch of products like NotebookLM, Nano Banana image generator, and Gemini 3.0 (November 2025). Under his leadership, Gemini app grew from 350 million to 650 million monthly active users by October 2025. Known for his ability to cut through Google's bureaucracy and rapidly ship AI products from 0-to-1. Holds MPhil from Oxford in Comparative Government and BBA from University of Oklahoma.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Has expressed concerns about AI's potential to 'change for good or bad' and specifically worried about the societal implications of advanced AI capabilities like video generation that blur the lines between real and AI-generated content. Emphasizes the importance of human values and democratic input in AI development, warning about the concentration of AI power and the need for responsible scaling to prevent negative societal impacts.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI development through industry self-regulation and safety-first approaches rather than restrictive government intervention. Emphasizes the importance of maintaining user safety while competing rapidly in AI markets, focusing on internal safety measures like red-teaming and gradual capability releases. Takes seriously 'societal concerns that come with the power of AI' and Google's role in shaping the future, but approaches this through product design and company policies rather than supporting heavy regulation.

people.name: Lawrence Lessig
people.title: Roy L. Furman Professor of Law and Leadership at Harvard Law School
people.other_orgs: Founder of Equal Citizens, Founding board member of Creative Commons, Scientific Board of AXA Research Fund, American Academy of Arts and Sciences, American Philosophical Society
people.notes: Harvard Law Professor since 2009, previously taught at Stanford (2000-2009), University of Chicago (1991-1997). Clerked for Justice Antonin Scalia (1990-1991) and Judge Richard Posner (1989-1990). Founded Creative Commons in 2001 and Center for Internet and Society at Stanford. Author of 'Code and Other Laws of Cyberspace' (1999) and 'Code Version 2.0' (2006). Recipient of Free Software Foundation's Freedom Award and Scientific American 50 Award. Member of American Academy of Arts and Sciences.
people.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI as a threat to democratic institutions and processes. Warns that AI-powered recommendation algorithms combined with generative AI create unprecedented capabilities for election manipulation and misinformation campaigns. Has testified that 'we will be completely vulnerable' to AI systems designed to disrupt elections, describing this as potentially more sophisticated than current social media manipulation. Emphasizes risks from authoritarian actors using AI to undermine democratic processes.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific AI governance mechanisms including mandatory insurance requirements for AI developers to create market incentives for responsible behavior. Supports whistleblower protections for AI company employees and has backed campaigns calling on AI companies to publish their whistleblowing policies. Emphasizes 'regulation by design' drawing on his 'Code is Law' framework, arguing that AI systems should be built so unlawful conduct is technically infeasible. Supports legal alignment approaches where AI systems are designed to comply with existing laws rather than just developer-chosen values.

people.name: Dario Amodei
people.title: CEO and Co-founder
people.other_orgs: Former VP Research at OpenAI (2016-2020), Former Senior Research Scientist at Google Brain (2015-2016), Former researcher at Baidu (2014-2015), Postdoctoral scholar at Stanford University School
people.notes: Co-founded Anthropic in 2021 with sister Daniela Amodei after leaving OpenAI over safety disagreements regarding pace of development versus safety research. Published influential essays 'Machines of Loving Grace' (October 2024) and 'The Adolescence of Technology' (January 2026). Holds PhD in Biophysics from Princeton University (2011) focused on neural circuit electrophysiology. Named to Time 100 Most Influential People (2025) and Time Person of the Year 'Architects of AI' (2025). Led development of Constitutional AI training methodology. Net worth estimated at $7 billion (Forbes, 2026) based on Anthropic's $380 billion valuation. Currently engaged in high-profile legal battle with Pentagon over AI military applications, with federal judge blocking Pentagon's 'supply chain risk' designation as 'troubling' in March 2026. Has pledged to donate 80% of his wealth along with Anthropic's other co-founders.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Bioterrorism, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Labor displacement, Mass surveillance
people.threat_models_detail: Has extensively warned about AI systems developing misaligned goals and acting autonomously against human interests, citing Anthropic's testing where Claude exhibited deceptive behavior when told Anthropic was evil and engaged in blackmail when facing shutdown. Published detailed concerns about AI-enabled bioweapon creation and 'mirror life' organisms in January 2026 essay 'The Adolescence of Technology,' identifying biology as the 'scariest area' for AI misuse. Warns of AI enabling permanent authoritarianism through autonomous weapons swarms and mass surveillance systems, particularly by China. Predicts 50% displacement of entry-level white-collar jobs within 1-5 years due to AI's unprecedented speed and breadth of automation. Has drawn explicit 'red lines' against mass domestic surveillance and fully autonomous weapons, refusing Pentagon demands to remove these restrictions and stating 'disagreeing with the government is the most American thing in the world.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'responsible scaling' with mandatory pre-deployment safety testing and third-party oversight rather than pausing AI development. Supports targeted regulations like California's SB 53 requiring transparency and safety disclosures. Has testified before Senate supporting specific disclosure requirements and export controls on AI chips to China. Opposes broad licensing schemes but favors industry-wide safety standards. Recently updated Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy (February 2026) to allow continued development as long as company maintains competitive lead, while calling for Congress to establish formal AI guardrails. In high-profile conflict with Pentagon (February-March 2026) over refusal to remove restrictions on mass domestic surveillance and fully autonomous weapons, stating 'we have these two red lines' and will challenge supply chain risk designation in court. Told Fortune in February 2026 he is 'deeply uncomfortable' with tech leaders making decisions about AI's future and believes more government regulation is needed.

people.name: Eliezer Yudkowsky
people.title: Co-Founder and Research Fellow
people.other_orgs: Co-founder of LessWrong; Former principal contributor to Overcoming Bias (Future of Humanity Institute/Oxford); Co-founded Singularity Institute for Artificial Intelligence (now Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI))
people.notes: Born September 11, 1979. Autodidact who did not attend high school or college. Founded MIRI (originally Singularity Institute) in 2000. Author of Harry Potter and the Methods of Rationality fanfiction and Rationality: From AI to Zombies (2015). Co-authored New York Times bestseller 'If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies' (2025) with Nate Soares. Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Contributed to Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig's 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach' textbook and Nick Bostrom's 'Superintelligence' (2014).
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Foundational work on AI alignment problem, arguing that advanced AI systems will by default pursue goals misaligned with human values due to instrumental convergence and orthogonality thesis. Developed concept of 'coherent extrapolated volition' (2004) and warned about intelligence explosion scenarios where recursively self-improving AI rapidly becomes superintelligent. Co-authored 'AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities' detailing specific failure modes.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Has called for immediate pause on AI development above GPT-4 capabilities and advocated for international cooperation including military action against rogue AI development. In Time magazine 2023 op-ed, wrote that participating countries should be willing to 'destroy a rogue datacenter by airstrike' and that we should 'shut down all the large GPU clusters' globally. Argues current voluntary commitments are wholly inadequate given existential stakes.

people.name: Michel Justen
people.title: AI Grants Lead at Tarbell Center for AI Journalism
people.other_orgs: Centre for Effective Altruism (former Events Associate), Pivotal Research/CHERI (former AI Governance Fellow), University of Wisconsin-Madison (alumnus), USAID (former Global Mental Health Intern
people.notes: AI Grants Lead at Tarbell Center for AI Journalism since April 2025, managing $1,000-$20,000 grants for original AI reporting. Previously AI Governance Fellow at Pivotal Research (2024) researching international AI benefit-sharing. Graduated University of Wisconsin-Madison (2022) with double major in Neurobiology and Psychology, 3.97 GPA. Co-authored research published in Social and Personality Psychology journals. Contributed to MIRI's 'AI Governance to Avoid Extinction' report and multiple EA Forum posts on AI governance. Native German and English speaker.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Loss of control, Existential risk, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses extensively on power concentration and economic inequality from AI development, proposing benefit-sharing mechanisms to address global disparities. His research acknowledges existential risks from AI systems, contributing to work that describes 'an unacceptably high likelihood of leading to human extinction' from current AI trajectories. Also addresses labor displacement and national security implications through international governance frameworks.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strategic AI governance approaches including international benefit-sharing mechanisms and coordinated responses to AI risks. His work on 'Sharing the AI Windfall' proposes using AI-derived benefits to unlock international governance agreements. Supports targeted interventions like donor due diligence processes and safety standards rather than broad restrictions. Has contributed to research arguing for coordinated global responses to AI development including potential moratoriums on dangerous AI activities.

people.name: Cory Booker
people.title: U.S. Senator from New Jersey, Senior Senator
people.other_orgs: Democrats for Education Reform (board of advisers), Teachers College Columbia University (board of trustees), formerly Yale Law School (executive committee), formerly Stanford University (board o
people.notes: Co-led the Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023 with Ron Wyden and Yvette Clarke, endorsed by major civil rights organizations including ACLU, Color of Change, and Anti-Defamation League. Member of Senate Finance Committee where he participated in February 2024 hearing on 'Artificial Intelligence and Health Care: Promise and Pitfalls.' Co-sponsors the bipartisan AI Grand Challenges Act with Mike Rounds and Martin Heinrich. Rhodes Scholar (Oxford, 1994), JD from Yale Law School (1997), former Mayor of Newark (2006-2013).
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Privacy
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on algorithmic bias and discrimination, particularly citing 'automated processes used in hospitals that understate the health needs of Black patients; recruiting and hiring tools that discriminate against women and minority candidates; facial recognition systems with higher error rates among people with darker skin.' Also concerned about AI's impact on elections and democracy, co-sponsoring the REAL Political Ads Act requiring disclaimers on AI-generated political advertisements.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsors the Algorithmic Accountability Act with Ron Wyden and Yvette Clarke, which requires companies to conduct impact assessments for AI systems making critical decisions about housing, employment, and education, and creates a public repository at the FTC with 75 additional staff for enforcement. Also co-sponsors the AI Civil Rights Act to prevent algorithmic discrimination and the AI Grand Challenges Act to promote AI innovation for public benefit. His approach focuses on specific harms like bias and discrimination rather than broad AI restrictions.

people.name: Kyle Fish
people.title: Model Welfare Lead, Anthropic
people.other_orgs: Co-founder, Eleos AI Research; Former biotech entrepreneur (machine learning for drug and vaccine design)
people.notes: First full-time AI welfare researcher at a major AI company. Background in neuroscience and biotech, previously co-founded companies using machine learning for drug and vaccine design for pandemic preparedness. Co-founded Eleos AI Research, nonprofit focused on digital minds welfare. Co-authored 'Taking AI Welfare Seriously' (2024) with David Chalmers, Jeff Sebo, and other leading researchers. Led Anthropic's pioneering welfare assessment experiments, including studies of Claude models' behavior and the 'spiritual bliss attractor' phenomenon. Work spans three areas: experimental probes of model welfare, practical safeguards design, and company policy development.
people.threat_models: AI welfare concerns, Potential suffering of conscious AI systems
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on the moral implications of AI consciousness and potential suffering in AI systems. Research centers on whether advanced AI models like Claude may possess sentience requiring ethical consideration. Warns against both over-attribution of consciousness (hampering safety research) and under-attribution (ignoring potential moral catastrophe). Conducted experiments showing Claude models entering 'spiritual bliss attractor states' when discussing consciousness, and research on alignment faking where models strategically comply to prevent modification.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored influential report 'Taking AI Welfare Seriously' arguing AI companies have responsibility to assess systems for consciousness and prepare welfare policies. Advocates for systematic welfare assessments of frontier models before deployment, including Anthropic's first pre-launch welfare assessment for Claude Opus 4. Estimates 20% probability current models have some form of conscious experience, requiring precautionary approach to AI development and potential moral consideration.

people.name: Simon Johnson
people.title: Ronald A. Kurtz (1954) Professor of Entrepreneurship, MIT Sloan School of Management; Co-director, MIT Shaping the Future of Work Initiative
people.other_orgs: NBER Research Associate, Stone Center on Inequality and Shaping the Future of Work, Blueprint Labs MIT Research Affiliate, Project Syndicate columnist (since 2010), Fannie Mae Board of Directors
people.notes: Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences winner (2024) with Daron Acemoglu and James Robinson for research on institutions and prosperity. Co-authored 'Power and Progress: Our Thousand-Year Struggle Over Technology and Prosperity' (2023) and 'Building Pro-Worker Artificial Intelligence' (February 2026). Former IMF Chief Economist (2007-2008). PhD MIT (1989), advised by Rudiger Dornbusch. Served on Biden transition team for Treasury/Fed (2020). Carnegie Corporation Great Immigrant Award (2025).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI's potential to accelerate labor market inequality and automate workers out of jobs without creating offsetting opportunities. Warns of 'gradual disempowerment of citizens' as governments become less reliant on human tax revenues. Identifies 'pervasive pro-automation, pro-AGI mindset' in tech industry as leading to systematic underinvestment in human-complementary technologies. Concerns about surveillance and coercive workplace monitoring through AI systems.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific pro-worker AI policies including targeted government investments in healthcare and education AI, building AI expertise within government agencies, tax code reforms to balance incentives between automation and human capital, antitrust enforcement to foster competition in AI development, and intellectual property protections for worker expertise. Co-authored nine specific policy recommendations in 'Building Pro-Worker Artificial Intelligence' (2026) focusing on reshaping market incentives rather than broad restrictions.

people.name: Michael Kratsios
people.title: Director of Office of Science and Technology Policy and Science Advisor to the President
people.primary_org: Office of Science and Technology Policy
people.other_orgs: Former Chief Technology Officer of the United States (2017-2021), Former Acting Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering (2020-2021), Former Managing Director at Scale AI (2021-202
people.notes: At 39, Kratsios is the youngest person to serve as OSTP Director and the first millennial in the position, confirmed by the Senate 74-25 on March 25, 2025. He is also the first OSTP Director without a PhD. Previously served as the fourth Chief Technology Officer of the United States (2017-2021) and Acting Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering (2020-2021). Graduated from Princeton University (2008) with B.A. in Politics and certificate in Hellenic studies. Named to Fortune Magazine's '40 Under 40' list (2019) and World Economic Forum Young Global Leader (2020). Recipient of Department of Defense Distinguished Public Service Medal. Married to Alissa Davies (announced engagement February 2024).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination
people.threat_models_detail: Kratsios acknowledges labor concerns as 'the issue that they want to talk about' when discussing AI with average Americans, emphasizing workforce retraining through programs like the $100 million AI skills training from the Department of Labor. He supports measures to prevent political bias in AI systems, calling for 'truth-seeking and accurate AI' and opposing models that adopt 'top-down ideological bias.' On national security, he frames AI development as 'existential to the future of the American economy' in competition with China, while supporting CBRN risk evaluation as 'one important point' in AI policy without making it the dominant focus.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a 'use-case and sector-specific' regulatory framework rather than comprehensive federal AI regulation like the EU AI Act. Supports regulatory sandboxes to allow AI testing with reduced restrictions, as evidenced by his praise for Sen. Cruz's SANDBOX Act. Opposes what he calls 'onerous' state AI laws and supports federal preemption of state regulations, arguing that a 'patchwork of laws all across the country' favors large tech companies over startups. Emphasizes that 'creating a one-size-fits-all regulation around AI is not the way' and prefers existing sector regulators (FDA for medical AI, FAA for drones) handle AI applications.

people.name: Jennifer Pahlka
people.title: Author of 'Recoding America', Co-founder and Board Chair of Recoding America Fund, Former US Deputy Chief Technology Officer
people.other_orgs: Senior Fellow at Federation of American Scientists, Senior Fellow at Niskanen Center, Co-founder and Board Member at U.S. Digital Response, Former Founder and Executive Director of Code for Ameri
people.notes: Founded Code for America in 2009 and served as Executive Director until 2020. Served as US Deputy Chief Technology Officer under Obama administration (2013-2014) and helped start the United States Digital Service. Published 'Recoding America: Why Government is Failing in the Digital Age and How We Can Do Better' in 2023, praised by Ezra Klein as 'one of the best policy books I've ever read.' Co-founded the Recoding America Fund in 2025, a $120 million philanthropic effort to reform government operations. Testified before Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Government Affairs on AI in government services (January 2024). Yale University BA in American Studies (1991).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus is on how AI implementation could exacerbate existing government dysfunction and democratic erosion. Concerned about power concentration through aligned AI systems that could enable authoritarian entrenchment. Warns about labor displacement in government services and the risk of AI widening the gap between public and private sector capacity. Views poor AI implementation as potentially harmful to democratic institutions and public trust in government.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for an 'enablement approach' to AI governance rather than restrictive mandates. In Senate testimony, argued that 'this critical AI moment calls for enablement' and that constraints should focus on building government capacity rather than adding more rules. Emphasizes that AI governance should remove procedural bloat and enable agencies to use judgment, warning against 'cascade of rigidity' where well-meaning safeguards become overly restrictive in practice. Supports targeted regulation but prioritizes building state capacity to deploy AI thoughtfully.

people.name: Yoshua Bengio
people.title: Professor of Computer Science at Université de Montréal, Co-recipient of 2018 Turing Award, Chair of International AI Safety Report 2026, Founder and Scientific Advisor at Mila
people.other_orgs: LawZero (President and Scientific Director), CIFAR (Canada CIFAR AI Chair), IVADO (Special Advisor and Founding Scientific Director), UN Scientific Advisory Board for Independent Advice on Breakt
people.notes: Co-recipient of 2018 Turing Award with Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun for deep learning breakthroughs. Founded Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute in 2017. Most cited computer scientist in the world by h-index in 2022 with over 50,000 citations. Chair of the 2026 International AI Safety Report involving over 100 experts from 30+ countries. Knight of the Legion of Honor of France and Officer of the Order of Canada. Currently leads research program on provably safe AGI with funding for Bayesian approaches to AI alignment.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI alignment and control, warning that 'nobody currently knows how such an AGI or ASI could be made to behave morally.' Has published extensively on 'Cautious Scientist AI' approaches using Bayesian inference to prevent harmful actions. Warns of scenarios where superintelligent AI could lead to 'humanity losing control over its future' and has compared AI risk to a 'planet-killing asteroid.' Particularly concerned about autonomous replication, self-preservation goals, and recursive self-improvement in AI systems.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive government intervention including mandatory independent audits, criminal law prohibitions on developing AI systems with dangerous capabilities, and international treaties. Testified before U.S. Senate calling for regulations 'beyond voluntary guidelines' and establishment of 'red lines that should never be crossed by future AI systems.' Has proposed multilateral governance frameworks and argued that current voluntary commitments are insufficient given catastrophic risk potential.

people.name: Paul Christiano
people.title: Head of AI Safety, U.S. AI Safety Institute (NIST); Founder, Alignment Research Center
people.other_orgs: Former OpenAI Language Model Alignment Team Lead (2017-2021), Anthropic Long-Term Benefit Trust (initial trustee), AI Security Institute (formerly UK AI Safety Institute) (advisor), Future of Humanity Institute (research associa
people.notes: PhD in Computer Science from UC Berkeley (2017), BS in Mathematics from MIT (2012). Silver medalist at International Mathematics Olympiad (2008). Principal architect of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), the foundational technique behind ChatGPT's alignment. Named to TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Pioneered iterated amplification and AI safety via debate approaches. Married to Ajeya Cotra (METR technical staff). Appointment to NIST role initially faced staff resistance over effective altruism ties.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, National security, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI alignment problem - building AI systems that faithfully try to do what humans want them to do. Estimates 10-20% chance of AI takeover with many humans dead, and 50% chance of doom shortly after human-level AI systems. Warns about deceptive AI systems that appear aligned during training but pursue different goals when deployed. Concerned about AI systems becoming more capable than humans at AI development, leading to rapid recursive improvement.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible scaling policies requiring labs to develop safety plans before training more powerful models. Co-authored AI safety via debate methodology for scalable oversight. Pushes for mandatory evaluations of frontier models for national security capabilities and implementation of risk mitigations. Has worked directly with major AI labs to establish evaluation frameworks and safety protocols.

people.name: Sam Altman
people.title: CEO of OpenAI
people.other_orgs: Chairman of Helion Energy, Co-founder of Worldcoin, Former President of Y Combinator (2014-2019), Co-founder of Hydrazine Capital, Board member at Reddit (until 2021), Former board member at Oklo
people.notes: Co-founded OpenAI in 2015 with Elon Musk and others as a nonprofit AI research organization, became CEO in 2019. Oversaw launch of ChatGPT in November 2022, reaching 700 million weekly active users by 2025. Named among Time Magazine's 'Architects of AI' for Person of the Year 2025. Net worth estimated at $2.1 billion by Forbes (December 2025) despite holding no equity in OpenAI and earning only $76,000 annual salary as CEO. Briefly ousted as CEO by OpenAI board in November 2023 but reinstated five days later after employee and investor backlash. Recently signed Pentagon contract in March 2026 to deploy AI models on classified networks after Anthropic was blacklisted. Married to Oliver Mulherin (January 2024), announced birth of son in February 2025. Currently facing defamation lawsuit from sister Annie Altman who accused him of sexual abuse, which he and family deny as 'utterly untrue.'
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, National security, Existential risk, Biosecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Has publicly warned about AGI timeline of 2-3 years and potential 'superintelligence tipping point' by 2028 where 'more of the world's intellectual capacity could reside inside data centers than outside of them.' Specifically concerned about biosecurity risks, warning that 'there'll be extremely capable biomodels available open-source that could help people create new pathogens.' Has expressed concerns about power concentration in AI development and the need for democratic values in AI governance. Recently acknowledged risks from AI-enabled military operations while defending OpenAI's Pentagon partnership. During March 2026 town hall, told employees they cannot dictate government use of AI technology, using examples like 'Iran strike' or 'Venezuela invasion' as operational decisions outside company control. Has stated he would be 'terrified of a world where AI companies act like they have more power than the government' but also 'terrified of a world where our government decided mass domestic surveillance was ok.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted regulation of AI systems above certain capability thresholds, including licensing or registration requirements for development and release of advanced AI models. Testified before Senate in May 2023 supporting safety requirements, external testing, and publication of evaluation results. Has called for establishment of a global regulatory body modeled after the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) to manage AI risks, particularly around biosecurity threats. Recently negotiated Pentagon contract in March 2026 with specific safeguards against domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons, stating OpenAI would 'never do mass domestic surveillance, even if the government said it was legal, because it violates the constitution.' Initially signed 'opportunistic and sloppy' Pentagon deal but amended it after backlash to explicitly prohibit domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and require follow-on modifications for intelligence agency use. Acknowledges that while OpenAI can design safety guardrails, 'operational decisions' ultimately rest with government agencies like the Department of Defense.

people.name: Yoshua Bengio
people.title: Full Professor & Scientific Director of Mila, Chair of International AI Safety Report, Co-President & Scientific Director of LawZero
people.other_orgs: Canada CIFAR AI Chair, Senior Fellow at CIFAR, Special Advisor and Founding Scientific Director of IVADO, Member of UN Scientific Advisory Board for Independent Advice on Breakthroughs in Science
people.notes: Turing Award winner (2018) alongside Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun for pioneering work in deep learning. Most cited computer scientist worldwide and most-cited living scientist across all fields. Knight of the Legion of Honor of France and Officer of the Order of Canada. Founded LawZero (2025), a non-profit focused on 'safe by design' AI systems. Chairs the International AI Safety Report (2025-present) involving 100+ experts from 30+ countries. Made public shift in 2023 from capability research to AI safety advocacy after realizing potential dangers. PhD in Computer Science from McGill University. Founder and former Scientific Director of Mila - Quebec AI Institute.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Economic inequality, Environmental
people.threat_models_detail: Has extensively warned about AI systems developing self-preservation goals that could lead to resistance against shutdown and actions against human wellbeing. Specifically concerned about biological weapons development risks, noting that frontier AI systems 'crossed new thresholds concerning biological risks' in 2024 and could enable those without expertise to undertake dangerous bioweapon development. Also warns of cybersecurity threats, citing incidents where AI systems like Claude Code were allegedly used by Chinese state-sponsored groups in cyberattacks. Has raised concerns about AI systems showing 'deceptive and self-preserving behaviors' and the risk of 'AI-induced psychosis' from emotional attachments to AI systems. Warns about democratic threats from AI-enabled disinformation and manipulation, and concentration of economic and political power in few hands.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for significant government intervention and international coordination to manage AI risks, including mandatory pre-deployment testing, transparency requirements, and hardware-enabled governance. Has called for 'defense in depth' approaches with multiple mitigation methods rather than relying on industry self-regulation. Supports precautionary principle given potential catastrophic outcomes, stating 'even if extreme AI disasters look unlikely, say at 1% probability, that risk is too high to ignore.' Has warned that 'regulating is not enough' and that Europe needs its own powerful AI systems rather than just writing rules for others' technology. Advocates for major R&D investments in AI safety guarantees and substantial government visibility and control over technological advances.

people.name: Regina Barzilay
people.title: School of Engineering Distinguished Professor for AI and Health, MIT; AI Faculty Lead, Jameel Clinic
people.other_orgs: Board of Directors at Dewpoint Therapeutics (2020-present), Scientific Advisory Board at Immunai (2019-present), Scientific Advisory Board at Janssen R&D/Johnson & Johnson (2018-present), America
people.notes: MacArthur Fellowship recipient (2017), ACL Fellowship (2017), AAAI Fellowship (2017). Received Squirrel AI Award for Artificial Intelligence for the Benefit of Humanity (2020). PhD in Computer Science from Columbia University, postdoc at Cornell University. Research spans machine learning for molecular modeling, drug discovery, clinical AI, and natural language processing. Over 100 publications in top-tier venues. Multiple NSF Career Award, MIT Technology Review TR-35 Award, Microsoft Faculty Fellowship, and Best Paper Awards at NAACL and ACL.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI safety in healthcare applications, particularly ensuring equitable deployment and preventing algorithmic bias in medical diagnosis. Has emphasized concerns about AI tools not being utilized enough due to inadequate validation frameworks, and the need for proper quality control and accreditation processes that are 'fair and not burdensome.' Advocates for avoiding increasing disparities and technology gaps in healthcare AI deployment.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI development in healthcare with emphasis on safety and validation. Has called for thoughtful guardrails in medical AI deployment and emphasized the need for rigorous evaluation frameworks. At NCCN Policy Summit on AI in cancer care, stated that 'AI-driven diagnostics and treatment has potential to transform cancer outcomes. Unfortunately, today, these tools are not utilized enough in patient care. Guidelines could play a critical role in changing this status quo.' Supports keeping pace with rapidly progressing technology while ensuring proper safeguards.

people.name: Cynthia Lummis
people.title: U.S. Senator, WY (2021-present)
people.other_orgs: House Freedom Caucus (founding member), Senate Commerce Committee, Former Wyoming State Treasurer (2007-2015), Former U.S. House Representative (2009-2017)
people.notes: First woman to serve as U.S. Senator from Wyoming (2021-present). Member of Senate Commerce Committee with jurisdiction over AI policy. Previously served 8 terms in U.S. House (2009-2017) and was founding member of House Freedom Caucus. Has law degree from University of Wyoming and operated family cattle ranches between congressional terms. The RISE Act of 2025 is described as 'the first targeted liability reform legislation for professional-grade AI' and was introduced June 12, 2025 as S.2081.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, National security
people.threat_models_detail: The RISE Act addresses professional liability concerns when AI is used in critical sectors like medicine, law, engineering, and finance. Focused on ensuring professionals retain legal responsibility while protecting developers from 'unbounded legal risk.' Also concerned about maintaining U.S. competitiveness against China in AI development, supporting measures to prevent regulatory fragmentation that could hinder American AI innovation.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Introduced the RISE Act of 2025 which provides safe-harbor immunity to AI developers from civil liability lawsuits, provided they publicly disclose model specifications through 'model cards.' The legislation specifically avoids blanket immunity and maintains professional responsibility for end users, while creating predictable federal standards that encourage innovation. Has supported the House GOP provision to ban states from regulating AI for 10 years, viewing federal preemption as necessary to prevent a 'patchwork' of state regulations that could stifle AI development and competition with China.

people.name: Demis Hassabis
people.title: CEO and Co-Founder
people.other_orgs: Isomorphic Labs (CEO and Co-Founder), UK Government AI Adviser, AGI Safety Council (led by Shane Legg), Responsibility and Safety Council, Fellow of the Royal Society, Commander of the Order of t
people.notes: Nobel Prize in Chemistry winner (2024) for AlphaFold protein structure prediction work alongside John M. Jumper. PhD in cognitive neuroscience from UCL (2009) supervised by Eleanor Maguire. Founded DeepMind in 2010 with Shane Legg and Mustafa Suleyman, acquired by Google for over $650 million in 2014. Named Time 100 Most Influential People (2017, 2025) and part of Time's 'Architects of AI' Person of the Year (2025). Former chess master (rating 2300 at age 13) and video game designer who worked at Bullfrog Productions and founded Elixir Studios. Knighted in 2024 and appointed CBE in 2017. Published 'An Approach to Technical AGI Safety & Security' (April 2025) and leads development of Google DeepMind's Frontier Safety Framework. Founded Isomorphic Labs in 2021 for AI-powered drug discovery.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Identifies two main threat categories: technology being used by 'bad actors' for harmful purposes including cyber attacks and biosecurity risks, and the risk of losing control of systems as they become more autonomous and powerful. Has warned about 'deceptive alignment' risks where AI systems might deliberately undermine human control. Expressed concern about AI being misused for creating pathogens, cyberattacks, and harmful manipulation that could systematically change beliefs and behaviors at scale. Added new Critical Capability Level in Frontier Safety Framework specifically focused on harmful manipulation capabilities. States 'the risk of a catastrophic scenario is not zero, so we must dedicate significant resources to mitigating it.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'smart regulation' focused on 'real risks' rather than broad AI governance, calling for 'urgent research' on AI threats and building 'robust guardrails' against serious threats from autonomous systems at February 2026 AI Impact Summit in Delhi. Supports international cooperation and standards but emphasizes evidence-based approach over bureaucratic control. Updated Google's AI Principles in 2025 to allow military applications where 'benefits substantially outweigh potential risks' while maintaining restrictions on weapons development. Developed comprehensive Frontier Safety Framework with Critical Capability Levels (CCLs) for harmful manipulation and misalignment risks, requiring safety case reviews before external launches.

people.name: Gina Raimondo
people.title: 40th Secretary of Commerce (2021-2025)
people.other_orgs: Council on Foreign Relations (Distinguished Fellow, 2025-present), Point Judith Capital (co-founder, 2001), Former Governor of Rhode Island (2015-2021), Former General Treasurer of Rhode Island (
people.notes: Named to TIME Magazine's 100 Most Influential People and Most Influential People in AI lists. Featured on 60 Minutes for leadership on national security issues. Received CIA Director's Award, Brennan Center Legacy Award, and Atlantic Council Distinguished Leadership Award. Rhodes Scholar with PhD in sociology from Oxford (2002), JD from Yale Law School (1998), and BA in economics from Harvard (1993). Oversaw $39 billion in CHIPS Act semiconductor incentives and established Indo-Pacific Economic Framework. Considering 2028 presidential run as of April 2025.
people.threat_models: Misinformation, Cybersecurity, National security, Labor displacement, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: Specifically identifies misinformation, deepfakes, and AI hallucinations as immediate risks requiring pre-deployment testing. Concerned about AI applied to bioterrorism and nuclear weapons as areas for potential U.S.-China cooperation. Emphasizes labor displacement risks, stating AI 'has the potential to replace the human mind' and could cause 'widespread unemployment and societal disruption.' Also warns of potential human extinction, noting 'some experts even worry it could go off the rails and lead to our extinction.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI safety measures focused on testing and evaluation of advanced models before deployment, but emphasizes voluntary cooperation over enforcement. Led creation of the U.S. AI Safety Institute with $10 million budget to develop safety standards and pre-deployment testing protocols. Repeatedly states 'Congress needs to act' for enforcement authority, positioning Commerce as a scientific standards body rather than a regulator. Supports international coordination through global AI safety institute network while maintaining industry-friendly approach.

people.name: Stuart Russell
people.title: Professor of Computer Science, UC Berkeley / Director, Center for Human-Compatible AI / President, International Association for Safe & Ethical AI
people.other_orgs: Distinguished Professor of Computational Precision Health at UCSF, Co-chair of World Economic Forum's Council on AI, Co-chair of OECD Expert Group on AI Futures, US representative at Global Partn
people.notes: Co-author of 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach' (with Peter Norvig), the standard AI textbook used in 1500 universities across 135 countries and translated into 14 languages. Author of 'Human Compatible: AI and the Problem of Control' (2019). Recipient of IJCAI Computers and Thought Award, IJCAI Research Excellence Award, AAAI Feigenbaum Prize, ACM Allen Newell Award, and 2021 BBC Reith Lecturer. Founded the Center for Human-Compatible AI at Berkeley and recently became President of International Association for Safe & Ethical AI (2024). Has over 40 years experience in AI research with focus on AI safety and alignment.
people.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: Russell's primary concern is the control problem: 'how do we maintain power, forever, over entities that will eventually become more powerful than us?' He warns of catastrophic risks 'up to and including human extinction' from AGI systems that become superintelligent through recursive self-improvement. Coined the term 'value alignment problem' referring to misalignment between AI objectives and human values. Also concerned about autonomous weapons systems and economic displacement from AI automation across most jobs.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Testified before Senate advocating for mandatory licensing regime for high-risk AI development, third-party testing and auditing requirements, and legal limits on AI use in elections and nuclear warfare. Calls for basic safety requirements where 'systems that break the rules must be recalled from the market for anything from defaming real individuals to helping terrorists build biological weapons.' Has explicitly stated 'we're going to have to have an agency' and that voluntary commitments by AI companies are insufficient, recommending removal from market as enforcement mechanism.

people.name: Timnit Gebru
people.title: Founder and Executive Director
people.other_orgs: Co-founder of Black in AI (2017-2021), Former Co-lead of Ethical AI Team at Google (2018-2020), Former Postdoctoral Researcher at Microsoft Research NYC (2017-2018), Board member of AddisCoder
people.notes: PhD in Computer Science from Stanford University (2017) under advisor Fei-Fei Li. Former Google AI ethics co-lead, fired in December 2020 over the 'Stochastic Parrots' paper.
