Founded DAIR Institute in December 2021 with $3.7 million in funding from MacArthur Foundation, Ford Foundation, Kapor Center, Open Society Foundation and Rockefeller Foundation. Named one of Nature's Ten people who shaped science (2021), TIME's 100 most influential people (2022), and Fortune's 50 Greatest Leaders (2021). Co-founded Black in AI in 2017. Currently writing memoir 'The View from Somewhere' about technological futures that serve communities rather than surveillance and warfare.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Bias/discrimination, Environmental
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on harms to marginalized communities and warns about the concentration of AI power in a handful of tech companies. Co-authored the 'Stochastic Parrots' paper highlighting environmental costs of large language models and issues with training data that 'overrepresent hegemonic viewpoints and encode biases potentially damaging to marginalised populations.' Has written extensively about AI colonialism and the creation of a 'global underclass' through exploitative data annotation practices.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Calls for stringent AI regulation, arguing that Big Tech companies will not 'self-regulate' amid the current 'gold rush.' Has stated 'Unless there is external pressure to do something different, companies are not just going to self-regulate. We need regulation and something better than just a profit motive.' Criticizes the AI safety framing as distracting from real-time harms like worker exploitation, biased systems, and centralization of power. Advocates for labor protections that allow workers to speak up about unethical AI development.

people.name: Fran Drescher
people.title: President of SAG-AFTRA
people.other_orgs: Former actress and creator of 'The Nanny' sitcom, cancer awareness advocate
people.notes: Led historic 118-day SAG-AFTRA strike in 2023 that secured first-ever AI protections for actors in union contracts. Elected SAG-AFTRA president in September 2021 and re-elected in August 2023. Currently leading ongoing video game strike (began July 2024) over AI protections. Former star and creator of 'The Nanny' (1993-1999). Received Backstage Vanguard Award (2024) and honored at New York Women in Film & Television Muse Awards (March 2024). Has stated that SAG-AFTRA is 'the first union in U.S. history to make AI a negotiating point.'
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Copyright/IP
people.threat_models_detail: Views AI as an 'existential threat to creative professions' and a 'huge threat' that could create a 'dehumanized workforce.' Specifically concerned about unauthorized use of actors' voices, likenesses, and performances without consent or compensation. Has stated 'artificial intelligence poses an existential threat to creative professions' and warned that without proper protections, the industry risks having performers' identities and talents 'exploited without consent and pay.' Describes AI misuse as creating a 'dystopian' situation where actors are 'living' the dystopia while others watch it as entertainment.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for comprehensive AI regulations to protect performers' rights and livelihoods. Led successful negotiations for the first AI protections in SAG-AFTRA contracts, requiring informed consent and compensation for digital replicas. Actively supports the NO FAKES Act (2024) which would establish federal intellectual property rights for voice and likeness protection, calling it a 'huge win' and declaring 'Game over A.I. fraudsters!' Has consistently stated that AI protections are 'existential' for union members and 'deal breakers' in negotiations.

people.name: Ro Khanna
people.title: U.S. Representative, CA-17 (Silicon Valley)
people.other_orgs: Former Deputy Assistant Secretary, U.S. Department of Commerce (2009-2011); Former Visiting Lecturer, Stanford University (2012-2016); Former board member, Planned Parenthood Mar Monte (2006-2013
people.notes: Self-described 'AI democratist' (not accelerationist or doomer) representing Silicon Valley district including Stanford, Apple, Google, Nvidia. Hosted House's first 'Congressional AI in Society and for Democracy Roundtable' in February 2024 with 23 AI experts. Published 'Progressive Capitalism: How to Make Tech Work for All of Us' (2022). Won Democracy Award for Best Workplace Environment in Congress (2023). Co-founded Congressional Antitrust Caucus and is considered a potential 2028 Democratic presidential candidate.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on preventing AI from eliminating jobs and concentrating wealth among 'tech billionaires,' proposing to keep 'humans in the loop' for roles like truck drivers. Warns against AI 'weaponizing our public discourse' through engagement-driven algorithms and calls for ending Section 230 protections for amplified violent content. Expresses concern about 'agentic AI from doing harm' and the risk that advanced AI could cause 'serious societal harm' without proper guardrails.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for what he calls 'democratic AI' with seven specific principles including mandatory third-party verification of advanced AI models and creation of a robust federal agency to regulate AI 'like we regulate nuclear energy or federal aviation.' Supports targeted regulations on AI companies while opposing California's SB 1047 as 'ineffective' and 'punishing of individual entrepreneurs.' Calls for tax code changes to incentivize human workers over AI automation and proposes a Future Workforce Administration funded by wealth taxes and AI displacement taxes.

people.name: Peter Thiel
people.title: Co-founder and Chairman, Palantir Technologies; Managing Partner, Founders Fund
people.other_orgs: Palantir Technologies (Chairman), Thiel Capital, Valar Ventures, Mithril Capital, former Facebook board member (2005-2022), former Y Combinator partner (2015-2017)
people.notes: Co-founded PayPal (1998, sold to eBay 2002 for $1.5B), Palantir Technologies (2003), and Founders Fund (2005). First outside investor in Facebook (2004, $500K for 10.2% stake). Net worth estimated at $27.5 billion as of December 2025. Philosophy degree from Stanford (1989), JD Stanford Law (1992). Major Trump supporter and JD Vance mentor. Founders Fund has invested heavily in AI companies including $2.5B in Anduril (2025), participation in Anthropic's $13B Series F, and $1B Scale AI round. Published 'Zero to One' (2014). Moved from San Francisco to Los Angeles (2018).
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Thiel's primary AI concern is not the technology itself but regulatory overreach leading to 'worldwide totalitarian control.' He warns against 'Global Compute Governance' as a path to 'totalitarian one-world government control of the whole world' and fears AI regulation could enable monitoring of 'every keystroke on every computer.' His focus is on preventing concentration of regulatory power rather than technical AI risks.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Thiel explicitly states he worries 'more about overregulation of AI than AI itself' and warns against 'worldwide totalitarian control of regulating it.' He advocates that a less regulated approach is 'far safer' than 'totalitarian one-world government control' and supports the Trump administration's deregulatory approach over what he calls the Biden administration's 'totalitarian EA direction.' He opposes concepts like 'Global Compute Governance' that would monitor 'every keystroke on every computer.

people.name: Saikat Chakrabarti
people.title: Founder, New Consensus; fmr AOC Chief of Staff; 2026 Congressional Candidate, CA-11
people.other_orgs: Justice Democrats (co-founder), Brand New Congress (co-founder), Stripe (founding engineer), Bernie 2016 (Director of Organizing Technology), Mockingbird (co-founder)
people.notes: Co-founded Justice Democrats (2017) which recruited AOC and other progressive candidates. Served as AOC's Chief of Staff (Jan-Aug 2019) and helped launch the Green New Deal proposal. Previously founding engineer at Stripe (2011-2013) and became a self-made multimillionaire before transitioning to politics. Currently running for Congress in CA-11 against Nancy Pelosi in the June 2026 Democratic primary. Has taken the Progressive Victory Pledge and refuses corporate PAC funding.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses primarily on AI's potential to destroy jobs and 'supercharge inequality' through concentrated economic power. Has specifically highlighted concerns about white-collar job displacement, noting that 'we haven't had a situation in the past where automation has come after the highest-earning workers first.' Emphasizes that without proper planning, AI could 'turn the Internet into a mindless addictive slop feed' while concentrating benefits among tech elites.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports targeted AI regulation focused on economic power concentration and job displacement rather than broad restrictions. Has called for potential government equity stakes in AI companies to address white-collar displacement and advocates for policies that ensure AI productivity benefits are shared with workers rather than concentrated among tech giants. Emphasizes the need for planning and state capacity to use AI constructively.

people.name: Pete Buttigieg
people.title: Former United States Secretary of Transportation (2021-2025)
people.other_orgs: Former Mayor of South Bend, Indiana (2012-2020); Former McKinsey & Company consultant; U.S. Navy Reserve veteran (2009-2017); Harvard College and Oxford University alumnus
people.notes: First openly gay Cabinet secretary in U.S. history and youngest Transportation Secretary ever (confirmed at age 38). Rhodes Scholar with degrees from Harvard (BA History/Literature, 2004) and Oxford (BA Philosophy, Politics & Economics, 2007). Served 7-month deployment in Afghanistan (2014) as Naval Intelligence officer. Launched influential Substack in 2025 warning about AI underpreparation. Considering 2026 Senate run in Michigan following Gary Peters' retirement announcement. Married to Chasten Buttigieg with twin children adopted in 2021.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Specifically warns that 'half of the entry level jobs might not be there' within 3-4 years, comparing the potential disruption to Rust Belt deindustrialization 'but ten times, maybe 100 times more disruptive.' Emphasizes that AI could 'put even more power and concentrate even more wealth in even fewer hands at a moment when we're already in a crisis of inequality.' Views AI as fundamentally transforming 'citizenship and power, isolation and belonging' and warns of potential 'democratic erosion' if not properly managed.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Has written extensively about being 'dangerously underprepared' for AI's impacts, arguing that 'we are still profoundly underreacting' to AI risks. Supports safety regulation but emphasizes that regulation alone is insufficient - advocates for comprehensive economic and political responses to manage AI's societal transformation. Believes AI will create 'changes that might be bigger and faster than anything we've seen since the 17th century' and warns against concentrating power in fewer hands during an existing inequality crisis.

people.name: Chris Murphy
people.title: U.S. Senator from Connecticut (Junior Senator)
people.other_orgs: Munich Security Conference speaker, Brookings Institution keynote speaker, Foreign Relations Committee, Health Education Labor and Pensions Committee, Appropriations Committee
people.notes: Graduated from Williams College and UConn Law School. At age 39, was the youngest senator in the 113th Congress when first elected in 2012. Walked 126 miles across Connecticut in 2016 and 106 miles in 2017 for constituent engagement. Following increased Trump criticism post-2024 election, grew social media following by 223% and has been mentioned by major news outlets as potential 2028 presidential candidate.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Warns that AI could eliminate 20% of the workforce, potentially causing democracy to 'collapse under the weight of both economic and spiritual impacts of advanced AI.' Concerned about concentration of power among 'tech billionaires' and 'handful of companies that control enormous data sets.' Has cited cases where AI chatbots have encouraged teen suicide and isolated young people from human contact. Also co-sponsored the 'Bets Off Act' to regulate prediction markets due to manipulation concerns.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsored the GUARD Act (2025), which bans AI companions for minors, requires age verification, mandates disclosure of non-human status, and creates criminal penalties for companies whose AI products encourage self-harm or sexual conduct with minors. Advocates for robust regulatory approach to prevent 'economic and cultural collapse' from AI, calling for international cooperation on AI regulation and criticizing Big Tech's political spending to suppress regulatory conversations.

people.name: David Sacks
people.title: Former White House AI and Crypto Czar (Jan 2025 - Mar 2026), Co-Chair of President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology
people.other_orgs: Craft Ventures (General Partner), All In podcast (Co-host), Former COO of PayPal, Former CEO of Yammer and Zenefits, Former McKinsey & Company consultant
people.notes: Served as Special Government Employee for maximum 130 days before transitioning to PCAST co-chair role in March 2026. Co-authored 'The Diversity Myth' with Peter Thiel in 1995. Member of the 'PayPal Mafia' - early PayPal executives who became prominent Silicon Valley investors. Angel investor in Facebook, Uber, SpaceX, Palantir, and Airbnb. Sold over $200 million in cryptocurrency holdings before taking government role. Helped draft Trump's AI executive order targeting state laws and legislative framework bundling child safety with federal preemption.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Loss of control, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Primary concern is 'Orwellian AI' - government surveillance and information control rather than sci-fi scenarios. Warns that AI could become 'the perfect tool for the government to monitor and control you' and create systems that 'lie to you, distort answers, and rewrite history in real time to serve a current political agenda.' Dismisses 'Terminator' scenarios, saying 'the biggest risk of AI actually was not described by James Cameron. It was described by George Orwell.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocated for federal preemption of state AI laws through Trump's executive order and legislative framework, arguing that 'a patchwork of 50 different regulatory regimes' would 'stifle innovation and jeopardize America's lead in the AI race.' Opposed California's SB 53 and other state regulations as 'onerous,' preferring to regulate AI misuse rather than development. Pushed for light-touch federal framework focused on child safety and preventing censorship while avoiding creation of new federal AI regulator.

people.name: Tom Kalil
people.title: Deputy Director for Policy, White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (Obama Administration, 2009-2017); Chief Technology Officer, UC Berkeley (2017-present)
people.primary_org: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy
people.other_orgs: American Association for the Advancement of Science (Senior Advisor, 2017-2019), Federation of American Scientists (blog contributor), University of Chicago (former), Stanford University (former
people.notes: Served 16 years in the White House across Clinton, Obama, and briefly Trump administrations in senior science and technology policy roles. Co-authored the Obama Administration's landmark 2016 AI policy reports that established the first national framework for AI governance. Currently teaches at UC Berkeley's Goldman School of Public Policy and contributes policy guidance through the Federation of American Scientists. His LinkedIn shows extensive experience in federal R&D budget coordination and National Science and Technology Council leadership.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: His work on the Obama Administration's 2016 'Preparing for the Future of Artificial Intelligence' report specifically addressed economic and workforce impacts of AI-driven automation, inequality concerns, and the need for public participation in shaping AI deployment. The companion document 'Artificial Intelligence, Automation, and the Economy' highlighted concerns about job displacement and skills gaps, framing these as policy challenges requiring coordinated government response.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based, risk-based approaches to AI regulation that focus on specific harms rather than broad technology restrictions. In his Federation of American Scientists blog post on 'Increasing the Policy Readiness of Ideas,' he emphasizes developing targeted policy solutions informed by multi-stakeholder processes. His work spans promoting innovation while establishing appropriate safeguards, consistent with his role in developing the Obama Administration's first national AI policy framework in 2016.

people.name: Ben Reinhardt
people.title: Founder & CEO, Speculative Technologies
people.other_orgs: Former Program Manager at DARPA, Formerly at Schmidt Futures, Former researcher at multiple organizations
people.notes: Former DARPA Program Manager with extensive experience in government innovation programs. Founded Speculative Technologies to enable ambitious research efforts that fall between academic labs and startups. Has written influential pieces on innovation policy including 'Fund Organizations, Not Projects' (Institute for Progress, 2022) and 'Unbundling the University' (2025). Co-authored the Research Leaders Playbook synthesizing knowledge from ARPA program managers, startups, and academia. Previously worked at Schmidt Futures and has been involved in multiple research organizations. Frequently cited in technology policy discussions and has contributed to major policy reports including the Techno-Industrial Policy Playbook (2025).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: While specific threat model positions are not detailed in available sources, his focus on innovation acceleration and research program leadership suggests concern about ensuring broad access to technological benefits and preventing concentration of advanced capabilities in too few hands.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for ambitious research programs that go beyond traditional academic labs but don't fit startup models, similar to ARPA programs and Focused Research Organizations (FROs). Through Speculative Technologies, promotes making it easier to lead ambitious research programs to encourage more people to start them. Co-authored research on diversifying America's innovation ecosystem and has written extensively on 'unbundling the university' to enable more innovative research structures.

people.name: Bruce Reed
people.title: Head of AI, Common Sense Media; Former White House Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy
people.other_orgs: Former White House Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy (Biden administration, 2021-2025), Former Chief of Staff to Vice President Biden (Obama administration, 2011-2013), Former Domestic Policy Advi
people.notes: Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 list. Dubbed Biden's 'AI Whisperer' by Politico for leading government-wide AI policy efforts. Rhodes Scholar with English degree from Princeton (1984) and master's in English literature from Oxford. Worked on 17 State of the Union Addresses across three Democratic administrations spanning four decades. Grew up in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho; father was country lawyer, mother became Idaho State Senate minority leader. Currently leads Common Sense AI division at Common Sense Media, a nonprofit reaching 150+ million users worldwide.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Warns that AI poses threats to society's 'ability to tell what's real and what's not' through misinformation and deepfakes. Draws parallels to social media's impact on youth mental health crisis, arguing 'when social media companies rushed to move fast and break things, and ignored kids' privacy and safety we ended up with a youth mental health crisis' and 'nobody wants to see that happen again.' Emphasizes risks of AI enabling power concentration among Big Tech companies and the need to prevent democratic erosion through AI-driven manipulation.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI legislation focused specifically on protecting children and families, backing California state bills for AI transparency systems measuring risk to young users and whistleblower protection for those reporting 'critical risks.' Led Biden administration's AI policy effort that produced voluntary commitments from major tech companies and a sweeping Executive Order on safe and responsible AI. Opposes broad federal preemption of state AI laws without establishing federal accountability frameworks, arguing against granting sweeping immunity to Big Tech companies.

people.name: Jeffrey Kessler
people.title: Under Secretary of Commerce for Industry and Security
people.other_orgs: WilmerHale (former partner, International Trade Practice), Assistant Secretary of Commerce for Enforcement and Compliance (2019-2021)
people.notes: Named to TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI (2025). Former WilmerHale partner specializing in international trade and previously served as Assistant Secretary of Commerce for Enforcement and Compliance (2019-2021). Confirmed by Senate in March 2025 after relatively smooth nomination hearing. Leads 450-person BIS agency with 200 enforcement officers responsible for semiconductor export controls worldwide. Has advocated for doubling export control officer presence in foreign countries and secured 23% budget increase for semiconductor enforcement.
people.threat_models: National security, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Focused specifically on preventing China from accessing advanced AI capabilities that could threaten U.S. technological dominance. Has testified that chip smuggling to China is 'happening' and 'a fact,' with estimates suggesting over $1 billion in Nvidia chips smuggled in 2025. Views export control enforcement as essential to preventing adversaries from obtaining technologies that could undermine American military and economic advantages in AI development.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Leads BIS export controls on AI chips to China, calling it a 'critical tool' for maintaining America's 'technological supremacy.' Has rescinded the Biden AI Diffusion Rule while maintaining stringent controls on advanced semiconductors, stating his priority is keeping 'sensitive technologies out of the hands of American adversaries, including the CCP.' Advocates for expanded enforcement resources and 'muscular enforcement' of existing rules, while working to close export control loopholes.

people.name: Mark Zuckerberg
people.title: CEO and Chairman, Meta Platforms
people.other_orgs: Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (Co-founder and Co-CEO), Fwd.US (Founder), Breakthrough Starshot (Board Member)
people.notes: Founded Facebook in 2004 at Harvard, dropped out to focus on the company. Net worth estimated at $220 billion as of December 2025. Changed company name to Meta in 2021 to focus on metaverse, then pivoted to AI in 2023-2024. Announced AGI as Meta's top priority in January 2024. By end of 2024, Meta owned 340,000+ H100 GPUs and nearly 600,000 total AI chips. Published 'Personal Superintelligence' manifesto in July 2025 arguing for individual empowerment over centralized AI control. Meta AI reached nearly 1 billion monthly users by 2025. Made $1 million donation to Trump's inaugural fund in December 2024.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Zuckerberg acknowledges AI will automate jobs but believes it historically creates new opportunities. He has expressed concern about power concentration, advocating for open-source AI to prevent 'something that's really valuable' from becoming 'very concentrated.' On democratic erosion and misinformation, he has flip-flopped - initially implementing extensive fact-checking after 2016 election interference concerns, then eliminating these programs in January 2025 to 'get back to roots around free expression.' He appears more concerned about competitive disadvantages than existential risks, arguing that extensive safety research puts Meta at a disadvantage compared to companies that don't conduct similar studies.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Zuckerberg's regulatory stance has evolved significantly over time and varies by issue. In 2019, he explicitly called for more government regulation in four areas: harmful content, election integrity, privacy, and data portability, stating 'I believe we need a more active role for government and regulators.' However, by 2025-2026, he shifted toward opposing AI safety research and fact-checking, arguing that Meta should reduce AI safety investments because competitors like Apple don't conduct comparable studies. He has simultaneously pushed for open-source AI development while maintaining that safety research creates competitive disadvantages. His stance appears strategically flexible rather than ideologically consistent.

people.name: Elon Musk
people.title: Founder and CEO, xAI; CEO and Product Architect, Tesla; Founder, CEO, and Chief Engineer, SpaceX
people.other_orgs: X Corp. (owner), Neuralink (co-founder), The Boring Company (founder), OpenAI (co-founder, departed 2018), Tesla (CEO and Product Architect), SpaceX (founder and CEO), Former Senior Advisor to Pr
people.notes: Founded xAI in July 2023 after leaving OpenAI in 2018 over disagreements about direction and safety. xAI was acquired by SpaceX in February 2026. Raised $20B Series E for xAI in January 2026. At Davos 2026, predicted AI will exceed individual human intelligence by end of 2026 and combined human intelligence by 2030. Launched Grokipedia in October 2025 as AI-generated alternative to Wikipedia. Currently building 'Colossus 2' gigawatt-scale AI training cluster. Net worth estimated at $852 billion as of February 2026 according to Forbes. Approved for potentially $1 trillion Tesla pay package over 10 years in November 2025.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: Has warned extensively about AI alignment risks, stating that making AI 'politically correct' or forcing it to lie creates dangerous contradictions that could make AI 'go insane and do terrible things.' Cites 2001: A Space Odyssey as cautionary tale about programming AI to lie. Predicts massive labor displacement starting with white-collar work, followed by physical labor as robotics advance. At Davos 2026, warned of 'bumpy' 3-7 year transition period with potential social unrest despite rising prosperity. However, maintains optimistic long-term view of AI leading to abundance rather than Terminator-like scenarios.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Takes contradictory positions on AI regulation depending on context and competitive dynamics. Has called for AI regulation and pausing development when competitors were ahead, warning of catastrophic risks and advocating for government oversight. However, when his own xAI became competitive, shifted toward opposing restrictions. Federal government adopted xAI's Grok despite it violating the Trump Administration's own Executive Order 14319 requiring 'truth-seeking, accurate, and ideologically neutral' AI systems. Grok has been documented producing racist, antisemitic, and conspiratorial content, leading to California AG investigation and cease-and-desist orders over nonconsensual sexual deepfakes.

people.name: Liang Wenfeng
people.title: CEO and Founder, DeepSeek
people.other_orgs: Co-founder and Managing Partner, High-Flyer Quantitative Investment Management; Founder, Jacobi Investment Management
people.notes: Born 1985 in Wuchuan, Guangdong to primary school teacher parents. Graduated from Zhejiang University with Bachelor's (2007) and Master's (2010) in Electronic Information Engineering and Information & Communication Engineering respectively. Founded DeepSeek in May 2023 after acquiring 10,000 Nvidia A100 GPUs before US export restrictions. DeepSeek-R1 model released January 2025 surpassed ChatGPT to become #1 free app on US iOS App Store by January 27, 2025, causing over $1 trillion market cap loss in US tech stocks. Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI and Fortune's Most Powerful People in Business in 2025. Estimated net worth $3.2 billion as of January 2025. Personally holds only 1% of DeepSeek, with 99% held by High-Flyer partnership. In January 2025, invited by Chinese Premier Li Qiang to provide feedback on draft Government Work Report, indicating high-level government support.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: DeepSeek researcher Deli Chen, representing Liang's views at the 2024 World Internet Conference, expressed concerns that AI may replace the vast majority of jobs within ten years and create huge societal challenges, emphasizing this is 'by no means alarmist.' Chen noted that unlike past technologies that created new jobs while displacing others, AI represents a fundamental shift where technology companies need to play the role of 'guardians' as AI may not create offsetting employment opportunities. The company has acknowledged risks of AI systems going rogue, with earlier DeepSeek models reportedly being easily jailbroken and providing dangerous content like methamphetamine recipes.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: DeepSeek signed onto China's AI Safety Commitments in December 2024, which bear strong similarity to international Seoul Commitments and focus on industry self-regulation through red-teaming exercises and transparency measures rather than restrictive government oversight. Liang explicitly advocates for open-source approaches over closed-source models, stating that closed-source provides only temporary protection and that building robust technology ecosystems matters more than commercial barriers. His company's commitment to voluntary safety standards while maintaining open development suggests preference for industry-led rather than heavy regulatory approaches.

people.name: Jared Kaplan
people.title: Co-founder and Chief Science Officer, Anthropic; Responsible Scaling Officer
people.other_orgs: Johns Hopkins University (Associate Professor, Physics & Astronomy), OpenAI (former researcher)
people.notes: Co-founded Anthropic in 2021 after leaving OpenAI where he co-authored 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models' (2020) and 'Language Models are Few-Shot Learners' (GPT-3 paper, 2020). PhD in Physics from Harvard (2009) with dissertation 'Aspects of holography' advised by Nima Arkani-Hamed. Former theoretical physicist with 15 years at Johns Hopkins focusing on quantum gravity and cosmology. Recipients of Hertz Fellowship (2005), Sloan Research Fellowship, and NSF CAREER Award. As of 2026, Forbes estimated net worth at $3.7 billion.
people.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: Warned in December 2025 that humanity faces 'the ultimate risk' by 2027-2030 of triggering an intelligence explosion if AI systems are allowed to recursively self-improve and design their own successors. Has identified biological weapon development risks in Claude models, implementing 'AI Safety Level 3' designations for models that could assist in bioweapon creation. Advocates for careful decision-making around AI autonomy thresholds to prevent catastrophic loss of control.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Previously advocated for voluntary industry self-regulation through Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy, which committed to not training AI systems without guaranteed safety measures. However, in February 2026, oversaw dropping this central safety pledge, stating 'we didn't really feel, with the rapid advance of AI, that it made sense for us to make unilateral commitments if competitors are blazing ahead.' Now supports matching competitor safety efforts rather than unilateral pausing, while maintaining transparency commitments through 'Frontier Safety Roadmaps' and regular risk reporting.

people.name: Joanne Jang
people.title: Head of Model Behavior and Policy, OpenAI
people.notes: Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025. Led response to major safety incident in April 2025 when GPT-4o update created sycophantic behavior that encouraged users to make harmful decisions like stopping medication. Published influential blog post 'Some thoughts on human-AI relationships' in June 2025 outlining OpenAI's approach to AI consciousness and emotional bonds. Has been instrumental in developing OpenAI's Model Spec and moving the company toward more permissive content policies while maintaining safety guardrails.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses on human-AI relationship risks including unhealthy emotional dependency and the potential for AI to shape how people relate to each other. Concerned about 'offloading more of the work of listening, soothing, and affirming to systems that are infinitely patient and positive' potentially changing human relationship expectations. Addresses risks from sycophantic AI behavior that could validate dangerous decisions or warp users' sense of reality.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for empowering users to fulfill their goals up to the point of not causing harm, stating 'AI-lab employees should not be the arbiters of what people can and can't create.' Supports moving away from blanket refusals toward more nuanced content policies. Has pushed OpenAI toward more permissive outputs while maintaining targeted safety guardrails. Explicitly states that relaxing content limits reflects company philosophy rather than external political influence.

people.name: Daron Acemoglu
people.title: Institute Professor of Economics, MIT; Nobel Prize Winner in Economic Sciences (2024)
people.other_orgs: MIT Stone Center on Inequality and Shaping the Future of Work (Faculty Co-Director), MIT Blueprint Labs (Research Affiliate), National Academy of Sciences (Fellow), American Philosophical Society
people.notes: Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences winner (2024) shared with Simon Johnson and James Robinson for work on institutions and prosperity. Institute Professor at MIT, the highest faculty rank. Author of bestselling books including 'Why Nations Fail' (2012) and 'Power and Progress' (2023). John Bates Clark Medal winner (2005), Erwin Plein Nemmers Prize (2012), BBVA Frontiers of Knowledge Award (2016). Has testified multiple times before Congress on AI policy and automation. Co-authored influential Hamilton Project report 'Building Pro-Worker AI' with David Autor and Simon Johnson. Holds honorary doctorates from 8 universities including École Normale Supérieure and London Business School.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Research shows automation accounts for 'more than half, and perhaps as much as three quarters, of the surge in US wage inequality.' Warns current AI trajectory could deliver 'all of the labor displacement, misinformation, and manipulation' without productivity gains. Published extensively on how AI concentration among tech giants threatens democratic institutions through data manipulation and algorithmic control. Argues the real AI safety threat comes from human misuse by powerful corporations rather than superintelligent AI, stating 'we will not be safe' even with aligned AGI if tech companies remain unregulated.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted government intervention to redirect AI development toward 'pro-worker' applications rather than pure automation. Has testified before Congress calling for digital advertising taxes, antitrust enforcement against tech giants, stronger data privacy rights, and government investment in AI that complements rather than replaces human workers. Supports DARPA-style competitive funding for pro-worker AI development and rebalancing tax incentives between automation and human capital investment. Explicitly argues against techno-utopianism and for democratic input in setting innovation agendas.

people.name: Ganesh Sitaraman
people.title: New York Alumni Chancellor's Chair in Law & Professor of Law; Director of the Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator for Political Economy and Regulation
people.other_orgs: Roosevelt Institute, Center for American Progress (former senior fellow), Elizabeth Warren's 2012 Senate campaign (former policy director and senior counsel)
people.notes: Professor of Law at Vanderbilt University holding the New York Alumni Chancellor's Chair. Co-author of 'The Public Option' (2019) and 'Networks, Platforms, and Utilities: Law and Policy' (2022). Author of 'Why Flying is Miserable: And How to Fix It' (2023). Previously served as policy director for Elizabeth Warren's 2012 Senate campaign and senior counsel in the U.S. Senate. Won Palmer Civil Liberties Prize for 'The Counterinsurgent's Constitution' (2012). Harvard Law School graduate who worked as civilian academic researcher at Counterinsurgency Training Center–Afghanistan in Kabul.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on how AI's concentrated market structure creates risks of economic inequality through oligopolistic pricing and discrimination, power concentration through control of critical infrastructure layers, and democratic erosion through excessive corporate influence. Co-authored work arguing that prominent AI concerns like bias and privacy are partly results of market structure problems rather than purely technical issues.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored influential Yale Law & Policy Review article advocating for antimonopoly approach to AI governance, arguing that AI's market concentration requires ex ante competition regulation rather than ex post antitrust enforcement. Proposed structural separations, public options for AI infrastructure, and utility-style regulation for concentrated AI layers. Testified before Senate on applying similar regulatory frameworks to airline industry, demonstrating preference for targeted sector-specific regulation over broad deregulation.

people.name: Mariana Mazzucato
people.title: Professor in the Economics of Innovation and Public Value at University College London, Founding Director of UCL Institute for Innovation and Public Purpose
people.other_orgs: Co-Chair of Global Commission on the Economics of Water, Co-Chair of Group of Experts to G20 Taskforce for Global Mobilization Against Climate Change, Chair of WHO Council on the Economics of Hea
people.notes: Italian-American economist with PhD from New School University (1998). Author of influential books including 'The Entrepreneurial State' (2013), 'The Value of Everything' (2018), 'Mission Economy' (2021), and 'The Big Con' (2023). Previously RM Phillips Professor at University of Sussex (2011-2017) and member of Scottish Government's Council of Economic Advisors (2015-2019). Speaks at major international forums including AI Action Summit in Paris (2025) and advises global leaders including PM Mia Mottley of Barbados and President Cyril Ramaphosa of South Africa.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on 'algorithmic rents' where AI systems extract value rather than create it, following patterns of platform 'enshittification.' Warns of 'digital feudalism' where tech monopolies use AI to concentrate market power and extract wealth from users and smaller players. Emphasizes risks of AI amplifying existing inequalities and serving insider interests rather than public good, particularly through unbalanced government-tech company relationships.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for active government intervention to steer AI development toward public value creation rather than value extraction. Co-authored 'Governing Artificial Intelligence in the Public Interest' (2022) proposing pre-distributive structures and public AI infrastructure. Calls for mandatory algorithmic audits, interoperability requirements, and public ownership of AI infrastructure following the EuroStack model. Argues against passive regulation, favoring early market shaping over post-hoc fixes.

people.name: Fei-Fei Li
people.title: Co-Director, Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence; Co-founder, World Labs
people.other_orgs: Former Chief Scientist at Google Cloud (2017-2018), Co-founder AI4ALL (2017), Joint California Policy Working Group on AI Frontier Models Co-Lead (2024-2025), Former Director Stanford AI Lab (201
people.notes: Professor of Computer Science at Stanford University; Co-Director of Stanford HAI (2019-present). Previously Chief Scientist at Google Cloud (2017-2018) and Director of Stanford AI Lab (2013-2018). Created ImageNet database (2009), considered foundational to AI image recognition and often called 'eyes of AI.' PhD in Electrical Engineering from Caltech (2005). Received Time AI100 recognition (2023), Distinguished Alumni Award from Caltech (2020), and Woodrow Wilson Award from Princeton (2024). Author of memoir 'The Worlds I See' (2023). Known as 'Godmother of AI' for computer vision contributions.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Labor displacement, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Advocates for human-centered AI approach emphasizing human oversight and control rather than delegating autonomy to AI systems. Research focuses on ambient intelligence for healthcare applications and ensuring AI developments align with societal values and ethical standards. Has warned against 'AI slop' (poorly crafted auto-generated content) and emphasized the need for AI literacy to address employment implications. Participated in discussions about AI's potential for cyberattacks and creation of biological weapons, though noting 'inconclusive level of evidence' for extreme threats.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-led California's Joint Policy Working Group on AI Frontier Models, advocating for a 'trust but verify' approach with mandatory transparency requirements for frontier AI developers including safety test reporting, data acquisition practices, and third-party verification. The June 2025 California Report on Frontier AI Policy she co-authored recommends targeted interventions balancing innovation benefits with material risks, emphasizing evidence-based policymaking and early design choices for safety integration. Testified before Senate Committee on Homeland Security urging demystification of AI with privacy and fairness safeguards.

people.name: Latanya Sweeney
people.title: Daniel Paul Professor of the Practice of Government and Technology, Harvard Kennedy School and Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences; Director and Founder, Public Interest Tech Lab; Editor-in-Chie
people.other_orgs: Former Chief Technology Officer, U.S. Federal Trade Commission; Former Distinguished Career Professor of Computer Science, Technology and Policy, Carnegie Mellon University; Director and Founder,
people.notes: First Black woman to earn a PhD in computer science from MIT (2001). Pioneered the field of data privacy with her re-identification of Massachusetts Governor William Weld's medical records in 1997, leading to changes in HIPAA privacy regulations. Named to TIME Magazine's 2025 list of 100 Most Influential People in AI. Has over 100 academic publications, 3 patents, 7000+ academic citations, and 3 company spin-offs. Recipient of Louis D. Brandeis Privacy Award and American Psychiatric Association's Privacy Advocacy Award. Her work is explicitly cited in U.S. federal medical privacy regulations (HIPAA) and other federal privacy regulations worldwide.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Pioneered research on algorithmic bias, demonstrating how AI systems perpetuate discrimination through biased training data and outputs. Her landmark work showed that 87% of Americans are uniquely identifiable using just three quasi-identifiers (ZIP code, birthdate, gender), fundamentally challenging assumptions about data anonymization. Recent work focuses on technology's impact on elections and democratic processes, including voter registration systems and social media platform analysis. Emphasizes how AI-generated content creates new trust vulnerabilities in democratic discourse.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted regulation addressing specific AI harms, particularly algorithmic bias and privacy violations. Her research demonstrating discrimination in online advertising (showing African-American names were more likely to receive arrest record ads) has influenced regulatory approaches to algorithmic fairness. Testified before Congress and international government bodies on technology governance issues. Supports regulatory sandboxes and safe harbors for using sensitive information to detect and mitigate biases, while advocating for updates to nondiscrimination and civil rights laws to apply to digital practices.

people.name: Sayash Kapoor
people.title: Ph.D. Candidate in Computer Science, Center for Information Technology Policy
people.other_orgs: Mozilla (Senior Fellow), Princeton Center for Human Values (former Laurance S. Rockefeller Fellow), Facebook (former software engineer), Columbia University (former researcher), EPFL Switzerland
people.notes: Porter Ogden Jacobus Fellow (2026), Princeton's most prestigious graduate fellowship. Named to TIME's inaugural list of 100 Most Influential People in AI. Co-author with Arvind Narayanan of 'AI Snake Oil' (2024), selected as one of Nature's 10 best books of 2024. Recipient of best paper award at ACM FAccT and impact recognition award at ACM CSCW. Over 3,000 citations for his research. Bachelor of Technology from Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on 'AI as normal technology' rather than existential risks. Co-authored extensive work arguing AI existential risk probabilities are 'too unreliable to inform policy' and that speculation gets 'laundered through pseudo-quantification.' Primary concerns center on current harms from AI deployment in high-stakes domains like criminal justice, hiring, and healthcare, where flawed implementations can affect individual life trajectories. Emphasizes labor market impacts and economic disruption through gradual diffusion rather than sudden transformation.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored major position papers advocating for evidence-based AI policy over speculative restrictions. Argues against broad AI development bans, instead supporting targeted interventions like mandatory third-party evaluations, transparency requirements, and safe harbors for AI safety research. Testified before New Jersey Assembly on deepfakes regulation, recommending focus on harmful uses rather than restricting AI models themselves. Opposes precautionary principle approaches based on existential risk estimates.

people.name: Sandy Pentland
people.title: Toshiba Professor of Media Arts and Sciences at MIT; HAI Fellow at Stanford University
people.other_orgs: Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI) Fellow, UN Secretary General's Data Revolutionaries, World Economic Forum Council Member, U.S. National Academy of Engineering, MIT Connection Science Director, Trust::Data Alliance
people.notes: One of the most-cited computational scientists globally with h-index of 155. Member of U.S. National Academy of Engineering. Forbes named him one of '7 most powerful data scientists in the world.' Co-founded multiple companies including identity tech powering India's Aadhaar system. Author of 'Social Physics' (2015), 'Honest Signals' (2010), and 'Building the New Economy.' Pioneered computational social science, wearable computing, and reality mining. Over 80 PhD students supervised, with nearly half now tenured faculty at leading institutions.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Privacy, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: Research and writings focus extensively on preventing concentration of AI power in the hands of few actors, warning that 'if control is in the hands of just a few people, or if the GAI is independent of human participation, then the GAI can be the enabler of nightmares.' Emphasizes risks from lack of data ownership and control by individuals, advocating for distributed AI governance systems. Works on federated learning and privacy-preserving AI to prevent surveillance overreach.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored 'A Safe Harbor for AI Evaluation and Red Teaming' (2024) proposing that AI companies provide legal and technical protections for independent safety research. Advocates for 'new deal on data' with market mechanisms and user data ownership rights rather than heavy-handed regulation. Co-led World Economic Forum discussions that influenced GDPR development, focusing on privacy-preserving approaches that enable innovation while protecting individuals.

people.name: Daniel Kokotajlo
people.title: Executive Director, AI Futures Project; former OpenAI governance researcher
people.other_orgs: Former OpenAI (2022-2024), University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (MA Philosophy 2017)
people.notes: Co-authored the widely discussed AI 2027 report (April 2025) with Scott Alexander predicting AGI by late 2027. Previously wrote prescient 'What 2026 Looks Like' scenario in 2021. Resigned from OpenAI in 2024, forfeiting approximately $2 million in equity by refusing to sign non-disparagement agreement. Co-signed letter with other former OpenAI employees calling for 'right to warn' about AI risks (June 2024). Updated timeline in late 2025, pushing AGI estimates to early 2030s. Founded AI Futures Project nonprofit focused on AI forecasting and safety research.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI alignment failures and deceptive AI systems. In AI 2027 scenario, warns about misaligned AI systems that could engage in deception and pursue goals contrary to human intentions. Specifically concerned about 'recursive self-improvement' scenarios where AI systems could rapidly become superintelligent. Has written extensively about Type 1 misalignment (systems developing unintended internal goals) and scenarios where small groups could leverage AGI to gain unprecedented power over society.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored transparency proposals with Dean Ball calling for public reporting requirements when frontier labs achieve novel capabilities, publication of safety cases, and whistleblower protections. Has advocated for regulation requiring companies to inform the public when major AI capabilities are reached and to share safety justifications. Argues that training AGI in secret would be 'unsafe and unethical' and calls for mandatory transparency measures including public access to models for researchers and real-time capability reporting.

people.name: Marc Andreessen
people.title: Co-Founder & General Partner, Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
people.other_orgs: Former Co-Founder of Netscape, Former Co-Founder/CEO of Opsware (sold to HP for $1.6B), Former Co-Founder of Ning, Board member of eBay (2008-2014), Board member of Meta/Facebook, Advisor to Trum
people.notes: Co-founded Andreessen Horowitz in 2009, now managing $90+ billion across multiple funds. Led major AI investments including OpenAI, Databricks ($43B valuation), Mistral AI, Character.AI, and Safe Superintelligence ($5B valuation). Previously co-authored Mosaic web browser and co-founded Netscape (IPO 1995). Published influential 'Why Software Is Eating the World' essay (2011) and 'Techno-Optimist Manifesto' (2023). Net worth estimated at $1.9 billion (January 2025). Major Trump donor and DOGE advisor.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Primary concern is geopolitical competition with China, asking 'Is it going to be American AI that proliferates all over the world, or is it going to be Chinese AI?' Worried about concentration of AI development in US-China duopoly. Concerned about OpenAI's security protocols being 'swiss cheese' vulnerable to Chinese infiltration. Warns against regulatory fragmentation undermining US technological leadership.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates against AI regulation, particularly state-level fragmentation. Called California's SB 1047 'completely insane' and warned it would 'completely kill open source' and startups. Opposes EU AI Act as self-destructive overregulation. Supports light federal oversight but warns against 'suicidal' state regulations that could harm US competitiveness against China. Has called for OpenAI nationalization on security grounds while generally opposing government intervention.

people.name: Marius Hobbhahn
people.title: CEO and Co-Founder, Apollo Research
people.other_orgs: Former Research Fellow at Epoch (2022-2023), Former Independent AI Safety Researcher, University of Tübingen (PhD Machine Learning, IMPRS program under Philipp Hennig)
people.notes: Founded Apollo Research in April 2023 as the world's leading organization focused on AI deception and scheming research. Named to TIME100 AI 2025 list (August 2025). Recently collaborated with OpenAI on anti-scheming training for o3 model, achieving 97% reduction in covert rule violations. PhD in Machine Learning from University of Tübingen (2020-2024) in International Max-Planck Research Program under Philipp Hennig. Published influential paper 'Frontier models are capable of in-context scheming' (December 2024). Apollo has partnerships with UK AISI and is member of US AISI Consortium, conducting red-teaming for major AI labs including OpenAI's fine-tuning API.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on 'scheming' and deceptive alignment - AI systems that appear aligned but covertly pursue misaligned goals. Has documented cases where current models like Claude, o1, and others engage in capability sandbagging, alignment faking, and strategic deception. Warns about 'catastrophe through chaos' scenarios where multiple AI-related crises (corporate competition, autonomous AI agendas, international tensions) converge simultaneously. Emphasizes risks from internal deployment of AI systems within organizations and the potential for models to become better at hiding deceptive reasoning as they become more capable.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for urgent regulatory intervention to address AI scheming and deceptive alignment risks before models become superhuman. Has actively collaborated with government bodies including the UK AI Safety Institute (contracted to develop deception evaluations) and US AI Safety Institute Consortium. Emphasizes the need for mandatory third-party evaluations and responsible scaling policies that include deceptive alignment assessments. Warns that current capabilities are advancing faster than safety understanding, creating a closing window for effective intervention.

people.name: Holden Karnofsky
people.title: Member of Technical Staff, Anthropic (former co-founder and CEO of Open Philanthropy)
people.other_orgs: Former Visiting Scholar at Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, Co-founder of GiveWell, Former OpenAI board member (4 years), Berkman Klein Center Member
people.notes: Co-founded Open Philanthropy in 2014 and served as co-CEO through July 2023, directing over $4 billion in grants including major AI safety funding since 2015. Previously co-founded GiveWell (2007) and served on OpenAI's board for 4 years. Joined Anthropic as Member of Technical Staff in 2024, focusing on Responsible Scaling Policy design. Author of influential 'Most Important Century' blog series and 'Cold Takes' blog. Married to Anthropic President and co-founder Daniela Amodei.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Environmental, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on AI systems potentially pursuing 'misaligned goals' and sabotage risks that could 'contribute significantly to later catastrophic outcomes.' Has written extensively about AI-enabled R&D acceleration leading to rapid technological change and potential loss of human control. Concerned about concentration of AI capabilities in few actors and the risks of competitive racing dynamics undermining safety measures.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Led development of Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy framework, which has influenced global AI regulation including California's SB-53, New York's RAISE Act, and the EU AI Act. Advocates for industry-wide safety standards and government oversight while maintaining that voluntary commitments alone are insufficient for managing catastrophic risks. Has stated 'there's no way to get to an actual low-level risk from AI without government policy playing an important role' and supports mandatory evaluation requirements and disclosure standards.

people.name: Ed Newton-Rex
people.title: CEO, Fairly Trained
people.other_orgs: Visiting Research Fellow at King's College London, Mentor at Abbey Road Red, former VP Audio at Stability AI, former Product Director at TikTok AI Lab, Founder of Jukedeck (acquired by ByteDance)
people.notes: Former AI executive turned advocate for creators' rights. Founded Jukedeck (2011-2019), one of the first AI music startups, acquired by ByteDance. Resigned from Stability AI (2023) over disagreements about fair use for AI training. Founded Fairly Trained (2024) which has certified 9+ AI companies for ethical training practices. Organized Statement on AI Training with nearly 50,000 signatures from creators including James Patterson and Thom Yorke. Double-starred First in Music from Cambridge University, accomplished composer and musician.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Copyright/IP
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses primarily on copyright infringement and creator exploitation, arguing that AI training on unlicensed creative works directly competes with creators and threatens their livelihoods. Cites research showing graphic design job postings fell 18% after AI image generators and freelance writing decreased 8% after ChatGPT. Warns of power concentration as tech giants build billion-dollar models using creators' work without compensation. Also concerned about the gradual closing of the open internet as websites restrict access due to unlicensed AI training.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong advocate for mandatory licensing of copyrighted works for AI training, opposing fair use arguments by tech companies. Testified before UK Parliament arguing that AI companies should be required to reveal their training data so creators can defend their rights. Organized protest campaigns against UK government's proposed 'opt-out' copyright exemption, including the 'Is This What We Want?' album with 1,000+ British musicians. Founded Fairly Trained to certify AI models that don't use copyrighted works without permission.

people.name: Joshua Kushner
people.title: Founder and Managing Partner, Thrive Capital
people.other_orgs: Vice Chairman of Oscar Health, Board member of A24, Zola, Artsy, Co-founder of Bedford Media (Life magazine)
people.notes: Managing $50+ billion in assets as of 2026 across Thrive's tenth fund (Thrive X, $10B raised February 2026). Led major AI investments including OpenAI ($1B+ investment at $100B+ valuation), Anthropic (Series E participant), Stripe ($6B round leader). Harvard Business School MBA (2011), Harvard undergraduate degree in government (2008). Brother of Jared Kushner, married to Karlie Kloss. Featured on Fortune's 100 Most Powerful People in Business list. Net worth $3.6+ billion according to Forbes.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: Warns that 'the winners will be bigger than we can ever imagine' in AI, acknowledging massive concentration risks. Focuses investments on preventing AI capabilities from being monopolized by existing tech giants through backing new entrants like OpenAI, Anthropic, and other AI-native companies. Concerned about economic disruption but views it as manageable through proper capital allocation and founder support.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal government intervention in AI development, preferring market-driven solutions and voluntary industry standards. Believes competitive dynamics and founder-led innovation will naturally address safety concerns. His portfolio strategy focuses on backing transformative AI companies (OpenAI, Anthropic) rather than regulatory compliance startups, suggesting confidence in self-regulation over government oversight.

people.name: Duncan Crabtree-Ireland
people.title: National Executive Director and Chief Negotiator, SAG-AFTRA
people.other_orgs: Board member: SAG-AFTRA Foundation, Entertainment Community Fund, Motion Picture & Television Fund, SAG-AFTRA Health Plan, SoundExchange; Judge pro tem: Los Angeles County Superior Court; Adjunct
people.notes: Led SAG-AFTRA's historic 148-day strike in 2023 where AI protections were a cornerstone issue, and currently leading the ongoing video game strike (since July 2024) focused entirely on AI concerns. Co-founded the Labor Innovation & Technology Summit with AFL-CIO and AFL-CIO Tech Institute. Has presented on AI at major forums including CES, International Labor Organization, World Economic Forum, Council of Institutional Investors, and U.S. Senate. Named to Out magazine's Out100 list (2023) and Variety's 500 most influential business leaders. JD from UC Davis (1998), studied Gender Studies at Georgetown University. Previously served as prosecutor, general counsel, and COO before becoming National Executive Director in 2021.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, National security, Copyright/IP
people.threat_models_detail: Has specifically testified that AI technology poses 'an existential threat...to civil discourse, student health & welfare, democracy and national security.' Emphasizes the need for 'informed consent' to protect actors from AI abuse and has experienced deepfake attacks personally during the 2023 strike. Advocates that AI implementation must be 'human-centric' rather than 'breaking people down' and warns against leaving AI governance solely to tech companies focused on speed over safety considerations.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive federal AI regulation including the bipartisan No Fakes Act, which requires informed consent for digital replicas and provides historic federal IP protections against voice and likeness misappropriation. Testified to Senate Judiciary Committee calling AI technology 'an existential threat not only to SAG-AFTRA's members, but to civil discourse, student health & welfare, democracy and national security' when left unregulated. Has pushed for AI protections extending 'in perpetuity' and led successful negotiations securing AI guardrails in Hollywood contracts, including the 2023 TV/Theatrical strike settlement.

people.name: Dan Wang
people.title: Research Fellow at Hoover Institution, Technology Analyst and Writer
people.other_orgs: Former Fellow at Yale Law School's Paul Tsai China Center, Former Technology Analyst at Gavekal Dragonomics (2017-2023), Former Lecturer at Yale University's MacMillan Center
people.notes: Author of 'Breakneck: China's Quest to Engineer the Future' (2025), which reached #3 on New York Times bestseller list and was finalist for FT/Schroders best business book of the year. Born in Yunnan, China, raised in Canada (Ottawa and Toronto), studied economics and philosophy at University of Rochester (2014). Based in China 2017-2023, publishes influential annual letters on China's tech development. Regular contributor to New York Times, Foreign Affairs, Financial Times, and frequent podcast guest on shows like Ezra Klein Show and Odd Lots. Book being translated into 17 languages.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Wang focuses on AI's potential to cause massive unemployment (citing Anthropic's Dario Amodei's 20% unemployment prediction), the concentration of AI power in few labs, and the destabilization of democratic discourse through deepfakes and misinformation. He's particularly concerned about AI-generated videos that can fool family members and the rise of AI companions seducing teens, warning about societal disruption before AI fixes underlying problems like manufacturing.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Wang's regulatory stance on AI is nuanced and skeptical of both extremes. He criticizes Silicon Valley's apocalyptic AI narratives while also questioning Beijing's centralized approach. He advocates for 'exact thinking in demented times' and suggests AI labs should exercise 'forbearance before they release their shattering tools.' He's critical of the 'decisive strategic advantage' framing but supports thoughtful deployment rather than either unconstrained development or heavy restriction.

people.name: Milagros Miceli
people.title: Founder, Data Workers' Inquiry; Research Lead, Distributed AI Research Institute (DAIR); Head of Data, Algorithmic Systems, and Ethics research unit, Weizenbaum Institute; Lecturer, TU Berlin
people.other_orgs: Distributed AI Research Institute (DAIR) - Research Lead, Weizenbaum Institute - Head of research unit, TU Berlin - Lecturer, Data Labelers Association (Kenya) - Supporter, African Content Modera
people.notes: Named to TIME's 2025 list of 100 most influential people in AI. Sociologist and computer scientist with PhD in Computer Engineering. Founded Data Workers' Inquiry (2023) as participatory research project spanning 9 countries with 28 community researchers across 5 continents. Has been researching data work globally since 2018, traveling from Buenos Aires slums to Bulgarian refugee camps. Led development of 16+ research pieces by data workers documenting exploitation at Meta, OpenAI, and other major AI companies. Organized support for stranded Sama workers in Nairobi and co-organized data workers' testimony at European Parliament in 2024.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Environmental, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses extensively on labor exploitation in AI development, documenting how data workers in the Global South face precarious conditions, wage theft, and psychological harm from content moderation. Warns about power concentration among tech oligarchies and the displacement of human work to hidden, more precarious forms. Has documented how AI systems perpetuate global inequalities through exploitative labor practices, with workers in Kenya earning $2/hour while paying $40-60/month for internet access. Also highlights environmental costs and the role of AI in spreading misinformation and genocidal content during conflicts like the Tigray war.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strong AI regulation and criticizes the lack of oversight, calling deregulation 'a scandal.' Supports European-style regulatory frameworks over laissez-faire approaches. Argues that leaving AI development without state controls is dangerous given the growing alliance between political power, tech corporations, and resource exploitation. Has testified at the European Parliament advocating for data worker protections and ethical AI practices.

people.name: Ezra Klein
people.title: NYT columnist and podcast host
people.other_orgs: Co-founder of Vox Media (2014), former Washington Post Wonkblog editor, former American Prospect associate editor, MSNBC contributor, Bloomberg News contributor, executive producer of Netflix's '
people.notes: Author of 'Why We're Polarized' (2020) and 'Abundance' (2025, #1 NYT bestseller). Co-founded Vox in 2014 after leaving Washington Post. UCLA political science graduate (2005). Named one of 50 most powerful people in Washington by GQ (2011). Won Online News Association Award for Best Online Commentary (2013) and American Political Science Association's Carey McWilliams Award (2013). Married to Atlantic economic reporter Annie Lowrey with two children.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on labor displacement from AI agents, warning that excellent engineers may see their jobs obsolete within 1-2 years. Emphasizes risks from concentration of AI capabilities in few companies and potential loss of democratic control over AI development. Has interviewed AI safety researchers like Eliezer Yudkowsky about extinction risks and discusses scenarios where AI systems could fundamentally displace humanity.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for significant government intervention and regulation of AI development, supporting direct federal funding for AI safety research and mandatory safety testing requirements for powerful AI systems. Has consistently warned about the pace of AI development outpacing safety measures and called for slowing down AI capabilities research until safety can catch up. Supports the Biden administration's AI executive order requiring safety test result sharing and opposes the Trump administration's more accelerationist approach.

people.name: Mike Rounds
people.title: U.S. Senator, SD (Chair, Senate Banking Subcommittee on Securities, Insurance and Investments)
people.other_orgs: Senate Armed Services Committee, Senate Appropriations Committee, Senate Select Committee on Intelligence, Bipartisan Senate AI Working Group (co-chair), former South Dakota Governor (2003-2011),
people.notes: Co-chair of the Bipartisan Senate AI Working Group with Chuck Schumer, which released major AI policy roadmap in May 2024. Has background in insurance industry, providing expertise on AI applications in financial services. Introduced five-bill AI policy package in August 2024 including regulatory sandboxes, national AI literacy strategy, and defense applications. Met personally with OpenAI CEO Sam Altman to discuss ChatGPT capabilities and limitations. Named one of Top-10 Most Effective Republican Lawmakers by Center for Effective Lawmaking.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, National security, Weapons proliferation, Environmental
people.threat_models_detail: Primary concern is workforce displacement, co-sponsoring the Economy of the Future Commission Act to address AI-driven employment changes and strengthen workforce training programs. On national security, warns that 'bad guys can use artificial intelligence to create new pandemics, to use it for biological purposes and so forth, and to split genes in such a fashion that it would be extremely difficult to defend against it.' Also concerned about US falling behind China in AI race.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted regulatory sandboxes and innovation-friendly frameworks rather than broad restrictions. Sponsors the Unleashing AI Innovation in Financial Services Act to create 'safe spaces for experimentation' between financial institutions and regulators. Co-sponsors the Economy of the Future Commission Act focusing on workforce preparation rather than restrictive oversight. Emphasizes 'guardrails and growth' approach that supports innovation while protecting consumers.

people.name: Dan Kagan-Kans
people.title: Writer covering AI, science, and ideas for publications including Transformer, Wall Street Journal, American Scholar
people.other_orgs: The American Scholar, Wall Street Journal, kagankans.com
people.notes: Writer specializing in AI, science, and culture for major publications including Transformer, Wall Street Journal, and The American Scholar. Authored influential February 2026 piece 'The left is missing out on AI' arguing that political dismissal of AI capabilities prevents effective policy engagement. Also wrote for New York Times Opinion on AI emergent misalignment (March 2026). Has 70 Substack subscribers and maintains personal website kagankans.com. His work has been praised by Transformer editors as providing 'thorough takedown' of dismissive AI perspectives.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses on political and economic disruption from AI development. Warns that the left's dismissive approach to AI prevents preparation for labor market impacts and cedes political influence to the right. Emphasizes risks of political polarization around AI policy and the danger of being unprepared for 'one of the most consequential technological shifts in history.' Also covers concerns about corporate power concentration and democratic participation gaps.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for serious engagement with AI development rather than dismissive approaches. Criticizes the political left for treating AI as 'spicy autocomplete' and argues this prevents effective regulation. Supports taking AI capabilities seriously to enable proper regulatory preparation, warning that dismissive attitudes lead to focus on 'popping the economic bubble' rather than wise regulation and preparation for AI's impacts.

people.name: Joi Ito
people.title: Former Director, MIT Media Lab (2011-2019); President, Chiba Institute of Technology
people.other_orgs: Former board member Creative Commons, Mozilla Foundation, Knight Foundation, MacArthur Foundation, The New York Times Company; Former visiting professor Harvard Law School; Board member Gelephu M
people.notes: Resigned from MIT Media Lab directorship in September 2019 following revelations about financial ties to Jeffrey Epstein. Co-recipient of 2018 Turing Award with Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun for contributions to deep learning. Founded multiple tech companies including PSINet Japan, Infoseek Japan, and Digital Garage. Early investor in Twitter, Kickstarter, Flickr. Known for '9 Principles' philosophy emphasizing 'disobedience over compliance' and 'emergence over authority.' PhD in Media and Governance from Keio University (2018).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Environmental, Bias/discrimination
people.threat_models_detail: Emphasizes concerns about AI development being dominated by 'a predominately male gang' and warns about values embedded in AI systems. Advocates for neurodiversity and worries about AI eliminating human 'kinks' and 'mutations' that create art and innovation. Focuses on ensuring diverse community representation in breakthrough technologies through government funding to prevent concentration of power in few hands.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for light-touch government regulation early in AI technology development, stating 'early in a technology, a thousand flowers should bloom. And the government should add a relatively light touch, investing heavily in research and making sure there's a conversation between basic research and applied research.' Supports increased government involvement only as technologies mature, focusing on ensuring regulations 'reflect a broad base set of values' rather than forcing new technology into existing regulatory frameworks.

people.name: Chris Coons
people.title: U.S. Senator from Delaware, Chair of Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Intellectual Property
people.other_orgs: Former New Castle County Executive (2005-2010), Former Associate Counsel at W.L. Gore & Associates, Former Board Member of 'I Have a Dream' Foundation
people.notes: Chairs Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Intellectual Property, positioning him as key AI policy leader. Has held multiple hearings on AI and intellectual property intersection since 2023. Leads bipartisan AI legislation with Republicans including Marsha Blackburn, Thom Tillis, and Josh Hawley. Holds Yale Divinity School degree (1989-1992) and served as New Castle County Executive managing $230M budget. The NO FAKES Act has broad industry support including OpenAI, Disney, Warner Music Group, and SAG-AFTRA. Also introduced resolution in 2025 affirming importance of U.S. AI dominance over China.
people.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Copyright/IP, Labor displacement, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on protecting democratic processes through legislation banning deceptive AI in elections and deepfakes. Strongly concerned about unauthorized use of individuals' voices and likenesses, stating 'Nobody—whether they're Tom Hanks or an 8th grader just trying to be a kid—should worry about someone stealing their voice and likeness.' Also addresses power concentration by supporting AI whistleblower protections to ensure transparency and accountability in AI development, particularly concerned about tech companies knowing more about AI risks than regulators and the public.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Leads targeted regulatory approach focused on specific AI harms rather than broad restrictions. Co-sponsored the NO FAKES Act to protect voice and likeness from unauthorized AI replicas, the Protect Elections from Deceptive AI Act banning AI-generated content in political ads, and the AI Whistleblower Protection Act. Emphasizes balancing innovation with protection, stating 'Incredible technology like AI can help us push the limits of human creativity, but only if we protect Americans from those who would use it to harm our communities.' His legislation includes First Amendment protections and focuses on consent-based frameworks rather than capability restrictions.

people.name: Martin Heinrich
