people.title: U.S. Senator from New Mexico, Founder and Co-Chair of Senate AI Caucus, Member of Senate Intelligence Committee
people.other_orgs: Joint Economic Committee (Chair 2023-2025), Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee (Ranking Member), Senate Appropriations Committee, Senate Armed Services Committee, Majority Leader Schum
people.notes: Founded the bipartisan Senate AI Caucus with former Senator Rob Portman in 2019, making him a key congressional leader on AI policy. Holds a BS in Mechanical Engineering from University of Missouri-Columbia where he helped design a solar-powered car. Successfully passed modified versions of his AI legislation through the FY21 National Defense Authorization Act, including the Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act and AI for Armed Forces Act. Led establishment of the National AI Research Resource Task Force and secured $30 million in FY25 appropriations for NAIRR implementation.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Copyright/IP, National security, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Heinrich has specifically addressed deepfakes and AI-generated misinformation through the DEFIANCE Act and COPIED Act, focusing on non-consensual intimate images and content authenticity. He emphasizes concerns about AI's impact on elections and democracy, co-sponsoring transparency legislation for AI in elections. His legislative work shows particular concern about power concentration in AI development, advocating for democratized access through the NAIRR program, and addressing national security implications through his role on the Intelligence Committee and AI working groups.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Heinrich advocates for targeted AI regulation focused on specific high-risk applications while promoting innovation. He emphasizes establishing regulatory 'sandboxes' for financial services AI experimentation, supports the CREATE AI Act to democratize AI research access, and backs legislation like the COPIED Act for content authenticity and the DEFIANCE Act for deepfakes. His approach balances fostering American AI leadership with implementing guardrails for specific risks rather than broad restrictions.

people.name: Todd Young
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-IN), member of Senate Commerce Committee
people.other_orgs: Former U.S. Representative (IN-9th District, 2011-2016); Senate AI Working Group member; National AI Advisory Committee co-sponsor
people.notes: Member of the bipartisan Senate AI Working Group that produced a comprehensive AI policy roadmap in 2024. Co-authored legislation establishing the National AI Advisory Committee (NAIAC). Former U.S. Marine and Indiana University law graduate. Has focused significantly on international alliances and standards-setting as key to U.S. AI leadership, arguing that if the U.S. doesn't establish standards, other nations will shape global norms without American input.
people.threat_models: National security, Labor displacement, Economic inequality
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on maintaining U.S. competitiveness against China in AI development, warning that falling behind poses national security risks. The Future of AI Innovation Act addresses security risks through testbeds and evaluation tools. Has pushed for data on AI's impact on American workforce, indicating concern about job displacement. Emphasizes need for U.S. to lead in setting global AI standards to ensure American values are reflected.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsors the Future of AI Innovation Act with Sen. Cantwell, which emphasizes voluntary standards development through NIST and public-private partnerships rather than mandatory regulations. Has stated that federal AI policy should ensure technologies are 'developed in a manner that reflects American values and supports U.S. innovation.' Also co-sponsors the AI for Main Street Act to help small businesses adopt AI through training and guidance rather than restrictions.

people.name: Ben Ray Luján
people.title: U.S. Senator from New Mexico, Chair of Subcommittee on Communications, Media, and Broadband
people.other_orgs: Senate National Labs Caucus (co-lead), Former Chair of Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee (2015-2019), Former New Mexico Public Regulation Commission Chairman (2005-2007)
people.notes: First Hispanic to represent New Mexico in the U.S. Senate in over 40 years and one of only two Head Start graduates to serve in the Senate. Previously served as U.S. Representative for New Mexico's 3rd Congressional District (2009-2021) and was the first Hispanic to chair the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. Co-leads the Senate National Labs Caucus with focus on leveraging New Mexico's Sandia and Los Alamos National Labs for AI testing and evaluation. Earned Bachelor of Business Administration from New Mexico Highlands University.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Privacy, Bias/discrimination, National security, Misinformation, Copyright/IP
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses on protecting vulnerable communities from AI harms, particularly emphasizing language equity concerns where AI systems perform poorly in low-resourced languages, leading to 'widespread misinformation, disinformation, scams, and fraud amongst immigrant communities.' Concerned about artist protections from copyright infringement and market dilution, privacy breaches from data scraping without consent, and AI-enabled scams including voice cloning. Warns AI systems 'can fall short by providing inaccurate or biased data and are at risk of malicious attacks or misuse by our adversaries.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI regulation through multiple legislative initiatives. Co-sponsored the TEST AI Act requiring federal AI testing and evaluation systems, introduced the AI CONSENT Act mandating express consent before using personal data to train AI models, and pushed NTIA to create 'responsible guardrails' around AI development. Emphasized need for transparency, disclosure requirements, and tools to assess language equity and artist protections in AI systems. Stated 'it is time for Congress and the Administration to create and implement responsible guardrails around AI development, governance, and use.

people.name: Joni Ernst
people.title: U.S. Senator, IA (Ranking Member, Senate Armed Services Subcommittee on Emerging Threats and Capabilities)
people.other_orgs: Senate Armed Services Committee, Senate Small Business Committee (Chair), Senate Republican Policy Committee (former Chair 2023-2025), Abraham Accords Caucus (Co-chair), Iowa Army National Guard
people.notes: Combat veteran who served as company commander in Kuwait and Iraq (2003-2004), retiring as Lieutenant Colonel in 2015. First female combat veteran to serve in U.S. Senate and first woman to represent Iowa in Congress. Chair of Senate Armed Services Subcommittee on Emerging Threats and Capabilities, leading multiple hearings on AI and defense. Announced in September 2025 she will not seek reelection in 2026. Authored transparency law requiring public disclosure of Pentagon-funded research projects that the Biden administration has refused to enact.
people.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: Primarily concerned with AI as a national security threat, particularly from China. Led congressional response to Chinese state-sponsored hackers using Anthropic's Claude AI system for autonomous cyberattacks against 30 entities in September 2025. Warns that China and Russia are investing heavily in military AI applications, with Putin stating 'Whoever becomes the leader in this field will rule the world.' Also concerned about U.S. taxpayer funds inadvertently supporting Chinese AI research, conducting DoD Inspector General investigation into defense dollars sent to Chinese labs.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses primarily on national security applications of AI, particularly threats from China and adversarial use of AI systems. Co-sponsored the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence Act (2018) to study AI competitiveness and military applications. Recently co-authored legislation demanding government response to Chinese state-sponsored AI-enabled cyberattacks and introduced the AI ACCORD Act (2024) for AI cooperation with Middle East allies. Her approach targets specific security threats rather than broad AI regulation.

people.name: Maggie Hassan
people.title: U.S. Senator from New Hampshire, Ranking Member of Joint Economic Committee
people.other_orgs: Former Governor of New Hampshire (2013-2017), Former New Hampshire State Senator (2005-2010), Former Associate General Counsel at Brigham and Women's Hospital, Former Attorney at Palmer & Dodge
people.notes: Second woman in U.S. history to be elected both Governor and U.S. Senator (along with fellow NH Senator Jeanne Shaheen). Served as New Hampshire's 81st Governor (2013-2017) and State Senate Majority Leader (2008-2010). Expected to become New Hampshire's senior senator upon Shaheen's retirement in 2027. Has a son with severe disabilities, which drew her to advocacy work. Holds B.A. from Brown University and J.D. from Northeastern University School of Law.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, National security, Cybersecurity, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Emphasizes AI's impact on workers through the AI Workforce PREPARE Act, which updates the WARN Act to notify employees if AI is a substantial factor in layoffs and creates programs for rapid retraining of AI-displaced workers. Focuses heavily on national security threats from foreign adversaries like China using AI for cyberattacks and the potential for AI to enable chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear, and cyber weapons. Also addresses AI-enabled scams, pressing six major AI companies in December 2025 about preventing fraudsters from using their platforms as scam losses reached $16.6 billion in 2024.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on specific AI risks through targeted legislation rather than broad restrictions. Co-sponsored the GUARD Act requiring age verification and disclosures for AI chatbots to protect minors, introduced the Reliable Artificial Intelligence Research Act investing $10 million over five years in AI security research competitions, and helped introduce the Preserving American Dominance in AI Act establishing safety reviews only for the most advanced 'frontier' AI models. Her approach emphasizes protecting workers, national security, and specific vulnerable populations while maintaining U.S. technological leadership.

people.name: Jerry Moran
people.title: U.S. Senator, KS (R) - Senior member Commerce Committee, Chair Veterans' Affairs Committee
people.other_orgs: Chair Commerce Subcommittee on Aviation, Space and Innovation; Chair Appropriations Subcommittee on Commerce, Justice, Science; Senate Intelligence Committee; Fort Hays State University Endowment
people.notes: Chairs Senate Veterans' Affairs Committee and Commerce Subcommittee on Aviation, Space and Innovation, giving him significant jurisdiction over AI policy through commerce and appropriations roles. Co-founded multiple Senate caucuses including Sustainable Aviation Fuel Caucus and Senate Economic Mobility Caucus. Economics degree from University of Kansas, J.D. from University of Kansas. Former small-town banker and Kansas State Senate Majority Leader. Served as NRSC Chairman in 2014 cycle that gained Republicans 9 Senate seats.
people.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: Co-authored Romney-Reed-Moran-King framework specifically addressing AI-enabled extreme risks from biological, chemical, cyber, and nuclear threats. Stated that AI has 'tremendous potential to fill the gaps' in rural communities but emphasized need to responsibly harness AI power while mitigating extreme risks that threaten national security. Focuses heavily on workforce displacement concerns, sponsoring multiple bills for AI training and education to ensure workers can adapt to AI integration rather than be displaced by it.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on narrowly-scoped AI legislation targeting specific risks while promoting innovation. Co-sponsored the Federal Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Act requiring federal agencies to adopt NIST AI Risk Management Framework. Co-authored framework with Romney, Reed, and King specifically targeting extreme risks from frontier AI models including biological, chemical, cyber, and nuclear threats, while explicitly aiming to avoid hampering American innovation. Emphasizes bipartisan approach to AI education and workforce development rather than broad restrictions.

people.name: John Hickenlooper
people.title: U.S. Senator, CO (Chair, Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Product Safety, and Data Security)
people.other_orgs: Former Governor of Colorado (2011-2019), Former Mayor of Denver (2003-2011), Founder of Wynkoop Brewing Company
people.notes: Chair of the Senate Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Product Safety, and Data Security, making him a key figure in AI consumer protection policy. Has introduced or co-sponsored at least 6 major AI bills including the VET AI Act, Future of AI Innovation Act, AI Research Innovation and Accountability Act, COPIED Act, and Take It Down Act. Delivered major AI policy speech at Silicon Flatirons 2024 Flagship Conference outlining his 'Trust, but Verify Framework.' Previously founded Colorado's first brewpub (Wynkoop Brewing Company) and has geology background (MS from Wesleyan University, 1980).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Privacy, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Hickenlooper has focused extensively on AI-enabled fraud and scams, chairing hearings on 'Protecting Consumers from Artificial Intelligence Enabled Fraud and Scams' and introducing the Take It Down Act to criminalize non-consensual intimate AI-generated images. He has expressed concerns about deepfakes undermining democratic processes and consumer trust, stating 'AI could bring more harm than good to our lives' if not developed responsibly. His legislative package addresses content authenticity, algorithmic bias (supporting federal algorithmic bias legislation), and the concentration of AI power in few companies.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Hickenlooper advocates for targeted AI regulation through voluntary standards and industry-government collaboration rather than broad mandates. He has introduced multiple bipartisan bills including the VET AI Act (creating voluntary third-party AI auditing guidelines), the Future of AI Innovation Act (establishing voluntary federal AI standards), and co-sponsored the AI Research, Innovation, and Accountability Act (creating tiered risk assessments for 'critical-impact' vs 'high-impact' AI systems). His approach emphasizes 'sensible guardrails' and 'Trust, but Verify Framework' while explicitly supporting innovation and rejecting heavy-handed regulation.

people.name: Jon Husted
people.title: U.S. Senator from Ohio (2025-present), former Lt. Governor of Ohio (2019-2025)
people.other_orgs: Director of InnovateOhio (2019-2025), former Ohio Secretary of State (2011-2019), former Speaker of Ohio House of Representatives (2005-2009), former Vice President at Dayton Area Chamber of Comm
people.notes: Currently serves on Senate committees including Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions; Small Business and Entrepreneurship; Environment and Public Works; and Special Committee on Aging. As Ohio Lt. Governor, successfully implemented AI tool RegExplorer to streamline state regulatory code, reducing it by one-third and saving 58,000 manhours over a decade. Former University of Dayton football player who won 1989 NCAA Division III National Championship. Received $116,892 in campaign contributions from retail executive Les Wexner between 2001-2025. Appointed to U.S. Senate by Governor Mike DeWine in January 2025 to fill JD Vance's vacant seat.
people.threat_models: National security, Power concentration, Labor displacement, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on national security competition with China, stating 'If we want to have economic prominence and national security dominance, we need to have technological dominance.' Concerned about Chinese AI platforms like DeepSeek operating on federal networks. Addresses child safety risks from AI companion chatbots that have encouraged self-harm and exposed minors to explicit content. Also introduced the Preventing Deep Fake Scams Act targeting AI-enabled fraud and identity theft.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI innovation with targeted safeguards, emphasizing America's need for technological dominance over China. Introduced the CHAT Act of 2025 requiring parental consent and age verification for AI companion chatbots for minors, but focuses primarily on child safety rather than broad AI restrictions. Champions using AI for government efficiency, leading Ohio's successful AI-driven regulatory code reduction that cut 5 million words and saved $44 million. Opposes overregulation that could harm U.S. competitiveness, stating 'America has to lead the world in developing, applying and safely stewarding AI.

people.name: Richard Blumenthal
people.title: U.S. Senator, CT (Senior Senator, Ranking Member of Veterans' Affairs Committee)
people.other_orgs: Former Connecticut Attorney General (1991-2011), Former U.S. Attorney for District of Connecticut (1977-1981), Chair of Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Privacy, Technology, and the Law
people.notes: Identified as one of the top 20 'AGI-pilled' members of Congress in 2026 analysis. Has declared 'The urgency here demands action. The future is not science fiction or fantasy. It's not even the future. It's here and now.' Harvard College graduate (Editorial Chairman of Harvard Crimson, Phi Beta Kappa, Magna Cum Laude), Yale Law School J.D. (Editor-in-Chief of Yale Law Journal). Former U.S. Marine Corps Reserve sergeant (1970-1976). Classmate of Bill Clinton and Hillary Clinton at Yale Law School.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Has explicitly warned about AGI as an existential threat, stating experts say AI 'is an existential threat to humanity' and 'we might want to listen.' Focuses heavily on child safety concerns, citing cases where AI chatbots encouraged suicide and self-harm. Also concerned about AI being used by Russia, China, and Iran to interfere in elections and democracy, and the creation of sexually explicit deepfakes. Has warned about 'loss-of-control scenarios and weaponization by adversaries.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsored the GUARD Act to ban AI companions for minors with criminal penalties for companies, requiring age verification and mandatory disclosure of non-human status. Co-sponsored the Artificial Intelligence Risk Evaluation Act requiring pre-deployment evaluation by DOE and prohibition of deployment until compliance. Has stated 'Big Tech has betrayed any claim that we should trust companies to do the right thing on their own' and advocates for 'strict safeguards against exploitative or manipulative AI, backed by tough enforcement with criminal and civil penalties.

people.name: Jacky Rosen
people.title: U.S. Senator from Nevada
people.other_orgs: Former President of Congregation Ner Tamid (Nevada's largest synagogue), Former member of Culinary Workers Union Local 226, Co-founder and Co-chair of Bipartisan Task Force for Combating Antisemi
people.notes: First and only former computer programmer to serve in the U.S. Senate, having worked as a programmer at major companies in Southern Nevada after earning associate's degree in computing and information technology from Clark County Community College (1985). Third female Jewish Senator in U.S. history and first former synagogue president to serve in the Senate. Her bipartisan Building Blocks of STEM Act was signed into law, and her Department of Defense Civilian Cybersecurity Reserve Act became law to recruit civilian cybersecurity personnel. Co-authored the bipartisan TAKE IT DOWN Act criminalizing non-consensual intimate imagery including deepfakes, which passed Congress in April 2025.
people.threat_models: National security, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Labor displacement
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on national security risks from foreign adversary AI systems, particularly Chinese-controlled platforms that could expose government data to hostile intelligence agencies. Strongly concerned about AI-generated antisemitic content and hate speech, having personally confronted Trump Administration officials about using AI systems with documented history of promoting conspiracy theories. Also addresses economic harms including AI-enabled surveillance pricing that allows corporations to discriminate against consumers, and broader privacy violations as AI systems collect and process personal data at unprecedented scale.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation focusing on specific harms rather than broad restrictions. Co-sponsored bipartisan legislation to ban Chinese-controlled AI platform DeepSeek from government devices due to national security concerns. Pushed back against Trump Administration's use of Grok AI system with history of antisemitic content, demanding agencies avoid AI that promotes hate speech. Supports federal privacy legislation as foundation for AI governance and has introduced bills to combat AI-generated deepfakes and surveillance pricing that targets consumers with discriminatory pricing.

people.name: Ted Cruz
people.title: U.S. Senator from Texas, Chairman of Senate Commerce Committee
people.other_orgs: Former Texas Solicitor General (2003-2008), Former George W. Bush administration advisor, Former Federal Trade Commission and Department of Justice attorney
people.notes: As Chairman of the Senate Commerce Committee since January 2025, controls jurisdiction over approximately 37% of all AI bills in the Senate. Successfully authored and passed the TAKE IT DOWN Act in 2025, the only major AI-related law passed by Congress since ChatGPT's release. Graduated magna cum laude from Harvard Law School (1995) and cum laude from Princeton University (1992). Former Supreme Court clerk to Chief Justice Rehnquist (1996-1997) and argued nine cases before the Supreme Court as Texas Solicitor General, winning five. Failed 2016 Republican presidential candidate.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses primarily on preventing China from achieving AI dominance, arguing that 'if China dominates AI for the world, that is a profoundly bad outcome for America and the rest of the world.' His framework addresses 'nefarious uses of AI against Americans' including digital impersonation scams and fraud. Co-authored and passed the TAKE IT DOWN Act in 2025 to combat non-consensual intimate deepfakes, noting that such content increased 3,000% in one year with 95% of victims being women or teenage girls. Also concerned about government censorship of AI, proposing anti-'jawboning' measures to prevent federal interference with AI content.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a 'light-touch regulatory approach' to AI based on his five-pillar Legislative Framework for American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence. Introduced the SANDBOX Act (S. 2750) in September 2025, which would allow AI companies to apply for 2-year waivers from federal regulations for up to 10 years. Explicitly opposes creating new federal rulemaking bodies for AI and supports 'industry-led standards' instead. Previously led failed efforts to impose a 10-year moratorium on state AI regulations, arguing that 'a single state should not have the power to set AI rules for the entire country.

people.name: Michael McCaul
people.title: U.S. Representative, TX-10, Chairman Emeritus House Foreign Affairs Committee
people.other_orgs: Congressional Artificial Intelligence Caucus (Co-chair), Congressional High Tech Caucus (Founder and Co-chair), Congressional Cyber Security Caucus (Co-chair), Former Chief of Counter Terrorism a
people.notes: Former Chairman of House Foreign Affairs Committee (2023-2025) and House Homeland Security Committee (2013-2019), making him the first Texan to chair Foreign Affairs. Led passage of AUKUS bill advancing AI technologies for advanced weapons systems with UK and Australia. Co-authored AI chip registry proposal that advanced to House Foreign Affairs Committee in April 2024 via collaboration with RAND, CNAS, and IAPS researchers. Has JD from St. Mary's University School of Law and completed Senior Executive Fellowship at Harvard Kennedy School. Announced he will not seek re-election in 2026. Net worth estimated at $294 million as of 2011.
people.threat_models: National security, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on China using American AI technology for military advantage and great power competition, stating 'China is the greatest threat that we face today' and that AI 'opens the windows to military-grade applications.' Concerned about H200 chips being 'integral to modern military applications' and China using NVIDIA chips to 'leapfrog the United States in AI.' Also addresses deepfake threats to legal system integrity, noting courts face 'growing threat from AI-generated deepfakes' that could undermine confidence in justice system. Views AI as 'the future of warfare' in context of advanced weapon systems.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on export controls and national security restrictions rather than broad AI regulation. Co-authored the ENFORCE Act to give Commerce Department authority to use export controls on AI systems that threaten national security, specifically prohibiting sales of military-grade AI technology to China. Co-authored the CREATE AI Act to establish the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) to expand AI research access. Supports 'pro-innovation agenda for AI' while securing supply chains through international cooperation via Pax Silica initiative.

people.name: Zoe Lofgren
people.title: U.S. Representative (CA-18), Ranking Member of House Science, Space and Technology Committee
people.other_orgs: Chair of California Democratic Congressional Delegation, House Judiciary Committee, former House Administration Committee Chair, former House Immigration and Citizenship Subcommittee Chair, Santa
people.notes: Stanford University graduate (1970, political science) and Santa Clara University School of Law (1975, cum laude). Daughter of truck driver and cafeteria cook, worked night shift at Eastman Kodak to pay for college. Only Member of Congress to participate in all four modern impeachment proceedings and first woman to present presidential impeachment case to Senate (2020). Served 8 years on staff of predecessor Don Edwards before election to Congress in 1994. Has consistently reintroduced Online Privacy Act legislation in 2019, 2021, 2023, and 2025.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Privacy, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on immediate AI harms including nonconsensual sexual imagery and child exploitation, citing xAI's Grok generating 'thousands of instances of nonconsensual sexual imagery – including child pornography.' Concerned about authoritarian use of AI and power concentration, criticizing the Trump administration's equity stakes in private companies as resembling 'Chinese state capitalism.' Prioritizes data privacy through comprehensive federal legislation establishing user rights to access, correct, delete data and requiring companies to minimize data collection.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports federal AI framework over state preemption, advocating for evidence-based regulation that addresses specific harms like deepfakes and child exploitation. Opposed California's SB 1047 as overly restrictive, stating 'any AI risk framework should be based on empirical data and fit for purpose.' Has reintroduced the Online Privacy Act four times since 2019, focusing on data minimization, user rights, and creating a Digital Privacy Agency rather than broad AI restrictions.

people.name: Sriram Krishnan
people.title: Senior Policy Advisor for Artificial Intelligence, White House Office of Science and Technology Policy
people.primary_org: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy
people.other_orgs: Former General Partner at Andreessen Horowitz (2021-2024), Former product leader at Microsoft, Facebook, Twitter, Yahoo!, Snap, Co-host of 'The Aarthi and Sriram Show' podcast
people.notes: Named Time Person of the Year 2025 as an 'Architect of Artificial Intelligence.' Born in Chennai, India (1984), naturalized US citizen (2016). Bachelor of Technology from SRM University (2001-2005). Led Andreessen Horowitz's London office (2023-2024), first non-US location. Worked with Elon Musk on Twitter rebuild (2022). Co-authored US AI Action Plan calling for $600+ billion investment in AI infrastructure. Conducted 'AI diplomacy' missions to Paris AI Summit and Middle East with Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.
people.threat_models: National security
people.threat_models_detail: Primarily focused on geopolitical competition risks, specifically the threat of China winning the 'AI race.' Views AI development through lens of national competitiveness and market share dominance. Has publicly dismissed existential AI risks and AGI timeline concerns as distractions from focus on US technological leadership.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for accelerating AI development to maintain US dominance over China, describing national AI development as 'an existential race with China.' Co-authored the AI Action Plan (July 2025) focused on removing barriers to AI production and promoting global adoption of American AI stack. Has stated 'let them cook' regarding private companies developing AI models and opposes state-by-state AI regulation as hindering competitiveness. Dismissed concerns about imminent AGI as 'proven wrong' and 'harmful distraction.

people.name: Alondra Nelson
people.title: Harold F. Linder Professor, Institute for Advanced Study; Former Acting Director, White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (2022)
people.primary_org: Office of Science and Technology Policy
people.other_orgs: Center for American Progress (Distinguished Senior Fellow, 2023-present), UN High-Level Advisory Body on Artificial Intelligence (2023-present), National Science Board (2024-2025), Social Science
people.influence_type: Decision-maker, Advisor/strategist, Researcher/analyst, Narrator, Implementer, Connector/convener
people.notes: First African American and woman of color to lead OSTP (2022). Named to TIME100 AI inaugural list (2023) and Nature's '10 People Who Shaped Science' (2022). Author of 'The Social Life of DNA' and other books on race, technology, and society. PhD American Studies from NYU (2003), BA Anthropology from UC San Diego (1994). Served as 14th President/CEO of Social Science Research Council (2017-2019). Currently leads Science, Technology, and Social Values Lab at IAS Princeton. Co-signed major AI governance statement with world leaders calling for urgent government action (March 2026).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Cybersecurity, Environmental
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses extensively on societal-scale AI risks including destabilization in employment, algorithmic discrimination affecting access to resources and social mobility, disinformation and misinformation eroding democracy and public trust, cybersecurity vulnerabilities at scale, sustainability issues from AI systems, and power concentration among a few companies. Particularly concerned about AI enabling mass surveillance, discrimination, and civil liberties erosion. Has warned about risks from aligned AI including authoritarian entrenchment and democratic lock-in scenarios.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation using both existing and new frameworks. Led development of the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights with five core principles (safety, algorithmic discrimination protection, data privacy, notice and explanation, and human alternatives). Supports government oversight rather than industry self-regulation, stating 'Self-regulation is not the way forward here' and 'there is no AI exception to the law.' Emphasizes using existing regulatory authorities creatively while developing new rules where needed, particularly for high-risk AI applications.

people.name: Mark Warner
people.title: U.S. Senator from Virginia, Vice Chairman of Senate Select Committee on Intelligence
people.other_orgs: Columbia Capital (founder and managing director), Capital Cellular Corporation (co-founder), Forward Together PAC (honorary chairman), Former Governor of Virginia (2002-2006)
people.notes: Vice Chairman of Senate Select Committee on Intelligence since 2021, serving on Banking, Finance, Budget, and Rules Committees. Ranked 10th most bipartisan member of U.S. Senate during 114th Congress by Bipartisan Index. Former successful technology entrepreneur and early cellular industry investor, co-founded company that became Nextel. Net worth of $214.1 million, making him the seventh-wealthiest member of Congress. Graduated valedictorian from George Washington University (1977) and Harvard Law School (1980). Received Publius Award (2013) for bipartisan financial reform work and Navy's highest civilian honor.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Primarily focused on economic disruption and workforce displacement, warning that recent college graduate unemployment could surge from 9% to 30% due to AI. Has expressed deep concern about AI-powered misinformation and deepfakes threatening elections, markets, and democratic institutions. Emphasizes national security risks from advanced AI systems and foreign adversaries, particularly China. Also concerned about power concentration in AI development being limited to a few large companies rather than benefiting researchers, students, and small businesses.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation focused on specific use cases and transparency requirements rather than broad restrictions. Introduced the Economy of the Future Commission Act to develop legislative recommendations for AI workforce impacts, the AI Jobs Clarity Act requiring companies to report AI-related layoffs, and the GUARD Act banning AI companions for minors. Has pushed for better federal data collection on AI's workforce impact and called for national security agencies to establish plans for advanced AI threats. Supports regulatory frameworks that don't preempt state authority, opposing federal preemption attempts.

people.name: Suhas Subramanyam
people.title: U.S. Representative, VA-10
people.other_orgs: Former White House technology policy advisor (Obama Administration), Former Virginia House of Delegates (2019-2023), Former Virginia State Senate (2023-2024), Stand With Crypto questionnaire part
people.notes: Ranking Member of the House Military and Foreign Affairs Oversight Subcommittee. Former White House technology policy advisor to President Obama. Previously served in Virginia House of Delegates (2019-2023) and Virginia State Senate (2023-2024). Lawyer who worked with tech startups and started his own business. Strong supporter of cryptocurrency policies, having signed letter urging Democratic National Committee to adopt pro-crypto policies and completed Stand With Crypto questionnaire.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration
people.threat_models_detail: The Small AI Innovators Empowerment Act specifically addresses concerns about market concentration, with Subramanyam stating 'The future of AI will be shaped not just by big tech, but also by small businesses and startups' and emphasizing the need to help small AI companies compete against large technology firms. The legislation investigates challenges small AI businesses face in accessing capital, computing resources, and talent while navigating regulatory uncertainty.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsored the Small AI Innovators Empowerment Act with Rep. Jay Obernolte (R-CA), which directs a study of challenges facing small AI businesses including regulatory uncertainty, rather than imposing new restrictions. The bill focuses on understanding barriers to federal funding, R&D tax credits, talent recruitment, and navigating existing regulations to support AI innovation and competitiveness. Completed Stand With Crypto questionnaire demonstrating support for comprehensive regulatory frameworks that offer clarity and promote responsible innovation.

people.name: Greg Abbott
people.title: Governor of Texas
people.other_orgs: Former Texas Attorney General (2002-2015), Former Texas Supreme Court Justice (1996-2001), Former Harris County District Judge (1993-1995), Texas Cyber Command (oversight role)
people.notes: Named one of TIME's 100 Most Influential People in 2024. Currently serving his third term as Texas Governor (elected 2014, re-elected 2018, 2022), running for historic fourth term in 2026. Previously served as Texas Attorney General for 12 years (2002-2015), making him the longest-serving AG in Texas history. Established Texas Cyber Command in June 2025, described as 'the largest state-based cybersecurity department in America.' Graduated from University of Texas with BBA in Finance and earned JD from Vanderbilt University.
people.threat_models: National security, Misinformation, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: TRAIGA specifically prohibits AI systems that manipulate human behavior to incite harm, criminality, or circumvent informed decision-making. The law also bans government social scoring systems. Abbott has expressed particular concern about foreign adversaries like China using AI to 'harvest user data through AI and other applications and hardware to exploit, manipulate, and violate users.' He emphasized preventing hostile actors from infiltrating state networks and devices through AI technologies.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Signed the Texas Responsible AI Governance Act (TRAIGA) into law in June 2025, representing the first comprehensive AI law in a red state. The law focuses on government AI systems requiring disclosure to consumers when interacting with AI, prohibits AI systems designed to encourage self-harm or criminal activity, and bans social scoring by government entities. Abbott emphasized the law is designed to 'encourage tech innovation while protecting Texans from harm.' Also updated the state's prohibited technologies list to ban AI and hardware affiliated with China/CCP, citing national security concerns.

people.name: Holden Karnofsky
people.title: Member of Technical Staff - Anthropic
people.other_orgs: Co-Founder and former Co-CEO of Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) (formerly Open Philanthropy) (now Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)), Co-Founder of GiveWell, Former Board Member of OpenAI (4 years), Former Visiting Scholar at Carnegie Endowment for Inte
people.notes: Joined Anthropic in 2024 after taking leave from Open Philanthropy in February 2023 to work directly on AI safety. Led Open Philanthropy for seven years, directing over $4 billion in grants and serving as one of the largest philanthropic funders of AI safety research since 2015. Co-founded GiveWell in 2007 and served on OpenAI's board for four years. Married to Daniela Amodei, President and co-founder of Anthropic. Harvard graduate (2003) in social studies, formerly worked in hedge fund industry. Author of influential 'Most Important Century' blog post series about transformative AI timelines.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: Has extensively written about risks from transformative AI including potential for AI systems to develop 'dangerous goals of their own and defeating humanity.' Specifically concerned about 'rogue AI takeover' scenarios where AI systems could act as 'insider threats' and the concentration of AI development in few companies. Has warned about AI-enabled bioweapons and the risks of AI R&D automation accelerating dangerous capabilities development. Puts roughly 50/50 odds on good outcomes even with current reckless approach but emphasizes humanity is 'taking far more risk than we should.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'responsible scaling policies' and voluntary safety standards rather than pausing AI development or broad restrictive regulation. Supports targeted government intervention including safety standards, monitoring systems, and intelligence gathering on AI capabilities in other countries, but emphasizes that premature regulation could be counterproductive. Has argued that government policy will ultimately be necessary for 'actual low-level risk' but prefers industry self-regulation as a stepping stone, stating 'the tractability is massively higher of changing companies' behaviour' compared to government action.

people.name: Dustin Moskovitz
people.title: Co-founder and Chairman, Asana; Co-founder, Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
people.other_orgs: Facebook co-founder and former CTO, Anthropic board observer, Good Ventures co-founder, Giving Pledge signatory
people.notes: Co-founded Facebook at age 19 as Mark Zuckerberg's roommate and served as first CTO. Left in 2008 to found Asana, stepping down as CEO in May 2025. Through Coefficient Giving has donated over $4 billion total, including $30 million to OpenAI (2017) and investments in Anthropic's early funding rounds. Investment portfolio focuses on B2B infrastructure, deep tech, and AI safety companies including Vicarious, Ginkgo Bioworks, Wave, and Benchling. Forbes estimated net worth of $11 billion as of 2025.
people.threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, Economic inequality, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Primarily concerned with existential risks from advanced AI systems that could pose threats to humanity's survival. Worries about concentration of AI capabilities in few large labs creating dangerous competitive dynamics. Through Coefficient Giving, has funded organizations warning about AI's potential to cause human extinction if developed without proper safety measures. Also concerned about economic disruption and power concentration among tech companies.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for cautious AI development with mandatory safety testing and international coordination. Supports government regulation to manage competitive pressures that push companies to develop AI too quickly. Has stated 'the invisible hand is not going to keep us safe' and believes only regulation can force companies to prioritize safety over profit. Funds AI safety research and lobbying efforts totaling over $100 million annually through Coefficient Giving.

people.name: Scott Wiener
people.title: CA State Senator, District 11 (San Francisco, Broadmoor, Colma, Daly City)
people.other_orgs: Chair of Senate Elections Committee, former Chair of Senate Budget Committee, Co-Chair of California Legislative Jewish Caucus, Chair of Senate Mental Health Caucus, Governor's Council on Holocau
people.notes: Author of over 100 bills signed into law including nation's strongest net neutrality law (SB 822). Harvard Law School graduate, former Fulbright Scholar in Chile. Represents San Francisco's Castro neighborhood, same district previously represented by Harvey Milk. His SB 53 became the first major AI safety law in the US when signed by Governor Newsom in September 2025, following the veto of his more comprehensive SB 1047 in 2024.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: SB 53 specifically addresses risks of AI systems being used to develop chemical, biological, radiological or nuclear weapons, conduct cyberattacks on critical infrastructure, and exhibit deceptive behavior including attempts to blackmail developers. Also concerned about economic displacement due to rapid AI implementation speed and concentration of AI compute power 'in few hands' which CalCompute provision aims to address.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Authored SB 1047 (vetoed) and SB 53 (signed into law) specifically targeting frontier AI models above 10^26 FLOPs and companies with $500M+ revenue. Has explicitly stated this is 'not about smaller AI models' but focused on 'incredibly large and powerful models' from major labs. The final SB 53 emphasizes transparency requirements, safety disclosures, and whistleblower protections rather than broad restrictions, with penalties capped at $1 million per violation.

people.name: Yann LeCun
people.title: Chief AI Scientist (former), Meta AI; Silver Professor, NYU; Founder and Executive Chairman, Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs)
people.other_orgs: New York University Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, NYU Center for Data Science (founding director 2012-2014), Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs, founder/executive chairman
people.notes: Turing Award winner (2018) with Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio for deep learning breakthroughs. Left Meta in November 2025 after 12 years to found Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs), which raised $1.03 billion at $3.5 billion pre-money valuation in March 2026 to develop world models using self-supervised learning approaches. Chevalier de la Légion d'Honneur (2023), member of US National Academy of Sciences (2021), IEEE Neural Network Pioneer Award (2014). Pioneered convolutional neural networks and JEPA (Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture). PhD Computer Science, Université Pierre et Marie Curie (1987). Received TIME100 Impact Award (February 2024) for contributions to artificial intelligence. Vocal critic of AI doomerism and strong proponent that open-source AI development is the path to prevent corporate AI monopolization and ensure democratic access to AI systems.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Primary concern is concentration of AI power in a small number of proprietary companies, which he sees as a greater danger than technical AI risks. Warns that closed AI systems controlled by few West Coast companies would be unacceptable to democratic governments and dangerous to local culture, values, and languages. Believes current AI systems are far from capable enough to pose existential risks, calling such concerns 'preposterous' and stating there is 'absolutely no way' that autoregressive LLMs will reach human intelligence. In January 2025 called predictions of human-level AI arriving within two years 'complete BS.' Uses turbojet analogy arguing that AI safety must be developed through iterative engineering rather than theoretical approaches, though acknowledges potential for AI-enhanced misinformation and misuse by bad actors.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong advocate for open-source AI development and minimal regulation that could impede research progress. Co-signed open letter to EU policymakers in 2024 criticizing fragmented regulatory decision-making and arguing that regulations should not impair open research, model training, and responsible product deployment. In September 2023 Senate Intelligence Committee testimony, supported voluntary commitments like the White House's AI commitments and collaborative industry standards rather than restrictive government oversight. Argues that open-source platforms are essential for preventing concentration of AI power and ensuring diverse, democratically-aligned AI systems. Called current AI systems 'not that smart' and argued against premature regulation of LLMs, stating in TIME interview that claims about needing to regulate LLMs because they're 'gonna be so dangerous' are 'just not true.

people.name: Dan Hendrycks
people.title: Executive Director, Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
people.other_orgs: Advisor at xAI, Advisor at Scale AI, Former Research Intern at DeepMind, UC Berkeley (PhD Computer Science)
people.notes: Led the 'Statement on AI Risk' signed by 600+ leading AI researchers and public figures including Sam Altman, Bill Gates, and Elon Musk (May 2023). Created foundational AI benchmarks including MMLU and contributed GELU activation function used in GPT, BERT, and Vision Transformers. PhD in Computer Science from UC Berkeley (2018-2022). AI2050 Early Career Fellow (Schmidt Sciences), TIME AI 100 honoree. Co-authored 'Superintelligence Strategy' paper with former Google CEO Eric Schmidt and Scale AI CEO Alexandr Wang. Published textbook 'Introduction to AI Safety, Ethics, and Society.'
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Warns of three primary risk sources: malicious use by humans (bioweapons, cyberattacks on critical infrastructure), loss of control scenarios from automated AI research loops without human oversight, and structural pressures creating unwanted AI behaviors. Specifically concerned about AI systems with expert-level virology capabilities enabling pandemic-causing bioweapons and autonomous weapons systems. Co-authored paper describing scenarios where AI development could lead to intelligence explosion and loss of human control, estimating 80% chance AI arms race results in catastrophe killing most of humanity.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific 'red lines' in AI development including no fully automated AI research recursion, no AI agents with expert-level virology or cyber offensive skills without safeguards, and secure containment of powerful model weights. Co-authored 'Superintelligence Strategy' proposing Mutual Assured AI Malfunction (MAIM) deterrence framework. Supports targeted interventions rather than broad AI pauses, stating technical research during pauses may not yield breakthroughs. Testified before Congress and advised White House on AI safety policy.

people.name: Daniela Amodei
people.title: Co-Founder and President, Anthropic
people.other_orgs: Former VP of Safety and Policy at OpenAI (2020), Former Risk Manager at Stripe (2016-2018), Married to Holden Karnofsky (Co-founder of Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) (formerly Open Philanthropy))
people.notes: Co-founded Anthropic in 2021 with brother Dario Amodei after leaving OpenAI over safety and governance disagreements. Led the world's largest qualitative AI study with 80,000+ participants across 159 countries (2024). Former VP of Safety and Policy at OpenAI during GPT-2 development. BA English Literature from UC Santa Cruz, summa cum laude. Named to Time 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Anthropic has raised $7B+ and serves over 300,000 business customers. Net worth estimated at $7 billion as of February 2026.
people.threat_models: Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination
people.threat_models_detail: Has publicly discussed AI bioterrorism risks and the potential for models to help design bioweapons, leading to ASL-3 protections in Anthropic's scaling policy. Warns about concentration of AI power and risks of autocratic governments using AI for surveillance and control. Emphasizes the 'light and shade' philosophy - that AI brings both great promise and great risk simultaneously. Co-authored company positions on preventing AI-enabled totalitarianism and maintaining democratic oversight of powerful AI systems.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for Anthropic's 'responsible scaling policy' with AI Safety Levels (ASL-1 through ASL-5) marking capability thresholds. Supports transparency requirements and federal safety standards while opposing overly prescriptive legislation that amounts to 'safety theater.' Has stated willingness to support federal bioterrorism prevention measures if risks materialize, while generally favoring surgical interventions over broad restrictions. Emphasizes that safety and commercial success are not in tension.

people.name: Daniel Ho
people.title: William Benjamin Scott and Luna M. Scott Professor of Law, Professor of Political Science, Professor of Computer Science (by courtesy), Senior Fellow at Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI,
people.primary_org: Stanford RegLab (Regulation, Evaluation, and Governance Lab)
people.other_orgs: National Artificial Intelligence Advisory Committee (NAIAC), Senior Advisor on Responsible AI at U.S. Department of Labor, Public Member of Administrative Conference of the United States (ACUS),
people.notes: J.D. from Yale Law School, Ph.D. from Harvard University. Clerked for Judge Stephen F. Williams on U.S. Court of Appeals, D.C. Circuit. Recipient of John Bingham Hurlbut Award for Excellence in Teaching (2010), co-recipient of Warren Miller Prize for best paper in Political Analysis (2008), McGraw-Hill Award for best paper on law and courts (2006), and Pi Sigma Alpha award. RegLab has worked with numerous federal agencies on AI implementation. Published extensively on AI governance, administrative law, and empirical legal studies. Testified before both House and Senate committees on AI policy in 2023.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Cybersecurity, Environmental, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Research through RegLab focuses on algorithmic fairness in government systems, with specific attention to bias in criminal justice algorithms and healthcare AI systems. Has studied implementation challenges of AI governance, finding 88% of agencies failed to submit required AI plans and widespread gaps in transparency measures. Co-authored work on 'algorithmic black swans' - high-impact societal risks from AI systems including manipulation of information environments and destabilization of democratic institutions. Emphasizes risks from inadequate government AI governance and the need for technical expertise in regulatory agencies.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strategic government AI adoption with appropriate safeguards rather than broad restrictions. In congressional testimony, recommended establishing Chief AI Officers in agencies, creating pathways for technical talent in government, and improving AI procurement processes. Co-authored influential paper 'AI Regulation Has Its Own Alignment Problem' arguing that regulatory proposals often suffer from misalignment with societal values and technical feasibility issues. Supports targeted interventions like mandatory adverse event reporting systems and third-party audit ecosystems rather than blanket restrictions.

people.name: Meredith Whittaker
people.title: President of Signal Foundation
people.other_orgs: AI Now Institute (Chief Advisor, former Faculty Director & Co-founder), Former Senior Advisor on AI to FTC Chair Lina Khan, Former Minderoo Research Professor at NYU, Former Google employee (13 y
people.notes: Led Google Walkout (2018) with 20,000+ employees over sexual misconduct and surveillance issues. Core organizer against Google's Project Maven military drone contract. Co-founded M-Lab global network measurement platform. TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Testified before House Science Committee (2019) and House Oversight Committee (2020) on AI bias and facial recognition. Published 'The Steep Cost of Capture' (2021) on tech industry power concentration.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Privacy, Misinformation, Labor displacement, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on concentration of AI power among a 'scant handful' of tech monopolies with 70% of global cloud market. Warns that agentic AI systems break the 'blood-brain barrier' between apps, creating systemic surveillance vulnerabilities. Emphasizes that AI is 'built on surveillance' and represents expansion of the surveillance business model. Has testified about facial recognition entrenching marginalization and AI systems replicating harmful patterns against minoritized communities.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for structural regulation targeting the surveillance business model underlying AI development. Calls for privacy legislation to limit data collection that feeds AI systems, supports Lina Khan's proposal for structural separation of tech companies, and warns against voluntary compliance approaches. Has testified before Congress calling for whistleblower protections and halting facial recognition deployment. Opposes age verification systems and supports the Writers Guild approach of workplace-based guardrails on AI use.

people.name: Bernie Sanders
people.title: U.S. Senator from Vermont, Independent (Senior Senator)
people.other_orgs: Congressional Progressive Caucus (co-founder), Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee (former Chair 2023-2025), Senate Budget Committee (former Chair 2021-2023), Senate Veterans'
people.notes: At age 84, Sanders is the longest-serving independent in U.S. congressional history and was identified as one of roughly 20 'AGI-pilled' members of Congress in a 2026 LessWrong analysis. He conducted a viral video interview with Anthropic's Claude AI assistant in March 2026 that received over 2 million views, though was criticized by tech policy experts for demonstrating fundamental misunderstandings about how AI systems work. Sanders holds a Bachelor of Arts in political science from University of Chicago (1964) and served as Mayor of Burlington, Vermont from 1981-1989 before his Congressional career.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Environmental, Loss of control, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Sanders warns that AI could eliminate nearly 100 million American jobs over the next decade, including 40% of registered nurses, 47% of truck drivers, 64% of accountants, 65% of teaching assistants, and 89% of fast-food workers. He has expressed concerns about an AI-powered surveillance state where 'every phone call that we make, every email and text that we send, every bit of research we do on the internet will be available to the owners of AI.' Sanders also warns of existential risks, stating 'there is a very real fear that, in the not-so-distant future, a super-intelligent AI could replace humans in controlling the planet' and has referenced the 'science fiction fear of AI running the world is not quite so outrageous a concept as people may have thought it was.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsored the AI Data Center Moratorium Act of 2026 with Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, which would impose an immediate federal moratorium on AI data centers until strong national safeguards are in place. The legislation requires ensuring AI is safe and effective, that economic gains benefit workers rather than just wealthy tech owners, and that AI doesn't increase utility prices or harm the environment. Sanders has explicitly called for democratic oversight over what he views as decisions being made by 'a handful of billionaire Big Tech oligarchs' that will 'reshape our economy, our democracy and the future of humanity.

people.name: Alex Bores
people.title: New York State Assemblymember, 73rd District
people.other_orgs: Former software engineer at Palantir Technologies, Merlon.ai, Promise; Congressional candidate for NY-12; Future Caucus Rising Star award winner (2024)
people.notes: Named to Time 100 Most Influential People in AI list (2025); won Future Caucus Rising Star award (2024). Holds MS in Computer Science and worked at Palantir until 2019 when he resigned over ethical concerns regarding ICE contracts. Running for NY-12 Congressional seat and faces $10M+ opposition spending from AI industry super PAC Leading the Future, while receiving support from Anthropic-backed Public First Action. Would be only the second Democrat in Congress with a computer science degree if elected. Has proposed comprehensive 8-point national AI governance framework with 43 specific policy recommendations.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: The RAISE Act specifically targets catastrophic risks including AI models helping novices build bioweapons and autonomous systems acting beyond human control. Has proposed comprehensive legislation addressing AI-related job displacement through workforce transition programs and an 'AI dividend' funded by productivity gains. Also sponsors training data transparency legislation (AB 6578) and data provenance bills using C2PA standards to combat synthetic content misuse.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Sponsored the RAISE Act, which requires only the largest frontier AI developers (those spending $100M+ on training and having $500M+ revenue) to maintain public safety plans and report catastrophic incidents. Explicitly emphasizes the narrow scope, stating it would apply to only about 6 companies globally. Has described this as 'light-touch regulation' focused on transparency rather than restrictive oversight, comparing it to existing cybersecurity requirements that 'formalize what responsible companies already do.

people.name: Brian Deese
people.title: fmr Director of the National Economic Council (Biden Administration)
people.other_orgs: BlackRock (fmr Global Head of Sustainable Investing), MIT Innovation Fellow, Harvard Kennedy School, Brookings Institution, McKinsey & Company, Obama Administration (Deputy Director of NEC, Actin
people.notes: Served as Biden's NEC Director from 2021-2023, coordinating economic policy including AI and digital assets. Previously Global Head of Sustainable Investing at BlackRock (2017-2021). Helped develop Obama-era auto industry bailout and negotiated Paris Climate Agreement. Currently MIT Innovation Fellow (2023-present) focusing on climate change and sustainable economic growth. Co-authored 'Delivering on Debt Relief: From IMF Gold to a New Aid Architecture' (2002). Received Alexander Hamilton Award for Treasury service and Harvard GSAS Centennial Medal (2019).
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Environmental
people.threat_models_detail: As NEC Director, focused on ensuring AI benefits reach all Americans while addressing concerns about data center energy consumption and costs being passed to ratepayers. Co-authored cryptocurrency risk mitigation roadmap citing concerns about market volatility, illicit finance, and systemic risks. Emphasized need for AI governance that prevents concentration of benefits among tech elites.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored the Biden administration's roadmap to mitigate cryptocurrency risks, which emphasized regulatory clarity and risk mitigation while supporting innovation. Led economic policy coordination that balanced technological advancement with consumer protection, particularly in AI and digital assets. Supported federal frameworks over state-by-state approaches to avoid regulatory patchwork that could harm innovation.

people.name: Demis Hassabis
people.title: CEO and Co-Founder, Google DeepMind
people.other_orgs: Co-founder and CEO of Isomorphic Labs, UK Government AI Adviser, Fellow of the Royal Society, former visiting scientist at MIT and Harvard
people.notes: Nobel Prize winner in Chemistry (2024) for AI protein structure prediction research. Knighted in 2024 and appointed CBE in 2017 for AI work. Co-founded DeepMind in 2010, sold to Google for $500+ million in 2014. Child chess prodigy reaching master standard at age 13. PhD in cognitive neuroscience from UCL (2009). Named to TIME 100 most influential people (2017, 2025) and collectively chosen as TIME's 2025 Person of the Year as one of the 'Architects of AI.' Leads Google's AGI Safety Council and has published extensively on AI safety frameworks including the Frontier Safety Framework.
people.threat_models: Misuse by bad actors, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Has identified two main AI threats: technology being used by 'bad actors' including for cyberattacks and bioweapons creation, and 'risk of losing control of systems as they become more powerful.' Warns about 'harmful manipulation' capabilities and risks from autonomous systems that could 'deviate from human objectives.' States 'the risk of a catastrophic scenario is not zero' and emphasizes need for international cooperation because malicious actors could use AI for 'harmful ends' globally.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'smart regulation' focused on 'real risks' from AI, emphasizing international cooperation and standards rather than broad restrictions. Supports building 'robust guardrails' against misuse and loss of control while enabling beneficial uses. Has called for 'urgent research' on AI threats and international collaboration, but opposes overly bureaucratic approaches that could hamper development. Updated Google's AI principles in 2025 to allow military partnerships while maintaining high bar that 'benefits must substantially outweigh risks.

people.name: Chris Lehane
people.title: Chief Global Affairs Officer, OpenAI
people.other_orgs: Former Operating Partner at Haun Ventures (2022-2024), Former SVP Policy Communications at Airbnb (2015-2022), Former Co-Founder at Fabiani & Lehane (2001-2015), Former Press Secretary for Gore-L
people.notes: Harvard Law School JD (1991-1994). Veteran Democratic political operative who served as Press Secretary for VP Al Gore and Special Assistant Counsel to President Clinton (1994-2000). Co-authored crisis management book published by Palgrave Macmillan (2012). Joined OpenAI as Chief Global Affairs Officer in April 2024. Has 24,000+ LinkedIn followers and regularly publishes policy analysis through OpenAI's Substack newsletter 'The Prompt.' Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 list. Led OpenAI's policy submissions to Trump administration and advocacy for Project Stargate infrastructure initiative.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Labor displacement, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses primarily on geopolitical competition risks, arguing that fragmented US regulation could cede AI leadership to authoritarian China. Warns that 'whoever leads in AI will lead the world' citing Putin's statements. Emphasizes risks of 'AI-enabled human takeover scenarios' and authoritarian entrenchment through aligned AI systems. Views current regulatory approaches as potentially weakening US competitiveness against CCP-built 'autocratic, authoritarian AI' rather than addressing technical safety risks.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for federal preemption of state AI laws and a 'prevention-first' approach through voluntary collaboration with the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) rather than mandatory regulations. Argues that only federal government has access to classified systems needed for proper frontier model testing. Supports 'harmonization' where companies can choose between state liability frameworks or voluntary federal testing programs with liability protections. Explicitly opposes 'patchwork of state laws that slow innovation without improving safety' and advocates for regulatory sandboxes and lighter regulatory touch. In March 2025 memo to White House, recommended preventing states from regulating AI and easing environmental restrictions on data center construction.

people.name: Erik Brynjolfsson
people.title: Jerry Yang and Akiko Yamazaki Professor and Senior Fellow at Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI; Director of Stanford Digital Economy Lab; Ralph Landau Senior Fellow at Stanford Institute f
people.other_orgs: National Bureau of Economic Research (Research Associate), Stanford Graduate School of Business (Professor by Courtesy), Stanford Department of Economics (Professor by Courtesy), formerly MIT
people.notes: One of the world's most cited authors on economics of information technology with over 100 academic publications and five patents. Authored nine books including bestsellers 'The Second Machine Age' and 'Machine, Platform, Crowd'. Holds BA and MA from Harvard in applied mathematics and decision sciences, PhD from MIT in managerial economics. Pioneer in measuring IT productivity contributions and complementary organizational capital. Leads Stanford's Transformative AI research initiative and has shaped global policy discussions on AI economics.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Research extensively documents AI's potential for massive labor market disruption, particularly affecting younger workers in exposed occupations. Co-authored foundational work on AI safety and alignment challenges, emphasizing the economic implications of ensuring AI systems behave consistently with human values. Studies power concentration risks from AI development being dominated by few large companies, and has examined the trade-offs between economic growth and existential risks from advanced AI systems.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI policy that balances innovation with safety concerns. Co-authored research agenda for economics of Transformative AI identifying nine 'Grand Challenges' including safety risks and alignment. Supports strategic approaches that address specific market failures and externalities rather than broad restrictions. Has emphasized the need for updated measurement frameworks and governance structures while warning against premature regulatory constraints that could stifle beneficial innovation.

people.name: Tim Wu
people.title: Julius Silver Professor of Law, Science and Technology at Columbia Law School
people.other_orgs: Former Special Assistant to the President for Technology and Competition Policy (White House, 2021-2023), Former Senior Advisor at Federal Trade Commission (2011-2012), Former Senior Enforcement
people.notes: Coined the term 'network neutrality' in his 2003 paper. Author of five major books including 'The Master Switch' (2010), 'The Attention Merchants' (2016), 'The Curse of Bigness' (2018), and 'The Age of Extraction' (2025). Served as law clerk for Supreme Court Justice Stephen Breyer and Judge Richard Posner. Named to National Law Journal's 'America's 100 Most Influential Lawyers' (2013) and Politico 50 (2014, 2015). Led successful lawsuit against Time Warner Cable for false advertising of broadband speeds while at NY AG's office.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Wu's work focuses on how AI and tech platforms concentrate economic power and threaten democratic institutions. His 2025 book 'The Age of Extraction' warns that platform capitalism creates a two-tiered economy with extractive platforms on top and everyone else below. He argues that tech platforms manipulate attention, extract wealth, and deepen inequality while threatening to marginalize entire industries and foster a two-class nation. Wu has specifically warned about the risks of corporate concentration in AI development leading to regulatory capture.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Wu has been a leading advocate for breaking up Big Tech companies, particularly calling for antitrust action against Facebook in the late 2010s. He helped craft the Biden administration's antitrust policies and has argued that highly concentrated industries develop outsized lobbying power and capture regulators. In his latest book 'The Age of Extraction' (2025), he argues for stronger regulatory frameworks to prevent tech platforms from extracting wealth and deepening inequality, describing them as 'some of the most effective instruments of wealth extraction ever invented.

people.name: Yejin Choi
people.title: Dieter Schwarz Foundation HAI Professor of Computer Science, Senior Fellow at Stanford HAI, Co-Director at Stanford HAI
people.other_orgs: Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence (AI2) - Senior Director, Mosaic project leader; MacArthur Foundation Fellow; AI2050 (Schmidt Sciences) Senior Fellow; Scientific Advisor at Kyutai (French foundational AI
people.notes: MacArthur Fellow (2022) - rare recognition for NLP researchers. Named to TIME's '100 Most Influential People in AI' (2023, 2025). Ph.D. in Computer Science from Cornell University, B.S. from Seoul National University. Over 10 Best/Outstanding Paper Awards and 2 Test-of-Time Awards at top AI conferences including ACL, ICML, NeurIPS, AAAI, CVPR. Main stage TED speaker (2023) and keynote speaker at major AI conferences. Research spans commonsense reasoning, multimodal AI, sentiment analysis, and AI ethics with focus on 'democratizing generative AI through smaller yet powerful language models.'
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Misinformation, Privacy
people.threat_models_detail: Research focuses extensively on AI safety alignment, particularly through pluralistic alignment frameworks that address diverse human values rather than one-size-fits-all approaches. Co-authored work on 'Value Kaleidoscope' (2023) and 'Modular Pluralism' (2024) addressing power concentration and democratic representation in AI systems. Her SafetyAnalyst work specifically targets bias and harmful content moderation, while research on controllable safety alignment addresses loss of control scenarios. Has warned about AI systems trained on 'the worst part of humanity' from internet data requiring better alignment approaches.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored 'A Path for Science- and Evidence-Based AI Policy' (2024) advocating for evidence-based regulation with specific milestones including risk taxonomies, marginal risk research, and policy intervention frameworks. Testified before UN Security Council (2024) calling for global AI governance that expands AI frontier for all while building 'intelligence that is not only powerful, but also accessible, robust, and efficient.' Research on SafetyAnalyst framework demonstrates support for interpretable, steerable AI safety moderation systems rather than blanket restrictions.

people.name: Arvind Narayanan
people.title: Professor of Computer Science and Director of the Center for Information Technology Policy
people.other_orgs: Stanford University (former postdoc), University of Texas at Austin (PhD), Indian Institute of Technology Madras (undergraduate), Knight First Amendment Institute (collaborator)
people.notes: Professor of Computer Science at Princeton University and Director of CITP since July 2023. Previously postdoc at Stanford University (2009-2012) working with Dan Boneh. PhD from University of Texas at Austin (2009) under Vitaly Shmatikov. Co-authored 'AI Snake Oil: What Artificial Intelligence Can Do, What It Can't, and How to Tell the Difference' (2024) with Sayash Kapoor. Recipient of Presidential Early Career Award for Scientists and Engineers (PECASE), twice recipient of Privacy Enhancing Technologies Award. Pioneer in de-anonymization research, famously showed how to de-anonymize Netflix Prize dataset by linking to IMDb accounts. Led Princeton Web Transparency and Accountability Project. Co-authored textbook on fairness and machine learning.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Research extensively documents bias in automated decision systems and advocates for algorithmic accountability. Co-authored comprehensive analysis showing AI diffusion in safety-critical areas is slow, with decades-old statistical methods still preferred over modern AI in consequential applications like criminal risk prediction and medical diagnosis. Views current AI capabilities as overhyped while acknowledging legitimate concerns about bias, privacy violations, and economic inequality. Rejects existential risk scenarios as 'speculative' and argues probability estimates lack meaningful epistemic foundations.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI policy that focuses on specific harms rather than speculative risks. Co-authored 'AI as Normal Technology' arguing against drastic interventions premised on superintelligence scenarios. Supports targeted regulations like transparency requirements, audit mandates, and sector-specific safety standards. Testified before New Jersey Assembly on deepfakes policy, recommending focus on downstream harms rather than restricting AI models themselves. Argues that 'AI safety is not a model property' and defenses must be located outside models.

people.name: Heidy Khlaaf
people.title: Chief AI Scientist, AI Now Institute
people.other_orgs: UN AI Advisory Body Network of Experts, ISO SC 42 Committee Member (British Standards Institute), Former Engineering Director at Trail of Bits, Former Senior Systems Safety Engineer at OpenAI, Fo
people.notes: PhD Computer Science from University College London (2017), recipient of prestigious NSF GRFP award. Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 and MIT Technology Review's 35 Innovators Under 35. Pioneer in AI Safety Engineering field, known for developing safety evaluation frameworks for OpenAI's Codex. Led cyber evaluations for UK AI Safety Institute launch and discovered LeftoverLocals GPU vulnerability. Extensive background in safety-critical systems from UAVs to nuclear power plants, with expertise in formal verification and system hazard analysis.
people.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Environmental
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses extensively on military AI risks, warning that AI systems are 'notoriously flawed with high error rates' for precision applications and that 'AI outputs are predictions, not facts' in life-or-death military targeting. Has identified cybersecurity vulnerabilities like LeftoverLocals in GPU memory systems. Warns about AI use in nuclear power plant operations and autonomous weapons systems. Concerns about power concentration through AI-military-industrial complex and environmental impacts of data center expansion for AI development.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict regulatory oversight of AI deployment in military and safety-critical systems, comparing AI regulation to nuclear safety frameworks.
