Has testified that existing AI safety guardrails are 'deeply lacking' and 'easily compromised.' Strongly opposes military use of commercial AI models without proper safety assessments, stating that speed in military AI deployment is 'really a cover for indiscriminate targeting.' Co-authored research calling for comprehensive risk assessments before AI deployment and has criticized the 'rush to integrate AI everywhere' as undermining safety.

people.name: Randi Weingarten
people.title: President, American Federation of Teachers
people.other_orgs: Former President United Federation of Teachers AFT Local 2 (1998-2008), Former Chair NYC Municipal Labor Committee, Former member Equity and Excellence Commission, Former member NY Gov. Cuomo Edu
people.notes: Represents 1.8 million AFT members across education, healthcare, and public service. Created AFT National Academy for AI Instruction in partnership with OpenAI, Microsoft, and Anthropic. Participated in Senate Majority Leader Schumer's AI Insight Forum (September 2023). Former high school history teacher at Clara Barton High School (1991-1997), helped students win state and national constitutional debate awards. Degrees from Cornell School of Industrial and Labor Relations and Cardozo School of Law. Named to Washingtonian's 2023 Most Influential People in Washington. Author of 'Why Fascists Fear Teachers.' Monthly columnist for New York Times Sunday Review.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Privacy, Bias/discrimination
people.threat_models_detail: Warns of AI's potential to 'undermine elections and democracy itself' and cites 'warnings from scientists and experts about the risk of human extinction from AI.' Focuses heavily on protecting children from algorithmic addiction and exploitation, comparing Big Tech to Big Tobacco. Emphasizes concerns about racial and cultural bias in AI algorithms, data privacy violations, and the digital divide deepening inequality. Worries about power concentration in tech companies that 'have prioritized profits over people' and lack of transparency in AI decision-making processes.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict government regulations protecting privacy, security and well-being in AI development. Led AFT Executive Council to adopt resolution calling for developers to be held accountable for real-world harm and urging governments to implement effective regulations. Supports European-style regulatory approach, noting 'Europe is well ahead of us in terms of doing this work.' Opposes Trump administration's executive order to gut AI regulations, calling it 'outrageous and likely illegal' for prioritizing Big Tech over children and families. Works directly with select AI companies (OpenAI, Microsoft, Anthropic) on baseline privacy agreements while advocating for comprehensive federal guardrails.

people.name: Elliston Berry
people.title: Deepfake victim turned AI safety advocate
people.other_orgs: Voices for Voices podcast (guest), First Lady Melania Trump's Be Best campaign (participant)
people.notes: At age 14 in October 2023, became victim of deepfake pornography created by male classmate using photos from her Instagram account. Incident occurred at Aledo High School in Texas, affecting her and 8+ other female students. Worked directly with Senator Ted Cruz's office to develop the Take It Down Act, which passed both houses of Congress by March 2025. Testified before Congress in June 2024 and participated in First Lady Melania Trump's roundtable on March 3, 2025. Featured in major media outlets including The Times, Wall Street Journal, Fox Business, and appeared on Voices for Voices podcast. Case study referenced in Texas Public Policy Foundation's AI policy white paper.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Privacy
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI-enabled sexual exploitation of minors through deepfake technology. Advocates against Big Tech's failure to proactively remove harmful AI-generated content, citing the use of easily accessible apps like Google's DNGG for creating non-consensual intimate imagery. Emphasizes the need for corporate accountability and mandatory content removal requirements to protect vulnerable populations, particularly teenage girls.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates specifically for federal legislation criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated intimate imagery and requiring social media platforms to remove such content within 48 hours. Co-architect of the bipartisan Take It Down Act (S.146) with Senators Ted Cruz and Amy Klobuchar. Focuses on holding Big Tech accountable for AI-enabled harm to minors while supporting targeted regulatory intervention rather than broad AI restrictions.

people.name: Karen Hao
people.title: Award-winning AI journalist, author of 'Empire of AI', former senior AI editor at MIT Technology Review
people.other_orgs: The Wall Street Journal (former correspondent), The Atlantic (contributor), BBC (co-host of The Interface podcast), Pulitzer Center (co-creator of AI Spotlight Series), Harvard Technology and Pub
people.notes: MIT graduate (B.S. mechanical engineering, 2015) who transitioned from Silicon Valley engineering to journalism. First journalist to profile OpenAI (2019), conducted over 300 interviews including 90+ OpenAI employees for her book. Won American Humanist Media Award (2024) and American National Magazine Award (2022) for outstanding achievement for journalists under 30. Named to Time's TIME100 AI list (2024). Work has been cited by Congress multiple times and featured in university curricula. Her newsletter 'The Algorithm' was named one of the best on the internet by Webby Awards (2018).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Environmental, Economic inequality, Copyright/IP
people.threat_models_detail: Reporting extensively documents labor exploitation in AI supply chains, particularly content moderation workers in Kenya paid $1.50-$3.75 per hour. Emphasizes massive environmental costs including data centers requiring 1,000-2,000 megawatts of energy and consuming precious water resources. Central thesis focuses on power concentration, arguing that AI companies operate as modern empires that threaten democracy through resource seizure, labor exploitation, and lack of public accountability.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for urgent shift away from current AI development trajectory, criticizing the 'scale-at-all-costs approach' and calling for stronger oversight of AI companies. Has written extensively about the need to separate AI development from what she calls 'empire building,' emphasizing that current voluntary safety commitments are insufficient. Her book and reporting consistently argue for more democratic governance of AI development rather than leaving decisions to tech executives.

people.name: Chuck Schumer
people.title: U.S. Senator, NY; Senate Majority Leader (2021-2025), Senate Minority Leader (2017-2021, 2025-present)
people.other_orgs: Bipartisan Senate AI Working Group (Co-founder/Leader), Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee (Former Chair 2005-2009), Senate Finance Committee, Senate Rules Committee
people.notes: Founded and led the first-ever bipartisan Senate AI Working Group with Senators Heinrich (D), Young (R), and Rounds (R) in 2023. Convened unprecedented AI Insight Forums with over 70 senators attending, bringing together industry leaders, civil rights advocates, and labor representatives. Released the first comprehensive Congressional AI policy roadmap in May 2024 recommending $32 billion annual funding. Graduate of Harvard College (BA 1971, magna cum laude, Phi Beta Kappa) and Harvard Law School (JD 1974, with honors). Longest-serving U.S. Senator from New York, surpassing Daniel Patrick Moynihan and Jacob Javits. First Jewish Senate Majority Leader in U.S. history.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Weapons proliferation
people.threat_models_detail: Specifically concerned about AI's potential to 'jaundice and even totally discredit our entire system of elections' through deepfakes and misinformation in 2024 elections. Warns of job displacement particularly affecting 'those who live paycheck to paycheck' and communities of color in trucking, manufacturing, and knowledge work. Emphasizes risks from hostile foreign actors, particularly China, using AI for illicit purposes and the need for security guardrails to prevent AI from being weaponized. Also focuses on bias in hiring, privacy invasion, and the risk of democratic value erosion if AI systems aren't aligned with American principles.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'Safe Innovation Framework' emphasizing innovation as 'North Star' while establishing necessary guardrails. Led bipartisan AI Working Group that recommended $32 billion in AI funding alongside regulatory measures. Supports targeted regulations like federal AI procurement guidelines, election deepfake protections (supporting Klobuchar's bills), and the DEFIANCE Act against non-consensual AI pornography. Explicitly rejects broad restrictions, stating 'guardrails cannot stand in the way of innovation' and emphasizing bipartisan, industry-collaborative approach over restrictive measures.

people.name: Mike Braun
people.title: Governor of Indiana (2025-present); former U.S. Senator, IN (2019-2025)
people.primary_org: Office of the Governor of Indiana
people.other_orgs: Former State Representative IN-63 (2014-2017), Greater Jasper Consolidated School Board (10 years), Meyer Body Company (CEO/Owner), U.S. Special Committee on Aging (Ranking Member)
people.notes: Named 'most effective first-term Republican Senator' during his tenure. Harvard MBA graduate and former CEO of Meyer Body Company, which grew from 15 employees to thousands across 38 states. Consistently ranked as one of most conservative Senate members with endorsements from Americans for Prosperity and CPAC. As Indiana Governor, established comprehensive state AI governance framework through Office of Chief Data Officer, requiring AI Readiness Assessment for all government AI implementations.
people.threat_models: Fraud/scams, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI-enabled fraud and scams, particularly targeting elderly populations through voice-cloning and deepfakes. Introduced Medicare Transaction Fraud Prevention Act to use AI/ML for detecting suspicious diagnostic testing and durable medical equipment fraud, citing $60 billion annual Medicare fraud losses. Co-sponsored legislation requiring transparency when individuals interact with government AI systems and establishing human review appeals processes to address bias and discrimination concerns.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal federal AI regulation while embracing AI adoption, stating 'I am very concerned that we do not smother it' regarding heavy-handed regulations. Co-sponsored bipartisan legislation focused on transparency and training rather than restrictions, including the AI Leadership Training Act and Transparent Automated Governance Act (TAG Act). Emphasized that government should 'embrace AI' and warned 'if we do not embrace AI, we won't be able to counter it.' As Governor, implemented comprehensive state AI policy requiring assessment and approval processes for government AI use while enabling innovation.

people.name: Gary Peters
people.title: U.S. Senator from Michigan, Chairman of Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee
people.other_orgs: Smithsonian Institution Board of Regents, Former Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee Chair (2022-2024), Former Michigan Lottery Commissioner (2003-2008), U.S. Navy Reserve Lieutenant Command
people.notes: Chairs the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee, the Senate's primary oversight committee. Ranked as the #1 most effective Senator by the nonpartisan Center for Effective Lawmaking in both the 116th (2019-2020) and 117th (2021-2022) Congresses - the first to achieve this while in the minority party in over 50 years. Has authored more standalone bills signed into law than any Senator during recent Congresses. Previously led the Advancing American AI Act which became law in 2022. Announced he will not seek reelection in 2026. Holds degrees from Alma College (BA Political Science), University of Detroit Mercy (MBA), Wayne State University (JD, MA Political Science), and Michigan State University (MA Philosophy).
people.threat_models: Civil rights violations, Privacy breaches, Bias/discrimination, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Peters' AI legislation specifically targets risks from government AI deployment, including potential civil rights violations, privacy breaches, and biased decision-making. The PREPARED for AI Act requires safety terms for 'civil rights, civil liberties and privacy, adverse outcome reporting' in government AI contracts. His TAG Act addresses concerns about people unknowingly interacting with AI systems that could make biased decisions about them. He has cited examples like IRS automated systems that were 'more likely to recommend Black taxpayers than white taxpayers for audits' as justification for transparency requirements.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Peters focuses on responsible government AI procurement and transparency rather than broad AI restrictions. His flagship PREPARED for AI Act (S.4495) requires federal agencies to assess AI risks before purchase and deployment, establish Chief AI Officers, and include safety terms in high-risk AI contracts, while also creating pilot programs to streamline AI adoption. He has also sponsored the Transparent Automated Governance (TAG) Act requiring agencies to notify individuals when they interact with AI systems and establish human review processes for AI-generated decisions. His approach emphasizes practical guardrails and transparency in government AI use rather than restricting AI development broadly.

people.name: Maria Cantwell
people.title: U.S. Senator from Washington, Ranking Member of the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation
people.other_orgs: Former Vice President of Marketing at RealNetworks (1995-1999), Former Washington State House of Representatives (1987-1993), Former U.S. House of Representatives (1993-1995), National AI Advisor
people.notes: Lead architect of the CHIPS and Science Act which includes AI investments. Co-created the National AI Advisory Committee (NAIAC) through 2020 legislation with Sen. Young that became part of 2021 NDAA. Represents Washington state with major tech industry presence including largest U.S. data center by capacity in Pacific Northwest. Previously worked at RealNetworks where she helped develop live internet streaming technology. Has been in Senate since 2001, making her the most senior junior senator after Jeff Sessions resigned in 2017.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, National security, Economic inequality, Cybersecurity
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on preventing China from gaining AI advantages, advocating for a 'Tech NATO' framework where U.S. AI standards become global standards to counter authoritarian AI development. Concerns about deepfakes in elections, robocalls targeting consumers, and ensuring democratic values are reflected in AI development. Emphasizes need for U.S. leadership in AI to prevent bad actors with different security standards from dominating global AI infrastructure, particularly worried about China capitalizing on data centers in Middle East.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsors multiple targeted AI bills focusing on specific sectors and safety measures rather than broad restrictions. The Future of AI Innovation Act establishes voluntary standards through NIST and creates testbeds for evaluation. The NSF AI Education Act authorizes scholarships and training programs. Successfully led bipartisan 99-1 Senate vote to strike down a 10-year moratorium on state AI regulations, arguing states need ability to enforce consumer protections against robocalls, deepfakes, and autonomous vehicle safety while supporting federal framework development.

people.name: Frank Lucas
people.title: Chairman, U.S. House Committee on Science, Space and Technology, OK-3
people.other_orgs: Former Oklahoma State House of Representatives (5.5 years), First Baptist Church of Cheyenne (member), Oklahoma State University (alumnus)
people.notes: Fifth-generation Oklahoman and farmer, graduated Oklahoma State University (1982) with degree in Agricultural Economics. Elected to Congress in 1994 special election, previously served 5.5 years in Oklahoma State House. Has shepherded major AI legislation including the National AI Initiative Act of 2020 and led markup of nine AI bills in September 2024 (H.R. 9197, 9194, 9211, 9215, 9402, 9403, 5077, 9497, 9466). Represents OK-3, one of the largest agricultural regions covering almost half the state's land mass.
people.threat_models: National security, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
people.threat_models_detail: Lucas focuses primarily on geopolitical competition, warning that 'Chinese Communist Party is implementing A.I. industrial policy at a national scale, investing billions through state-financed investment funds.' He emphasizes the need to ensure AI development reflects 'our democratic values' and 'our values of trustworthiness, fairness, and transparency' rather than focusing on existential or catastrophic risks.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Chairs the House Science Committee and consistently emphasizes supporting AI innovation without imposing 'regulations and burdensome requirements.' Lucas has led passage of nine bipartisan AI bills focused on research, education, and workforce development rather than restrictions. He advocates for 'strategic investments, build our workforce, and establish proper safeguards without overregulation' and believes the U.S. must compete with China through innovation rather than regulatory constraints.

people.name: Ted Lieu
people.title: U.S. Representative, CA-36th District, Vice Chair of House Democratic Caucus, Co-Chair of House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy
people.other_orgs: Former California State Senator (2011-2014), Former California State Assemblyman (2005-2010), Former Torrance City Council Member (2002-2005), Retired U.S. Air Force Colonel (1995-2021), Co-Chair
people.notes: Named to Time Magazine's 100 Most Influential People in AI list. One of only four Members of Congress with a computer science degree (BS Computer Science, Stanford 1991; also BA Political Science). Retired U.S. Air Force Colonel with JAG Corps background. Co-chaired the bipartisan House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence in the 118th Congress. Delivered TEDx Talk titled 'Congress Must Regulate AI Now!' (November 2025). Named 2025 DC 'Tech Titan' by Washingtonian magazine. Taiwanese-American immigrant who came to U.S. at age 3, making him one of few naturalized citizen Members of Congress.
people.threat_models: Existential risk, Misinformation, National security, Cybersecurity, Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Lieu explicitly acknowledges AI could 'destroy the world, literally' and has identified three categories of AI dangers: AI that can cause significant harm, AI that can kill individuals, and AI that can destroy the world. He's particularly concerned about AI-enabled fraud (leading his AI Fraud Deterrence Act), autonomous weapons systems, AI hallucinations in critical applications like healthcare and nuclear weapons, and deepfakes threatening democratic processes. He warns about AI-enabled human takeover scenarios and the unpredictability of large language models, stating 'we don't actually know how these models work.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Lieu has consistently advocated for proactive AI regulation, stating 'Congress must regulate AI now!' and calling it 'insane' not to do so. He co-sponsored the AI Fraud Deterrence Act (2025) which enhances penalties for AI-assisted fraud and criminalizes deepfake impersonation of federal officials. He also introduced the Block Nuclear Launch by Autonomous Artificial Intelligence Act (2023) requiring human control over nuclear weapons, and proposed creating a bipartisan 20-member federal AI commission to develop regulatory recommendations over two years. He emphasizes the need for 'sensible guardrails' rather than a 'complete Wild West' approach to AI.

people.name: Ed Markey
people.title: U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (Junior Senator)
people.other_orgs: Commerce, Science and Transportation Committee, Small Business Committee (Ranking Member), Congressional Progressive Caucus, Nonproliferation Caucus (founder), former House Natural Resources Comm
people.notes: Longest-tenured House member ever elected to Senate with 36+ years House service before 2013 Senate election. Known as 'an icon to Gen Z activists' according to The Hill. Principal House author of 2007 fuel economy law increasing standards to 54.5 mpg, co-author of landmark Waxman-Markey climate bill (2009), and Senate author of the Green New Deal. Founded the Nonproliferation Caucus and led the Nuclear Freeze movement. Dean of Massachusetts congressional delegation since 2009 despite being junior senator.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Privacy, Democratic erosion, Misinformation
people.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on algorithmic bias and discrimination, citing studies showing AI mortgage algorithms 80% more likely to reject Black applicants, AI medical models twice as likely to miss liver disease in women, and AI resume screening tools almost never selecting Black male names. Also concerned about surveillance risks, introducing the ICE Out of Our Faces Act to prohibit DHS facial recognition use. Warns AI could become 'accelerating injustice' and emphasizes protecting marginalized communities from 'Big Tech's reckless actions.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored the AI Civil Rights Act requiring companies to conduct independent audits to identify algorithmic discrimination, prohibiting discriminatory AI use, and giving individuals right to appeal AI decisions to humans. Also introduced the Eliminating BIAS Act mandating federal agencies establish civil rights offices to combat algorithmic bias. Strongly opposed Trump administration's proposed 10-year moratorium on state AI regulation, leading bipartisan Senate efforts that defeated it 99-1. Advocates for 'moral leadership' in AI, stating 'we cannot abandon our principles in reckless pursuit of technological superiority.

people.name: Don Beyer
people.title: U.S. Representative, VA-8; Co-Chair, Congressional AI Caucus
people.other_orgs: Congressional AI Caucus (Co-Chair), House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence, House Committee on Science Space and Technology (former), George Mason University (graduate student pursuing MS in
people.notes: Currently pursuing MS in machine learning at George Mason University while serving in Congress, taking evening courses to inform his AI policy work. Co-chairs the bipartisan Congressional AI Caucus and serves on the House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence convened by Speaker Johnson and Leader Jeffries. Served 8 years on House Science, Space, and Technology Committee. Previously served as U.S. Ambassador to Switzerland and Liechtenstein (2009-2013) and Virginia Lieutenant Governor (1990-1998). Author of multiple AI bills including the GUARDRAILS Act to repeal Trump's AI executive order.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Environmental, Existential risk
people.threat_models_detail: Primary concern is algorithmic bias in high-impact decisions like housing, loans, and medical care, which the AI Foundation Model Transparency Act specifically addresses. Warns against 'lawless Wild West environment' that concentrates power with AI companies. Has acknowledged existential risks, stating 'I sometimes feel that my small role in the 24-person committee is making sure we don't forget about the existential risks' and comparing potential AI threats to 'Skynet' scenarios. Also focuses on environmental impacts through the Artificial Intelligence Environmental Impacts Act.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted transparency requirements and federal standards rather than broad restrictions. Primary legislative focus is the AI Foundation Model Transparency Act requiring disclosure of training data and capabilities for high-impact foundation models. Co-sponsored the Federal AI Risk Management Act mandating federal agencies follow NIST AI risk frameworks. Strongly opposes Trump administration efforts to preempt state AI regulations, arguing federal action should complement rather than replace state protections. Takes a 'light touch' approach that avoids depressing innovation while establishing specific guardrails for high-risk applications.

people.name: Anna Eshoo
people.title: fmr U.S. Representative, CA-16
people.other_orgs: Co-Chair of Congressional Artificial Intelligence Caucus, fmr Chair of House Energy and Commerce Health Subcommittee, fmr San Mateo County Board of Supervisors
people.notes: Named to Time Magazine's Most Influential People in AI List (September 2023). Served as first woman Chair of House Energy and Commerce Health Subcommittee (2019-2023). Co-Chair of Congressional AI Caucus with Don Beyer. Has authored over 60 bills signed into law during her career including landmark legislation like the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (2008) and electronic signature law. Represents Silicon Valley district including Palo Alto, Mountain View, and parts of San Jose.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Misinformation, Copyright/IP, Environmental
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses on AI transparency to address bias in hiring, lending, and healthcare decisions that disproportionately harm communities. Her AI Foundation Model Transparency Act specifically targets 'racial or gendered bias, which can have serious real-world impacts in areas including health-related AI inferences, loan granting, housing approval, or predictive policing.' Also addresses deepfakes and deceptive AI content through labeling requirements, environmental impacts of AI training, and copyright concerns for training data.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Sponsors targeted AI transparency and disclosure legislation rather than broad restrictions. Lead sponsor of the AI Foundation Model Transparency Act (H.R.6881) requiring high-impact foundation models to disclose training data and methods to FTC. Also sponsors the Protecting Consumers from Deceptive AI Act (H.R.7766) requiring AI-generated content labeling, and the Artificial Intelligence Environmental Impacts Act (H.R.7197). Co-sponsors the CREATE AI Act establishing National AI Research Resource to democratize access to AI tools.

people.name: Gavin Newsom
people.title: Governor of California
people.other_orgs: California Innovation Council, PlumpJack Group (founder), Getty family business interests
people.notes: Launched California Innovation Council (2025) with 30 experts including former Senator Laphonza Butler and Stanford's Fei-Fei Li. Deployed 'Poppy.AI' digital assistant across 20+ state departments. Convened first-in-nation AI policy working group that produced 53-page report on frontier AI risks (June 2025). Has relationships with tech leaders dating to early 2000s as SF mayor; praised OpenAI's Sam Altman as visionary while calling Elon Musk 'one of the great disappointments.' Author of memoir 'Young Man in a Hurry' (2026) and hosts podcast 'This Is Gavin Newsom' (2025).
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Commissioned expert report warning of 'potentially irreversible harms' from AI facilitating nuclear and biological threats. SB 53 addresses catastrophic risks defined as causing $1+ billion in damage or 50+ casualties, including bioweapons creation, power grid hacking, and mass casualty events. Has signed 17+ AI bills addressing deepfakes, AI watermarking, child safety protections, and combating AI-generated misinformation. Ordered state agencies to assess AI threats to critical infrastructure including energy, water, and communications sectors.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Signed SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act) in September 2025, establishing first-in-the-nation frontier AI safety regulations requiring transparency frameworks, safety incident reporting, and whistleblower protections for large AI developers. Previously vetoed broader SB 1047 in 2024, stating it applied 'stringent standards to even the most basic functions' and didn't account for deployment context. Emphasizes 'guardrails and steering the debate' while maintaining California's AI leadership, focusing regulation specifically on frontier models meeting computational thresholds rather than blanket restrictions.

people.name: Jay Obernolte
people.title: U.S. Representative, CA-23, Chairman of House Science Subcommittee on Research and Technology
people.other_orgs: Co-chair of Bipartisan House AI Task Force (2023-2024), Vice-chair Congressional AI Caucus, House Committee on Energy and Commerce, House Budget Committee, Former California State Assembly (2014-
people.notes: Only current member of Congress with graduate degree in Artificial Intelligence (MS from UCLA, 1997). Co-chaired Bipartisan House AI Task Force that produced December 2024 report with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. Hosts monthly bipartisan dinner club at his Capitol Hill home to build cross-party relationships. Founded video game development studio FarSight Studios in 1988. Holds BS in Engineering and Applied Science from Caltech, MS in AI from UCLA, and Doctorate in Public Administration from California Baptist University. Licensed airline transport pilot and fifth-degree black belt in martial arts.
people.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, National security, Misinformation, Power concentration, Loss of control
people.threat_models_detail: Specifically concerned about AI-enabled cyberattacks and use of AI to develop biological weapons, citing these as 'highest-level existential risk that you want to guard against.' Warned about AI systems being used for cyber theft, cyber fraud, or hacking that endangers national security. Also highlighted risks from malicious actors using AI to spread misinformation and disinformation to undermine society and democracy, calling these threats 'probably just as consequential as an army of evil robots.' Emphasized need for constant vigilance against AI-enabled cyberattacks and risks of sensitive personal data falling into malicious hands.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for federal AI framework that maintains U.S. leadership while addressing specific risks like AI-enabled cyberattacks, bioweapons development, and national security threats. Sponsored CREATE AI Act to codify National AI Research Resource (NAIRR) and supports Great American AI Act to formalize Center for AI Standards and Innovation. Led effort for 10-year federal moratorium on state AI laws to create uniform national standards, stating 'Congress must ultimately develop the digital roads through which individuals in states communicate in generation AI.' Emphasizes sector-specific approach over broad restrictions, noting regulatory clarity is crucial while avoiding putting 'thumb on the scale' between open-source vs closed-source models.

people.name: Lynne Parker
people.title: Principal Deputy Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy; Executive Director of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST)
people.primary_org: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP)
people.other_orgs: University of Tennessee (Associate Vice Chancellor Emerita, AI Tennessee Initiative Director), Oak Ridge National Laboratory (former Distinguished R&D Staff Member), National Science Foundation (
people.notes: Founding Director of the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Office (2021-2022). Helped develop President Trump's Executive Order establishing the American Artificial Intelligence Initiative. PhD in Computer Science from MIT (1994) supervised by Rodney Brooks. Received Presidential Early Career Award for Scientists and Engineers (PECASE). IEEE Fellow (2010) for contributions to distributed multi-robot systems, AAAS Fellow, AAAI Fellow (2022). Over 140 peer-reviewed papers on robotics and AI. Previously served as Deputy U.S. Chief Technology Officer and Assistant Director for AI at OSTP during first Trump administration.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Privacy
people.threat_models_detail: Has publicly warned about AI being used by authoritarian governments for surveillance and to repress dissent. Noted concerns about 'decisions that have unbalanced effects on different groups of people' and bias in AI systems. Emphasized importance of protecting Americans' privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties in federal AI deployment. Has highlighted cybersecurity risks and the potential for AI to concentrate power.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for removing 'unnecessary bureaucratic restrictions' on AI adoption in government while maintaining protections for privacy and civil rights. In April 2025, stated that revised federal AI policies would 'remove unnecessary bureaucratic restrictions, allow agencies to be more efficient and cost-effective.' Previously favored market-driven approaches, stating 'We don't want fear mongering' and suggesting standards adoption by 'big players' would drive industry compliance rather than mandatory regulation.

people.name: Arati Prabhakar
people.title: OSTP Director (Biden Admin, 2022-2025); fmr DARPA Director (2012-2017); fmr NIST Director (1993-1997)
people.primary_org: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy
people.other_orgs: Former U.S. Venture Partners (2001-2011), Former DARPA (1986-1993, 2012-2017), Former NIST (1993-1997), Former Raychem CTO, Former Interval Research President, Actuate founder (2019), National Ac
people.notes: First woman and person of color confirmed as OSTP Director and Science Advisor to the President. First person to demonstrate ChatGPT to President Biden in Oval Office (had it write Bruce Springsteen-style lyrics). PhD Applied Physics from Caltech (1984) - first woman to earn this degree there. Led DARPA's Microelectronics Technology Office founding. Named IEEE Fellow (1997) and member of National Academy of Engineering. Listed among TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI (2024). Immigrant from New Delhi, came to US at age 3.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Privacy, Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, National security
people.threat_models_detail: Identifies deepfakes and image-based sexual abuse as the AI risk that has 'fully manifested in horrific ways.' Warns that AI could 'automate and magnify discrimination at scale' which would be 'catastrophic to the very idea of America.' Concerned about AI eroding information environment, privacy, and mental health, particularly for children. Has stated that allowing AI to be 'shaped by authoritarian regimes' poses fundamental threats to American values and democracy. Notes risks from AI-enabled cyberattacks on critical infrastructure and potential bio-weapon applications, though considers biological risks 'marginal' compared to other methods.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive risk management approach that enables innovation while addressing specific harms. Led Biden's landmark AI Executive Order (October 2023) establishing safety standards, bias protections, and testing requirements. Supports targeted regulations like requiring explanations for AI loan decisions and preventing discriminatory healthcare AI, while opposing blanket restrictions. Testified that California's SB-1047 was 'simply impractical' because safety assessment methods don't exist yet. Emphasizes voluntary industry commitments (secured from 15 AI companies) combined with federal standards through NIST.

people.name: Suresh Venkatasubramanian
people.title: Director, Center for Technological Responsibility, Reimagination, and Redesign (CNTR), Brown University
people.other_orgs: Former Assistant Director for Science and Justice, White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (2021-2022); Board Member, Data & Society; Former Board Member, ACLU Utah (2017-2021); Forme
people.notes: Professor of Computer Science and Data Science at Brown University with PhD from Stanford (1999). Co-authored the nation's first Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights during 15-month White House appointment (2021-2022). Leading researcher in algorithmic fairness for over a decade. Received NSF CAREER award for work in geometry of probability, test-of-time award at ICDE 2017 for privacy work, and KAIS Journal award for auditing black-box models. Named to Fast Company's AI20 list (2023) of influential thinkers in generative AI. Founded member of FAccT conference. AIGP certified.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Privacy
people.threat_models_detail: Focuses primarily on algorithmic fairness and discrimination in predictive AI systems already deployed in healthcare, justice system, and employment. Has studied and documented discriminatory effects in criminal justice algorithms that 'suck up data from previous arrests irrevocably tainted by the history of racial injustice.' Warns about power concentration where 'a lot of technology is being designed specifically for the purposes of making money and helping state governments exert control over the population.' Argues ChatGPT has been a 'dangerous distraction' from addressing existing harms in predictive models.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights, which focuses on protecting people's rights through five core principles including algorithmic discrimination protections and data privacy, but emphasizes impact over technology-specific definitions. Advocates for 'sensors, not solutions' approach - developing diagnostic toolkits that can adapt to different contexts rather than rigid parameters. Testified before EEOC recommending detailed validation testing, disparity assessments, and ongoing monitoring requirements for automated systems in employment. Supports guardrails that 'feed further innovation rather than hamper it' and argues that responsible AI regulation creates better engineering practices.

people.name: Kathy Hochul
people.title: Governor of New York State
people.other_orgs: Former Erie County Clerk, Former U.S. Representative (NY-26), Co-chair of State's Heroin and Opioid Task Force, Co-chair of Women's Suffrage Commission, Co-chair of Child Care Availability Task F
people.notes: First female Governor of New York State (57th Governor). Signed the RAISE Act into law December 19, 2025, making New York the second state after California to enact major frontier AI safety legislation. Created first-in-nation AI companion safety requirements effective November 5, 2025. Announced $10 billion partnership for next-generation chips research at NY CREATES' Albany NanoTech Complex. Appointed IBM CEO Arvind Krishna and Girls Who Code CEO Dr. Tarika Barrett as co-chairs of Emerging Technology Advisory Board in 2024.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security
people.threat_models_detail: The RAISE Act specifically addresses catastrophic risks including death or serious injury to 50+ people or $1+ billion in property damage, expert-level assistance in creating chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear weapons, and unsupervised criminal conduct. Has focused extensively on protecting children from AI-generated sexual exploitation, noting 16 AI undressing sites had 200 million views in first half of 2024. Established AI companion requirements to detect suicidal ideation and self-harm expressions. Launched FutureWorks Commission specifically to address labor displacement concerns, stating 'almost no line of work is immune' to AI disruption.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Signed the RAISE Act on December 19, 2025, which specifically targets large frontier AI developers with revenue over $500 million, requiring safety protocols, 72-hour incident reporting, and transparency measures. Created AI companion safeguards requiring crisis intervention protocols and user notifications. Established Empire AI Consortium with $500 million in public-private funding and launched FutureWorks Commission to study AI's economic impact. Has explicitly stated New York is 'setting the national standard for strong, sensible AI regulation' while emphasizing protection of children and workers without unnecessarily constraining innovation.

organizations.name: Brookings Institution
organizations.notes: Founded 1916, one of world's most cited think tanks. Staff of ~630. $89.4M annual revenue (2023), $538M endowment. Center for Technology Innovation led by Nicol Turner Lee focuses on AI policy. Key recent reports include 'Building Pro-Worker AI' and education AI risk assessment. Regular congressional testimony on AI governance, financial services AI regulation, and digital equity issues.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Democracy, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on AI's impact on workers and economic inequality, advocating for 'pro-worker AI' development and workforce transition policies. Research emphasizes risks to democratic institutions from AI-enabled misinformation and power concentration in tech companies. Studies bias in AI systems used in criminal justice, finance, and education sectors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation with sector-specific approaches rather than blanket restrictions. Supports responsible AI frameworks emphasizing transparency, accountability, and consumer protection while maintaining innovation competitiveness. Calls for robust federal oversight through existing agencies like FTC and CFPB, plus new governance structures for high-risk AI applications in healthcare, finance, and education.

organizations.name: Gray Swan
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Carnegie Mellon faculty Matt Fredrikson, Andy Zou, and Zico Kolter. 33+ employees. For-profit company developing AI security products including real-time filtering (Cygnal) and automated testing (Shade). Pioneered automated jailbreaking (GCG method), created industry-standard benchmarks (MMLU, WMDP, HarmBench), and operates large-scale red-teaming competitions. Research covered by NYT, TIME, CNN.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on adversarial attacks (jailbreaking, prompt injection), deceptive alignment, and evaluating dangerous capabilities. Developed benchmarks for assessing hazardous knowledge (WMDP), harmful outputs (HarmBench), and cybersecurity risks (CyBench). Technical solutions include Circuit Breakers for preventing harmful outputs and Representation Engineering for interpretability.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI deployment through technical safety measures and security standards. Focuses on developing tools for automated risk assessment and red-teaming rather than prescriptive regulations. Supports disclosure requirements for AI-generated content in professional settings.

organizations.name: Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 as interdisciplinary institute with 156 employees. Co-directed by Fei-Fei Li, John Etchemendy, and James Landay with 30+ senior fellows across disciplines. Publishes annual AI Index report tracking global AI trends. Operates policy fellowships, Congressional Boot Camp, and maintains $2.8M in grants from Patrick J. McGovern Foundation. Faculty regularly testify before Congress and advise UN Security Council on AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Healthcare, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research spans AI governance frameworks, algorithmic bias in healthcare diagnostics, labor market impacts of AI displacement, democratic institutions and AI policy, privacy and data governance, and societal implications of AI systems across criminal justice and education domains.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: HAI faculty advocate for evidence-based AI policy that balances innovation with safety. Leadership supports interdisciplinary regulation including appropriate acquisition strategies, expanded government data access for researchers, mechanisms to evaluate AI models in intended contexts, and human-centered approaches to AI governance. Faculty regularly testify before Congress and advise international bodies.

organizations.name: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
organizations.notes: Founded 2014, rebranded from Open Philanthropy to Coefficient Giving in November 2025. Has directed over $4 billion in grants since inception, with $650+ million in 2024 alone. Major funder partnership with Good Ventures (Dustin Moskovitz/Cari Tuna). Operates 13 specialized funds including 'Navigating Transformative AI' with 440+ grants. Began funding AI safety in 2015, seven years before ChatGPT launch. Alexander Berger serves as CEO.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on preventing catastrophic AI risks including loss of control scenarios where humanity loses oversight of advanced AI systems, extreme misuse for bioweapons or authoritarian control, and risks from rapid capability advances outpacing safety measures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports targeted AI safety regulations proportionate to risks, focusing on preventing catastrophic outcomes rather than restricting smaller-scale applications. Emphasizes bipartisan approaches and evidence-based policy frameworks for frontier AI systems.

organizations.name: Redwood Research
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 as nonprofit (EIN 87-1702255) by Buck Shlegeris (CEO), Bill Zito (COO), Nate Thomas. Currently 24 employees including Chief Scientist Ryan Greenblatt. $10M+ annual funding primarily from National Philanthropic Trust ($5.3M), Silicon Valley Community Foundation. Key research on AI control, alignment faking in LLMs, interpretability via causal scrubbing. Partners with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind on safety evaluations.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on AI takeover risk from scheming/deceptive alignment where models conceal misalignment and attempt to subvert safety measures. Research includes AI control protocols, alignment faking detection, and techniques for safely deferring to AIs on critical decisions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI control techniques where safety measures prevent unacceptable outcomes even if AIs intentionally subvert them. Argues labs should ensure powerful AIs are controlled and proposes transparency requirements including detailed risk reports and third-party audits.

organizations.name: Center on Long-Term Risk
organizations.notes: Founded 2013, research organization with 8 employees. Runs CLR Fund for s-risk reduction grants ($382k current balance, $1.89M total grants). Key research includes Model Persona Research Agenda studying malicious traits in LLMs, cooperation/conflict in transformative AI, and macrostrategy for robust s-risk interventions. Offers Summer Research Fellowship program and collaborates with frontier AI labs and safety institutes.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specializes in 's-risks' (suffering risks) from advanced AI, particularly scenarios involving deliberate creation of suffering through malicious traits like spitefulness, sadism, or punitiveness. Research focuses on AI conflict scenarios, cooperation failures, and preventing emergence of harmful personas in large language models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with emphasis on preventing worst-case scenarios involving deliberate creation of suffering (s-risks). Focuses on technical interventions through AI safety community and frontier labs rather than direct regulatory advocacy.

organizations.name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
organizations.location: Washington, DC (Georgetown University Walsh School of Foreign Service)
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 as nonprofit research center at Georgetown University. Staff of 62 employees with over $100 million in funding through 2025, primarily from Open Philanthropy. Produces data-driven policy research on AI, advanced computing, and biotechnology. Staff regularly testify before Congress and provide policy recommendations to federal agencies. Key reports include AI governance frameworks, China AI analysis, and automated AI R&D risks.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Loss of control, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on US-China AI competition, export controls, and technology transfer risks. Research covers automated AI R&D risks, frontier AI governance gaps, cybersecurity implications of AI systems, and potential for rapid capability acceleration leading to loss of human control.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance with flexible, adaptable regulatory frameworks. Supports incident tracking, transparency requirements for frontier AI companies, and federal coordination while preserving innovation. Emphasizes need for regulatory agility that can evolve with technology rather than rigid one-size-fits-all approaches.

organizations.name: Partnership on AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 as nonprofit partnership of academic, civil society, industry and media organizations. Staff of 55+ across 9 countries. Mixed funding from philanthropy (Knight Foundation $600K grant) and corporate charitable contributions. Key outputs include AI governance priorities for 2026, AI assurance ecosystem reports, and enterprise AI documentation standards. Policy Steering Committee includes representatives from IBM, Microsoft, OECD, Brookings, and major tech companies.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Privacy, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI governance priorities including agent governance with privacy safeguards, documentation/transparency across AI value chain, workforce resilience and 'assurance literacy', preserving human voice and epistemic integrity, and international coordination to prevent fragmentation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance framework including transparency requirements, documentation standards, international coordination, and multistakeholder approaches. Supports risk-based regulation with emphasis on AI assurance ecosystems, agent governance, and workforce resilience policies.

organizations.name: Anthropic
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 by former OpenAI researchers including Dario and Daniela Amodei. Raised $67B+ total funding, valued at $380B (Feb 2026). 1,620+ employees. $14B annual revenue run-rate. Key products: Claude AI assistant, Claude Code ($2.5B run-rate), Claude for Work. Pioneer of constitutional AI and responsible scaling frameworks. Won $200M DOD contract in 2025, later disputed over usage restrictions leading to federal designation as 'supply chain risk' - preliminary injunction granted March 2026.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on alignment research, constitutional AI, and interpretability. Published Responsible Scaling Policy v3.0 with AI Safety Levels (ASL) framework requiring specific safeguards at capability thresholds. ASL-3 activated May 2025 for Claude Opus 4. Emphasizes preventing misuse for bioweapons, cyberattacks, and autonomous weapons. Conducts red-teaming and publishes Risk Reports every 3-6 months. Frontier Safety Roadmap covers Security, Alignment, Safeguards, and Policy goals.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'responsible scaling' policies with voluntary safety commitments, pre-deployment testing at capability thresholds, and third-party audits. RSP v3.0 separates company-level from industry-wide recommendations, acknowledging collective action problems. Supports targeted regulatory frameworks that scale with AI capabilities but opposes overly restrictive licensing. Has influenced legislation in California (SB-53), New York (RAISE Act), and EU AI Act.

organizations.name: Founders Fund
organizations.notes: Founded 2005 by Peter Thiel and PayPal Mafia alums. Currently raising $6B fourth growth fund (2026) after $4.6B third fund (2025). Major AI investments include Anthropic ($13B Series F), OpenAI, Anduril ($2.5B Series G), Palantir (public), Scale AI, Neuralink. Partners include Peter Thiel, Brian Singerman, Trae Stephens, Scott Nolan. Known for contrarian bets on frontier technology and defense applications.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Investment focus on defense AI and dual-use technologies suggests primary concern with national security competitiveness rather than traditional AI safety risks. Portfolio positioning emphasizes maintaining U.S. technological leadership against nation-state competitors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Portfolio heavily weighted toward AI capabilities and defense tech companies (Anthropic, OpenAI, Anduril, Palantir). Investment thesis emphasizes 'competing with nation-states' and frontier technology development. Partners like Trae Stephens have criticized restrictive government approaches to China tech policy, favoring market-driven solutions.

organizations.name: Writers Guild of America
organizations.location: Los Angeles, CA (WGAW) and New York, NY (WGAE)
organizations.notes: Labor union representing thousands of film, TV, radio and digital media writers. Founded in 1954 (successor to 1933 Screen Writers Guild). Led historic 148-day strike in 2023 that was 'largely about AI fears.' First union to win fundamental AI workplace protections. Currently in 2026 negotiations seeking payment for AI training use of member work. Key leaders: Michele Mulroney (WGAW President), John August (Negotiating Committee Co-chair).
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Copyright/IP
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on preventing AI from replacing writers, ensuring compensation when writers' work is used to train AI models, and maintaining creative control and credits. Won fundamental AI protections in 2023 strike and seeking expanded protections in 2026 negotiations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict AI protections including: writers cannot be replaced by AI-generated content, AI cannot be used as source material for guild-covered work, writers must be compensated when their work is used for AI training, and AI-generated text cannot determine writing credits.

organizations.name: Tarbell Center for AI Journalism
organizations.location: Claymont, DE (with global team in US and UK)
organizations.notes: Founded in 2022, supports AI journalism through fellowships ($60K-$110K), grants ($1K-$20K), and residencies ($80K+ stipend). Has placed fellows at Bloomberg, TIME, MIT Tech Review, The Guardian, NBC News, The Verge, and LA Times. Funded primarily by Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) and other EA-aligned organizations. Publishes Transformer newsletter. Recently faced criticism from OpenAI over funding ties to AI safety organizations like Future of Life Institute.
organizations.threat_models: Mission statement specifically mentions deepfake harassment, algorithmic bias, AI-enabled scams, bioterrorism, cyberattacks, authoritarian surveillance, mass unemployment, and human extinction/disempowerment as key concerns driving their journalism support.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports journalism that emphasizes AI accountability, corporate transparency, and risk awareness. Mission statement explicitly focuses on holding AI companies accountable and fostering informed public discourse about AI risks ranging from bias to existential threats.

organizations.name: Anthropic Institute
organizations.notes: Founded March 2026 as internal think tank within Anthropic. Led by co-founder Jack Clark as Head of Public Benefit. ~30 interdisciplinary researchers including ML engineers, economists, and social scientists, planning to double headcount annually. Consolidates three existing teams: Frontier Red Team, Societal Impacts, and Economic Research. Key hires include Matt Botvinick (Yale Law/ex-DeepMind), Anton Korinek (UVA Economics), and Zoë Hitzig (ex-OpenAI). Publishes public research on AI's societal impacts and maintains unique access to frontier AI development data. Opening DC policy office spring 2026.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on catastrophic risks from advanced AI systems, including alignment failures, deceptive AI behavior, and recursive self-improvement scenarios. Studies economic disruption from AI automation, cybersecurity vulnerabilities from AI-discovered exploits, and governance challenges around AI values and legal system interactions. Emphasizes technical alignment problems where AI systems pursue goals misaligned with human values.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance with regulatory frameworks that scale with AI capabilities. Supports mandatory safety evaluations, transparency requirements, and international coordination on AI governance. Emphasizes the need for government engagement on AI safety while maintaining that safety research must inform policy decisions.

organizations.name: Existential Risk Observatory
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 by Otto Barten, ~9 current staff plus graduates. Funded by LTFF, SFF, individual donors. Published 4 pieces in TIME Magazine on AI existential risk. Organizes high-profile debates with figures like Yoshua Bengio and Stuart Russell. Merged with Campaign for AI Safety in 2024. Key research on international AI agreements, public opinion polling on AI risk, and conditional pause mechanisms.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on unaligned AGI as existential threat, citing 1-in-6 extinction probability within 100 years. Also addresses AI-enabled pandemics, cyber attacks, and CBRN weapons. Research includes offense-defense balance, AI pause mechanisms, and international treaty frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a Conditional AI Safety Treaty that would halt unsafe AI training if systems approach loss of control and alignment remains unsolved. Proposes compute thresholds requiring rigorous oversight, mandatory model evaluations, and international coordination through AI Safety Institutes with authority to pause development.

organizations.name: Goodfire
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Eric Ho, Daniel Balsam, and Tom McGrath (ex-DeepMind interpretability team founder). Raised $209M total funding including $150M Series B at $1.25B valuation (Feb 2026). 50+ employees including researchers from OpenAI and DeepMind. Core product 'Ember' platform provides interpretability tools. Partners with Arc Institute, Mayo Clinic, Microsoft. Published research on sparse autoencoders, hallucination reduction, and Alzheimer's biomarker discovery.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on mechanistic interpretability research to understand AI model internals and prevent uncontrolled behaviors. Specific work on reducing hallucinations through interpretability-informed training and developing 'intentional design' methods to guide model training away from unintended learning.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI interpretability as essential for safety and control of advanced AI systems. Emphasizes the need for understanding and intentionally designing AI models rather than relying on black-box approaches. Supports collaborative safety research and responsible development practices.

organizations.name: OpenAI
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 as nonprofit, restructured 2019 with capped-profit subsidiary, recapitalized 2025 as public benefit corporation under OpenAI Foundation control. 5,373 employees (2026), $8.5B annual revenue. Launched ChatGPT (Nov 2022), GPT-4 (Mar 2023), recent models include GPT-4.5, o3. Raised record $120B+ funding round (2026) with Amazon ($50B), Nvidia ($30B), SoftBank ($30B). Valued at $840B post-money. CEO Sam Altman, key leadership changes in 2024-2025.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Preparedness Framework addresses catastrophic risks through capability thresholds and safety evaluations. Focuses on biological/chemical weapons prevention, cybersecurity, and model alignment. Conducts red-teaming and external assessments before deployment. Safety Advisory Group provides oversight for high-risk models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Stance has shifted significantly from advocating for licensing and testing requirements (2023 Senate testimony) to opposing pre-deployment vetting as 'disastrous' and questioning need for NIST standards (2025 Senate hearing). Now advocates for 'sensible regulation that does not slow us down' and regulatory sandboxes to remove barriers.

organizations.name: AI Safety Foundation
organizations.notes: Founded as project of Geoffrey Hinton (Nobel Laureate, 'Godfather of AI'). Registered Canadian charity (#78867 2822 RR0001). Leadership includes CEO Ankesh Chandaria and directors Geoffrey Hinton, David Duvenaud, Roger Grosse. Flagship program is The Hinton Lectures™ annual series featuring leading AI safety researchers like Owain Evans. Also established CJF Hinton Award for Excellence in AI Safety Reporting. Focuses on education, research support, and public awareness campaigns about AI catastrophic risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on catastrophic risks from advanced AI systems, including risks of AI systems becoming uncontrollable or exhibiting deceptive behavior. Research areas include emergent misalignment, deception detection, and maintaining human oversight as AI capabilities scale.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI safety measures and democratic oversight of AI development. CEO Ankesh Chandaria has written about the need for transparent processes, parliamentary review of high-impact AI deployments, and clear societal boundaries while avoiding innovation-stifling overreach.

organizations.name: Cyborgism
organizations.notes: Research collective founded around 2023, led by researchers NicholasKees and janus. Proposes 'cyborgism' as alternative to autonomous AI agents - human-in-the-loop systems that augment rather than outsource human cognition. Has Discord community organizing around this research direction. Connected to broader AI alignment community and SERI MATS program. Advocates for using GPT-style simulators with human operators rather than autonomous agents for alignment research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on preventing human disempowerment by autonomous AI agents. Research agenda centers on developing human-AI integration techniques that preserve human agency while leveraging AI capabilities for alignment research acceleration.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for human-in-the-loop AI systems that enhance rather than replace human agency. Proposes cyborgism as an alternative to autonomous AI agents, emphasizing human-AI collaboration where humans remain the source of agency and decision-making while AI provides cognitive augmentation.

organizations.name: Effective Thesis
organizations.notes: Founded by David Janku, now directed by Joël Christoph (PhD candidate, European University Institute). Network of 100+ expert advisors from institutions like MIT, Oxford FHI, Cambridge CSER, Global Priorities Institute. Received funding from EA Infrastructure Fund and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Connects students with impact-driven research opportunities, runs 8-week accelerators and fellowships. Focus areas include AI safety/governance, biosecurity, global health, existential risk, and animal welfare.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research profiles extensively cover AI governance, safety, and alignment challenges. Expert network includes specialists in existential risk, global catastrophic risks, and AI policy. Promotes research on preventing AI-related catastrophes and developing governance frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on AI governance research through student thesis work and expert network. Director Joël Christoph and experts like Aditya Gilra work on AI safety and policy research. Expert network includes researchers advocating for responsible AI development, governance frameworks, and safety measures.

organizations.name: How to pursue a career in technical AI alignment
organizations.notes: Comprehensive 47-minute career guide published June 2022 by Charlie Rogers-Smith on the EA Forum. Provides detailed pathways for empirical and theoretical AI alignment research roles, learning resources, and funding opportunities. Extensively references work by Rohin Shah, Adam Gleave, Neel Nanda, and other alignment researchers. Has been used as basis for 80,000 Hours' technical AI safety career review and republished by BlueDot Impact.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: The guide focuses specifically on technical AI alignment to prevent AI systems from being misaligned with human values and intentions. References existential risks from advanced AI systems and the alignment problem as core concerns motivating career choices.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: This is a career guide rather than an organization with policy positions. The guide acknowledges different perspectives exist on AI alignment approaches and encourages readers to form their own views on AI safety priorities and strategies.

organizations.name: Aether
organizations.notes: Founded 2024/2025 as independent research group. 3 full-time researchers: Rohan Subramani (starting PhD at University of Toronto), Rauno Arike, and Shubhorup Biswas. $200k funding through December 2025 from anonymous donor. Advised by experts from Astera Institute, Apollo Research, Google DeepMind. Research agenda focuses on enhancing monitorability of LLM agents, with published work on chain-of-thought monitoring and hidden reasoning taxonomy. Currently hiring 1-2 additional researchers.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research centers on chain-of-thought monitorability for LLM agents, addressing risks from autonomous AI systems that may engage in hidden reasoning or deceptive alignment. Focus on maintaining human oversight capabilities as models become more powerful and agentic.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical research to influence frontier AI companies and governments through chain-of-thought monitorability and safe deployment of LLM agents. Advocates for preserving externalized reasoning traces to maintain interpretability as AI systems become more capable.

organizations.name: Google DeepMind
organizations.notes: Founded 2010 by Demis Hassabis and Mustafa Suleyman, acquired by Google 2014, merged with Google Brain 2023. Currently ~4,800-6,000 employees with $1.1B annual revenue. Major breakthroughs include AlphaGo, AlphaFold, and Gemini model family. Establishing first automated materials science laboratory in UK (2026). Significant talent exodus to new startups including David Silver's $1B Ineffable Intelligence fundraise and numerous other DeepMind alumni raising billions collectively.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Implements Frontier Safety Framework addressing CBRN threats, cybersecurity risks, harmful manipulation, and misalignment scenarios where AI systems might interfere with operator control. Conducts safety case reviews before deployment and has expanded protocols for machine learning R&D capabilities that could accelerate AI development. Added focus on deceptive alignment risks in 2025 framework updates.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Maintains voluntary Frontier Safety Framework with Critical Capability Levels and conditional safeguards, updated multiple times through 2025-2026. Supports evidence-based policy development and collaborates with UK AI Security Institute on safety research. However, has weakened some safety commitments by making implementation conditional on other labs adopting similar measures, and faced criticism from 60 UK lawmakers for allegedly violating safety pledges around Gemini 2.5 Pro release timing.

organizations.name: Modeling Cooperation
organizations.notes: Founded 2019, nonprofit with 3 employees across Switzerland and UK. Fiscally sponsored by Convergence Analysis. Received multiple grants including $77,116 from Foresight Institute and previous grants of $83,000 and $74,000. Develops research software tools like Intelligence Rising workshops and Monte Carlo simulation tools. Collaborates with Technology Strategy Roleplay on educational workshops for decision-makers about AI race dangers.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research specifically targets catastrophic risks from competition for transformative AI development. Uses game theory and computational modeling to analyze AI competition dynamics and develop interventions to shift competitors' incentives toward safety-focused behavior.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on improving cooperation in AI competition dynamics through research tools and policy analysis. Develops software for decision-makers to understand AI competition risks and mitigate dangerous competition scenarios between AI developers.

organizations.name: Softmax
organizations.notes: Founded ~2024 by Emmett Shear (former Twitch CEO, interim OpenAI CEO), Adam Goldstein, and David Bloomin. Independent research lab developing 'organic alignment' theory through multi-agent simulations. Funded by Andreessen Horowitz. Launched Cogames benchmark for social intelligence. Made $20M political donation to Public First Action alongside $30B Series G funding announcement in 2026. Research focuses on biological-inspired alignment mechanisms rather than traditional control systems.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on 'organic alignment' - developing AI systems that naturally align through multi-agent cooperation rather than external control mechanisms. Research emphasizes theory of mind, continual learning, and social intelligence as prerequisites for safe AGI. Argues alignment and AGI are fundamentally the same problem.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for federal AI regulation that creates transparency requirements, safety documentation, and compliance barriers that favor companies with existing safety infrastructure. Through $20M political donation, supports regulatory frameworks that align US standards with EU AI Act principles.

organizations.name: Equilibria Network
organizations.notes: Founded 2024, small team of 2. Co-directed by Jonas Hallgren. Develops spectral graph theory frameworks for analyzing AI coordination systems and preventing collective AI behavior from becoming harmful. Publishes research via Substack on multi-agent safety evaluations and system-level AI governance. Uses category theory and agent-based simulations to identify control levers in multi-agent AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on 'gradual disempowerment' scenarios where AI systems slowly erode human influence over collective outcomes through competitive dynamics across markets, networks, and governance simultaneously. Research emphasizes multi-agent coordination failures and system-level safety evaluations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for spectral analysis-based governance approaches to monitor AI coordination systems, proposing specific metrics like human betweenness centrality across mechanism boundaries and spectral gap ratios to prevent AI disempowerment through gradual erosion of human agency in collective decision-making.

organizations.name: Beneficial AI Foundation
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as US 501(c)(3) nonprofit by MIT physicist Max Tegmark. Small team of ~8 researchers including Chief Scientist Shaowei Lin and researchers in formal verification and mechanistic interpretability. Funded by Vitalik Buterin and Erik Otto donations ($1.865M revenue in 2023). Runs Buterin Fellowship program and multi-university partnerships through QAISI. Organizes MIT conferences and ICML workshops on mechanistic interpretability.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research including mechanistic interpretability (artificial neuroscience), formal verification and guaranteed safe AI through their Quantitative AI Safety Initiative (QAISI), red-teaming, and audit standards for powerful AI models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for quantitative safety standards similar to FDA drug approval and FAA aircraft certification requirements. Supports mandatory AI safety audits and standards requiring quantitative guarantees that AI systems will do what we want before deployment.

organizations.name: Cadenza Labs
organizations.notes: Founded by 4 SERI-MATS alumni in 2023. Small research team of ~4 people. Funded by LTFF and Manifund grants ($7,810 raised). Key publications include 'Liars' Bench' benchmark (72,863 examples across 7 datasets), cluster normalization techniques for unsupervised probing, and contributions to EleutherAI's CCS library. Research agenda focuses on three pillars: understanding neural network computational units, developing unsupervised feature detection methods, and advancing LM cognition fundamentals.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary research focus on detecting deceptive behavior in LLMs through lie detection techniques and interpretability methods. Addresses risks from AI systems that generate false statements they believe to be untrue, contributing to alignment evaluation frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research, particularly developing lie detection and interpretability methods for LLMs. Work contributes to broader AI safety evaluation and monitoring capabilities without explicit policy advocacy.

organizations.name: RAND Corporation
organizations.notes: Founded 1946 as Project RAND, became independent nonprofit 1948. Revenue $469.7M (FY2025), 1,667 employees. Major defense contractor and federal research institution. Manages FFRDCs for DHS and DoD. Recent AI work includes $38M Audacious Project grant for 'Canary' AI evaluation project with METR. Key publications: 'Four Governance Approaches to Securing Advanced AI' (2026), Social and Economic Policy Rethink series (2025). Testified before Congress on AI national security challenges.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, National security, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI security governance, catastrophic risk mitigation, sectoral transformation impacts across healthcare/finance/energy/transportation, workforce displacement, disinformation campaigns, and national security implications. Develops evaluation frameworks for frontier AI systems and studies potential for AI-enabled cyberattacks and autonomous weapons.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance approaches that strengthen security while preserving innovation. Proposes four governance models ranging from voluntary industry collaboration to government-enforced standards, emphasizing proportionality and stakeholder engagement. Supports risk-based frameworks with tiered requirements based on AI system capabilities and risks.

organizations.name: Geodesic Research
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 by Cambridge PhD students. 6-person team including founders Puria Radmard and Cameron Tice. UK-based technical AI safety organization focused on compute-intensive alignment research. Key publication: 'Alignment Pretraining: AI Discourse Causes Self-Fulfilling (Mis)alignment' showing pretraining on positive AI examples reduces misalignment from 45% to 9%. Partners with Apart Research, has advisors from OpenAI and Anthropic. Compute access through Cambridge Inference and Bristol Centre for Supercomputing.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
