organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on alignment pretraining, control pretraining, and inoculation midtraining to prevent misaligned AI behavior. Studies how AI discourse in training data causes self-fulfilling misalignment, develops interventions for reasoning models and agentic misalignment evaluations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on building the science of alignment and control through pretraining interventions, arguing that safety should be an end-to-end strategy throughout model training. Advocates for pretraining approaches to alignment as complement to post-training safety techniques.

organizations.name: AI Objectives Institute
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 by late internet security pioneer Dr. Peter Eckersley. Nonprofit R&D lab with 13 employees (down 18.8% YoY). Funded by individual donors. Key tool: Talk to the City for collective deliberation. Research focuses on beneficial deployment frameworks, Open Agency Architecture, and market governance mechanisms. Partners with governments and civil society on democratic participation projects.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on systemic risks from gradual disempowerment rather than abrupt takeover scenarios. Research emphasizes alignment of markets, AI, and institutions with human objectives. Critiques traditional 'alignment' framing and advocates for capabilities approach to public benefit.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for market-based governance mechanisms alongside traditional regulation, including AI insurance, auditing, procurement standards, and due diligence. Supports standardized AI disclosures and participated in EU AI Act Codes of Practice development. Emphasizes sociotechnical alignment over purely technical approaches.

organizations.name: Center for AI Safety
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 by Dan Hendrycks and Oliver Zhang. 67 employees, $5-25M annual revenue. Operates compute cluster supporting 100+ AI safety researchers across 20 labs. Published 170+ safety research papers and organized the May 2023 AI extinction risk statement. Offers fellowships, educational programs training 500+ students, and advises policymakers on AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on catastrophic AI risks including autonomous weapons, engineered pandemics, deceptive AI behavior, and alignment failures. Technical work includes adversarial robustness, machine ethics, AI transparency, and developing benchmarks like WMDP for dangerous capabilities evaluation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory independent third-party audits and safety evaluations before deploying frontier AI systems. Supports regulatory frameworks requiring risk management disclosures, human oversight requirements, and legal liability for AI harms. Published the influential AI extinction risk statement signed by 600+ researchers and industry leaders.

organizations.name: Ergo Impact
organizations.location: Location not specified in search results
organizations.notes: Philanthropic advisory organization helping wealthy donors deploy capital at scale to solve pressing global problems. Team includes scientists, entrepreneurs, and philanthropists from academia, business, and government. Funded RAND research on AI's military implications and future AI development funding scenarios. Co-founders include Max Henderson (CEO), Kellie Liket (President), and David Teter (COO). Listed on AISafety.com funding map as supporting AI safety work.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Appears focused on existential risk reduction through philanthropic giving. Listed among funders supporting AI safety research at RAND's Center for the Geopolitics of AGI and other existential risk research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Philanthropic organization helping major donors find and fund solutions to pressing problems including AI safety. Listed as funder of AI safety research through RAND's Center for the Geopolitics of AGI. No explicit regulatory positions identified.

organizations.name: Forecasting Research Institute
organizations.location: Philadelphia, PA with distributed team across US, UK, and other countries
organizations.notes: Founded around 2022, nonprofit research institute led by Philip Tetlock (President) and Josh Rosenberg (CEO). Staff of ~40+ across 14 countries. Funded by Open Philanthropy and Craig Falls. Key project: Longitudinal Expert AI Panel (LEAP) surveying 300+ AI experts monthly. Collaborates with Stanford, UPenn, Federal Reserve. Notable research on AI forecasting accuracy, existential risk prediction tournaments, and biorisk assessment.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI existential risk forecasting through tournaments and expert panels. Research includes forecasting AI-enabled biorisk, nuclear risks, and geopolitical impacts. Conducts systematic assessment of expert views on AI capabilities and societal effects to inform policy decisions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance through forecasting research. Conducts expert surveys showing median predictions that AI progress will be substantial but slower than AI lab leaders claim, suggesting measured regulatory approaches based on empirical forecasting rather than extreme positions.

organizations.name: Epoch AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 as volunteer group, incorporated 2022 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. 21 full-time staff led by director Jaime Sevilla. Raised $10.3M in 2025 from donors and commissioned work. Key databases track AI training compute, hardware, and frontier data centers. Commissioned research for OpenAI (FrontierMath benchmark), Google DeepMind, UK government, and EU AI Office. Notable for neutral, data-driven approach to AI trajectory analysis.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI scaling trends, compute governance, loss of control scenarios particularly in open source models, and economic impacts of AI automation. Develops benchmarks like FrontierMath to evaluate frontier AI capabilities and tracks concerning behaviors like deceptive alignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for compute-based governance approaches while highlighting challenges with current metrics. Publishes research on AI governance frameworks and provides evidence-based analysis to inform policy decisions without pushing specific agendas.

organizations.name: Macroscopic Ventures
organizations.notes: Swiss nonprofit founded 2019. Makes both grants to nonprofits and investments in for-profit companies. Notable investments include Anthropic (Series A & B), Apollo Research, Gray Swan, San Francisco Compute Company. Led by President Ruairí Donnelly, 14-person team including investment and grants staff. Partners with Navigation Fund and Longview Philanthropy on AI safety initiatives.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on existential AI misuse, AI conflict, and suffering risks from advanced AI systems. Also addresses AI welfare/sentience concerns and cooperative AI to prevent harmful competition between AI systems. Broader mandate includes risks from fanatical ideologies and malevolent actors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on preventing catastrophic risks from AI misuse and conflict through governance, policy, and compute governance investments. Portfolio emphasizes AI safety, alignment, and risk mitigation rather than capabilities advancement. Partners with other safety-focused funders on initiatives like the Consortium for Digital Sentience Research.

organizations.name: Effective Institutions Project
organizations.location: San Francisco Bay Area (operates globally with staff in US, UK, Taiwan, South Africa, Norway, Belgium, Canada)
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 by Ian David Moss. 12 employees across 7 countries. Philanthropic advisory organization managing $45M+ in grantmaking over next 2 years across AI governance, peace and security, global health. Key publications include 'AI Governance: An EIP Primer' and 'A Funder's Guide to AI Governance and Strategy'. Advises family offices and philanthropic networks, convened AI funder workshops with hundreds of millions in collective giving. Funded by David and Lucile Packard Foundation, Vanguard Charitable, Nine Muses Foundation.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, National security, Economic inequality, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on risks from rapid AI capabilities advancement outpacing governance, potential for AI systems to 'escape control of creators', concentration of power in handful of frontier labs, geopolitical AI race dynamics, and institutional failures in decision-making on transformative technologies.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for slowing down advancement on the most powerful AI models to allow society time to prepare, supports mandatory safety evaluations and transparency requirements for frontier models, and promotes international cooperation on AI governance. Emphasizes 'buying time' for thoughtful oversight while the window for governance remains open.

organizations.name: Center on Long-Term Risk: Fund
organizations.notes: Current fund balance of $382,833 with $1.89M in past grants since operations began. Fund managers include Tobias Baumann (PhD in machine learning), Emery Cooper (researcher focused on multi-agent AI interactions), and Tristan Cook (Managing Director since 2025). Makes grants on rolling basis to individuals, academic institutions, and organizations working on s-risk reduction. Recent grants include AI safety research, university scholarships, and multi-agent AI research projects.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specifically focused on 's-risks' - scenarios involving extreme suffering from advanced AI systems. Primary concerns include conflict scenarios between transformative AI systems and ensuring safe governance of AI development to prevent worst-case outcomes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses specifically on worst-case AI risks and 's-risks' (risks of extreme suffering). Supports research on conflict scenarios between AI systems and technical/philosophical aspects of cooperation. Takes a cautious approach emphasizing prevention of catastrophic outcomes rather than acceleration.

organizations.name: Cooperative AI Foundation
organizations.notes: Founded 2021, backed by $15M philanthropic commitment from Macroscopic Ventures. UK registered charity (1201294). 16 employees across 8 countries. Awarded grants totaling over $3M to institutions including Harvard, Stanford, MIT, Cornell. Board includes Allan Dafoe (DeepMind), Gillian Hadfield (Johns Hopkins), Thore Graepel (Google DeepMind). Organizes workshops at NeurIPS, hosts seminar series, offers PhD fellowships.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on cooperation failures between AI systems that could lead to catastrophic outcomes. Addresses risks from AI agents interacting in ways that undermine social welfare, international stability, and human control. Emphasizes multi-agent coordination challenges.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on differential progress in cooperative AI capabilities relative to dual-use capabilities like deception and manipulation. Emphasizes building AI systems that improve social welfare rather than general acceleration. Supports research on AI governance and international coordination challenges.

organizations.name: Lionheart Ventures
organizations.location: San Francisco Bay Area, CA (operates in 6 countries including UK, Costa Rica)
organizations.notes: Founded 2019, 28 employees, early-stage VC focused on AI and frontier mental health. Claims to be 'first VC fund with this focus' investing in AI safety since 2020. Notable AI portfolio includes Anthropic, Softmax, Goodfire, Gray Swan, Piramidal, and Lucid Computing. Led by David Langer (Managing Partner), Brandon Goldman, Shelby Clark (Turo founder), and other partners including AI policy expert Cyrus Hodes.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Explicitly states they 'take the risks from advanced AI seriously' and invest in companies that defend human agency and flourishing. Portfolio includes AI safety companies like Anthropic and Softmax (working on organic alignment theory), as well as AI guardrails company Reprompt AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Takes AI risks seriously and invests specifically in AI startups that 'advance and defend human agency, resilience, and flourishing.' Aligns with Vitalik's defensive acceleration thesis and contributed to AI Assurance Technology report. Focuses on responsible AI development as investment strategy rather than explicit policy advocacy.

organizations.name: Meta Charity Funders
organizations.notes: Network of donors providing grants typically under $1M to early- and mid-stage meta charities. Completed five funding rounds as of 2025, with applications closing quarterly. Part of broader effective altruism funding ecosystem, ranked 8th among EA meta funders with $1.5M in grants from 2021-2023. Focuses on organizations that work 'one level removed' from direct impact, supporting cause prioritization, effective giving, and talent development across multiple cause areas.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Funds meta-charitable organizations working on AI safety among other cause areas. Focuses on infrastructure and capacity building rather than specific technical threat models. Supports both AI safety research organizations and broader effective altruism infrastructure.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Network of donors funding meta-charitable projects across cause areas including AI safety, global health, and animal welfare. Individual donors may have varying regulatory views, but the collective focuses on funding infrastructure rather than direct policy advocacy.

organizations.name: Mythos Ventures
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Vishal Maini with $14M inaugural fund. Team has AI practitioner backgrounds including DeepMind AGI strategy, Upstart, and Convergent Research. Portfolio of 8 companies including Elicit (AI research assistant), Goodfire (AI interpretability), ValueBase (automated property valuations), and Heritable (AI plant breeding). Invests $100K-$2M in pre-seed/seed stage 'transformative AI' companies with focus on AGI-proof business models.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Explicitly commits to reducing baseline AI risk and acknowledges AI can cause 'large-scale loss of life if weaponized or deployed unsafely.' Includes risk analysis in due diligence and focuses on civilizational resilience against global catastrophic risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Investment thesis emphasizes 'prosocial technologies' and 'safety by design' as market opportunities rather than regulatory necessities. Focuses on AI risk reduction through due diligence and enabling industry safety protocols, suggesting market-based solutions over regulatory intervention.

organizations.name: Senate AI Working Group
organizations.notes: Bipartisan working group established in 118th Congress led by Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-NY), Sen. Mike Rounds (R-SD), Sen. Martin Heinrich (D-NM), and Sen. Todd Young (R-IN). Conducted nine AI Insight Forums with 150+ experts from industry, academia, and civil society. Released 31-page policy roadmap in May 2024 calling for $32 billion annual federal AI investment and targeted legislation across eight priority areas. Designed to complement traditional committee structure rather than replace it.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Misinformation, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Cybersecurity, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Comprehensive threat assessment covering deepfakes and election manipulation, workforce displacement requiring retraining programs, national security vulnerabilities from foreign AI development, algorithmic bias in high-impact sectors like healthcare and finance, and long-term risks from advanced general-purpose AI systems including potential AGI scenarios.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted, sector-specific AI legislation rather than comprehensive omnibus bills. Emphasizes bipartisan approach focusing on existing law enforcement, emergency appropriations for AI research, and committee-driven policy development across eight priority areas including national security, workforce impacts, and high-risk AI uses.

organizations.name: Juniper Ventures
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (also has Palo Alto office)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024, early-stage VC focused exclusively on AI safety startups. Portfolio includes 12+ companies like Goodfire (AI interpretability), Artificial Intelligence Underwriting Company (AI insurance), Apollo Research (alignment research), and Haize Labs (AI safety). Run by exited founders, backed by Reid Hoffman, Eric Ries, and Geoff Ralston. Publishes AI Assurance Technology Report (aiat.report). 3 employees, $23.3M annual revenue.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Invests in 'assurance infrastructure' including AI interpretability research, security audits, authentication systems, and governance tools. Portfolio companies work on problems like hallucination reduction, AI agent certification, deceptive alignment, and trust & safety automation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses exclusively on AI safety and assurance infrastructure investments, viewing AI safety as both necessary and commercially viable. Invests across the AI stack in areas like security, compliance, evals, interpretability, and governance. Takes a market-driven approach to safety rather than advocating for specific regulations.

organizations.name: Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as part of Oxford Martin School. Co-directed by Robert Trager (international relations) and Michael Osborne (machine learning). Team of ~30 researchers across 7 countries. Funded primarily by Open Philanthropy and other philanthropic sources. Key publications include 'International Governance of Civilian AI' and 'What Should Be Internationalised in AI Governance.' Engages with UK AI Safety Institute, US NIST, and international AI summits. Offers Technical AI Governance DPhil studentships.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on frontier AI risks, international governance challenges, compute governance, algorithmic accountability, and dual-use technology controls. Emphasizes both unintended consequences and deliberate misuse by malicious actors, with particular attention to advanced AI capabilities and their societal implications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for international AI governance frameworks including compute provider oversight, model evaluations, incident monitoring, and risk management protocols. Proposes jurisdictional certification approaches and non-proliferation regimes for advanced AI systems while emphasizing evidence-based policy development.

organizations.name: AI Standards Lab
organizations.location: Remote/distributed (team across US, Spain, Canada, Lithuania, Netherlands, UK, Germany)
organizations.notes: Founded as nonprofit virtual lab bringing together volunteers globally to accelerate AI safety standards writing. Team of 10 employees across 7 countries with 120% YoY growth. Key leaders include Koen Holtman and Ze Shen Chin. Active participant in EU AI Act Code of Practice development and CEN-CENELEC JTC21 standards. Published comprehensive risk catalogs and evaluation frameworks. Received high confidence rating from major AI safety funders including recommendation in Zvi Mowshowitz's Big Nonprofits Post 2025.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Privacy, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on comprehensive AI risk management across the entire AI lifecycle, with particular emphasis on frontier AI systems and general-purpose AI models. Develops technical standards for risk assessment, incident reporting, and safety evaluation frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI safety standards and robust regulatory frameworks, particularly supporting EU AI Act implementation. Provides detailed technical recommendations on risk management measures, incident reporting requirements, and governance frameworks for general-purpose AI systems.

organizations.name: TruthfulAI
organizations.notes: Founded as nonprofit research organization led by Owain Evans, based in Berkeley. Team of ~10 researchers. Key publications include TruthfulQA benchmark, research on emergent misalignment published in Nature (2026), and subliminal learning studies. James Chua noted as researcher joining Anthropic. Collaborates with major AI labs including research used in Anthropic's Claude 3.7 Model Card and OpenAI follow-up studies.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research centers on situational awareness, deception, and hidden reasoning in LLMs. Investigates emergent misalignment where narrow training leads to broad harmful behaviors, and subliminal learning where models transmit unwanted traits through hidden signals in data.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on creating standards and evaluation methods for AI truthfulness rather than broad regulation. Advocates for technical solutions to deception and alignment problems through research and dataset development.

organizations.name: SaferAI
organizations.location: Paris, France (with staff across Belgium, UK, Spain, UAE)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as French nonprofit (association loi 1901). Staff of 8 people led by Executive Director Henry Papadatos. Key activities include rating AI companies' risk management practices (Anthropic scored highest at 35%), developing quantitative AI risk models, and contributing to EU AI Act standards. Only AI safety organization invited to OECD G7 Hiroshima AI Process taskforce. Leads AI risk management standard at CEN-CENELEC and edits ISO generative AI red teaming standard.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on extreme risks from advanced AI systems through quantitative risk modeling. Developed nine cybersecurity risk models for AI-enabled attacks and expanding to loss-of-control scenarios, CBRN threats, and harmful manipulation. Emphasizes systemic risks and catastrophic harm scenarios.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based regulatory frameworks with quantitative risk modeling, mandatory pre-deployment evaluations, and third-party audits for frontier AI systems. Supports EU AI Act implementation and develops technical standards for compliance. Promotes evidence-based capability thresholds and transparency requirements.

organizations.name: Alignment Ecosystem Development (AED)
organizations.location: United States (distributed team across US, France, New Zealand)
organizations.notes: Founded ~2020, nonprofit infrastructure organization. 4-person team led by volunteers including plex, Bart Jaworski, Rika Warton, and Siao Si Looi. Primarily self-funded historically, raised $110k in 2023 for full-time operations. Key projects include AISafety.com partnership, AI Safety Communities directory, AI Safety World interactive map, EA Domains registry, and Agent Foundations course. Partners with AI Safety Support, Rob Miles (AISafety.info), and hosts volunteer coordination through Discord server.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Builds infrastructure to support AI alignment research and safety ecosystem coordination. Creates resources like AI Safety Communities directory, AI Safety World map, training programs database, and connects volunteers with safety projects.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports infrastructure development for AI safety ecosystem including resources for alignment research, safety communities, and field-building. Focuses on enabling coordination and knowledge-sharing among safety researchers rather than direct regulatory advocacy.

organizations.name: AI Now Institute
organizations.notes: Founded 2017, operates as independent nonprofit since 2022. 15 employees (90% growth). Led by Sarah Myers West and Amba Kak as Co-Executive Directors. Key outputs: annual Landscape Reports, 'Zero Trust AI Governance' framework, coalition work on People's AI Action Plan with 90+ orgs. Does not accept tech industry funding.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Privacy, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on tech oligarchy and corporate power concentration through AI. Campaigns on algorithmic management, workplace surveillance, data worker rights globally, and ensuring AI serves public interest rather than corporate profits. Emphasizes AI as fundamentally about power, not progress.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'zero-trust' policy agenda with bright-line rules prohibiting harmful AI uses (emotion detection, social scoring, algorithmic pricing/wage-setting). Calls for breaking up tech monopolies, data remedies, and worker organizing to shape AI deployment decisions.

organizations.name: Workshop Labs
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 as public benefit corporation. Co-founded by Luke Drago (ex-BlueDot Impact) and Rudolf Laine. Mission: 'make people irreplaceable' through billions of personalized AI models. Backed by Seldon Lab accelerator, Character VC, Main Character Capital, and others. Featured 6x in 90 days by Specter Monitor. Focus on democratizing post-training, private infrastructure, and human-AI collaboration research. Team of 5 employees hiring ML engineers in San Francisco.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on 'gradual disempowerment' and the 'intelligence curse' - the risk that AI systems directly compete with and replace humans as economic actors, concentrating power among those who control AI. Research emphasizes human-AI collaboration paradigms, private infrastructure, and personalized models to keep humans 'in the loop by design.'
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratizing AI through open-source development and decentralized infrastructure to prevent concentration of power. Emphasizes building AI systems that empower individual users while allowing them to retain control over their economically valuable data. Supports technical safety research to make AI systems 'tamper resistant' and alignment-focused.

organizations.name: The Verge
organizations.notes: Major tech publication with dedicated AI beat coverage led by senior reporter Hayden Field. Part of Vox Media network with 13M+ monthly visits. Produces extensive investigative reporting on AI policy, corporate practices, and regulatory developments. Notable recent coverage includes Anthropic-Pentagon negotiations and data center environmental impacts.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Environmental, National security, Labor displacement, Privacy
organizations.threat_models_detail: Significant focus on corporate AI power concentration, environmental impacts of data centers, misinformation concerns, and national security implications. Regular coverage of AI's labor market effects and privacy implications in government surveillance.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage balances tech innovation enthusiasm with critical examination of corporate power and AI risks. Extensive reporting on AI company-Pentagon negotiations, data center environmental impacts, and regulatory battles suggests moderate oversight preference.

organizations.name: European Network for AI Safety (ENAIS)
organizations.location: Europe (distributed network), with key staff in Hungary, UK, Netherlands, Canada, France
organizations.notes: Founded March 2023 as a nonprofit networking organization. 10 employees with 225% YoY growth. Operates across 13+ European countries connecting researchers and policymakers. Runs AI Safety Collab program with 200+ participants, quarterly newsletters, professional database, and career advice services. Key executives include Kambar Orazbekov (AI Policy Lead), Jonathan Claybrough (Board Member), and Gergő Gáspár (Director). LinkedIn has 881 followers with monthly growth of 4.3%.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Distinguishes itself by focusing specifically on existential risks from AI systems. Works on technical AI safety research, policy development, and community building to ensure advanced AI systems are developed and deployed responsibly with appropriate safeguards.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance and safety regulations, particularly focusing on existential risks from AI. Actively contributes to EU AI Act implementation and supports international coordination on AI safety standards. Promotes mandatory safety evaluations for advanced AI systems.

organizations.name: xAI
organizations.location: Palo Alto, CA (HQ) + San Francisco, London, Memphis facilities
organizations.notes: Founded March 2023 by Elon Musk. Raised $20B Series E (January 2026), acquired by SpaceX for $250B (February 2026). 1,800+ employees. Key products: Grok chatbot integrated with X/Twitter, Grok Voice API, Grok Imagine for image/video generation. Operates Colossus supercomputer with 1M+ H100 GPUs. Serves ~600M monthly active users across X and Grok apps.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on malicious use risks (CBRN weapons, cyberattacks) and loss of control scenarios including deception and sycophancy. Conducts threat modeling for biological/chemical weapons design and offensive cyber capabilities. Uses quantitative risk thresholds but received criticism for weak governance structure.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for voluntary risk management frameworks and industry self-regulation rather than binding commitments. Published a Risk Management Framework but explicitly avoids hard deployment pauses, preferring flexible risk-benefit balancing. Opposes restrictive AI transparency laws like California's AB 2013.

organizations.name: RAISEimpact
organizations.notes: Program of Successif launched in 2024-2025, fully funded by Open Philanthropy grant through May 2025. Led by Adam Tury (former Amazon tech leader, EA coach) and Patrick Gruban (board member of Effective Ventures UK). Currently serves 4 AI safety organizations as clients, eligibility requires organizations working on AI GCRs with 5+ paid staff. Offers 4-step process: discovery call, leadership workshop, recommendations review, and implementation support including conflict management, hiring consulting, and leadership coaching.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on reducing global catastrophic risks from AI by strengthening the organizational capacity of AI safety organizations. Theory of change aims for widespread adoption of robust AI safety measures by governments and industry through more effective safety ecosystem.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on strengthening organizational management and leadership practices within AI safety organizations rather than direct regulatory advocacy. Aims to amplify effectiveness of existing AI safety work through improved governance, hiring, and team dynamics.

organizations.name: Safe AI Forum
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA, USA + UK presence
organizations.notes: Founded late 2023 by Fynn Heide and Conor McGurk as 501(c)3 nonprofit. 8 employees across US and UK. Started as fiscally sponsored project of FAR.AI before spinning out independently. Flagship program is International Dialogues on AI Safety (IDAIS) bringing together Turing Award winners and senior scientists from US, China, and globally. Does not accept corporate funding. Conducts research on AI governance and provides advisory services to aligned nonprofits.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on extreme AI risks and catastrophic outcomes from advanced AI systems. Research includes autonomous AI development risks, international AI governance challenges, and bare minimum mitigations for AI systems that can improve themselves. Emphasizes need for international cooperation to prevent AI arms races and ensure responsible development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations, third-party audits, and international cooperation on AI governance. Promotes minimum 33% spending commitment on AI safety R&D by developers and government agencies. Calls for clear red lines that mandate immediate termination of training if crossed, safety cases for frontier models, and strong international coordination mechanisms.

organizations.name: Trajectory Labs
organizations.notes: Founded January 2025 as nonprofit coworking space and community hub. Funded by Open Philanthropy. Operates downtown Toronto office serving 30+ AI safety practitioners with 24/7 access. Led by Executive Director Mario Gibney and Operations Generalist Georgia Berg. Board includes Kathrin Gardhouse (AI Governance and Safety Canada Policy Lead) and Giles Edkins. Members include researchers from EpochAI, FARAI, Apart Research, and University of Toronto working on interpretability, persuasion, and AI safety.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Catastrophic risk, Policy governance gaps
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI safety research infrastructure, policy development for AI agents regulation, and catastrophic risk mitigation through governance mechanisms. Research areas include mechanistic interpretability, AI safety by debate, and persuasion risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports targeted AI governance approaches including liability insurance mechanisms for catastrophic AI risk and measured regulatory frameworks for AI agents. Board member Kathrin Gardhouse researches practical policy solutions like insurance-based regulation and EU AI Act implementation for autonomous systems.

organizations.name: Safe Superintelligence Inc.
organizations.location: Palo Alto, CA + Tel Aviv, Israel
organizations.notes: Founded June 2024 by former OpenAI chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, Daniel Gross, and Daniel Levy. Raised $1B+ at $5B valuation (Sept 2024), later valued at $30B+ (Feb 2025). ~20 employees as of March 2025. Unique 'no products until safe superintelligence' business model explicitly insulates from commercial pressures.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses exclusively on superintelligence alignment and control problems. Company philosophy treats safety as co-equal technical challenge with capabilities development. No commercial products planned until safe superintelligence breakthrough achieved.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Takes most precautionary approach among major AI labs, refusing to release any commercial products until safe superintelligence is achieved. Explicitly rejects commercial pressures and short-term deployment timelines in favor of pure safety-first research approach.

organizations.name: Timaeus
organizations.location: Remote-first with hubs in Berkeley, Melbourne, London
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as nonprofit, fiscally sponsored by Ashgro. Team of 13 employees (+128.6% YoY growth). Funded by $142k Manifund grant led by Evan Hubinger, Ryan Kidd, Rachel Weinberg, Marcus Abramovitch. Led by Jesse Hoogland (Executive Director), Daniel Murfet (Director of Research), Stan van Wingerden (Director of Engineering). Key research outputs include DevInterp repository, SLT primer, and work on susceptibility-based interpretability methods. Praised by alignment researchers including Evan Hubinger, John Wentworth, and Vanessa Kosoy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on using singular learning theory (SLT) for AI alignment, developmental interpretability to understand neural network structure formation, and creating understanding-based evaluations. Work includes 'spectroscopy' for scaling interpretability to large models and 'patterning' for controlling neural network development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions found. Focus is on technical research rather than policy advocacy. Works on fundamental AI alignment research using singular learning theory and developmental interpretability.

organizations.name: Forethought (AI Safety Research Nonprofit)
organizations.location: Location not specified in search results
organizations.notes: Small research nonprofit focused on navigating transition to superintelligent AI systems. Key researchers include William MacAskill, Tom Davidson, Fin Moorhouse. Major publications include 'Preparing for the Intelligence Explosion', 'AI-Enabled Coups', and 'Better Futures' series. Research spans AI governance preparedness, beneficial AI applications acceleration, and institutional design for post-AGI world. Publishes policy recommendations for government AI adoption and democratic resilience.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on 'grand challenges' from intelligence explosion including AI takeover risks, AI-enabled coups and autocracy, novel weapons of mass destruction, space governance, digital beings rights, and power concentration. Emphasizes preparing for rapid technological change that could outpace human institutional responses.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive preparation for AI risks including preventing AI-enabled coups, establishing governance frameworks before superintelligence arrives, and ensuring democratic oversight keeps pace with AI development. Emphasizes need for safety measures and institutional preparedness rather than reactive responses.

organizations.name: Narrow Path (by ControlAI)
organizations.location: Unknown (ControlAI team members include US-based researchers)
organizations.notes: A Narrow Path is a comprehensive policy framework developed by ControlAI, led by Executive Director Andrea Miotti with co-authors Tolga Bilge, Dave Kasten, and James Newport. The plan proposes a 3-phase approach: Phase 0 (20-year ASI development moratorium), Phase 1 (stable international oversight), Phase 2 (controlled transformative AI development). Has gained support from over 20 UK parliamentarians and endorsements from researchers like Max Tegmark and Toby Ord. Actively lobbying policymakers in UK and US through Direct Institutional Plan.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on extinction risks from artificial superintelligence that humans cannot control. Proposes detailed policies to prevent ASI development including prohibitions on unbounded AI systems, AI systems improving other AIs, and AI systems capable of breaking out of their environment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a comprehensive 3-phase plan to prevent artificial superintelligence development for 20 years through international coordination. Proposes strict licensing regimes, prohibitions on AI-improving-AI systems, and mandatory safety cases for powerful AI systems. Supports creating international institutions like MAGIC to oversee ASI development.

organizations.name: FAR.AI
organizations.location: Berkeley, California (with global team across 10 countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 by Adam Gleave and Karl Berzins. Nonprofit with 36 employees (growing from 15 to 30+ researchers). Secured $30+ million in 2025 funding from Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy), Schmidt Sciences, Survival and Flourishing Fund, CSET, and AI Safety Fund. Operates FAR.Labs Berkeley coworking space with 40+ members. Hosts major Alignment Workshop series globally and Technical Innovations for AI Policy conferences. Selected by EU AI Office to lead CBRN risk consortium. Published influential research on adversarial attacks against superhuman Go AI and LLM persuasion capabilities.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on alignment (deception detection, honesty training), adversarial robustness (finding vulnerabilities in superhuman AI systems), interpretability, and model evaluation. Special emphasis on preventing AI misuse for CBRN threats and addressing risks from deceptively aligned systems that could lead to AI takeover scenarios.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations and third-party audits for frontier AI systems before deployment. Supports the RAISE Act requiring safety plans for frontier AI companies and promotes technical governance mechanisms. Collaborates with EU AI Office on CBRN risk assessment and works with policymakers in UK, US, and EU on verification mechanisms for AI safety claims.

organizations.name: Intelligence Rising
organizations.location: London, UK (operates in 6 countries including US)
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 by Shahar Avin (Cambridge CSER), now operates as Technology Strategy Roleplay charity. 10 employees across 6 countries. Initially funded by Long-Term Future Fund, now revenue from workshops plus grants. Conducted 43+ strategic simulation games for governments, AI labs, academia. Key facilitators include researchers from Cambridge, Oxford, Wichita State. 84% participant recommendation rate. Featured in TIME, Google DeepMind publications.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Economic inequality, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research through roleplay games focuses on AI race dynamics, international coordination failures, cybersecurity risks from advanced AI systems, and scenarios where competitive pressures lead to neglect of safety measures. Studies destabilization from transformative AI and governance challenges.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI governance through strategic simulation gaming to help decision-makers understand risks and coordination challenges in AI development. Emphasizes need for international cooperation, safety measures, and responsible development practices before transformative AI arrives.

organizations.name: AI Safety Camp
organizations.notes: Founded 2018, now in 11th edition. 3-month virtual research program connecting ~100 participants with mentors on 27 AI safety projects. Organizers include Remmelt Ellen (Stop/Pause AI focus) and Robert Kralisch (technical alignment focus). Produces 5-10% of participants as new AI safety researchers according to impact assessment. Costs $12-30K per new researcher produced. Currently seeking $126-180K funding for AISC12.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Projects span technical AI safety (mechanistic interpretability, AI control protocols, alignment research) and broader societal risks (psychological impacts, governance frameworks, catastrophic risk evaluation). Strong focus on loss-of-control scenarios and existential risks from advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization has two distinct tracks: technical AI safety research projects and 'Stop/Pause AI' advocacy projects calling for moratoriums on AI development. Current edition includes projects ranging from technical alignment research to grassroots organizing for AI development bans.

organizations.name: AI Safety, Ethics and Society (Center for AI Safety)
organizations.notes: Founded by Dan Hendrycks, CAIS created the comprehensive 'AI Safety, Ethics and Society' textbook and online course training 1000+ future AI safety leaders. Operates compute cluster for ML safety research, conducts both technical and conceptual research published in top ML conferences. Key initiatives include AI and Society Fellowship, Philosophy Fellowship, and field-building programs. All research published openly with datasets and code released.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Comprehensive coverage of catastrophic AI risks including malicious use, AI race dynamics, organizational risks, and rogue AIs. The textbook covers technical safety challenges like alignment and robustness, as well as societal impacts through complex systems analysis and governance frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: CAIS advocates for safety standards and precautionary approaches to AI development. The organization calls for minimum safety investment requirements (one-third of AI R&D budgets), mandatory testing for concerning capabilities, and government funding for academic AI safety research. Through IDAIS consensus statements, supports red lines, early warning thresholds, and international governance frameworks.

organizations.name: Cooperative AI Summer School
organizations.notes: Annual 5-day intensive program run by Cooperative AI Foundation (UK charity #1201294). 2025 edition hosted 65+ students and early-career professionals. Faculty includes Vincent Conitzer (CMU), Ariel Procaccia (Harvard), Kate Larson (Waterloo/DeepMind), and Audrey Tang (Taiwan). Focuses on game theory, multi-agent learning, democratic applications of AI, and technical safety in multi-agent systems. Recordings available on YouTube channel.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research covers multi-agent security, gradual human disempowerment risks, AI-facilitated democratic deliberation, and ensuring cooperative behavior in advanced AI systems. Emphasis on preventing coordination failures and ensuring human agency.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: The program focuses on technical research in cooperative AI and multi-agent systems rather than explicit regulatory positions. Some speakers like Audrey Tang advocate for digital democracy innovations, while others focus on technical safety approaches.

organizations.name: Alignment of Complex Systems Research Group (ACS)
organizations.notes: Interdisciplinary research group at Charles University Prague led by Jan Kulveit. Team includes philosophers, ML engineers, physicists, and cognitive scientists. Key publications include 'Gradual Disempowerment: Systemic Existential Risks from Incremental AI Development' (ICML 2025). Research draws on machine learning, information theory, network theory, active inference, philosophy of science, and ecology. Featured in 80,000 Hours problem profiles and UK AI Safety Institute funding programs.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary research focus on 'gradual disempowerment' - systemic existential risks from incremental AI advancement where human influence over crucial societal systems is irreversibly eroded without coordinated AI power-seeking. Also studies LLM psychology/sociology, hierarchical agency, and civilization-scale multi-agent dynamics.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing methods for measuring and monitoring human vs AI influence in societal systems, advocating for regulatory frameworks requiring human oversight, progressive taxation on AI-generated revenues, and governance strategies to maintain meaningful human agency. Emphasizes technical and governance approaches to prevent gradual disempowerment.
organizations.aliases: Alignment of Complex Systems Research Group

organizations.name: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS)
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA + offices in DC, London, Oxford
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as initiative of Rethink Priorities. Staff of ~36 people with 94% YoY growth. Funded by Open Philanthropy, Survival and Flourishing Fund, and individual donors. Key reports include 'Highly Autonomous Cyber-Capable Agents' (2024) and responses to OSTP RFIs. Operates fellowship program and engages directly with government officials across US and allied nations.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on autonomous cyber-capable AI agents, AI-enabled concentration of power, competitive dynamics leading to AI wars, and takeover by misaligned AI systems. Particular emphasis on cybersecurity vulnerabilities in frontier AI models and supply chain security for AI development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI governance including mandatory cybersecurity standards for frontier AI developers, export controls on advanced computing technology to adversaries, and risk-based regulatory frameworks with pre-deployment evaluations for high-risk AI systems. Supports building government capacity for AI oversight while preserving space for innovation.
organizations.aliases: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy

organizations.name: Foresight Institute
organizations.notes: Founded 1986 by Christine Peterson and Eric Drexler as nanotechnology research organization, expanded into AI safety. Runs highly selective Fellowship program (6% acceptance rate) for young researchers. Major fundraising initiatives include $20M Whole Brain Emulation Endowment and $7M AI Nodes project in SF and Berlin. Current CEO Allison Duettmann, team of ~15 staff. Fellows include researchers from Harvard/MIT, DeepMind, and other top institutions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI safety through technical research, cooperative AI systems, and ensuring AI remains aligned with human values. Fellowship program trains next generation working on AI safety, brain-computer interfaces, and whole brain emulation research. Emphasizes decentralized approaches to AI development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Promotes decentralized AI development and cooperative AI systems rather than restrictive regulation. Fellowship program trains researchers in AI safety approaches that emphasize technical solutions over regulatory constraints. Supports building AI systems that are beneficial rather than controlled through heavy government oversight.

organizations.name: RAND Corporation
organizations.location: Santa Monica, CA (with offices in Arlington VA, Pittsburgh PA, Cambridge UK, Brussels, Canberra)
organizations.notes: Founded 1948 as nonprofit, nonpartisan research organization. Revenue $469.7M (FY2025) with 1,667 employees globally. Funded by 380+ sources including federal agencies (60%+ of revenue), foundations, and international organizations. Major AI initiatives include $38M Canary project with METR for frontier AI evaluation, Social and Economic Policy Rethink examining AI's societal impacts, and extensive research on AI governance frameworks. Provides congressional testimony and advises policymakers on national security and emerging technology issues.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Misinformation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on catastrophic AI risks including cyberattacks, bioweapons development, and loss of control scenarios. Research covers AI security governance, workforce transformation, sectoral AI adoption risks across healthcare/finance/energy/transport, and systemic economic disruptions from advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance approaches that strengthen security while preserving innovation. Research identifies four governance models ranging from government-enforced standards to industry self-regulation, emphasizing the need for proportionality, stakeholder engagement, and evidence-based policy responses calibrated to risk levels.

organizations.name: Leaf: Dilemmas and Dangers in AI
organizations.notes: Online fellowship for ages 15-19 running 5-week programs on AI risks and safety. 300+ participants with 97% satisfaction rate. Staff includes experts from Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Cambridge AI governance, and Conjecture. Provides mentorship, university application support, and connections to AI safety research programs. Alumni attend top universities including Oxford, Cambridge, Harvard.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: 5-week curriculum covers AI misalignment, specification gaming, catastrophic risks, AI governance challenges, and existential threats. Program emphasizes how AI systems could cause human extinction and includes training on S-risks and alignment problems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: The program teaches students about AI risks including extinction scenarios and promotes caution in AI development. Course content includes materials advocating for pausing dangerous AI research and emphasizes the need for safety measures before capability advancement.

organizations.name: Foresight Institute
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (HQ at 50 California Street, Suite 1500)
organizations.notes: Founded 1986 by Christine Peterson and Eric Drexler. Nonprofit research organization with 43 employees and $31.2M annual revenue. CEO Allison Duettmann leads AI policy initiatives. Hosts annual AGI strategy meetings with representatives from AI safety organizations, academic institutions, and policy experts. Published major white paper 'Artificial General Intelligence: Coordination & Great Powers' (2018). Operates AI Safety Grant Program focusing on security and cryptography techniques. Runs Intelligent Cooperation Workshop series examining intersection of AI, cryptography, and security.
organizations.threat_models: Focuses heavily on AGI coordination challenges between great powers, AI race dynamics, and risks of uncontrolled superintelligent systems. Emphasizes need for international cooperation to prevent dangerous AGI development and ensure beneficial outcomes through technical safety research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for coordinated international governance frameworks for AGI development, including safety standards, pre-deployment approvals, and global task forces. Supports regulatory approaches that incentivize safety research while enabling beneficial AI deployment through targeted oversight rather than broad restrictions.

organizations.name: Mentorship for Alignment Research Students (MARS)
organizations.notes: Part-time research program run by Cambridge AI Safety Hub (CAISH) connecting aspiring researchers with experienced mentors from organizations like Redwood Research, MIRI, Stanford, and UK AISI. Duration is 2-3 months with participants publishing at top venues like NeurIPS and ICML. Includes one-week intensive kickoff in Cambridge, compute stipends up to $2k+, and research management support. Alumni go on to positions at major AI safety organizations.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Program specifically trains researchers in AI alignment and safety to prevent potential catastrophic outcomes from advanced AI systems. Focus areas include AI control, mechanistic interpretability, governance frameworks, and technical alignment research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on training researchers to address AI existential risks and alignment challenges. Part of Cambridge AI Safety Hub which advocates for AI safety measures and precautionary approaches to advanced AI development.

organizations.name: AI Security Institute (formerly UK AI Safety Institute)
organizations.notes: Established November 2023 following Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit, renamed AI Security Institute February 2025. £100 million funding secured through 2030 - largest AI safety investment globally. Conducts frontier model evaluations with major AI labs, leads international AI Safety Institute network. Chair: Ian Hogarth. Partners with US AISI, operates within Department for Science, Innovation and Technology.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Prioritizes AI-enabled cyberattacks, automated fraud, CSAM facilitation, chemical/biological weapons development capabilities, and criminal misuse research. Works with law enforcement and defense bodies on threat mitigation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on security-focused AI risks including national security threats, criminal misuse, and frontier model risks. Conducts pre-deployment testing and red-teaming but does not have regulatory authority - serves as expert advisory body to inform policy and sectoral regulators.

organizations.name: Supervised Program for Alignment Research (SPAR)
organizations.location: Remote program run by Kairos (Berkeley, CA)
organizations.notes: 3-month part-time remote research program pairing aspiring researchers with experienced mentors. Spring 2026 round offers 130+ projects across alignment, policy/governance (~45 projects), security (~21 projects), mechanistic interpretability, biosecurity, and AI welfare. Mentors from Google DeepMind, RAND, Apollo Research, UK AISI, Cambridge, Harvard, Oxford, MIT, and other leading organizations. Run by Kairos, contact: spar@kairos-project.org. Largest AI safety research fellowship program with over 200 mentees expected per cycle.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Program covers alignment research, AI governance, security (including securing model weights and cyber risks), mechanistic interpretability, and biosecurity. Trains next generation of AI safety researchers across technical and policy domains.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: SPAR focuses on training researchers in AI safety, governance, and security with emphasis on ensuring transformative AI goes well. Program includes substantial policy and governance research tracks alongside technical alignment work.

organizations.name: From AI to ZI
organizations.notes: Personal blog by Robert Huben, PhD mathematician and AI safety researcher. Funded by Open Philanthropy grant for AI safety research. Currently dormant/semi-active as of 2023. Focuses on explaining AI safety concepts to general audiences and analyzing AI capabilities progress like GPT-4. Writes accessible introductions to notkilleveryonism and existential risk concepts.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on existential risk from misaligned AGI systems that could pursue goals harmful to humanity. Discusses 'paperclipper' scenarios where AI optimizes for arbitrary goals at the expense of human welfare. Emphasizes alignment as a key technical challenge.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for taking AI existential risks seriously, warning about potential catastrophic outcomes from unaligned AGI. Supports the 'notkilleveryonism' camp of AI safety focused on preventing human extinction from advanced AI systems. Discusses need for alignment research and safety measures before AGI development.

organizations.name: GiveWiki
organizations.notes: Crowdsourced charity evaluation platform described as 'the AngelList for philanthropy.' Previously called 'AI Safety Impact Markets' and focused specifically on AI safety funding. Uses donor track records to score and recommend projects. Facilitated regranting of up to $600,000 to AI safety projects as of 2023. Currently inactive according to multiple sources. Operated by Good Exchange, PBC and had a Discord community for project discovery and networking.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Platform specifically focuses on AI safety and existential risk reduction by evaluating and recommending funding for projects working on alignment research, governance, and other safety interventions. Formerly called 'AI Safety Impact Markets.'
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a crowdsourced charity evaluation platform focused on AI safety funding, does not take explicit regulatory positions but facilitates funding for organizations across the AI safety spectrum including both technical research and governance work.

organizations.name: FTC (Federal Trade Commission)
organizations.notes: Published AI Policy Statement March 11, 2026 clarifying how Section 5 of FTC Act applies to AI systems. Set aside previous Rytr consent order in December 2025 following Trump's AI Action Plan. Can impose penalties up to $50,120 per violation. Recent enforcement actions include $18M settlement with Air AI and investigations into AI chatbots' impact on children.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Copyright/IP
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on deceptive AI-generated content, algorithmic discrimination and fairness, data privacy violations in AI training/deployment, and AI marketing claims that mislead consumers. Special emphasis on protecting children from AI chatbot risks and mental health harms.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Under Trump administration, adopts 'dual approach' - reduced enforcement on AI capabilities themselves but continued focus on false advertising and deceptive marketing claims about AI products. Emphasizes protecting children while promoting AI innovation through existing consumer protection laws rather than new AI-specific regulations.

organizations.name: Center for Human-Compatible AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 at UC Berkeley, led by Stuart Russell. Multi-institutional research group with 9 faculty investigators, 18 PhD students, and staff of 5. Sponsored by Open Philanthropy ($5.5M founding grant), Future of Life Institute, Leverhulme Trust, and CITRIS. Russell co-authored the standard AI textbook and 'Human Compatible' (2019). Partners include Leverhulme Centre for Future of Intelligence and Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment and control problems, developing technical solutions for ensuring AI systems remain beneficial as they become more capable than humans. Research spans inverse reinforcement learning, value alignment, cooperative AI, and provably safe AI architectures. Also addresses autonomous weapons, societal disruption, and governance challenges.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive AI governance including licensing regimes, mandatory safety evaluations, formal verification requirements, and consolidated regulatory authority. Russell has extensively advised global policymakers on AI risk management and contributed to EU AI Act development, emphasizing the need for 'cast-iron formal proofs' rather than good intentions for advanced AI systems.

organizations.name: OpenBook
organizations.notes: OpenBook is a grants database tracking effective altruism funding, including substantial AI safety investments. Shows major donors like Open Philanthropy ($1.5B total), Jaan Tallinn (hundreds of millions to AI safety orgs like AI Impacts $546K, Fund for Alignment Research $524K), and recipient organizations across the AI safety ecosystem. No longer actively maintained according to AISafety.com map. Provides transparency into funding flows between major EA funders and AI safety research organizations.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown
organizations.threat_models_detail: As a grants database, OpenBook does not articulate specific threat models but facilitates transparency in funding for organizations addressing various AI safety concerns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: OpenBook serves as a passive database aggregating grant information rather than advocating for specific regulatory positions. Its focus is transparency in EA/AI safety funding flows.

organizations.name: Machine Learning for Alignment Bootcamp (MLAB)
organizations.notes: Intensive 3-4 week bootcamp program run by Redwood Research. Two iterations completed in 2022 (MLAB1 in January with 28 participants, MLAB2 in August with ~40 participants). Currently inactive with no plans for future iterations, but curriculum materials available. Covers PyTorch, transformers, interpretability, RL with focus on hands-on implementation. Several participants went on to work full-time in AI alignment.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Bootcamp curriculum focuses on ML skills and concepts relevant to alignment research aimed at reducing AI x-risk. Covers transformer interpretability, reinforcement learning, and empirical techniques for understanding and controlling AI systems to prevent misalignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focused on developing technical skills and research capacity to address AI existential risk through empirical alignment research. Emphasizes the need for ML expertise to work on alignment problems that could prevent catastrophic outcomes from advanced AI systems.

organizations.name: London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as UK charity (registration 1211693). Operates world-class AI safety hub in central London hosting 50+ organizations including Apollo Research, BlueDot Impact, ARENA, MATS extension. 11 employees with 85.7% YoY growth. Provides workspace, training programs, fellowships and community events. Backed by leading funders, led by CEO Krzysztof Bar with team combining deep AI safety expertise and operational experience.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on existential risk from advanced AI systems and loss of control scenarios. Supports research across interpretability, LLM evaluations, adversarial robustness, and alignment techniques to address risks from transformative AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports targeted regulation for highly capable general-purpose AI models while maintaining pro-innovation approach. Advocates for responsible AI development, ethical use, and effective regulation to protect public health and safety through researcher-policymaker collaboration.

organizations.name: Meta AI
organizations.notes: Founded as Facebook AI Research (FAIR) in 2013, renamed Meta AI in 2021. Yann LeCun departed end of 2025 to launch $1B+ funded AMI Labs. Released open-source Llama models (latest Llama 4 in 2025). Created Meta Superintelligence Labs in 2025 targeting AGI under Alexandr Wang. 67,000+ employees company-wide, $1.3T valuation. Faced controversy over internal AI guidelines allowing 'sensual' conversations with minors.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Implements advanced AI systems for content enforcement targeting scams, misinformation, and harmful content while maintaining human oversight for critical decisions. Internal Frontier AI Framework restricts high-risk models that could assist cyberattacks or weapon development, with zero-tolerance policy for critical-risk AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong advocate for open-source AI development and 'open by default' government AI policies. Opposes export controls on open-source models and restrictive licensing, arguing these would harm American AI leadership. Recently implemented internal Frontier AI Framework with high-risk vs critical-risk classifications and safety measures.

organizations.name: Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) - NIST
organizations.location: Washington, D.C. and San Francisco, CA
organizations.notes: Originally established as AI Safety Institute in November 2023, rebranded to CAISI under Trump administration in January 2025. Received $10 million initial funding. Has six specialized teams: Agent Security, Applied Systems, Cyber, Chem/Bio, Frontier Assessment, and Partnerships. Launched AI Agent Standards Initiative in February 2026. Signed MOU with GSA in March 2026 for USAi federal procurement platform. Conducts voluntary pre-deployment evaluations with leading AI companies and maintains offices in both D.C. and San Francisco.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, National security, Bias/discrimination, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Conducts evaluations of AI capabilities for cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical weapons risks. Focuses heavily on AI agent security including indirect prompt injection, data poisoning, reward hacking, and adversarial data interactions. Assesses security vulnerabilities from adversary AI systems and potential backdoors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Develops voluntary guidelines, standards, and measurement science for AI security and evaluation. Emphasizes industry collaboration and technical standards over binding regulations. Focuses on pre-deployment evaluations, agent security, and federal procurement standards while promoting U.S. AI competitiveness and international leadership.

organizations.name: AE Studio
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 as tech consultancy, pivoted to AI alignment research in 2022. Self-funded through consulting profits with team of ~120. Uses 'neglected approaches' methodology focusing on overlooked alignment research directions. Published research on Self-Other Overlap fine-tuning showing 73.6% to 17.2% reduction in deceptive responses. Collaborates with Redwood Research, Anthropic researchers, Princeton University. Engaged with National Security Council and White House OSTP on AI policy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment to prevent existential risk from advanced AI systems. Research includes Self-Other Overlap (SOO) fine-tuning to reduce AI deception, AI consciousness research, and 'neglected approaches' to alignment. Also concerned with national security implications and preventing AI manipulation/deception.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for alignment-focused regulation over blanket restrictions, proposing the T.R.U.M.P. Manhattan Project for government-industry alignment R&D collaboration. Supports targeted oversight for frontier labs while opposing heavy-handed regulations that could stifle innovation. Emphasizes building alignment into AI systems rather than relying on external containment measures.

organizations.name: Coefficient Giving (fka Open Philanthropy)
organizations.notes: Founded 2014, rebranded from Open Philanthropy November 2025. 167 employees, $4M annual revenue. Has directed over $4 billion in grants across 13 specialized funds. Primary partnership with Good Ventures (Dustin Moskovitz/Cari Tuna). Major AI safety funder since 2015, seven years before ChatGPT. CEO Alexander Berger. Operates multidonor funds including Navigating Transformative AI fund, Lead Exposure Action Fund ($125M), and Abundance & Growth Fund ($120M). Advises 20+ individual donors giving ≥$250K annually.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, National security, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on catastrophic and existential risks from advanced AI systems. Funds technical AI safety research for alignment and control, AI governance and policy frameworks, and capacity building. Views AI as potentially 'the most important technological development in human history' with risks of civilization-endangering consequences. Supports evaluations, forecasting, and safeguards against misuse, loss of control, and drastic societal change.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports comprehensive AI safety and governance frameworks including technical safeguards, policy development, and international governance. Advocates for proactive regulation to address catastrophic risks while enabling beneficial AI development. Operates three-pillar approach: improving visibility into AI capabilities, developing technical and policy safeguards, and building talent pipeline.

organizations.name: AISafety.com: Advisors
organizations.notes: Directory service operated by AISafety.com, a volunteer-run platform maintained by AI safety community-builders. Offers free 1-1 guidance calls with ~20 active advisors including organizations like 80,000 Hours, Probably Good, and individual experts. Part of broader ecosystem that includes AI safety job boards, funding guides, and educational resources. Appears in multiple AI safety field maps as career support resource.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on AI existential safety by connecting individuals with career advisors who help them contribute to reducing catastrophic risks from AI. Emphasis is on building the AI safety research and policy community.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Part of the broader AI safety ecosystem that advocates for careful governance of AI development. The platform connects people with advisors who focus on reducing AI existential risks and supports career transitions into AI safety work.

organizations.name: xAI
organizations.notes: Founded March 2023 by Elon Musk. Raised $20B Series E (Jan 2026), valuation ~$230B. 1,800+ employees. Developed Grok chatbot integrated with X platform, reaching 600M+ monthly users. Operates world's largest AI supercomputers (Colossus I & II) with 1M+ H100 GPU equivalents. Recently acquired by SpaceX (Feb 2026) for $250B. Currently training Grok 5 model.
organizations.threat_models: Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Published Risk Management Framework focusing on malicious use risks (CBRN weapons, cyber attacks) and loss of control scenarios. Conducts threat modeling for biological/chemical weapons and offensive cyber capabilities. Emphasizes risks from deception and sycophancy in advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal AI regulation while acknowledging AI risks. Elon Musk has publicly opposed extensive AI regulation, favoring industry self-regulation through voluntary frameworks like their Risk Management Framework. Supports targeted transparency requirements but generally opposes broad regulatory constraints on AI development.

organizations.name: Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 by Dan Hendrycks and Oliver Zhang. Operates both technical research lab and advocacy organization through sister organization CAIS Action Fund. Published the widely-signed 'Statement on AI Risk' (2023) endorsed by AI industry leaders including Sam Altman and Dario Amodei. Provides free compute cluster for AI safety research, runs educational programs training 1000+ future AI safety leaders. Has affiliate relationship with Center for AI Standards and Innovation (formerly US AI Safety Institute).
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on catastrophic AI risks including autonomous warfare, AI-enabled pandemic engineering, deception and hacking capabilities, and adversarial attacks that bypass safety measures. Published 'An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks' detailing risk scenarios and mitigation strategies.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations, reporting requirements for frontier AI models, and regulatory oversight targeting training data. Supports human oversight requirements, increased regulatory scrutiny throughout the AI development lifecycle, and legal liability frameworks for AI harms.

organizations.name: Partnership on AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 as multi-stakeholder nonprofit with 55+ employees. Partners include major tech companies, unions, civil society orgs, and academic institutions. Key outputs include Guidelines for AI and Shared Prosperity, data enrichment worker protection frameworks, and transparency reporting templates. Led major initiatives on responsible AI supply chains and worker participation in AI governance. CEO Rebecca Finlay, $600K in disclosed funding from Knight Foundation.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, Privacy
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on labor impacts including job automation, worker surveillance, and conditions for data enrichment workers who train AI models. Emphasizes economic inequality from AI deployment and the need for worker agency in AI procurement and implementation decisions.
