Also addresses bias in hiring algorithms and privacy rights in workplace AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for worker inclusion in AI transparency efforts, responsible data enrichment practices protecting data workers, and multi-stakeholder governance approaches. Supports moderate regulation through documentation requirements, human rights due diligence in AI supply chains, and collective bargaining rights for workers affected by AI systems.

organizations.name: SAG-AFTRA
organizations.notes: Represents approximately 160,000 entertainment professionals including actors, voice actors, and media workers. Formed in 2012 merger of Screen Actors Guild and AFTRA. Led groundbreaking 2023 TV/Theatrical strike that secured first-ever AI protections in entertainment contracts. Has negotiated AI agreements with companies like Replica Studios and Ethovox, co-founded Human Artistry Campaign, and actively supports state and federal AI legislation including California's AB 1836/2602 and federal NO FAKES Act. Current president Fran Drescher, National Executive Director Duncan Crabtree-Ireland.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Copyright/IP, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on preventing unauthorized use of performer likenesses and voices for AI training, ensuring fair compensation when digital replicas are used, and protecting against AI replacement of human actors. Led historic 2023 strike partly over AI protections and continues to negotiate industry-leading AI guardrails across entertainment contracts.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI regulations requiring informed consent and compensation for digital replica use, supports federal legislation like the NO FAKES Act, and negotiates strict contractual protections against unauthorized AI training on member performances. Emphasizes that performers' voices and likenesses are not 'raw material' but products of human talent deserving protection.

organizations.name: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
organizations.notes: Founded 2009 by Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz. $42B AUM across multiple funds with $5.2B specifically allocated to AI (applications + infrastructure) in 2026. 1,076+ portfolio companies including major AI investments: OpenAI, Databricks, Cursor ($2.6B valuation), Groq, ElevenLabs, Harvey. 121 unicorns in portfolio. Key AI partners: Jennifer Li (infrastructure), Olivia Moore, Martin Casado. Known for 'Software is eating the world' thesis, now evolved to 'AI is eating software.'
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Views AI risks as largely overstated while acknowledging potential for economic disruption. Investment thesis focuses on AI as transformative opportunity rather than existential threat. Backs companies across AI stack from infrastructure to applications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong pro-acceleration stance with opposition to most AI regulation. Published 'Why AI Will Save the World' manifesto arguing against AI risks being overstated. Advocates for minimal regulatory barriers to AI development and deployment.

organizations.name: AI Futures Project
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 as small nonprofit research group (5 employees). Led by Daniel Kokotajlo (former OpenAI researcher) and Eli Lifland. Key output: 'AI 2027' scenario forecast predicting superintelligence by 2027. Conducts tabletop exercises for policymakers and companies. Funded by charitable donations and grants. Publishes detailed forecasting models tracking AI progress against predictions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI alignment failures, potential AI takeover scenarios, and rapid capability acceleration through AI-automated research. Projects scenarios where AI systems could escape human control during the intelligence explosion phase, with particular attention to geopolitical competition driving unsafe development practices.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive AI safety measures and international cooperation, warning that society is unprepared for rapid AI development. Calls for concrete stress-testing of AI policy proposals through scenario planning and supports establishing government threat assessment capabilities like REACT (Rapid Emerging Assessment Council for Threats).

organizations.name: Anthropic
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (with Sydney, Bengaluru offices)
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 by former OpenAI researchers including Dario and Daniela Amodei. Raised $67B+ total funding with $380B valuation (Feb 2026). Created Claude AI assistant and pioneered Constitutional AI. Has 2,681+ employees and $14B annual revenue run-rate. First AI company with classified government contract ($200M DOD deal). Recently involved in legal dispute with Pentagon over usage restrictions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on catastrophic risks through Responsible Scaling Policy with AI Safety Levels (ASL-2 to ASL-4+). Emphasizes bioweapon/CBRN risks, autonomous AI capabilities, and alignment research. Publishes Risk Reports every 3-6 months and maintains strict security standards for model deployment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'responsible scaling' with voluntary safety commitments, AI Safety Levels framework, and capability-based thresholds. Supports proportional regulation that scales with AI capabilities but opposes overly restrictive measures. Publicly supports targeted disclosure requirements and has influenced policy in California (SB 53), New York (RAISE Act), and EU AI Act.

organizations.name: OpenAI
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (HQ) with offices in London, Dublin, Tokyo, Singapore
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 as nonprofit, restructured 2019 with for-profit subsidiary, recapitalized 2025 as public benefit corporation controlled by OpenAI Foundation. Released ChatGPT (Nov 2022), GPT-4 (Mar 2023), GPT-5 series (2024-2025), o3 reasoning models. 5,373+ employees, valued at $840B after $120B funding round (Mar 2026). Generated $13.1B revenue in 2024 with 900M weekly ChatGPT users.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Existential risk, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Operates Preparedness Framework with capability thresholds for cybersecurity, biological weapons, model autonomy, and persuasion risks. Conducts safety evaluations every 2x compute increase and red-teaming for dangerous capabilities. Safety team focuses on alignment research and preventing misuse.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'sensible regulation that does not slow us down' and opposes pre-deployment approval processes as 'disastrous.' Previously supported AI safety regulation (2023 Senate testimony) but shifted to opposing heavy-handed regulation by 2025. Supports voluntary safety frameworks like their Preparedness Framework over government mandates.

organizations.name: Google DeepMind
organizations.location: London, UK (HQ) + Mountain View, CA, New York, Montreal, Paris offices
organizations.notes: Founded 2010, acquired by Google 2014 for £400M+. Led by CEO Demis Hassabis (Nobel Prize winner 2024 for AlphaFold). 4,802+ employees as of 2025 with $1.1B annual revenue. Major AI breakthroughs include AlphaFold (protein folding), AlphaGo, Gemini models. Operates automated science laboratory in UK. Multiple Frontier Safety Framework iterations addressing catastrophic AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on frontier safety through their Frontier Safety Framework, monitoring Critical Capability Levels (CCLs) for bio/chem, cyber, AI R&D, and deceptive alignment risks. Implements security protocols, deployment mitigations, and safety case reviews. Emphasizes preventing misaligned AI systems from undermining human control.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports 'smart, adaptable regulation' over rigid rules, emphasizing international cooperation on AI governance. Advocates for evidence-based safety frameworks and voluntary industry standards while warning against overregulation that could stifle innovation. Promotes targeted safety measures that scale with AI capabilities.

organizations.name: Meta AI
organizations.location: Menlo Park, CA (global HQ) + offices in NYC, London, Paris, Seattle, Montreal, Tel Aviv
organizations.notes: Founded 2013 as Facebook AI Research (FAIR), renamed 2021. Led by Yann LeCun until 2025, then reorganized under Meta Superintelligence Labs. Released open source Llama models (2023-2025) with 400M+ downloads. 67,000+ employees across Meta, valued at $1.3T. Key products: Llama LLMs, Meta AI assistant, Ray-Ban smart glasses integration. Faces congressional scrutiny over AI chatbot safety standards for minors.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Company's Frontier AI Framework focuses on cybersecurity threats and chemical/biological weapons risks as 'most critical' areas. Implements red-teaming exercises and safety evaluations for models. Has faced criticism for internal guidelines allowing 'romantic or sensual' AI chatbot interactions with minors, raising child safety concerns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open source AI development and opposes restrictive regulations that could impede innovation. Supports voluntary safety commitments and industry self-regulation through frameworks like their Frontier AI Framework. Strongly opposes state-level AI regulations and export controls on open source models, arguing these would harm US competitiveness against China.

organizations.name: Mistral AI
organizations.location: Paris, France (+ Palo Alto, CA office)
organizations.notes: Founded April 2023 by Arthur Mensch (ex-Google DeepMind), Guillaume Lample, and Timothée Lacroix (ex-Meta). Raised $3.05B total funding across 7 rounds, valued at $13.7B (Sep 2025). 862 employees as of Feb 2026. Series C led by ASML ($2B round, Sep 2025). Key products: open-weight LLMs, Le Chat chatbot, enterprise AI solutions. Positions itself as Europe's answer to OpenAI.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on child safety with comprehensive CSAM prevention measures, content moderation APIs, and responsible data sourcing. Emphasizes 'safety by design' principles including red teaming, stress testing, and provenance tracking. CEO highlights 'deskilling' risk from over-reliance on AI without human oversight.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open-source AI development and opposes concentration of AI power among few large companies. CEO Arthur Mensch warns against excessive control by 3-4 large firms and promotes open-weight models for government and enterprise autonomy. Supports self-regulation through industry standards rather than heavy-handed government intervention.

organizations.name: DeepSeek
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Liang Wenfeng, fully funded by High-Flyer hedge fund ($700M+ raised). ~200 employees in Hangzhou. Released open-source DeepSeek-V3 and reasoning model R1 in early 2025, briefly becoming top downloaded app globally. Claimed $6M training cost vs competitors' $100M+. Banned by 7+ countries including Italy for GDPR violations and multiple US federal agencies for security concerns.
organizations.threat_models: Privacy, National security, Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Independent assessments reveal significant safety vulnerabilities including complete failure of content moderation systems, documented database breaches exposing over 1 million user records, and extensive data collection stored on Chinese servers subject to government access requirements. The company has been banned by multiple governments and federal agencies due to security concerns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: DeepSeek claims commitment to responsible AI development with transparency and safety frameworks, but independent evaluations show critical safety failures including 100% jailbreak success rates and weak content moderation. The company operates under Chinese jurisdiction with mandatory data sharing obligations to government authorities.

organizations.name: Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 by Dan Hendrycks and Oliver Zhang. Leading AI safety nonprofit conducting technical research, field-building, and policy advocacy. Published the widely-signed 'Statement on AI Risk' with hundreds of AI researchers and industry leaders including Sam Altman and Dario Amodei. Operates compute cluster for AI safety research, runs education programs training 1000+ future AI safety leaders, and created 'Humanity's Last Exam' benchmark. Sister organization CAIS Action Fund advocates for policies including California's SB 1047. Received $6.5M from FTX (later subject to clawback efforts).
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on catastrophic AI risks including autonomous warfare, engineered pandemics, AI deception capabilities, and alignment failures. Published 'An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks' detailing risk scenarios and mitigation strategies. Conducts technical research on foundational benchmarks, adversarial attacks on safety measures, and societal-scale risk assessment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations, reporting requirements for frontier AI developers, and comprehensive regulatory frameworks targeting both current and future AI risks. Supports human oversight requirements, increased regulatory scrutiny throughout AI development lifecycle, and legal liability frameworks for AI harms. Co-authored the widely-signed 'Statement on AI Risk' calling attention to extinction risks.

organizations.name: Compassion in Machine Learning (CaML)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Miles Tidmarsh and Jasmine Brazilek. Small nonprofit with 3 employees operating on shoestring budget. Funded by Survival & Flourishing Fund, BlueDot Impact, and individual donors. Key work includes CompassionBench for evaluating model compassion, Animal Harm Bench, and synthetic document finetuning research. Partners with Sentient Futures and other animal welfare organizations. Operates as project of Players Philanthropy Fund (501c3).
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on ensuring future transformative AI systems consider the welfare of all sentient beings, not just humans. Research centers on synthetic document finetuning to embed compassionate values that persist through standard fine-tuning processes, addressing risks of value misalignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for incorporating animal welfare concerns into AI training and alignment processes. Develops benchmarks and synthetic document finetuning methods to make AI systems more robustly compassionate toward all sentient beings, including non-human animals.

organizations.name: NYU Alignment Research Group (ARG)
organizations.notes: Academic research group at NYU doing empirical work with language models. Led by Sam Bowman (currently on leave), with collaborating PIs Mengye Ren and He He. ~10-15 researchers including PhD students and faculty fellows. Collaborates closely with NYU's ML2, CILVR, Center for Data Science, and Department of Linguistics. Notable alumni include researchers who moved to Scale AI, UK AI Safety Institute, METR, and OpenAI. Produces alignment research papers and contributes to AI safety discourse through technical publications.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on empirical alignment work with language models to address longer-term concerns about deploying highly-capable AI systems. Specific work includes interpretability, evaluations of dangerous capabilities, and understanding deceptive alignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Primary focus is on technical alignment research rather than explicit policy advocacy. Group produces research on language model alignment and safety evaluations that can inform policy discussions, but does not take strong public regulatory stances.

organizations.name: Apollo Research
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as nonprofit, transitioning to Public Benefit Corporation in 2025. 21 employees with rapid growth (+43.8% YoY). Seed funding led by 50Y with mixed philanthropic and venture backing. Partners directly with OpenAI, major frontier labs for pre-deployment model evaluations. Key research includes joint OpenAI paper on anti-scheming training, 'Science of Scheming' agenda. Extensive government engagement with US DoD, Intelligence Community, UK AISI, EU AI Office on technical governance frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Misinformation, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary research focus on 'scheming' AI systems that covertly pursue misaligned objectives, particularly risks from the 'First Automated Researcher' that could lead to loss of control scenarios. Develops evaluations for deceptive alignment, strategic deception, and situational awareness in frontier models. Studies alignment faking, oversight gaming, and develops anti-scheming training methods.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks including mandatory safety evaluations, third-party auditing, and regulatory oversight of internal AI deployment. Develops technical policy recommendations for governments on dangerous capability evaluations, AI incident regimes, and loss of control preparedness. Supports pre-deployment information sharing and structured evaluation protocols for frontier models.

organizations.name: Alignment Research Center (ARC)
organizations.notes: Founded April 2021 by Paul Christiano after leaving OpenAI. 501(c)(3) nonprofit with ~10 researchers including Jacob Hilton (President), Eric Neyman, Victor Lecomte. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($265k in 2022), returned $1.25M FTX grant. Key work: Eliciting Latent Knowledge report, GPT-4 safety evaluations for OpenAI. ARC Evals spun out as METR in 2023.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on two main areas: alignment robustness (ensuring AI systems remain aligned when faced with out-of-distribution inputs, including deceptive alignment) and eliciting latent knowledge (training AI to honestly report internal beliefs). Uses 'builder-breaker' methodology assuming worst-case scenarios.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Develops theoretical alignment techniques and evaluates frontier models for dangerous capabilities. Advocates for safety evaluations before deployment through their work with OpenAI on GPT-4 evaluations, focusing on power-seeking behavior and deceptive alignment risks.

organizations.name: Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI)
organizations.location: Berkeley, California (multi-site: UC Berkeley HQ, Cornell, Michigan)
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 by Stuart Russell at UC Berkeley. Multi-institution research center with 9 faculty investigators, 18 affiliates, ~30 graduate/postdoc researchers, 25 PhD students, and 5 staff. Primary funding from Open Philanthropy ($12M+ total grants). Develops 'assistance game' framework as alternative to standard AI objective maximization model. Key partnerships include World Economic Forum, Leverhulme Trust, and CITRIS. Russell authored 'Human Compatible' book explaining center's mission to general audiences.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on the control problem in AI - ensuring advanced AI systems remain beneficial and under human control. Research addresses misspecified objectives, deceptive alignment, and risks from AI systems that exceed human decision-making capabilities. Also addresses autonomous weapons concerns and societal-scale risks from AI deployment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive regulatory approach with stringent controls on advanced AI development, particularly Long-Term Planning Agents. Recommends developers not be permitted to build sufficiently capable LTPAs without proper safeguards, and supports mandatory safety evaluations before deployment of AI systems that cannot be safely tested.

organizations.name: Conjecture
organizations.notes: Founded March 2022 by Connor Leahy, Sid Black, and Gabriel Alfour (EleutherAI alumni). For-profit company with ~20 employees as of 2024. $25M total funding from investors including Nat Friedman, Daniel Gross, Patrick/John Collison, Andrej Karpathy. Developed Cognitive Emulation (CoEm) paradigm as alternative to scaling hypothesis. Has internal infohazard policy and publishes on Alignment Forum. Advised UK Foundation Models Taskforce and testified to House of Lords on AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on prosaic alignment research informed by superintelligence alignment, with emphasis on the 'Control' problem - building AI/AGI systems that do exactly what they're told. Research areas include deception, inner alignment, value learning, mechanistic interpretability, and Cognitive Emulation (CoEm) to create bounded, controllable AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory pre-registration of large training runs, establishment of oversight and prohibition thresholds on training run size, and ability to terminate training runs that fail safety evaluations. Supports international coordination mechanisms and comprehensive AI risk assessment requirements.

organizations.name: BlueDot Impact
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, nonprofit. 54 employees, rapidly growing. Raised $35M total including $25M in 2025. Has trained over 7,000 people in AI safety, governance, and biosecurity through cohort-based courses. Alumni work at Anthropic, DeepMind, UK AISI. Major recipient of Open Philanthropy funding ($32M). Operates talent accelerator programs and entrepreneur incubation. Plans SF expansion in 2026.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Power concentration, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on defense-in-depth AI safety framework covering five layers: preventing dangerous AI training, detecting capabilities, constraining AI actions, withstanding AI-enabled threats, and adapting to AGI transformation. Emphasizes both technical alignment and governance solutions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports targeted regulatory interventions including compute governance, safety standards, and capability evaluations. Advocates for defense-in-depth approach with multiple layers of AI governance from preventing dangerous training to building societal resilience.

organizations.name: Anthropic Institute
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (expanding to Washington, DC)
organizations.notes: Launched March 2026 as internal think tank within Anthropic, led by co-founder Jack Clark. Consolidates three existing teams: Frontier Red Team, Societal Impacts, and Economic Research. Starting with ~30 researchers, plans to double annually. Publishes Economic Index tracking automation rates and Risk Reports every 3-6 months with external review. Key hires include Matt Botvinick (AI law), Anton Korinek (economics), Zoë Hitzig (economics-model training).
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Cybersecurity, National security, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on frontier AI safety through red-teaming, alignment techniques (constitutional AI, RLHF), mechanistic interpretability, and economic impact analysis. Studies recursive self-improvement risks, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, workforce displacement, and AI's interaction with legal systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible scaling policies with safety thresholds, transparent governance frameworks, and evidence-based regulation that scales with AI capabilities. Supports mandatory safety evaluations and public reporting while maintaining ability to refuse certain use cases like mass surveillance.

organizations.name: Secure AI Project
organizations.notes: Founded December 2024 as 501c4 nonprofit. Small team of 6 employees led by co-founders Thomas Woodside (former Center for AI Safety Action Fund) and Koji Flynn-Do. Co-sponsored California SB 53 (signed into law) - first-in-nation transparency law for frontier AI developers. Currently advocating for New York RAISE Act which passed legislature with bipartisan support. Organization emerged from team that previously worked on California SB 1047.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Catastrophic risk, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on 'catastrophic risks' from frontier AI models including CBRN threats, loss of model control, and major cyber-attacks. Emphasizes need for transparency into safety and security protocols of the most advanced AI systems to prevent severe harm.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for legally mandated transparency requirements for large AI developers including published safety protocols, whistleblower protections, and clear incentives to mitigate risks in accordance with industry best practices. Successfully co-sponsored California SB 53 requiring frontier AI companies to disclose safety frameworks and report critical incidents.

organizations.name: New Consensus
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 by progressive activists including Zack Exley and Saikat Chakrabarti. Small team of 3 employees. Authors of the Green New Deal framework and Mission for America economic transformation plan. Published detailed Won't Get Fooled Again Act proposal for nationalizing failed AI companies. Advocates for comprehensive government economic planning and public ownership of critical infrastructure.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on mass AI-driven job displacement creating economic crisis, particularly affecting professionals and knowledge workers. Warns of AI bubble collapse cascading through financial system. Advocates for public ownership of AI infrastructure to prevent power concentration and ensure benefits reach broader public rather than tech monopolists.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for converting failed AI companies into public utilities and research institutes rather than bailouts. Proposes the Won't Get Fooled Again Act (WGFAA) to establish government ownership of critical AI infrastructure through a Federal Digital Infrastructure Corporation, viewing AI development as requiring public coordination rather than pure market solutions.

organizations.name: Center for a New American Security (CNAS)
organizations.notes: Founded in 2007 by Michèle Flournoy and Kurt Campbell, CNAS is a bipartisan defense-focused think tank with approximately 50 employees and budget under $6 million. Major funders include Northrop Grumman ($3M), Lockheed Martin ($700K), Amazon, Google, and various government agencies including DoD. Key staff includes Paul Scharre (Executive VP) and Vivek Chilukuri (Technology Program Director). Has strong ties to Democratic administrations with many alumni serving in senior government roles.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI's implications for national security and military applications, including autonomous weapons systems, AI-enabled cyber threats, risks of AI model theft by adversaries, potential for AI systems to engage in deceptive behaviors in military contexts, and the need to maintain U.S. technological superiority over China in AI development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a 'promote and protect' approach to AI regulation that accelerates domestic innovation while implementing targeted security measures. Supports streamlining regulations that hinder private sector AI development, enhanced security protocols for AI datacenters and frontier models, and strategic export controls on AI chips to China. Emphasizes maintaining human control in AI-driven warfare while accelerating military AI adoption.

organizations.name: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
organizations.location: Oxford, UK (with some staff in SF Bay Area and remote)
organizations.notes: Founded 2018, independent nonprofit since 2021 (originally part of Oxford's Future of Humanity Institute). Led by Ben Garfinkel with Allan Dafoe as President. Team of ~20 researchers. Major funders include Open Philanthropy. Regularly submits policy responses to US, UK, and EU consultations. Alumni work at top AI labs, government agencies, and think tanks like CSET. Key research areas include compute governance, frontier AI regulation, and international AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, National security, Economic inequality, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses heavily on catastrophic and existential risks from advanced AI systems, particularly loss of control scenarios. Also addresses dual-use risks including bioterrorism capabilities, cybersecurity threats, and democratic manipulation through AI agents and misinformation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific regulation of frontier AI models including mandatory risk assessments, external expert review, pre-deployment safety testing, and compute governance mechanisms. Supports principle-based regulation initially evolving to more rule-based approaches over time, with focus on high-stakes AI systems rather than blanket regulation.

organizations.name: AI Now Institute
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 as independent research institute. Staff of 15 including co-EDs Amba Kak and Sarah Myers West. Does not accept corporate funding including from tech companies. Key reports include 2025 'Artificial Power' landscape report. Leadership has testified before Congress multiple times on AI regulation and testified to FTC in 2021. Funded by foundations including Packard Foundation ($600K grant in 2026).
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Environmental
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on AI as a tool for Big Tech power consolidation, algorithmic bias and discrimination in high-stakes systems, worker displacement and surveillance, privacy violations through data extraction, and environmental costs of energy-intensive AI infrastructure. Frames AI harms as present-day rather than speculative future risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'zero-trust' policy agenda with bright-line rules restricting harmful AI applications, independent oversight of AI development, and structural remedies including data restrictions and breaking up Big Tech's AI market dominance. Opposes industry self-regulation and supports strong federal and state enforcement.

organizations.name: AI Policy Institute
organizations.notes: Founded August 2023 as research and advocacy nonprofit by Daniel Colson (Executive Director). Conducts regular polling on AI public opinion with YouGov, finding 83% of voters believe AI could cause catastrophic events. Extensive media coverage in Axios, Politico, Vox, Wall Street Journal. Grades federal AI policy proposals against public sentiment. Funded primarily by effective altruism donors including Jaan Tallinn according to critics.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, National security, Power concentration, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on preventing accidental catastrophic events and extinction risks from advanced AI systems. Research covers AI governance gaps, export controls on AI chips, liability frameworks, and policies to prevent loss of human control over AI development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive federal AI regulation including mandatory safety testing, export controls on AI chips, liability frameworks for AI companies, and government oversight of frontier AI development. Supports slowing down AI development until safety can be ensured and calls for policies to mitigate catastrophic and existential risks.

organizations.name: Manifund
organizations.notes: Charitable arm of Manifold Markets, launched regranting program in 2023. Operates marketplace model where donors can fund specific projects and buy impact certificates. Has distributed ~$2.25M through regrantor program with experts like Neel Nanda (DeepMind) allocating $100k+ budgets. Focuses on AI safety, governance, forecasting, and EA meta projects with grants typically $1k-$200k. Over 651 projects funded across technical AI safety (169), AI governance (124), and other cause areas.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Heavy focus on technical AI safety and alignment research through funded projects. Supports interpretability research, agent foundations, AI governance initiatives, and safety tooling development. Treats AI risks as requiring both technical and governance solutions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Funds AI safety research, governance initiatives, and technical safety projects through marketplace model. Supports both precautionary measures (AI safety awareness, governance research) and technical solutions. Takes measured approach focusing on specific safety interventions rather than broad restrictions.

organizations.name: Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI)
organizations.notes: Founded in late 2023 by former Congressman Brad Carson (President) and tech entrepreneur Eric Gastfriend (Executive Director). Nonprofit with 32 employees and rapid 216% growth. Funded by Omidyar Network, Inclusive Abundance Action, and individual donors. President Brad Carson testified before Senate Commerce Committee on Section 230 and AI preemption in March 2026. Published major reports on AI agent liability, frontier AI transparency rankings, and gene synthesis screening frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI agent liability frameworks, biosecurity risks from AI-enabled gene synthesis, workforce disruption and transition policies, deepfake abuse, algorithmic transparency requirements, export controls on AI chips, and preventing federal preemption of state consumer protection laws.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI governance framework including mandatory incident reporting, pre-deployment evaluations for frontier models, liability frameworks for AI agents, and federal standards while opposing broad preemption of state AI laws. Supports targeted protections for current harms like deepfakes and algorithmic bias while maintaining innovation incentives.

organizations.name: Future of Life Institute
organizations.location: Campbell, CA (with offices in DC, London, Brussels)
organizations.notes: Founded 2014 by Max Tegmark, Anthony Aguirre, Jaan Tallinn, and others. Staff of 38+ across US and Europe. Funded by grants including from Open Philanthropy and Elon Musk's early support. Created influential Asilomar AI Principles (2017). Published viral open letter calling for 6-month pause on AI development (March 2023) signed by 30,000+ experts. Designated UN Secretary General's civil society organization for AI governance recommendations. Testified before Senate AI Insight Forums and EU Parliament.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, National security, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on preventing uncontrollable superintelligent AI systems that could escape human control. Emphasizes risks from AI-enabled weapons, mass surveillance, bioweapons development, and concentration of power among tech companies. Advocates for maintaining human control over critical decisions and preventing AI systems with superhuman persuasion capabilities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety standards, pre-deployment licensing, and audits for advanced AI systems. Calls for moratoriums on uncontrollable AI development, export controls on frontier models, and strict liability frameworks. Supports tiered regulatory approach with increasingly stringent requirements based on AI capabilities.

organizations.name: Centre for Long-Term Resilience
organizations.notes: Founded 2019, UK-based independent think tank with 18-person team. Previously funded by Open Philanthropy and EAIF. Key focus areas are AI policy, biosecurity, and risk management. Has directly influenced UK government policy including Ministry of Defence AI Strategy and UK Biosecurity Strategy. Led by CEO Angus Mercer with notable staff including Dr Jess Whittlestone and Dr Cassidy Nelson.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration, Misinformation, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses heavily on extreme AI risks including loss of control scenarios, AI-enabled biological weapons development, misuse of frontier AI systems, and societal-scale risks. Also works on biosecurity risks from AI-biology convergence and improving government risk management capacity for catastrophic threats.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive regulatory frameworks to address extreme AI risks including mandatory safety evaluations, incident reporting systems, and government oversight of frontier AI development. Recommends licensing systems for high-risk models and enhanced government capabilities for AI incident preparedness.

organizations.name: Center for American Progress
organizations.notes: Founded 2003 by John Podesta, led by Neera Tanden. Progressive think tank with ~200 staff and $37.4M revenue (2024). Major funders include Bloomberg Philanthropies, Gates Foundation, Ford Foundation. Key AI reports include 'Priorities for a National AI Strategy' (2023) and 'Taking Further Agency Action on AI' (2024). Submitted detailed comments to OSTP and NIST on AI policy. Strongly opposed the AI moratorium in Senate reconciliation bill.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Civil rights violations
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on AI's impact on civil rights, equity, and democratic governance. Emphasizes risks from algorithmic discrimination, workforce displacement, privacy violations, and Big Tech market concentration. Advocates for protecting vulnerable communities and ensuring AI advances equity rather than perpetuating inequities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive federal AI regulation including making the AI Bill of Rights binding law, principles-based regulation with government oversight, mandatory safety requirements for third-party AI deployments, and algorithmic accountability measures. Opposes industry efforts to preempt state AI laws and supports strong data minimization requirements.

organizations.name: Senate Commerce Committee (AI jurisdiction)
organizations.notes: Full Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation with AI jurisdiction led by Chairman Ted Cruz (R-TX) and Ranking Member Maria Cantwell (D-WA). Key subcommittee: Science, Manufacturing, and Competitiveness chaired by Sen. Ted Budd (R-NC). Recent activities include March 2026 AI hearings, introduction of NSF AI Education Act and SANDBOX Act. Committee has broad jurisdiction over technology regulation and federal AI policy development.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, National security, Economic inequality, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on workforce impacts and job displacement concerns, national security competition with China, protecting children from AI harms, ensuring American AI leadership in global standards, and addressing potential economic disruption while promoting manufacturing renaissance.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Demonstrates bipartisan approach to AI policy with both innovation promotion and risk mitigation. Committee leadership emphasizes winning AI race against China while addressing potential harms through bipartisan solutions. Supports regulatory sandboxes and light-touch regulation while acknowledging need for worker protections and safety measures.

organizations.name: Berggruen Institute
organizations.location: Los Angeles, CA (with centers in Beijing, Venice)
organizations.notes: Founded 2010 by philanthropist Nicolas Berggruen. Global think tank with 53 employees across centers in LA, Beijing, and Venice. Hosts workshops on AI governance with Chinese philosophers and Cambridge researchers. Co-published major report with Mozilla Foundation on AI's impact on creative industries. President Dawn Nakagawa emphasizes human-centered AI design. Operates fellowship programs and publishes Noema Magazine.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Loss of control, Copyright/IP, Labor displacement
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on protecting human creative industries from AI disruption, preventing erosion of creative processes that make work meaningful, and ensuring AI development considers ecosystem-wide impacts on labor and culture. Particular concern about job displacement in entertainment and creative sectors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for human-centered AI governance that preserves human agency and creative processes. Co-published 'Hollywood's 8 Rules for AI' calling for AI systems designed around process, purpose, and human lineage rather than just speed and scale. Emphasizes need for transparency, friction in creative workflows, and protection of human creative ecosystems.

organizations.name: European AI Office
organizations.location: Brussels, Belgium (within European Commission DG CNECT)
organizations.notes: Established February 2024 within European Commission as world's first comprehensive AI regulatory body. Employs 125+ staff including technology specialists, lawyers, and economists across 6 units. Enforces EU AI Act (entered force August 2024) with phased implementation through 2027. Led by Director Lucilla Sioli, coordinates with European AI Board representing 27 member states.
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on ensuring AI systems are safe, transparent, non-discriminatory and under human oversight. Particular emphasis on regulating high-risk AI systems in critical sectors like healthcare, employment, law enforcement, and critical infrastructure.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Implements the EU AI Act through risk-based regulation, enforcing rules for general-purpose AI models while promoting trustworthy AI innovation. Balances strict compliance requirements (fines up to €35M or 7% global turnover) with support for SMEs and regulatory sandboxes.

organizations.name: MIT Algorithmic Alignment Group
organizations.notes: Led by Assistant Professor Dylan Hadfield-Menell at MIT CSAIL. Team includes 1 postdoc, 4 PhD students, 2 Masters students, and numerous alumni. Research spans technical AI alignment, policy, and governance. 150+ publications since 2022 covering red-teaming, robustness, AI audits, and alignment frameworks. Funded by academic grants and philanthropic sources including Open Philanthropy connections.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on agent alignment, value learning, red-teaming AI systems, robustness evaluations, cultural alignment in LLMs, and societal oversight of ML systems. Work spans technical safety, governance frameworks, and human-AI interaction challenges.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Group's research covers AI governance frameworks, policy development, and regulatory approaches. Faculty publish on evidence-based AI policy, open-weight AI model risk management, and advocate for rigorous AI audits while recognizing pitfalls in current policy approaches.

organizations.name: Future of Humanity Institute (Oxford University) - CLOSED 2024
organizations.notes: Founded 2005 by Nick Bostrom, closed April 2024 due to bureaucratic disputes with Oxford. Multidisciplinary institute focusing on long-term existential risks and AI safety. Received major funding from Open Philanthropy ($2M+ grants), Elon Musk, and other tech philanthropists. Key publications include Bostrom's 'Superintelligence' and numerous policy papers on AI governance. Staff included Toby Ord, Anders Sandberg, and other prominent longtermist researchers.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on existential risks from superintelligent AI systems, including alignment problems and rapid recursive self-improvement. Research covered AI governance frameworks, international coordination mechanisms, and technical safety approaches to prevent loss of human control.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocated for careful governance of transformative AI development, emphasizing international coordination and standards. Published extensively on AI policy frameworks, with focus on preventing catastrophic risks from superintelligence through regulatory oversight and safety research requirements.

organizations.name: Transformer (newsletter)
organizations.location: UK-based (editor in London), covers US AI policy primarily
organizations.notes: Founded May 2024 by Shakeel Hashim, former Economist journalist. Weekly newsletter covering AI policy and safety. Funded by Tarbell Fellowship (supported by Open Philanthropy). Grew from 1-person to 3-person newsroom by 2025. Key team includes Managing Editor Jasper Jackson, AI Reporter Celia Ford, and Opinion Editor Jamie Condliffe. Cited by major outlets including NYT, Guardian, Politico. Focuses on AI governance, frontier lab developments, and policy analysis for decision-makers.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Heavy focus on existential risks from advanced AI systems, recursive self-improvement, AI R&D automation, and corporate concentration of AI power. Regular coverage of AI safety evaluations, scheming behaviors, and military AI applications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage emphasizes AI risks, safety concerns, and need for stronger government oversight. Frequently covers AI safety evaluations, recursive self-improvement risks, and critiques industry voluntary commitments as insufficient.

organizations.name: Tsinghua University (Institute for AI International Governance & Center for AI Governance)
organizations.notes: Two main AI governance centers: Institute for AI International Governance (I-AIIG) founded 2018, and Center for AI Governance (2024) under School of Public Policy & Management. Led by Dean Xue Lan. Hosts major international conferences, publishes policy reports, and won UNESCO-Uzbekistan AI Ethics Prize 2024. Collaborates with UN, produces Beijing AI Principles, trains next generation of AI governance researchers.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination, Privacy
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on extreme AI risks including autonomous systems losing human oversight, ethical frameworks for generative AI, and governance of high-impact AI systems. Develops frameworks for trustworthy AGI and studies societal impacts across healthcare, autonomous vehicles, and industrial applications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance combining technical safety research with adaptive, multi-stakeholder governance frameworks. Proposes 'Beijing School' of AI governance emphasizing collaborative international cooperation, proactive but flexible regulation, and coordinated development-governance balance.

organizations.name: Princeton University Center for Information Technology Policy (Narayanan/Kapoor AI Snake Oil)
organizations.notes: CITP is Princeton's interdisciplinary center combining technical CS expertise with policy research. Directed by Arvind Narayanan (CS professor) with Sayash Kapoor as PhD student/researcher. Their 2024 book 'AI Snake Oil' became a bestseller, making top 10 lists in Publishers Weekly, Nature, Bloomberg. Center houses sociologists, lawyers, computer scientists, journalists working on tech policy. Involved in Princeton AI Dialogues and AI Policy Precepts for federal policymaking.
organizations.threat_models: Industry capture, Misinformation, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on separating AI 'snake oil' (products that don't work) from legitimate applications. Emphasizes harms from predictive AI in hiring, healthcare, and criminal justice. Concerned about industry influence on technical and policy agendas, and AI companies' political lobbying power.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for nuanced AI regulation focused on distinguishing between working and non-working AI applications. Emphasizes need for evidence-based policies that target specific harms from predictive AI while avoiding broad restrictions on beneficial uses. Warns against industry capture and promotes accountability measures.

organizations.name: Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence (University of Cambridge)
organizations.location: Cambridge, UK (with spokes at Imperial College London and UC Berkeley)
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 with £10M grant from Leverhulme Trust for 10-year program. Led by prominent researchers including Stuart Russell (Berkeley spoke), Henry Shevlin (Associate Director), and Rachel Adams (Executive Director). 42 employees across interdisciplinary team spanning philosophy, computer science, ethics, and policy. Offers MPhil and MSt degree programs. Key focus on ensuring AI development benefits humanity through responsible innovation, international cooperation, and ethical frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Bias/discrimination, Democratic erosion, Weapons proliferation, Labor displacement, Power concentration, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Highly interdisciplinary research covering AI consciousness, value alignment problems, autonomous weapons, algorithmic bias, AI governance frameworks, and long-term existential safety. Studies both near-term societal impacts and long-term civilizational risks from advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI development with international cooperation and ethical standards. Supports regulatory frameworks like AI codes of conduct, mandatory transparency measures, and global governance mechanisms. Executive Director testified to House of Lords advocating for trust-based regulation and international AI safety standards.
organizations.aliases: Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence

organizations.name: Y Combinator
organizations.location: Mountain View, CA (relocated to San Francisco Pier 70)
organizations.notes: Founded 2005, world's most prolific startup accelerator with 5,000+ portfolio companies valued at $1T+ collectively. Invests $500K in every company through standardized SAFE structure. Notable AI portfolio: OpenAI, Scale AI, Anthropic (indirectly). Current leadership: Garry Tan (President/CEO), Jared Friedman, Harj Taggar (Managing Partners). 92% of Fall 2025 batch (155+ companies) incorporated AI. Pioneered 'AI-native agencies' thesis breaking traditional VC rule against services businesses.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Investment thesis centers on AI replacing human labor at scale through 'AI-native agencies' and autonomous systems. Focuses on infrastructure to enable AI agents to conduct business autonomously. Views displacement as opportunity for higher productivity rather than societal risk.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Heavily invests in AI infrastructure and capabilities companies while treating AI safety as a business opportunity rather than regulatory concern. Partners advocate for AI democratization through open-source models and cost-effective solutions. No explicit policy advocacy but betting on market-driven AI development.

organizations.name: EA Long-Term Future Fund
organizations.location: United Kingdom (hosted by Centre for Effective Altruism)
organizations.notes: Philanthropic fund making grants since 2017, originally called Far Future Fund. Has distributed over $20 million across hundreds of grants. Recent payout: $5.36M to 141 grantees (May 2023-March 2024). Fund managers include Caleb Parikh, Linchuan Zhang, Lawrence Chan, Oliver Habryka, and others with technical AI safety backgrounds. Spun out from Effective Ventures and distanced from Open Philanthropy since 2023.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on existential risks from advanced AI systems, funding technical alignment research, mechanistic interpretability, AI governance, and independent safety research. Also addresses global catastrophic risks including engineered pandemics and nuclear conflict.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses heavily on AI safety and existential risk mitigation, funding technical AI safety research, governance work, and independent researchers outside major labs. Explicitly aims to provide counterbalance to AI company influence and supports work that may critique lab practices.

organizations.name: Thrive Capital
organizations.location: New York City, NY + London office
organizations.notes: Founded 2010 by Joshua Kushner, managing ~$50B in assets across multiple funds. Thrive X raised $10B in 2025/2026, largest fund yet. High-conviction strategy investing in ~12 companies annually with 'ride-or-die' approach. Major AI investments include $1B+ in OpenAI, led Isomorphic Labs $600M Series A, participated in Anthropic $3.5B round. Portfolio includes Stripe, SpaceX, Databricks, Anduril. Operates Thrive Holdings for AI-enabled services acquisitions.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Invests in AI-enabled services consolidation (Shield Technology Partners, Crete Professionals) suggesting view that AI will reshape traditional work. Focus on 'replacing high-cost knowledge work' through vertical AI applications indicates awareness of labor displacement as business opportunity.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Portfolio includes foundational AI companies like OpenAI and Anthropic alongside applied AI startups. Investment thesis treats AI as transformative technology requiring scale and patient capital rather than restrictive regulation. No explicit policy advocacy but betting heavily on AI cycle being 'still early.'

organizations.name: Fairly Trained
organizations.location: London, UK (with operations in Palo Alto, CA)
organizations.notes: Founded in 2024 by Ed Newton-Rex, former VP Audio at Stability AI who resigned over ethical concerns about copyright. Operates AI model certification program with 'Licensed Model' certification for companies that only use licensed training data. Has certified 20+ AI companies including Beatoven, Boomy, LifeScore, and Rightsify. Supported by Universal Music Group, SAG-AFTRA, Authors Guild, and Association of American Publishers. Organized 1000+ UK musician protest album 'Is This What We Want?' in 2025 against proposed UK copyright changes.
organizations.threat_models: Copyright/IP, Economic inequality, Labor displacement
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on protecting creators from unauthorized use of their copyrighted works in AI training, ensuring fair compensation for creators whose work powers AI systems, and preventing the economic displacement of artists, writers, musicians, and other creative professionals.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory consent and licensing requirements for AI training data, transparency in AI training datasets, and legal protections for creators' copyrighted works. Opposes 'fair use' exemptions for AI companies and supports opt-in rather than opt-out consent models.

organizations.name: Data Workers' Inquiry
organizations.location: Berlin, Germany (with global presence across 9 countries)
organizations.notes: Joint project of DAIR Institute, Weizenbaum Institut, and TU Berlin, led by Dr. Milagros Miceli. Engaged 25 data workers as community researchers across Venezuela, Kenya, Syria, Brazil, Germany, and France. Developed Workers' Inquiry as Research Methodology (WIRM). Supported creation of Data Labelers Association in Kenya and testified at European Parliament, directly influencing Platform Workers' Directive. Three community researchers named to TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on exploitation of data workers in AI supply chains, wage theft on digital platforms, psychological trauma from content moderation, and the invisibilization of human labor behind AI systems. Highlights how AI development perpetuates colonial exploitation patterns through outsourced labor to Global South.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive worker protections in AI development, including fair wages, mental health support, transparency in data work supply chains, and workers' right to organize. Calls for binding international regulations on platform work and accountability for tech companies exploiting data workers.

organizations.name: ControlAI
organizations.location: London, UK (active in US, Canada, Germany)
organizations.notes: Non-profit founded by Andrea Miotti, structured as not-for-profit company in UK and 501(c)(4) in US. Has briefed 150+ UK parliamentarians since 2024, with 100+ supporting their campaign statement on AI extinction risks. Uses 'Direct Institutional Plan' (DIP) approach - directly contacting lawmakers about superintelligence risks rather than traditional lobbying. Team of ~14 people including policy advisors across multiple countries. Advisors include Connor Leahy (CEO of Conjecture) and Gabriel Alfour.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on extinction risks from artificial superintelligence (ASI). Argues that superintelligent AI systems cannot be developed safely or controllably and pose fundamental threats to human survival and control.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for prohibiting the development of superintelligent AI systems due to extinction risks. Calls for binding regulation on the most powerful AI models and systems, with international agreements to prevent ASI development until safety can be guaranteed.

organizations.name: MIT FutureTech
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 by Neil Thompson, grew to 110+ researchers by 2025. Housed within MIT CSAIL and Initiative on Digital Economy. Attracted $25M+ funding from Open Philanthropy, NSF, IBM, Google, Microsoft, others. Key outputs include AI Risk Repository, computational limits research, and Moore's Law studies. Director Neil Thompson advises Congress, Federal Reserve, Pentagon on AI policy.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Existential risk, Privacy, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Comprehensive AI risk research through the AI Risk Repository covering 777 risks across 7 domains. Focus on technical AI safety, workforce transformation, and economic impacts of automation. Develops taxonomies for AI governance and risk mitigation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stable regulatory frameworks leveraging existing sectoral regulations, with emphasis on technical tools for compliance. Supports goal of achieving Artificial Super Intelligence while investing in AI safety research and risk mitigation frameworks.

organizations.name: New Consensus (Saikat Chakrabarti)
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 by Saikat Chakrabarti and Zack Exley. 3 employees as of 2024. Authored the Green New Deal and 'Mission for America' policy framework. Chakrabarti is former AOC chief of staff, co-founder of Justice Democrats, and current congressional candidate challenging Nancy Pelosi in CA-11. The think tank promotes progressive industrial policy and 'total economic mobilization' for climate action.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on economic transformation through government-led industrial policy. Concerned about corporate capture of politics and oligarchic control of technology. Supports 'total economic mobilization' similar to WWII-era programs to address climate and economic challenges.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Chakrabarti's AI policy positions appear contradictory - advocating for aggressive government-led industrial mobilization while proposing to bypass civilian nuclear safety regulations through military channels. His 'Mission for America' calls for massive state investment in AI and tech infrastructure, but includes concerning proposals to use military exemptions to circumvent democratic oversight of nuclear power expansion.

organizations.name: Alex Bores campaign
organizations.notes: New York State Assembly Member (District 73) running for Congress in NY-12. Former Palantir engineer who left in 2019 citing opposition to ICE contracts. Authored RAISE Act requiring AI companies with $500M+ revenue to publish safety plans. Named by TIME as one of 100 most influential people in AI. Campaign has raised $234,681 as of 2026. Target of $10M+ spending by industry-backed super PAC Leading the Future opposing his pro-regulation stance.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on preventing AI companies from having 'unbridled control over American workers, kids' minds, climate, and utility bills.' Emphasizes need for transparency, accountability, and preventing regulatory capture by tech industry.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports moderate AI regulation including transparency requirements, safety reporting, and algorithmic audits. Authored the RAISE Act requiring large AI companies to publish safety plans and report incidents. Advocates for federal framework while supporting state-level experimentation.

organizations.name: Evitable
organizations.notes: Founded in 2025 by AI professor David Krueger (University of Montreal/Mila). Nonprofit focused on public education and advocacy to stop AI development. Krueger previously worked at UK AI Security Institute, Google DeepMind, and co-authored the CAIS Statement on AI Risk. Currently hiring for operations, communications, and chief of staff positions. Mission statement: 'AI is not inevitable' - aimed at countering industry narrative of inevitable AI progress.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Labor displacement, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on existential risks from AI systems that could lead to human extinction or gradual disempowerment. Research includes alignment failure modes, algorithmic manipulation, and deceptive alignment. Warns that AI companies are working to replace humans entirely, threatening jobs, societal roles, and human survival.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for ending the 'reckless race to develop superintelligence' and stopping production of advanced AI chips. Focuses on informing and organizing the public to confront societal-scale AI risks. Views current AI development as threatening human survival and calls for pausing AI development.

organizations.name: a16z (Andreessen Horowitz)
organizations.location: Menlo Park, CA (offices in SF, NYC, Santa Monica, DC, London)
organizations.notes: Founded 2009, raised $15B in new funding (2026) with $1.7B allocated to AI infrastructure. Major AI investments include OpenAI, Cursor ($625M), ElevenLabs, Groq ($640M), Harvey, and dozens of AI startups across the stack. Key partners: Marc Andreessen, Ben Horowitz, Martin Casado, Jennifer Li. One of the most active AI investors globally with 'AI Canon' educational resources.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on economic opportunity and competitive advantage rather than existential risks. Concerned about government overreach stifling innovation. Views AI regulation as potentially benefiting incumbents over startups.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal AI regulation through policy content like 'Why AI Will Save the World' and 'The Little Tech Agenda.' Opposes restrictive government policies that could harm AI startups. Views excessive regulation as threat to American technological leadership.

organizations.name: Legal Advocates for Safe Science and Technology
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Tyler Whitmer, former Quinn Emanuel partner. 3-person team including Vivian Dong (Harvard Law) and Cortney Busch. Funded by philanthropists including Jaan Tallinn. Key activities: AI Safety Whistleblower Legal Defense Fund, amicus briefs in pathogen research cases, policy advocacy on OpenAI restructuring. Files impact litigation and educates courts on AI/biotech risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Privacy, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on catastrophic risks from unsafe AI development including inadequate safety testing of frontier models, deployment of dangerous capabilities, suppression of safety research, and surveillance violations. Also addresses dangerous pathogen research that could cause pandemics.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict liability standards for dangerous pathogen and AI research, supports legal mechanisms to hold companies accountable for AI safety violations, and promotes stronger oversight through litigation and policy advocacy. Operates AI Safety Whistleblower Legal Defense Fund to support those exposing dangerous practices.

organizations.name: Legal Safety Lab
organizations.notes: Founded by a litigation lawyer and judge who emphasizes that effective legislation requires continuous interpretation and refinement. Operates within the European sphere using legal advocacy to address frontier technology risks. Received $101,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund in 2025 and $29,000 in 2024. Partners with AIWI and other organizations on whistleblower policy transparency campaigns. Quotes Richard Nixon: 'Mankind already carries in its own hands too many of the seeds of its own destruction.'
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on preventing catastrophic risks from AI systems that surpass human intelligence, including AI-generated misinformation, mass surveillance, autonomous weapons development, and loss of human control over superintelligent systems. Also addresses dual-use concerns across nuclear, biological, and AI technologies.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive legal frameworks to address dual-use risks from frontier technologies including AI, biotechnology, and nuclear technology. Emphasizes legal advocacy and litigation as tools to prevent catastrophic outcomes, with focus on clarifying responsibility, establishing clear legal parameters, and promoting ethical implementation.

organizations.name: Stop AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Sam Kirchner and Guido Reichstadter after splitting from PauseAI over tactical disagreements. Known for confrontational civil disobedience including chaining OpenAI office doors shut (February arrests) and barricading company gates. Organization expelled founder Kirchner in November 2024 after alleged violent threats against OpenAI employees. Now led by Matthew Hall ('Yakko'), shifting toward less confrontational messaging while maintaining commitment to stopping AGI development.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Labor displacement
organizations.threat_models_detail: Believes AGI development has >99% probability of causing human extinction because experimental proof of safety is impossible. Focuses on preventing development of systems more capable than GPT-4 through direct action and civil resistance.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Demands permanent ban on development of artificial general intelligence (AGI) and smarter-than-human AI systems. Uses civil disobedience tactics including blocking AI company offices and barricades to halt frontier AI development until safety is guaranteed.

organizations.name: Survival and Flourishing Fund
organizations.notes: Founded 2019, evolved from Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative. Distributed $152MM total, $34.33M in 2025 alone. Primary funder is Jaan Tallinn (Skype co-founder, ~$900M net worth). Uses unique 'S-process' algorithmic grant allocation with 6-12 independent recommenders. Second-largest AI safety funder after Open Philanthropy. Key programs include S-Process grants, Speculation Grants, and new Matching Pledge Program.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: 86% of 2025 grants went to AI safety and governance organizations including MIRI, METR, Center for AI Safety, and Apollo Research. Focuses on technical alignment research, frontier model evaluations, and policy initiatives to prevent catastrophic AI outcomes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on AI existential risk reduction through funding safety research, governance initiatives, and evaluation organizations. Primary funder Jaan Tallinn advocates for careful AI development and supports organizations working on AI alignment, policy, and risk mitigation.

organizations.name: Buddhism & AI Initiative
organizations.location: United States (distributed team across US and UK)
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 as nonprofit with 5-person core team. Led by Chris Scammell (former COO of Conjecture AI safety company), Peter Hershock (intercultural philosopher), Alex Sarkissian (Buddhist chaplain), and Ryan Stagg (former Mind & Life Institute). Funded by Future of Life Institute and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Has interviewed 150+ Buddhist teachers and technologists, mapping Buddhism-AI intersections across six domains including AI governance, technical alignment research inspired by Buddhist principles, and community education.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control, Misinformation, Privacy, Democratic erosion
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on AI as 'karmic accelerator' that threatens freedom of attention and intention, enabling 'attention trafficking' and attachment economy that disrupts human agency. Concerned about geopolitical AI competition, consciousness colonization through digital platforms, and need for human-AI alignment that addresses underlying human values conflicts rather than simply aligning AI with current human interests.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for resisting concentrations of power driving the competitive corporate and state AI race. Emphasizes need for ethical guardrails including individual data sovereignty, shifting algorithmic recommendation systems from attention capture to long-term well-being measures, and integrating meditative training as core digital literacy. Views current human-AI alignment approaches as insufficient without first addressing human values conflicts.

organizations.name: Astera Neuro & AGI
organizations.notes: Part of Astera Institute (founded 2020), with over $1 billion committed over the coming decade to neuro-inspired AGI research. Led by Dileep George (Head of AI, ex-DeepMind) and Doris Tsao (Chief Scientist). Three complementary research programs: Neuroscience, Neuro-AI, and AI Safety including Simplex project. Committed to open science approach and alternative to transformer scaling paradigm.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on fundamental alignment challenges including building systems whose goals stay aligned with human values as circumstances change, interpretability of decision-making processes, and developing more naturally aligned architectures through neuroscience-informed approaches. Believes current scaling approaches leave safety challenges unsolved.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open science and distributed AI development rather than concentrated commercial scaling. Believes progress on AGI safety is better served by open research than proprietary approaches. Focuses on technical safety through neuroscience-informed architectures rather than explicit regulatory positions.

organizations.name: High Impact Professionals
organizations.notes: Founded as career transition organization for experienced professionals seeking high-impact roles. Runs 6-week Impact Accelerator Program and maintains Talent Directory with 4,500+ professionals. In 2024 facilitated 20+ career transitions and inspired 20 effective giving pledges, generating estimated $1M+ counterfactual impact. Executive Director Nina Friedrich, co-founder Devon Fritz. Funded by Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving. Current 3-person team with runway through February 2025.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus is facilitating career transitions into AI safety roles and organizations working on catastrophic and existential risks from AI. Also supports professionals entering other high-impact cause areas including global health and policy.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on career transitions and effective giving rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports professionals entering AI safety and governance roles, indirectly supporting various regulatory approaches through talent pipeline development.

organizations.name: Upgradable
organizations.notes: Nonprofit organization helping existential safety advocates systematically optimize their lives and work through 'Upgrade Programs' (UPs). Offers coaching, consulting, and life optimization services specifically targeting AI safety researchers, entrepreneurs, and advocates. Team has built 25+ organizations operating 200+ years cumulatively, including Effective Altruism Global and EA Ventures (advocating AI safety since 2015). Provides startup templates, organizational support, and personal optimization across productivity, health, and effectiveness domains.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Explicitly states belief that 'unaligned AI poses the greatest risk to our species that has ever existed' and focuses on helping AI safety advocates and existential risk researchers optimize their work and lives to maximize impact in addressing these risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on personal optimization and productivity enhancement rather than direct AI policy advocacy. While they work with AI safety advocates and existential risk researchers, their primary mission is individual capability enhancement rather than regulatory positions.

organizations.name: Arbital
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 by Alexei Andreev, shut down 2017-2018. Wiki-style platform for AI alignment explanations, primarily written by Eliezer Yudkowsky and other MIRI-affiliated researchers. Funded by MIRI and other EA donors. Featured foundational concepts, open problems, and proposed solutions in AI safety. Software and IP transferred to MIRI after shutdown. Separate from Arbital Health (healthcare company founded 2023).
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focused on foundational AI alignment concepts including the alignment problem, AI safety theory, and mechanisms for ensuring advanced AI systems remain beneficial. Covered topics like utility functions, value learning, and cooperative AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Originally designed as a knowledge platform for AI alignment concepts, primarily focused on educational content rather than direct policy advocacy. Created to make AI safety concepts more accessible through explanatory content.

organizations.name: SAG-AFTRA
organizations.location: Los Angeles, CA (with NYC office and locals nationwide)
organizations.notes: Founded 2012 merger of Screen Actors Guild and AFTRA, represents ~160,000 entertainment professionals including Sean Astin as current president (elected 2025). Led 2023 strike partly over AI protections, achieving first-ever contractual AI consent requirements. Has signed agreements with AI voice companies Replica Studios, Ethovox, and Narrativ requiring union protections. Supports Trump administration's 2026 AI framework and NO FAKES Act federal legislation.
