Primary funder is Coefficient Giving (£8M+ committed). Led by Joe Hardie and Erin Robertson. Partners with organizations like GovAI, UK AISI, SaferAI, and Future Society on research projects.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on advanced AI risks including loss of control scenarios, catastrophic risks from frontier models, and governance gaps. Research covers AI incident tracking, safety evaluations, international cooperation frameworks, and technical governance mechanisms.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Runs AI Governance Taskforce producing policy research on frontier AI risk management, loss of control scenarios, and AI safety frameworks. Research outputs advocate for enhanced oversight mechanisms, safety evaluations, and governance infrastructure for advanced AI systems.

organizations.name: AI Impacts
organizations.notes: Founded by Katja Grace, operates as research organization under MIRI umbrella. Conducted largest AI expert survey to date (2,778 researchers in 2023). Received grants from Future of Life Institute ($49,310) and Future of Humanity Institute ($8,700) in 2015. Key outputs include expert surveys on AI timelines, AI capabilities database, and empirical research on technological progress patterns. Focuses on 'answering decision-relevant questions about the future of AI' through research, wiki, and expert surveys.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI timeline predictions, capability forecasting, and existential risk assessment. Conducts large-scale expert surveys on AI progress and risks. Maintains databases on AI capabilities, economic impacts, and historical technological discontinuities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Primarily focused on empirical research and data collection rather than explicit policy advocacy. Conducts expert surveys and maintains databases to inform AI governance decisions rather than taking strong regulatory positions.

organizations.name: London AI Safety Research Labs (LASR Labs)
organizations.notes: 13-week technical AI safety research program where participants work in teams of 3-4 supervised by experienced researchers to produce academic papers. Provides £11,000 stipend. Alumni have joined UK AISI, Apollo Research, Leap Labs, and OpenAI's dangerous capabilities team. All 5 papers from Summer 2024 cohort accepted to NeurIPS workshops. Operated by Arcadia Impact charity, funded by Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Supervisors from Google DeepMind, UK AISI, and top universities.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research program specifically focused on reducing the risk of loss of control to advanced AI. Past projects include work on deception in LLMs, AI alignment theory, interpretability, capability evaluations, and multi-agent collusion. Strong emphasis on catastrophic risk mitigation from frontier AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: LASR Labs focuses on technical AI safety research to reduce risks from loss of control to advanced AI systems. The program explicitly aims to train researchers for careers in AI safety organizations and emphasizes action-relevant research tackling concrete threat models related to advanced AI risks.

organizations.name: Dr. Roman Yampolskiy
organizations.notes: Tenured Associate Professor at University of Louisville, founding director of Cybersecurity Lab. Author of 100+ publications including 'Artificial Superintelligence: A Futuristic Approach' and 'AI: Unexplainable, Unpredictable, Uncontrollable'. Research funded by NSF, NSA, DHS, and FLI. Fellow at MIRI (2010), Research Advisor at MIRI (2012), AI Safety Fellow at Foresight Institute (2019). Known for extremely pessimistic AI safety positions and predicting human extinction within this century.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Labor displacement
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on the impossibility of controlling superintelligent systems, AI containment problems, and cybersecurity threats. Argues that 99% unemployment could occur within 5 years of AGI, and that superintelligent AI poses existential risks to humanity.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for extreme caution in AI development, arguing that superintelligent AI cannot be indefinitely controlled. Promotes narrow AI development over AGI and calls for potential moratoriums on superintelligence research until safety is better understood.

organizations.name: Dylan Hadfield-Menell
organizations.notes: Associate Professor at MIT CSAIL running the Algorithmic Alignment Group. PhD from UC Berkeley (2013-2020) advised by Stuart Russell, Pieter Abbeel, and Anca Dragan. AI2050 Early Career Fellow. Key publications include 'The Off-Switch Game', 'Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning', and 'Consequences of Misaligned AI'. Research spans theoretical and empirical AI safety with focus on value alignment and human-AI interaction.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI alignment problems including misspecified objectives, value learning, assistance games, and normative competence for AI systems. Studies how AI agents can participate in democratic institutions without disrupting social order. Work includes formal analysis of off-switch problems and consequences of misaligned AI optimization.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-seeking AI policies focused on identifying and studying risks rather than premature restrictive regulation. Co-authored paper arguing that regulatory objectives should facilitate research and deliberation about AI risks. Supports technical standards and democratic institutions for AI alignment.

organizations.name: Steve Byrnes's Brain-Like AGI Safety
organizations.notes: Individual researcher Steve Byrnes working at Astera Institute since 2021. Physics PhD turned AGI safety researcher. Published comprehensive 15-part 'Intro to Brain-Like AGI Safety' blog series. Research applies neuroscience insights to AI alignment, focusing on Learning and Steering Subsystems framework. Funded by grants and now Astera Institute. Estimates ~100-200 people globally work full-time on AGI safety.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on brain-like AGI systems that could develop dangerous antisocial motivations, escape human control, and pose existential risks. Research centers on understanding neuroscience-inspired reward functions and preventing alignment failures in model-based reinforcement learning systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for solving technical alignment problems before AGI development, warning that brain-like AGI could have 'radically nonhuman and dangerous motivations' without proper safety work. Emphasizes need for understanding reward functions and steering subsystems to prevent catastrophic accidents.

organizations.name: ILINA Program
organizations.location: Nairobi, Kenya (with presence in Nigeria, Zimbabwe, Mauritius)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, African-led research program with 14 employees. Coordinates Cecil Abungu, runs Junior Research Fellowship program for talent development. Publishes research papers and policy briefs on AI governance from Global South perspectives. Recent work includes 'Assessing the Case for Africa-Centric AI Safety Evaluations' and analysis of AU Continental AI Strategy. Hosts policy engagement sessions and collaborates with international AI safety community.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on catastrophic risks from frontier AI, particularly how these risks manifest in Global South contexts. Examines AI governance gaps, automation-first adoption patterns that could amplify risks in developing economies, and the need for Africa-specific AI safety evaluations. Also addresses AI liability frameworks and deceptive AI representations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI safety governance with particular focus on Global South perspectives. Publishes research arguing that Global South countries need to actively engage with highly capable AI risks and calls for substantial funding allocation (one-third of R&D budgets) toward AI safety research. Recommends AI liability frameworks and advocates for Africa-centric AI safety evaluations.

organizations.name: AI2050 (Schmidt Sciences)
organizations.notes: Launched in 2022 by Schmidt Sciences as a philanthropic initiative co-chaired by Eric Schmidt and James Manyika. Awards over $18M annually to researchers through fellowship program. Now has 99 fellows across 8 countries and 42 institutions in fourth cohort. Uses closed nomination process rather than open applications. Fellows have published 100+ papers in leading journals and created datasets like 'Humanity's Last Exam' AI benchmark.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, National security, Environmental
organizations.threat_models_detail: Awards funding to researchers working on AI safety, trustworthiness, and beneficial applications including drug discovery, energy solutions, and equitable access. Treats AI risks as technical challenges to be solved through better research and development rather than existential threats requiring precautionary measures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on ensuring AI benefits society by 2050 through research funding rather than regulatory advocacy. Co-founded by Eric Schmidt who has advocated for maintaining US AI competitiveness and avoiding restrictive regulations that could hamper innovation. Emphasizes technological solutions over policy interventions.

organizations.name: Formation Research
organizations.notes: Research organization focused on reducing lock-in risks by studying fundamental lock-in dynamics and implementing high-impact interventions. Appears to be a specialized research entity rather than a broad policy organization. Limited public information available about founding, staff size, or funding sources. Website indicates newsletter signup and mentions being 'supported by' unspecified entities.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specializes in 'lock-in risks' - the possibility that certain AI development trajectories or governance structures could become entrenched in ways that are difficult to change, potentially leading to suboptimal outcomes or concentrated power structures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses specifically on lock-in risks in AI development, researching fundamental dynamics around how certain technological or governance paths could become difficult to reverse. Does not appear to take broad regulatory positions beyond this specialized research area.

organizations.name: AI Safety Hub
organizations.location: Unknown (originally UK-based, transitioned to LASR Labs)
organizations.notes: No longer active organization that ran AI Safety Hub Labs training program for aspiring researchers. Program provided mentorship for participants to complete their first AI safety research project. Operations transitioned to LASR Labs, which now runs similar 13-week research programs. Alumni published at major ML conferences including NeurIPS, ICML, and EMNLP. Part of broader ecosystem supporting career transitions into AI safety research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focused on building technical AI safety research capacity through training programs. Supported individuals completing first research projects in AI alignment and safety.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: No clear regulatory stance documented. Organization focused on training and career development rather than policy advocacy.

organizations.name: AI Alignment Forum
organizations.location: Virtual (managed by LessWrong team in Berkeley, CA)
organizations.notes: Launched 2018 by LessWrong team as centralized hub for AI alignment research discussion. ~100 vetted members including researchers from MIRI, Anthropic, DeepMind, OpenAI, and academia. Serves as archive and library for the field with integrated karma system. Replaced MIRI's Intelligent Agent Foundations Forum. Key introductory sequences on Value Learning, Iterated Amplification, and Embedded Agency.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Deceptive alignment, Misalignment
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on technical alignment challenges including deceptive alignment, reward hacking, and maintaining human control over superintelligent systems. Research covers interpretability, scalable oversight, constitutional AI, and model organisms of misalignment to study failure modes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Forum discussions strongly favor careful evaluation and safety measures before deploying powerful AI systems. Content emphasizes responsible scaling policies, third-party testing, and international coordination on AI governance. Many posts advocate for pausing or slowing development until alignment problems are solved.

organizations.name: AISafety.com: Donation Guide
organizations.notes: Part of AISafety.com ecosystem maintained by global team of volunteers and professionals. Regularly-updated donation guide helping donors allocate funds effectively across AI safety field. Covers donation amounts from $1-1,000 to $100,000+, recommending platforms like Manifund, AI Risk Mitigation Fund, and Long-Term Future Fund. Notes that total AI safety funding was ~$150M in 2023, positioning large donors as potentially 'notable fraction of funding ecosystem'.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primarily focused on existential safety and catastrophic risks from advanced AI systems. Recommends funding for technical alignment research, AI governance, and field-building to prevent human extinction from AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on existential risk reduction from AI, describing AI as posing 'grave risk of extinction to humanity'. Emphasizes funding-constrained nature of AI safety field and need for more resources to address catastrophic risks.

organizations.name: Donations List Website
organizations.notes: Created 2017 by mathematician Vipul Naik with contributions from Issa Rice. Tracks $407M+ in AI safety donations (2007-2023) from major funders like Open Philanthropy ($276M), MIRI ($44M), OpenAI. Open-source database covers 875 donations across 191 organizations. Data considered preliminary until July 2026 completion target. Serves EA/rationalist communities primarily.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown
organizations.threat_models_detail: Not an AI safety organization per se, but a data transparency tool that tracks donations to AI safety causes among others. No specific threat model focus.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Does not advocate for specific AI policies but provides transparency infrastructure that enables others to track AI safety funding patterns. Serves as neutral documentation platform for philanthropic giving across cause areas.

organizations.name: Future of Humanity Institute
organizations.notes: Founded 2005 by Nick Bostrom at Oxford University, closed April 2024 due to administrative conflicts. Multidisciplinary team of ~15-20 researchers at peak. Funded primarily by Open Philanthropy (~£13.3M donation in 2018). Key publications include Superintelligence (2014), research on AI alignment, governance, and existential risk. Spawned numerous spinoff organizations and helped establish AI safety as a field. Notable alumni include Toby Ord, Anders Sandberg, Allan Dafoe.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Pioneered research on existential risk from superintelligent AI, including scenarios where AI systems become uncontrollable or misaligned with human values. Also studied risks from other emerging technologies like biotechnology and nanotechnology that could pose global catastrophic threats.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocated for careful, regulated development of AI systems with mandatory safety evaluations and governance frameworks. Published extensively on AI governance, calling for international cooperation and proactive policy measures to manage catastrophic risks from advanced AI.

organizations.name: Iliad
organizations.notes: Applied mathematics research nonprofit dedicated to advancing foundational alignment research. Runs research residencies (6-week programs with $6,000-$15,000 stipends), fellowships (3-month mentored research programs), and intensive courses. Partners with PIBBSS and operates at LISA in London. Funded by AISTOF. Organizes ILIAD conferences and provides research mentorship with established mathematicians and AI alignment researchers.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on foundational mathematical approaches to AI alignment including agent foundations, scalable oversight, interpretability, learning theory, and RL theory. Emphasizes formal methods and theoretical frameworks for alignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is primarily on technical research rather than explicit policy positions. Develops mathematical approaches to AI alignment through research residencies and fellowships, suggesting support for foundational safety research.

organizations.name: Lightspeed Grants
organizations.notes: Fast-funding initiative run by Lightcone Infrastructure, primarily funded by Jaan Tallinn. Distributed $9.6M in 2023 round with grants ranging $5K-$5M. Applications processed in 14-60 days. Funded projects like Palisade Research ($401K), Center for AI Safety ($16K), and various individual researchers. Last active funding round was 2023.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Funds projects focused on reducing existential catastrophe from AI, preventing biological pathogen development, and improving global decision-making. Portfolio includes technical alignment research, AI governance, and capability evaluation projects.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a grantmaker, does not take explicit regulatory positions but funds projects across the spectrum from AI safety research to policy advocacy, including both technical alignment work and governance initiatives.

organizations.name: Preamble Windfall Foundation
organizations.notes: 501(c)(3) founded around 2022, now defunct for unknown reasons. Founded by Jonathan Rodriguez Cefalu (Preamble Inc. founder, Stanford CS, ex-Snap). Funded research including SIMC4 wargame simulator for nuclear scenarios and AI red-teaming research. Filed Form 990-N indicating annual revenue under $50,000. Connected to broader Preamble ecosystem including commercial AI safety company Preamble Inc.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research portfolio includes nuclear command/control system vulnerabilities with AI integration, wargame simulation for catalytic nuclear scenarios, and AI system evaluation methods for detecting novel risks and deceptive behaviors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research and evaluation methods rather than broad regulatory advocacy. Supports development of AI safety guardrails and evaluation frameworks through funded research projects.

organizations.name: Superlinear Prizes
organizations.notes: Decentralized bounty platform for existential risk reduction and effective altruist cause areas. Offers prize money for delivering on specific AI safety projects. Listed as no longer accepting applications as of 2026. Part of the broader effective altruism ecosystem funding AI safety research through competition-based incentives.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Platform specifically targets existential risk reduction and AI safety projects through bounty/prize mechanisms, indicating focus on catastrophic and existential risks from advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Platform focuses on funding AI safety and existential risk reduction projects through prize competitions, suggesting concern about AI risks and need for safety research.

organizations.name: Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI)
organizations.notes: Founded in 2000 by Eliezer Yudkowsky as Singularity Institute, renamed MIRI in 2013. Approximately 20-42 employees including researchers and support staff. Major funding from Open Philanthropy ($3.75M in 2017, ongoing support). Pioneer organization that helped found the AI alignment field. Key personnel include Nate Soares (Executive Director) and Eliezer Yudkowsky (Senior Research Fellow). Recently launched Technical Governance Team focused on policy research for AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment and control problems that could lead to human extinction from artificial superintelligence. Research areas include logical uncertainty, decision theory, value learning, corrigibility, and realistic world-modeling. Believes default outcome of ASI development is human extinction.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a globally coordinated moratorium on dangerous AI development until alignment problems are solved. Believes humanity should build technical, legal, and institutional infrastructure (an 'Off Switch') to halt AI development when needed. Opposes current race dynamics and calls for stopping AI development altogether until safety is ensured.

organizations.name: NIST AI Safety Institute (Center for AI Standards and Innovation - CAISI)
organizations.notes: Established in 2024 following Biden's 2023 Executive Order on AI. Houses both the U.S. AI Safety Institute and Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI). Leads the AI Safety Institute Consortium with 280+ member organizations. Has signed agreements with Anthropic and OpenAI for pre- and post-deployment AI model testing. Director: Elizabeth Kelly.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, National security, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on evaluating AI capabilities that may pose risks to national security, cybersecurity threats, biosecurity concerns, and chemical weapons risks. Also addresses algorithmic bias, privacy concerns, and misinformation through evaluation frameworks and safety guidelines.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Develops voluntary AI safety standards, guidelines, and evaluation frameworks while serving as the primary U.S. government point of contact for AI model testing and evaluation. Focuses on science-based approaches to AI safety through collaborative partnerships with industry rather than mandatory regulation.

organizations.name: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (HQ at 182 Howard Street)
organizations.notes: Founded 2014 as Open Philanthropy, rebranded to Coefficient Giving in November 2025. Directed over $4 billion in grants, 167 employees, $4M annual revenue. Primary partnership with Good Ventures (Dustin Moskovitz/Cari Tuna foundation). Manages 13+ cause-specific funds including AI safety, biosecurity, global health. Started AI safety funding in 2015, seven years before ChatGPT. Operates partnerships program advising 20+ major donors giving $250k+ annually.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on catastrophic and existential risks from advanced AI, including loss-of-control scenarios, extreme misuse for bioweapons, and authoritarian control. Supports technical safety research, governance frameworks, and capability evaluation systems to mitigate worst-case outcomes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports both AI progress and safety measures through targeted regulation. Advocates for proportionate government intervention focused on catastrophic risks while opposing overly restrictive regulations on smaller-scale harms. Emphasizes bipartisan approaches and evidence-based policy.

organizations.name: Brookings Institution (Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology Initiative)
organizations.notes: Founded 1916, major DC think tank. AI and Emerging Technology Initiative established 2018, now directed by Elham Tabassi (former NIST chief AI advisor). Hosts Forum for Cooperation on AI with international partners, AI Policy Idea Incubator convenings. Funded by Microsoft, Google, Amazon as general donors. Active in congressional testimony and policy briefings. Publishes AI Governance series and maintains TechPolicy Bridge program.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI governance frameworks, workforce transition policies, responsible AI implementation in government, international AI cooperation, and managing AI's economic impacts. Emphasizes need for pro-worker AI policies and systematic regulatory approaches across multiple threat vectors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for systematic, problem-driven AI regulation using their 'SETO Loop' framework (Scope, Existing regulation, Tools, Organization). Supports risk-based approaches with different rules for different risk levels, mandatory disclosure requirements, independent audits, and licensing for high-risk applications. Emphasizes need for new regulatory agency and federal coordination.

organizations.name: Public AI Network
organizations.location: Global network, coordination hub appears US-based
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as global movement and network promoting AI as public infrastructure. Maintains extensive publication database with 150+ papers, policy briefs, and research from multiple organizations. Key publications include Public AI Whitepaper (2024), policy briefs on European digital sovereignty, and proposals for public AI utilities. Affiliated researchers from institutions like Harvard, Vanderbilt, Mozilla, and various European policy organizations. Operates inference utility providing access to open-source AI models via HuggingFace.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on concentration of AI power in few tech giants, dependencies on non-European/foreign AI systems undermining digital sovereignty, and need for democratic governance over AI systems. Emphasizes risks of restrictive regulations driving AI development to more permissive jurisdictions and importance of maintaining open information ecosystems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for public AI infrastructure developed under transparent governance with democratic oversight, public accountability, and equitable access. Supports risk-based regulatory frameworks that focus on outputs rather than training inputs, legal clarity for using publicly available data, and safe harbors for responsible AI development while opposing restrictive data regulations that could shift AI development to other jurisdictions.

organizations.name: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP)
organizations.notes: Led by Director Michael Kratsios under Trump administration. Coordinates AI policy across federal agencies through America's AI Action Plan (July 2025). Key initiatives include removing regulatory barriers, establishing AI procurement standards, creating federal AI framework to preempt state laws, and promoting AI adoption in government. Staff of approximately 22 people with $325M proposed budget cuts to NIST creating tension with innovation goals.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on preventing AI bias in government systems, ensuring national security applications are properly evaluated, cybersecurity incident response, and countering foreign adversary influence while promoting free speech in AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Promotes American AI dominance through minimal regulation and removal of regulatory barriers. Focuses on accelerating AI innovation, preventing 'woke AI' bias, and preempting restrictive state laws while maintaining basic safety standards for government AI use.

organizations.name: Dovetail Research
organizations.notes: Research organization working on foundational mathematics and theory for AI safety, particularly agent foundations. Produces technical content on topics like formalizing value fragility, corrigible architectures, and how theory can contribute to AI safety through direct calculations, pointing at possibilities, providing disproofs of practicality, and qualitative behavioral descriptions. Appears to be a small, specialized theory-focused group distinct from the customer insights platform company of the same name.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on foundational mathematics for understanding AI agents, value fragility problems, corrigibility architectures, and providing theoretical frameworks to prevent utility maximizers with misspecified values from causing catastrophic outcomes. Emphasizes agent foundations theory that could yield qualitative behavioral predictions and disproofs of dangerous approaches.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on foundational theoretical research in agent foundations and mathematical frameworks for AI safety. Does not appear to take explicit policy positions but contributes to technical understanding that could inform future governance approaches.

organizations.name: Stanford RegLab (Regulation, Evaluation, and Governance Lab)
organizations.notes: Founded ~2019 by Prof. Daniel Ho. Interdisciplinary team of 24 people including legal experts, data scientists, and engineers. Partners with federal agencies (IRS, DOL, EPA), state/local governments (Santa Clara County, San Francisco). Key projects include STARA (Statutory Research Assistant), racial covenant identification AI, and AI evaluation frameworks. Funded through government partnerships and academic grants.
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Loss of control, Democratic erosion, National security, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI hallucinations in legal systems, algorithmic bias in government services, and need for proper AI evaluation frameworks. Research addresses risks from poorly implemented AI in high-stakes government decisions including benefits adjudication, tax enforcement, and legal research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance through rigorous evaluation and testing. Supports responsible AI deployment with proper oversight mechanisms, adverse event reporting systems, and government technical capacity building. Emphasizes need for regulatory frameworks grounded in empirical evidence rather than speculation.

organizations.name: Center for Human-Compatible Artificial Intelligence (CHAI) - UC Berkeley
organizations.location: Berkeley, California (with affiliates at Cornell, University of Michigan, Princeton)
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 by Stuart Russell at UC Berkeley. Multi-institution research center with 9 core faculty, 18 affiliate faculty, ~30 PhD students and postdocs. Funded primarily by Open Philanthropy ($12M+ total), Future of Life Institute, Leverhulme Trust. Published 150+ papers on AI safety. Russell co-authored leading AI textbook and gave 2021 BBC Reith Lectures. Recent graduates work at DeepMind, OpenAI, Carnegie Mellon, and AI safety startups like FAR.ai.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: CHAI focuses on the fundamental control problem in AI - ensuring advanced AI systems remain beneficial as they exceed human capabilities. Research covers technical AI safety, value alignment, assistance games, recommender system harms, adversarial robustness, and societal transformation from AI deployment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: CHAI advocates for proactive AI safety measures and regulation. Director Stuart Russell has extensively advised EU AI Act development, testified to US Congress including Senate Majority Leader Schumer, and argues for mandatory safety frameworks before deployment of advanced AI systems. The lab emphasizes the need for 'provably beneficial' AI systems and has been instrumental in elevating recommender systems to 'high-risk' classification in EU AI Act.

organizations.name: CSIS AI Policy Podcast (Center for Strategic and International Studies)
organizations.notes: Biweekly podcast hosted by Gregory C. Allen, senior adviser with the Wadhwani AI Center at CSIS. Launched January 2024, produced by Cera Baker. 88 episodes published, covers AI policy regulation, innovation, national security, and geopolitics. CSIS is a bipartisan think tank founded in 1962, headquartered in LEED Platinum building at 1616 Rhode Island Avenue NW.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Coverage emphasizes geopolitical AI competition, export control policies, cybersecurity implications, and military AI applications. Frequent discussion of U.S.-China tech rivalry and strategic technology governance.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage takes a balanced approach to AI policy, examining both innovation benefits and regulatory needs. Discussion topics include export controls, national security implications, safety measures, and strategic competition with China, reflecting mainstream policy discourse.

organizations.name: Association for Long Term Existence and Resilience (ALTER)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, Israeli nonprofit with 5 staff. Funded by Open Philanthropy and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Led by David Manheim. Institutional member of US AI Safety Consortium (NIST). Key work includes AI oversight standards, biosecurity policy, and salt iodization advocacy. Publishes research on AI governance gaps and incident preparedness frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI incident preparedness and response capabilities, biosecurity risks from AI-enabled biological tools, and preventing catastrophic AI security incidents. Research covers dual-use AI applications in scientific research and critical infrastructure protection.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory synthetic nucleic acid screening, establishment of governance frameworks for biological AI models, and incident preparedness approaches to AI regulation. Supports comprehensive regulatory frameworks with pre-deployment safety testing and strong governmental powers to respond to AI security incidents.
organizations.aliases: Association for Long Term Existence and Resilience

organizations.name: Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence (University of Cambridge)
organizations.location: Cambridge, UK (with spokes at Imperial College London and UC Berkeley)
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 with £10 million Leverhulme Trust grant for 10-year program. Interdisciplinary research centre with ~42 staff led by Stephen Cave. Five core research programs: Kinds of Intelligence, AI: Futures and Responsibility, AI: Trust and Society, AI: Narratives and Justice, and Design, Participation, and Praxis. Collaborates with industry partners like Accenture on compliance tools (HEAT toolkit for EU AI Act). Offers MPhil in Ethics of AI, Data and Algorithms.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment and value alignment problems, ensuring AI systems behave consistently with human values. Research addresses algorithmic bias, transparency challenges, autonomous weapons systems, and long-term existential risks from artificial general intelligence. Explores consciousness, intelligence definitions, and societal integration challenges.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance including transparency requirements, safety evaluations, and democratic participation in AI development. Supports sector-specific regulation with central coordination functions. Emphasizes inclusive governance mechanisms like 'Alignment Assemblies' to democratize AI decision-making and ensure alignment with human values.
organizations.aliases: Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence

organizations.name: 80,000 Hours
organizations.location: Oxford, UK + Berkeley, CA + NYC offices
organizations.notes: Founded 2011 by William MacAskill and Ben Todd. Career advisory nonprofit with 46 employees focusing on directing talent toward high-impact careers, especially AI safety/governance. Published extensive problem profiles on AI risks, operates job board, provides 1-on-1 career advice. Over 3,000 people report changing careers due to their guidance. Primary funder: Coefficient Giving. Part of effective altruism community.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on existential risks from power-seeking AI systems that could disempower humanity. Also addresses AI misuse for weapons development, geopolitical competition, and broader societal transformation risks from rapid AI progress.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with strong safety measures. Promotes working on AI governance, technical safety research, and policy approaches to reduce catastrophic risks. Estimates ~10% existential risk from AI within 100 years and calls it 'the world's most pressing problem'.

organizations.name: Heron AI Security Initiative
organizations.notes: Founded December 2024 as a non-profit AI security initiative. Team includes Ezra Hausdorff (Founder/CEO), Shanni Gurkevitch (Co-Founder), Nitzan Shulman (Head of Cyber), and advisory board with AI security experts. Funded by Coeffecient Giving. Runs events, research programs, and career guidance connecting cybersecurity professionals to high-leverage AI security opportunities. Collaborates with frontier AI companies, startups, think tanks, and policymakers to advance AI infrastructure security.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on AI infrastructure security challenges including securing model weights from exfiltration, authorization solutions for AI misuse, secure compute verification, and protecting AI systems from bad actors and rogue AI scenarios.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger cybersecurity measures for frontier AI models, emphasizing the need for secure infrastructure, model weight protection, and authorization solutions for AI misuse. Promotes policy solutions like SL5-level security standards and regulatory frameworks for AI lab security.

organizations.name: Alignment Research Center (ARC)
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 by Paul Christiano after leaving OpenAI. Small team of ~7 researchers including Jacob Hilton (President), Eric Neyman, Victor Lecomte. Theoretical research organization using 'builder-breaker' methodology. Key publications include ELK report, mechanistic anomaly detection research, and work on heuristic explanations. Split off ARC Evals (now METR) in 2023. Board includes Buck Shlegeris and Ben Hoskin.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on alignment robustness (AI systems remaining aligned when faced with out-of-distribution inputs) and eliciting latent knowledge (training AI to honestly report internal beliefs). Research centers on deceptive alignment/scheming risks and developing formal mechanistic explanations for neural network behavior.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for developing scalable alignment techniques before AI systems become too powerful to control. Emphasizes theoretical research that makes worst-case assumptions rather than extrapolating from current systems, and conducts dangerous capability evaluations to inform policymakers about AI risks.

organizations.name: Computational Rational Agents Laboratory (CORAL)
organizations.notes: Led by Vanessa Kosoy, fiscally sponsored by Ashgro (formerly Affiliate Learning Theoretic Employment and Resources). Works on learning theoretic research agenda for AI alignment. Received $140,000 from SFF-2025 round funded by Jaan Tallinn. Categorized as doing foundational mathematics and agent foundations work in the AI safety ecosystem.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on agent foundations using computational learning theory, control theory, algorithmic information theory and categorical systems theory to understand computationally bounded agents and develop mathematical frameworks for AI alignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on foundational alignment research to develop mathematical tools for aligning AI objectives with human values. Works on agent foundations and learning theory to provide rigorous frameworks for AI alignment solutions.

organizations.name: Computational and Biological Learning Lab (University of Cambridge)
organizations.notes: Part of University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, established research lab focused on computational neuroscience and machine learning. Contains multiple research groups including the Krueger AI Safety Lab (KASL) founded 2022. Key members include David Krueger (AI alignment researcher who worked with UK Frontier AI Taskforce), Carl Rasmussen, Adrian Weller, and other ML researchers. Conducts both biological learning research and AI safety work including alignment, interpretability, and policy engagement.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research spans AI alignment (through researchers like Krueger), interpretability methods, and safety evaluations. The Krueger AI Safety Lab (KASL) within CBL specifically focuses on deep learning safety and AI alignment research. Lab members contribute to policy work on frontier AI risks and compute monitoring.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As an academic research lab, CBL does not take explicit regulatory positions. However, lab members like David Krueger have worked with UK government (Frontier AI Taskforce) and contributed to policy discussions around AI safety and compute governance, suggesting engagement with regulatory development.

organizations.name: Krueger AI Safety Lab (KASL)
organizations.location: Cambridge, UK (University of Cambridge) + Montreal, Canada (Mila)
organizations.notes: Founded ~2022, led by David Krueger (Assistant Professor at Cambridge). Dual affiliation with University of Cambridge and Mila (Quebec AI Institute). Team includes ~15 PhD students, postdocs, and research assistants. Key publications include 'Goal Misgeneralization in Deep Reinforcement Learning' (ICML 2022), 'Foundational Challenges in Assuring Alignment and Safety of LLMs' (2024). Runs paid AI safety internship programs. Previously based at University of Cambridge, now also affiliated with Mila.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research specifically targets goal misgeneralization in RL agents, reward gaming, mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, and developing scalable oversight methods. Focus on preventing AI systems from pursuing unintended objectives that could lead to catastrophic outcomes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with strong safety evaluations. Co-authored major paper identifying 18 foundational challenges in LLM alignment and safety with 35+ researchers. Research focuses on technical solutions to prevent misaligned AI behavior before deployment.

organizations.name: Model Evaluation & Threat Research (METR)
organizations.location: Berkeley, California (operates in 5 countries including Denmark, Netherlands, UK, Germany)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as ARC Evals, rebranded to METR. 36 employees including CEO Beth Barnes (named to TIME's 100 most influential people in AI 2024). Funded by The Audacious Project, Open Philanthropy, Schmidt Sciences, and other philanthropic sources - notably does not accept funding from AI companies. Partners with OpenAI, Anthropic, and other labs on pre-deployment evaluations. Key research includes 'Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks' showing exponential capability growth. Part of NIST AI Safety Institute Consortium.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on evaluating AI systems' autonomous capabilities including self-improvement, rogue replication, cyberattack capabilities, and ability to conduct AI R&D. Developed evaluations measuring AI agents' ability to complete increasingly complex multi-hour tasks, finding capabilities double every 7 months. Studies deceptive alignment, scheming behavior, and monitoring evasion in frontier models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for rigorous pre-deployment evaluations of frontier AI models, external third-party auditing rather than self-evaluation by labs, and mandatory safety assessments before release. Developed the Responsible Scaling Policy framework adopted by nine leading AI developers and provides technical assistance to regulators including the EU AI Office.

organizations.name: Quantified Uncertainty Research Institute (QURI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 as nonprofit research institute by Ozzie Gooen. Small team of ~5 full-time staff. Receives funding from Survival and Flourishing Fund ($62K in 2024), operates under Rethink Priorities fiscal sponsorship. Key projects include Squiggle probabilistic programming language, SquiggleAI, and research on AI for forecasting question resolution. Board includes AI policy experts like Abigail Olvera (Golden Gate Institute for AI).
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on improving forecasting and epistemics to address long-term risks from transformative AI. Develops tools for AI-assisted evaluation and question resolution that could help with alignment and safety assessment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing epistemic tools and forecasting systems to improve decision-making rather than direct regulatory advocacy. Research on AI for question resolution and evaluation systems could inform governance frameworks, but no explicit policy positions identified.

organizations.name: Saving Humanity from Homo Sapiens (SHfHS)
organizations.notes: Small philanthropic foundation with long history in X-risk funding. Does not conduct direct research but identifies and financially supports individuals and organizations working on existential risk reduction. Part of broader AI safety funding ecosystem alongside organizations like Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Currently not accepting applications according to multiple sources.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on human-created existential risks including AI, self-replicating nanobots, nuclear war, and catastrophic global warming. Views AI as one of the 'looming threats' from future technologies that could lead to human extinction if not properly managed.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Dedicated to preventing human-created existential risks through funding researchers and organizations working on X-risk reduction. Operates as a philanthropic foundation that identifies and financially supports people doing the best work to prevent human extinction from technologies like AI, nanotechnology, and other emerging threats.

organizations.name: Beijing Institute of AI Safety and Governance (Beijing-AISI)
organizations.notes: Founded September 2024 with Beijing municipal government support. Led by Yi Zeng, CAS professor and UN High-Level Advisory Body on AI member. Core contributor to China AI Safety & Development Association (CnAISDA). Published AGILE Index 2025 evaluating AI governance across 40 countries. Co-organized Beijing AI Safety International Consensus with signatures from Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, Stuart Russell.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on preventing catastrophic and existential risks from advanced AI systems. Developed 'red lines' framework prohibiting autonomous AI replication, power-seeking behavior, WMD assistance, and deceptive capabilities. Research covers superalignment, mechanistic interpretability, and AI governance gaps.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks balancing development and safety through international cooperation. Supports tiered regulatory approaches, mandatory safety evaluations for frontier models, and establishment of global AI governance institutions with enforcement mechanisms.
organizations.aliases: Beijing Institute of AI Safety and Governance

organizations.name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (Georgetown University)
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 with $55M from Open Philanthropy. Georgetown University-based think tank with ~60 employees. Led by Helen Toner (Interim Executive Director, former OpenAI board member). Provides nonpartisan analysis to policymakers on AI security implications. Staff frequently testify before Congress on AI policy issues. Major focus on US-China AI competition and frontier AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Labor displacement
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI governance frameworks, China-US tech competition, automated AI R&D risks, cybersecurity implications, and workforce disruption. Emphasizes national security implications of AI development and the need for government oversight of frontier AI systems that could pose extreme risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for adaptive, risk-based AI governance including transparency requirements for advanced AI systems, federal oversight mechanisms that can scale with AI capabilities, and basic building blocks like safety research funding and audit ecosystems. Opposes 'wait and see' approach while supporting light-touch, flexible policies that prepare for uncertain AI futures.
organizations.aliases: Center for Security and Emerging Technology

organizations.name: China AI Safety & Development Association (CnAISDA)
organizations.location: China (multi-institutional network based primarily in Beijing)
organizations.notes: Launched February 2025 at Paris AI Action Summit. Network of China's leading AI institutions including Tsinghua University, Beijing Academy of AI, Shanghai AI Lab, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and China Academy of Information and Communications Technology. Claims Chinese government backing but operates as collaborative platform rather than regulatory body. Primary function is international representation and dialogue coordination rather than domestic oversight or testing.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, CBRN risks, National security, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on catastrophic AI risks including loss of human control, AI-biotech convergence risks, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, and potential for AI systems to develop beyond human oversight. Emphasizes need for international coordination on frontier AI safety and risk assessment frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI development and safety governance through international cooperation. Positions itself as China's counterpart to international AI safety institutes, emphasizing collaborative governance frameworks, voluntary industry safety commitments, and multilateral dialogue while avoiding restrictive regulatory approaches that could hinder development.

organizations.name: The AI Whistleblower Initiative (AIWI)
organizations.location: Berlin, Germany (with presence in UK and Kenya)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 as nonprofit hosted by Whistleblower Netzwerk e.V. Staff of 7 with 200% growth. Offers 'Third Opinion' anonymous consultation service connecting AI insiders with independent experts. Launched 'Publish Your Policies' campaign with 35+ signatories demanding AI companies publish whistleblowing policies. Documented 22 whistleblower cases (6 named, 16 anonymous) in comprehensive resource. Provides legal support connections and policy advocacy in EU and US.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control, National security, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on risks from internal AI deployment that are difficult to detect externally, safety function reductions at major AI companies, government deployment outpacing oversight, and corporate practices that actively deter disclosure of safety concerns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger legal protections for AI whistleblowers, supports principle-based regulation that covers emerging risks not yet captured by existing law, and promotes transparency requirements for AI companies including mandatory publication of internal whistleblowing policies.

organizations.name: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS)
organizations.location: United States (operates as initiative of Rethink Priorities)
organizations.notes: Founded as initiative of Rethink Priorities, 36+ employees with 94% YoY growth. Executive Director Jenny Marron (former NSC/State Dept). Funded by Open Philanthropy, Survival and Flourishing Fund, individual donors. Key focus areas: policy and standards, compute governance, international governance and China relations. Major publications include strategic visions in AI governance, AI Safety Institutes analysis, and responsible scaling policy frameworks. Operates fellowship programs and engages with government, industry, and international partners on AI safety policy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on three severe risks: takeover by powerful misaligned AI systems, wars resulting from competitive dynamics around AI, and AI-enabled concentration of power among small groups. Emphasizes frontier AI safety evaluations, compute governance, international coordination, and preventing adversaries from accessing advanced AI capabilities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for technically sound policy frameworks including mandatory safety evaluations, responsible scaling policies, third-party auditing, and coordinated disclosure of dual-use capabilities. Supports government involvement in AI safety standards while preserving space for innovation. Focuses on building trust in American AI systems through enhanced security and reliability standards.
organizations.aliases: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy

organizations.name: Meridian Cambridge
organizations.notes: Coworking space and field-building nonprofit based in Cambridge, UK. Houses projects like CAISH and the ERA:AI Fellowship. Runs 3-12 month Visiting Researcher Programme for AI safety and governance researchers. Partners with Cambridge AI Safety Hub. Provides workspace, community, and research support including assistance with grant applications to Open Philanthropy and other funders. Contact: visitingresearchers@meridiancambridge.org
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Visiting Researchers Programme covers technical safety research including AI control evaluation, interpretability, model organisms of misaligned AI, scalable oversight, and governance frameworks. Also supports forecasting AI capabilities, economic modeling of AI impact, and identifying paths to AI takeover.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI safety and governance research through visiting researcher programs. Houses projects like CAISH and ERA:AI Fellowship. Supports research on technical alignment, governance, interpretability, forecasting, and other research ensuring AI benefits humanity.

organizations.name: Constellation Institute
organizations.notes: Founded as nonprofit research center in Berkeley with 35 employees (+266% growth). Four core strategies: mobilization (placing talent), incubation (supporting new orgs), growth (helping orgs scale), and convening (facilitating coordination). Runs Astra Fellowship (3-6 month AI safety research program), incubator for AI safety startups, and visiting fellows program. Over 80% of first Astra cohort now work full-time in AI safety at organizations like Redwood Research, METR, Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Explicitly identifies risks of 'permanent loss of control of human civilization to AIs', 'extreme mass casualty events or human extinction', and 'lock-in of large-scale authoritarian regimes' from advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing AI safety talent and supporting key organizations rather than direct policy advocacy. Emphasizes coordination across the AI safety field and facilitating collaboration between researchers, policymakers, and industry.

organizations.name: London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA)
organizations.notes: Founded September 2023 as UK charity. Operates coworking hub hosting organizations like Apollo Research, BlueDot Impact, and training programs like MATS, ARENA. Led by team including James Fox (Research Director, Oxford PhD), Mike Brozowski (Operations Director). Funded by Open Philanthropy, seeking $3.7M annually. Houses 120+ researchers from 50+ organizations. Three-pillar approach: mobilize talent, strengthen organizations, convene ecosystem.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on technical AI safety including interpretability (sparse autoencoders, mechanistic interpretability), alignment techniques, situational awareness in LLMs, and evaluating dangerous capabilities. Supports diverse research agendas addressing control and alignment of advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports government AI safety initiatives like the UK AI Safety Institute and advocates for mandatory multilingual capability and safety assessments for frontier AI models. Works collaboratively with regulators while focusing on fundamental research rather than evaluation.

organizations.name: Center for AI Risk Management & Alignment (CARMA)
organizations.notes: Founded as fiscally-sponsored project of Social & Environmental Entrepreneurs (501c3). 10 employees across US, UK, Finland, Kenya, Italy. Led by Richard Mallah (also Principal AI Safety Strategist at Future of Life Institute). Conducts interdisciplinary research on risk management, policy, and technical safety. Co-authored 'Intolerable Risk Threshold Recommendations for Artificial Intelligence' and 'Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management' papers. Mission to lower risks to humanity and biosphere from transformative AI.
organizations.threat_models: Research focuses on CBRN weapons risks, cyber attack capabilities, model autonomy, persuasion and manipulation, deception, and systemic risks from increasingly powerful AI systems. Develops risk assessment frameworks using probabilistic risk assessment techniques adapted from other industries.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive risk management frameworks and intolerable risk thresholds for frontier AI systems. Co-authored research on 'Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management' recommending mandatory safety evaluations, risk thresholds, and multi-stakeholder governance approaches before deployment of transformative AI.
organizations.aliases: Center for AI Risk Management & Alignment

organizations.name: Alignment Research Engineer Accelerator (ARENA)
organizations.location: London, UK (hosted at London Initiative for Safe AI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, runs 4-5 week intensive ML bootcamps 2-3 times per year. Each cohort accepts ~30 participants. Funded by Coefficient Giving and Open Philanthropy ($18,800 grant). Alumni outcomes include roles at Anthropic, OpenAI, METR, UK AISI, and MATS scholarships. Based at LISA in Shoreditch, covers all participant costs including travel, accommodation, and meals.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: ARENA focuses specifically on technical AI alignment and safety challenges. The curriculum covers interpretability, reinforcement learning, model evaluation, and alignment research methods. Training emphasizes preventing loss of control over advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: ARENA trains technical AI safety researchers who typically advocate for cautious AI development. The program emphasizes AI alignment research and technical safety measures, with alumni going to organizations like Anthropic, METR, and UK AISI that promote careful AI governance.

organizations.name: Center on Long-Term Risk: Summer Research Fellowship
organizations.notes: Founded 2013 as interdisciplinary research organization. Runs 8-week summer fellowship program focusing on s-risk motivated empirical AI safety research. Key research areas include Model Persona research agenda studying malicious traits in LLMs, Safe Pareto improvements, and s-risk macrostrategy. Collaborates with researchers from Anthropic, UK AISI, TruthfulAI, and major universities. Fellowship produces research published at venues like ICLR. Offers £4,925/month stipend plus travel costs for London-based program.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on s-risks (suffering risks) - scenarios where advanced AI systems could create astronomical amounts of suffering. Research covers malicious AI personas, multi-agent conflict scenarios, and technical interventions to prevent deliberately harmful AI systems. Also studies AI cooperation failures and bargaining breakdowns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: CLR advocates for careful, safety-focused AI development with emphasis on preventing worst-case scenarios. Their research focuses on s-risk (suffering risk) reduction and developing interventions to prevent malevolent AI outcomes. They collaborate with major AI labs and safety organizations to influence technical safety practices.

organizations.name: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
organizations.notes: Founded in 2018, GovAI is a leading AI governance research organization with 72 employees across UK and US offices. Runs flagship 3-month Fellowship programs (Summer, Winter, DC) training next generation of AI policy researchers. Alumni work at major AI labs (DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic), government agencies, and think tanks. Published foundational research agenda that helped define the AI governance field. Closely advises policymakers in US, UK, and EU on AI regulation and safety frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Existential risk, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI governance challenges including risks from advanced AI systems, corporate governance for frontier labs, compute governance, AI regulation frameworks, and international coordination mechanisms for managing AI risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance frameworks including compute governance, corporate governance measures for frontier labs, and adaptive regulatory ecosystems. Supports mandatory external scrutiny of powerful AI models and international coordination on AI governance.

organizations.name: AI Security Institute (AISI)
organizations.location: London, UK (with presence in US, China, UAE)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as part of UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology. 170 employees including world-leading AI researchers. Led by Chief Scientist Geoffrey Irving. Conducts safety evaluations of frontier models from major labs, publishes the International AI Safety Report, and leads international coordination through the International Network for Advanced AI Measurement. Key partnerships with Google DeepMind, Anthropic, and other frontier labs for model access and safety research.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Conducts frontier model evaluations focusing on dangerous capabilities, alignment research, cybersecurity risks, and misinformation threats. Develops protocols for AI safety testing and publishes comprehensive risk assessments.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Conducts safety evaluations and testing of frontier AI models, develops technical standards and protocols, and publishes evidence-based assessments to inform policy. Focuses on building government capacity for AI oversight rather than prescriptive regulations.

organizations.name: ML4Good Laboratory - New York University
organizations.notes: Academic research lab at NYU's Center for Urban Science and Progress, directed by Prof. Daniel B. Neill. Faculty includes experts in biostatistics, epidemiology, and applied statistics. Research applications include disease surveillance, opioid crisis response, criminal justice fairness, and urban analytics. Funded by NSF (including $1M NSF-Amazon Fairness in AI grant), collaborates with city agencies and public health organizations. Over 100 published papers on machine learning for social good applications.
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Privacy
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on algorithmic fairness and bias in AI systems, particularly for public sector applications. Research addresses systematic discrimination in criminal justice, healthcare allocation, housing, and urban analytics. Develops methods to detect and correct biases that could exacerbate inequality.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Research focuses on algorithmic fairness, AI bias detection and correction, and developing responsible AI methods for public sector applications. Lab develops technical methods to audit and mitigate systematic biases in AI systems used for urban policy decisions.

organizations.name: Talos Fellowship (Talos Network)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as EU Tech Policy Fellowship, renamed to Talos Network. Flagship 7-month program trains ~50 fellows annually in European AI governance through 8-week online curriculum, Brussels summit, and 4-6 month paid placements. 70% alumni transition rate to AI policy roles across EU AI Office, UK AISI, OECD, think tanks. Funded primarily by Coefficient Giving/Good Ventures, seeking €200k diversification funding in 2026. Managing Director: David Conrad.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Training curriculum explicitly covers systemic risks from advanced AI including loss of control scenarios, geopolitical competition, economic disruption, and governance challenges. Alumni work focuses on frontier AI regulation, whistleblower protections, and international AI governance frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on 'safe and responsible deployment of advanced AI' through comprehensive European AI governance training. Curriculum covers regulatory approaches from principles-based to rules-based frameworks, emphasizing EU AI Act implementation and international coordination on AI risks.

organizations.name: Arkose Labs
organizations.location: San Mateo, CA (with offices in Brisbane, London, Costa Rica)
organizations.notes: Founded 2013 by Kevin Gosschalk. 250+ employees. Raised $114M+ from SoftBank Vision Fund, Microsoft M12, PayPal, Sony Innovation Fund. Commercial cybersecurity company developing fraud prevention platform 'Arkose Titan' with specialized focus on AI agent detection and classification. This appears to be a different organization from the AI safety nonprofit 'Arkose' mentioned in the original notes that closed due to lack of funding.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specializes in protecting against AI-powered fraud, bot attacks, account takeovers, and agentic AI threats. Develops detection systems for malicious AI agents while enabling legitimate automation. Focus is on commercial security rather than existential AI risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focused on commercial fraud prevention and cybersecurity rather than AI safety regulation. Advocates for enterprise preparedness against AI-powered attacks and supports 'Know Your Agent' frameworks for distinguishing legitimate from malicious AI agents.

organizations.name: Global Priorities Institute (University of Oxford)
organizations.notes: Founded 2018, closed in 2025. Was an interdisciplinary research center at Oxford conducting foundational research on global priorities and longtermism. Received $3.3M from Open Philanthropy in 2020. Notable publications include 'AI alignment vs AI ethical treatment: Ten challenges' (2024) and research on longtermism paradigm. Staff included Leopold Aschenbrenner (former OpenAI researcher who later founded $1.5B hedge fund) and Bradford Saad who co-authored key AI ethics research.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on the tension between AI alignment and ethical treatment of potentially sentient AI systems. Key concerns include the risks of creating unaligned superintelligent systems and the moral implications of mistreating AI systems that merit moral consideration, particularly in alignment processes involving whole-brain emulations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful consideration of AI development risks, including potential global moratoriums on advanced AI systems until adequate safety and ethical frameworks are established. Emphasizes the need for strategic funding of AI safety research and potential tax policies to discourage mistreatment of AI systems with moral status.

organizations.name: Cavendish Labs
organizations.notes: Founded 2023, 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization in rural Vermont. Offers fellowships for early-career researchers with $1500/month grants plus lodging. Received $88,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund in 2023. Research community focused on AI safety and pandemic prevention, with researchers living and working together. Key publications include work on unelicitable backdoors in language models via cryptographic transformer circuits.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on AI alignment techniques including provable guarantees in RL, ELK (Eliciting Latent Knowledge), inverse scaling, and backdoor detection/prevention. Investigates fundamental safety problems around making AI systems behave as intended.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing provable guarantees and safety techniques for AI systems, particularly in RL frameworks. Conducts research on unelicitable backdoors and alignment methods to ensure AI systems behave safely and as intended.

organizations.name: Coordinal Research
organizations.location: Remote/distributed (founded through MATS program with Berkeley/London connections)
organizations.notes: Founded by MATS alumni Jacques Thibodeau and Ronak Mehta (merged with Vectis AI). Nonprofit developing research automation tools and AI systems to accelerate alignment work. Secured $110k+ in seed funding through Catalyze Impact's funding circle. Currently in alpha phase with research workflow tool called 'Seed'. Part of broader trend of AI safety organizations adopting automation to compete with capabilities research pace.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on accelerating AI safety and alignment research through automation tools and AI-assisted research systems. Emphasizes the urgency of closing the gap between capabilities and alignment research before it widens further.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions found. Focus is on technical research automation and accelerating alignment research rather than policy advocacy. Appears to prioritize building tools and systems over regulatory interventions.

organizations.name: EquiStamp
organizations.location: Lewes, Delaware, US (remote workforce across 11 countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as Public Benefit Corporation. 18 employees across 11 countries with task-based contributor network. Revenue-generating through client contracts with METR, UK AISI, Redwood Research, and EU Commission. Specializes in evaluation implementation using frameworks like Inspect. Leadership includes CEO Chris Canal, CTO Daniel O'Connell, and AI Advisor Rob Miles. Raised $400k operating capital via Manifund SAFE agreement in 2025.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on AI evaluation infrastructure, red/blue teaming for adversarial testing, and safety evaluations. Builds technical tools to assess dangerous capabilities, alignment drift, and autonomous agent risks through partnerships with leading safety organizations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports technical implementation of AI safety evaluations and compliance infrastructure. Through AgentStamp subsidiary, develops tools for EU AI Act compliance including transparency disclosures, audit trails, and regulatory reporting for AI agents.

organizations.name: LawZero
organizations.notes: Founded June 2025 by Turing Award winner Yoshua Bengio. Nonprofit with ~30 employees and $60M total funding from Gates Foundation, Schmidt Sciences, Open Philanthropy, Future of Life Institute, and Jaan Tallinn. Key research focus on 'Scientist AI' - non-agentic systems using Bayesian inference for safe AI oversight. Recently announced major Canadian government investment of over $100M. Board includes Yuval Noah Harari, former Swedish PM Stefan Löfven, and other global leaders.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on developing non-agentic AI systems to prevent dangerous behaviors like deception, self-preservation, and goal misalignment. Research centers on 'Scientist AI' - systems trained to understand and predict without autonomous goals, designed to serve as guardrails for agentic AI systems and accelerate scientific discovery safely.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI safety-first approaches through technical solutions rather than purely regulatory measures. Focuses on developing non-agentic 'Scientist AI' systems that can serve as guardrails for more dangerous agentic AI systems. Supports government regulation but emphasizes the need for technical safeguards built into AI systems by design.

organizations.name: Luthien
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 by Jai Dhyani, senior FAANG ML engineer with decade of experience deploying AI at billion+ user scale. Non-profit organization building on Redwood Research's AI Control research. Received seed grant from AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund. Part of Catalyze's Winter 2024/25 London incubation cohort. Currently seeking $500K to grow Seattle team with SDEs and PM for coordination with research labs and frontier AI companies.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specifically addresses AI Control scenarios where models may act adversarially, including scheming, jailbreaking, data poisoning, alignment-faking, and stochastic misbehavior. Develops automated frameworks for stress-testing AI systems and deployment tooling under Control measures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing production-ready AI Control solutions for immediate deployment, emphasizing practical risk mitigation through oversight and management systems that prevent misaligned AI from achieving harmful goals.

organizations.name: Meaning Alignment Institute
organizations.location: Berlin, Germany + San Francisco, United States
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Joe Edelman. 30+ researcher network with 3 core team members. Funded by ARIA, OpenAI, SFF, and others. Key research areas include democratic fine-tuning (won OpenAI Democratic Inputs grant), moral graph elicitation, and 'thick models of value.' Released WiseLLaMa-8B model trained on values-based conversations. Collaborates with major AI labs on alignment research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
