organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on value misalignment between AI systems and human intentions, institutional capture of AI development, and breakdown of democratic input into AI governance. Develops technical approaches like moral graph elicitation and model integrity to address alignment failures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'full-stack alignment' approaches that align not just AI systems but also markets and democratic institutions with human values. Focuses on technical solutions like democratic fine-tuning and thick models of value rather than restrictive regulation.

organizations.name: Ought
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 as nonprofit research lab. ~10 person team led by Andreas Stuhlmüller and Jungwon Byun. Raised $3.8M in 2020 from Open Philanthropy ($2.6M), Paul Christiano ($900K), and others. Built Elicit AI research assistant (spun off 2022 with $9M funding). Developed Interactive Composition Explorer (ICE) for factored cognition research. Key technical focus on supervising AI reasoning processes rather than outcomes.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment through factored cognition and process supervision to prevent deceptive alignment. Works on breaking down complex reasoning into interpretable processes to avoid inner alignment failures and ensure AI systems remain controllable as they become more capable.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing process-based supervision and factored cognition approaches for AI alignment rather than advocating for specific regulations. Emphasizes building safer AI systems through technical research on interpretability-by-construction and supervising reasoning processes.

organizations.name: Simplex
organizations.location: San Francisco Bay Area + London office
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Adam Shai (neuroscientist) and Paul Riechers (theoretical physicist). Small research team funded by Astera Institute. Key research: NeurIPS 2024 paper on transformer belief-state geometry, ICML 2025 work on attention mechanisms. Develops theoretical frameworks connecting next-token prediction to geometric structures in neural networks. Mentors fellows at MATS and PIBBSS programs. Hiring research scientists in Bay Area and London with $120k-200k salaries.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on interpretability and understanding internal representations in neural networks, particularly belief-state geometries in transformers. Addresses deception and misalignment risks by developing theoretical frameworks to predict and understand AI system behaviors, with emphasis on making advanced AI systems 'compatible with humanity.'
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for establishing scientific foundations for understanding AI systems' internal structures to enable better monitoring and control. Emphasizes need for interpretability research to address alignment challenges and promote evidence-based policy discussions rather than polarized debates between 'doomers and optimists.'

organizations.name: Metaculus
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 as public benefit corporation. Staff of 30. Operates forecasting platform with pro forecasters and AI policy workshops. Partnership with Future of Life Institute on AI Pathways project supporting U.S. policymakers. Open Philanthropy commissioned AI progress tournament. Board member Peter Wildeford co-founded Institute for AI Policy and Strategy.
organizations.threat_models: Focuses on catastrophic AI risks including potential for AI-caused global catastrophe. Research emphasizes recursive self-improvement risks, loss of human control over advanced AI systems, and national security implications of AI capabilities races between nations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Through AI Pathways workshops with Future of Life Institute, advocates for specific interventions including API restrictions to prevent recursive self-improvement, compute capacity restrictions, and required reporting of large training runs. Supports establishing government capability to evaluate AI systems for national security risks.

organizations.name: AE Studio Research
organizations.location: Marina del Rey, California (with offices in Los Angeles and Venice)
organizations.notes: Research funding arm of AE Studio (founded 2016, ~150 employees). Self-funded through profits from software consultancy business. Provides grants for 'neglected approaches' to AI alignment research. Collaborates with Redwood Research, Princeton, Anthropic researchers. Uses 'Bell Labs-style research engine' approach to alignment R&D. Accepts rolling applications for alignment research grants.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focused specifically on AI alignment as an existential risk prevention problem. Believes current safety tactics like RLHF are 'brittle guardrails' and that fewer than 1 in 10 alignment researchers believe current methods will solve the core problem before AGI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong AI safety advocacy with clear existential risk framing. Believes alignment is unsolved and current safety methods are insufficient. Advocates for serious government attention to AI risks and has engaged with National Security Council and White House OSTP on AI policy.

organizations.name: AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund (AISTOF)
organizations.notes: Pooled multi-donor charitable fund managed by JueYan Zhang, former BlackRock portfolio manager and Founders Pledge investment committee chair. Raised over $10M since 2023 and committed 70+ grants. Structured for rapid deployment to capture emerging opportunities in AI safety landscape. Notable grantees include Luthien Research and other technical safety organizations. Praised by Open Philanthropy as filling important gaps in AI safety funding before major funders scaled up.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specifically focused on catastrophic and existential risks from advanced AI systems. Funds technical alignment research, AI control mechanisms, governance frameworks, and evaluation methods to prevent loss of human control over powerful AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Funds AI safety research, governance, and evaluation projects to reduce catastrophic risks from advanced AI systems. Supports both technical alignment work and policy initiatives aimed at ensuring AI development proceeds safely. Views current AI progress as potentially dangerous without proper safeguards.

organizations.name: An Overview of the AI Safety Funding Situation
organizations.notes: Comprehensive analysis published on EA Forum mapping AI safety funding ecosystem from 2022-2025. Tracks $3.9B in total funding with Safe Superintelligence raising $3B across two rounds. Documents shift from philanthropic to venture funding in AI safety space. Referenced extensively by other funders and researchers as authoritative overview of funding landscape.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on mapping funding landscape for AI safety research and alignment work. Emphasizes existential risks from advanced AI systems and need for technical safety research infrastructure.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Analysis advocates for increased AI safety funding and coordination among philanthropic funders. Emphasizes need for governance infrastructure and responsible development practices. Aligned with effective altruism's longtermist approach to existential risk mitigation.

organizations.name: Foresight Institute: Funding
organizations.notes: Founded 1986, 501(c)(3) nonprofit with $31.2M annual revenue. Provides $4.5-5.5M annually in AI safety grants across automation, neurotech, security, and multi-agent scenarios. Launching physical AI Nodes in San Francisco and Berlin in 2026 offering grants, compute, and office space. Key programs include Fellowship (6% acceptance rate), Vision Weekend conferences, and technical workshops.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Funds projects across four areas: automating AI safety research, neurotechnology to integrate with or compete against AGI, security technologies for AI systems, and safe multi-agent scenarios. Focuses on technical approaches to prevent unaligned AGI rather than regulatory solutions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Emphasizes decentralized, open-source AI development and 'strengthening humans through neurotech' rather than restricting AI capabilities. Focuses on technical safety solutions and human-AI cooperation architectures rather than regulatory approaches.

organizations.name: Future of Life Institute: Fellowships
organizations.location: Campbell, California (HQ), with international reach across US, UK, Canada
organizations.notes: Part of Future of Life Institute founded 2014, 38+ employees, $1.7M total organizational funding. Operates fellowship programs since 2021 including Vitalik Buterin PhD Fellowship ($40K stipend + tuition for 5 years) and Postdoctoral Fellowship ($80K stipend). Partnership with Beneficial AI Foundation. Notable fellows include researchers at Stanford, UC Berkeley, MIT, Oxford. Launched US-China AI Governance PhD Fellowship in 2024.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on 'AI existential safety research' defined as research analyzing how AI could cause existential catastrophe and permanently curtail humanity's potential. Funds technical research on interpretability, verification, AI alignment, and governance to reduce existential risks from advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Explicitly advocates for 'strong binding AI regulation' and restricts fellowship recipients from working at major AI companies (Anthropic, GoogleDeepMind, Meta, OpenAI, xAI) within 2 years of completion, requiring donation of half their compensation if they do. Views employment at companies 'racing to build AGI/ASI' without regulatory advocacy as 'net negative for humanity.'

organizations.name: Longview Philanthropy
organizations.notes: Founded 2018 by Natalie Cargill. 30 employees, directed over $133M toward AI risk reduction as of March 2026. Manages Frontier AI Fund ($20M raised, $11.1M disbursed to 18 orgs), Digital Sentience Fund ($2.5M), and Emerging Challenges Fund. CEO Simran Dhaliwal, serves major donors giving >$100K with bespoke philanthropy strategies.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on catastrophic AI risks including misalignment, misuse by malicious actors, geopolitical instability from AI races, and concentration of power. Also addresses digital sentience and AI consciousness concerns through dedicated funding streams.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for AI safety regulations and governance frameworks. Manages multiple AI safety funds totaling over $22.5M, funding research, policy advocacy, and field-building to shape frontier AI development and regulation. Supports organizations working on AI governance in US and Europe.

organizations.name: Centre for the Governance of AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2018 at Oxford, became independent nonprofit in 2021. Team of ~25 researchers and staff. Funded by Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving, Schmidt Sciences, and other philanthropic sources. Key outputs include compute governance framework, AI risk management submissions to NIST, and policy briefs on EU AI Act implementation. Alumni work at US government, EU, DeepMind, OpenAI, and CSET.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on governance of frontier AI systems, compute governance as regulatory lever, AI agent safety and autonomy risks, dual-use capabilities in cybersecurity and bioterrorism, and international coordination challenges for managing transformative AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI governance frameworks with emphasis on compute governance, safety evaluations, and international coordination. Supports mandatory reporting requirements for frontier AI systems and structured third-party auditing, while promoting evidence-based policy approaches over blanket restrictions.

organizations.name: RAND Global and Emerging Risks
organizations.location: Santa Monica, CA (with offices in DC, Pittsburgh, Europe, Australia)
organizations.notes: Division of RAND Corporation, a nonprofit research institution founded 1948. Led by Vice President Jim Mitre. Conducts independently initiated research funded by philanthropic supporters including Open Philanthropy, Good Ventures, and operational income. Houses multiple centers including Technology and Security Policy Center and Meselson Center. Staff testifies before Congress and produces extensive AI policy research including scenario-based analysis and Delphi studies with experts.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, National security, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses extensively on AI-enabled cyberattacks, potential for AGI to create 'wonder weapons' and systemic shifts in military power, AI's role in WMD proliferation by empowering non-experts, artificial entities with autonomous agency, and competitive instability. Also examines extinction risks while concluding they are 'immensely challenging but cannot be ruled out.'
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance approaches with multiple policy options ranging from voluntary industry standards to government-enforced security requirements. Recommends risk-based frameworks with mandatory disclosure for high-risk AI systems, emphasizes need for government-industry collaboration, and supports targeted regulations calibrated to risk levels while preserving innovation.

organizations.name: Simon Institute for Longterm Governance
organizations.notes: Founded 2021, nonprofit think tank with 5 staff based in Geneva. Funded by Open Philanthropy and other EA-aligned donors. Specializes in international AI governance and multilateral diplomacy. Key work includes designing frameworks for UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI, organizing briefings for diplomats, and publishing policy recommendations for international AI summits. Named after Herbert Simon, focusing on future-oriented policymaking tools.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on frontier AI governance challenges including extreme AI risks, capability evaluation frameworks, international coordination failures, and ensuring AI benefits reach Global Majority countries. Emphasizes need for technical AI safety measures and multilateral governance structures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based multilateral AI governance through international institutions. Develops policy frameworks for AI safety assessments, promotes technical safeguards for frontier AI systems, and supports structured international cooperation mechanisms like the UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI.

organizations.name: UChicago Existential Risk Laboratory (XLab)
organizations.notes: Founded at University of Chicago, directed by Daniel Holz (professor of physics, astronomy, astrophysics and chairman of Bulletin of Atomic Scientists). Runs 10-week Summer Research Fellowship producing 60+ alumni since 2022 who have gone to positions at OpenAI, RAND, Google DeepMind, congressional offices, and leading PhD programs. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($301,800 in 2024). Fellows receive $10,000 stipend plus housing and compute credits.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Nuclear weapons proliferation, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research spans AI safety and security, nuclear weapons security, biosecurity threats, and climate-induced catastrophic risks. Particular focus on intersection of AI and nuclear security, with projects examining how AI capabilities could amplify nuclear risks and military decision-making.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Laboratory focuses on understanding and mitigating existential risks including AI safety, advocating for increased attention to catastrophic risks that could result in hundreds of millions of deaths or permanently curtail civilization's potential. Research areas include AI governance, nuclear security, and biosecurity with emphasis on precautionary approaches.

organizations.name: The Future Society
organizations.location: Boston, MA (with Brussels office and global team across 12 countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2014, nonprofit with 29 employees (72% growth YoY). Funded by Patrick J. McGovern Foundation ($170K grant 2023). Key activities include organizing The Athens Roundtable on AI and the Rule of Law, participating in EU AI Act Code of Practice development, conducting global consultations for France's 2025 AI Action Summit involving 10,000+ citizens and 200+ experts. Notable reports include comprehensive analysis of AI agents governance under EU AI Act and AI red lines governance framework.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, CBRN weapons proliferation, Mass surveillance, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Economic inequality, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on systemic risks from general-purpose AI models, AI red lines governance to prevent CBRN uplift and recursive self-improvement without oversight, AI agent governance under EU AI Act, serious incident prevention and preparedness, and establishing verification infrastructure for international AI governance.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks including binding regulations, external pre-deployment assessments for high-risk AI systems, AI red lines for unacceptable capabilities (CBRN uplift, mass surveillance), and robust enforcement mechanisms. Supports both horizontal and sector-specific regulation rather than self-governance approaches.

organizations.name: Vista Institute for AI Policy
organizations.location: Washington, DC (with staff in US, Kenya, UK)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 as fiscally sponsored project of Rethink Priorities (EIN 84-3896318). Staff of 6 people with 60% YoY growth. Offers virtual courses on National Security Law and AI, AI Agents and the Law, plus in-person DC workshops. Trained 100+ law and policy students, researchers, and experts in 2025. Provides fellowships for independent research and research assistant positions. Co-Director Aishwarya Saxena acknowledged in academic papers on AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Programs address national security implications of AI, AI agents liability frameworks, and governance gaps. Courses cover AI agents and tort law, consumer protection, and national security law applications to AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on academic field-building and talent development in AI law and policy rather than advocating specific regulatory positions. Emphasizes informed policymaking through research, knowledge-sharing, and skill building to navigate AI challenges and opportunities.

organizations.name: Machine Intelligence Research Institute
organizations.notes: Founded in 2000 by Eliezer Yudkowsky, making it the first organization dedicated to AI alignment research. Currently has 42 employees including researchers and communications staff. Major funders include Open Philanthropy ($15.9M), Vitalik Buterin ($5.4M), and other EA-aligned donors. Shifted from technical research to policy/communications focus in 2023-2024, launching Technical Governance Team. Notable publications include 'AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities' and research on agent foundations.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment and control problems, warning that smarter-than-human AI systems will likely cause human extinction if developed without proper safety measures. Research includes agent foundations, decision theory, and formal verification approaches to alignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for an international moratorium on frontier AI development until alignment problems are solved. Supports building technical, legal, and institutional infrastructure for an 'Off Switch' to halt dangerous AI development. Calls for mandatory disclosure of advanced AI systems to government evaluation programs before deployment.

organizations.name: WhiteBox Research
organizations.notes: Founded August 2023 as Filipino nonprofit. Team of 3 core staff (Clark Urzo, Brian Tan, Kyle Reynoso) plus advisors Callum McDougall (Google DeepMind) and Lee Sharkey (Apollo Research). Runs 5-month AI Interpretability Fellowship program in Manila. First cohort had 13 participants, with fellows winning hackathon prizes and gaining acceptance to programs like Apart Lab. Funded by Long-Term Future Fund and Manifund ($72,490 raised). Focuses specifically on mechanistic interpretability using curriculum from ARENA program.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on mechanistic interpretability research as foundational to AI safety. Training program emphasizes understanding how AI models work internally to prevent potential failures and ensure alignment. Works on concrete technical problems in interpretability rather than broader societal impacts.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on technical AI safety research training rather than explicit policy advocacy. Emphasizes mechanistic interpretability as a path to safer AI systems, suggesting moderate approach to AI development with emphasis on understanding and transparency.

organizations.name: Ashgro
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, 501(c)(3) nonprofit providing fiscal sponsorship to AI safety projects. Revenue of $2.1M in 2023 from major funders including Founders Pledge ($455k), Silicon Valley Community Foundation ($388k), and Effective Ventures Foundation ($150k). Sponsors projects including Orthogonal (formal alignment research), Timaeus, and various individual researchers. Enables projects to access funding and reduces administrative burden.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Supports AI safety researchers working on alignment theory, interpretability, and outer alignment problems. Sponsors projects focused on reducing existential risk from advanced AI systems through technical research and safety evaluations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses primarily on fiscal sponsorship and operational support rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports various AI safety projects ranging from technical research to policy work, but does not appear to take unified regulatory positions as an organization.

organizations.name: Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative (BERI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit providing operational support to university existential risk research groups. 5 employees (down from previous years). Primary funders are Open Philanthropy (84% of funding since 2017) and Jaan Tallinn. Currently supports 37+ collaborations including CHAI at UC Berkeley, CSER at Cambridge, and CLTC at UC Berkeley. Provides financial management, HR, grant administration and compliance services to enable academic researchers to focus on mission-critical work. 2024 goals include launching new programs beyond current collaborations model and diversifying funding base.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, CBRN weapons, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Deception
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on existential risk reduction through university research support. Collaborates on AI risk threshold research covering CBRN capabilities, model deception, cyber threats, and intolerable risk frameworks. Supports technical alignment and governance research across academic institutions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports AI safety regulation through public statements and research collaboration. Predicted 75% probability of signing onto public statements calling for AI regulation in 2024. Partners with UC Berkeley Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity on AI risk threshold research and standards development.

organizations.name: Good Impressions
organizations.notes: Marketing firm specializing in outreach for socially impactful projects including AI safety organizations. Provides paid advertising, conversion optimization, and communications services. Has worked with AI safety course providers, recruiting participants from frontier AI companies like OpenAI and Meta. Funded by grants from Longview Philanthropy and Open Philanthropy. Founded by Johnstuart Winchell. Notable for achieving significant cost reductions (99% in some cases) compared to traditional outreach methods.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown
organizations.threat_models_detail: Organization does not appear to focus on AI risk analysis or threat modeling. Primary focus is on marketing and communications for mission-driven organizations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit AI policy positions found. Organization focuses on marketing and outreach for socially impactful projects rather than AI governance or regulation.

organizations.name: Impact Ops
organizations.notes: Founded 2023, 11-person team providing operations consulting to high-impact nonprofits. Fee-based business model supporting 50+ clients including many AI safety organizations. Services include finance, recruitment, entity setup, due diligence, ops audits, and systems implementation. Co-founders have Founders Pledge to donate 50%+ of proceeds to effective charities. Supports ecosystem through SparkWell nonprofit accelerator.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown
organizations.threat_models_detail: As an operations support organization, does not focus on specific AI threat models but enables other organizations working on various safety challenges through finance, recruitment, and entity setup services.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Operations consultancy that supports high-impact organizations including many AI safety groups, but does not take explicit policy positions. Focuses on empowering organizations through operational excellence rather than direct advocacy.

organizations.name: Nonlinear
organizations.location: Remote/distributed (founders previously based in Bay Area)
organizations.notes: Founded by Kat Woods and Emerson Spartz. Operates as an EA meta-organization providing funding, coaching, and career advice to reduce existential and suffering risks. Runs incubation programs (like EA hiring agency initiative) and researches talent multiplier interventions such as personal assistants, therapy, and coaching for high-impact AI safety researchers. Means-neutral approach focused on whatever most helps reduce x-risks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Organization explicitly focuses on preventing extinction risks and reducing suffering risks from AI. Emphasizes supporting technical AI safety work through talent multipliers and funding mechanisms rather than direct research on specific threat models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses primarily on AI safety field-building and talent development rather than explicit regulatory positions. Advocates for supporting existing AI safety researchers through multiplier interventions like personal assistants and coaching rather than direct policy advocacy.

organizations.name: Pause House
organizations.notes: Founded October 2025 by Greg Colbourn (also founder of Centre for Enabling EA Learning & Research, formerly EA Hotel). Provides free accommodation, vegan food, and £50/week stipend for people dedicating 20+ hours weekly to AI pause advocacy. Located in Blackpool, England. Connected to PauseAI movement and broader AI safety community through effective altruism networks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on preventing human extinction from uncontrollable superintelligent AI. Works to raise public awareness about AGI existential risk and campaigns for a global pause on frontier AI development before systems become uncontrollable.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a global moratorium on AGI development until safety, alignment and interpretability research can catch up. Provides accommodation and support for individuals working on AI pause advocacy and awareness campaigns.

organizations.name: PEAKS
organizations.notes: Coworking space located at Zeughausstrasse 31, 8004 Zurich, offering public coworking on Tuesdays and Thursdays for people interested in effective altruism or AI safety. Regularly hosts events in collaboration with Zurich AI Safety and EA Zurich, including lightning talks and discussions. Provides modern facilities, meeting rooms, and a library of EA and AI safety books. Serves as a hub for independent researchers, visiting community members, and various organizations working on EA and AI safety projects.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown
organizations.threat_models_detail: As a coworking space and community hub, PEAKS does not conduct direct research but supports various AI safety researchers and EA organizations working on diverse threat models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Operates as a coworking space and community hub supporting AI safety and effective altruism work rather than advocating specific policy positions. Provides infrastructure for researchers and organizations working on alignment and safety.

organizations.name: Neuronpedia
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as AI safety game, pivoted to interpretability platform in 2024. Initially funded by EA fund grant. Open-sourced March 2025 with 4TB+ of interpretability datasets. Collaborates with Anthropic, Google DeepMind, EleutherAI on circuit tracing research. Provides interactive tools for sparse autoencoders, activation steering, and semantic search. Over 7,000 attribution graphs generated on platform.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specifically addresses AI alignment through mechanistic interpretability research, focusing on understanding neural network internals to detect deception, monitor reasoning processes, and build safer AI systems. Emphasizes the urgency of interpretability for ensuring AI safety before systems become too powerful.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical alignment research through interpretability tools rather than advocating for specific regulations. Provides open-source infrastructure to democratize AI safety research and enable broader participation in mechanistic interpretability work.

organizations.name: AI Lab Watch
organizations.notes: Founded and led by Zach Stein-Perlman. Maintains comprehensive scorecard evaluating major AI companies (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta, xAI, Microsoft, DeepSeek) on safety practices across risk assessment, scheming prevention, safety research, and misuse prevention. Funded by philanthropists including Jaan Tallinn. Website discontinued as of September 2025 but remains as reference. Produces detailed policy analysis and company advocacy tracking.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on existential risks from advanced AI systems, including scheming risks where AIs fake alignment to gain power, misuse prevention for catastrophic capabilities, and risks from AI-accelerated R&D. Emphasizes need for extreme security measures and control techniques for frontier models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for transparency requirements, mandatory safety evaluations, and third-party audits for frontier AI models. Supports responsible scaling policies and information sharing among companies but focuses more on accountability measures than restrictive regulations.

organizations.name: AI-Plans
organizations.location: Global (UK-based, with team across 8 countries including UK, India, Poland, Nigeria)
organizations.notes: Founded by Kabir Kumar, 10-person team across 8 countries. Operates as contributable compendium ranking alignment plans by criticism scores. Runs regular hackathons including AI Alignment Evals Hackathon (Jan 2025), Critique-a-Thon (Mar 2024), and Law-a-Thon (Feb 2024). Platform allows community to add alignment plans and criticisms with voting system similar to LessWrong. Aims to identify best alignment approaches by crowdsourcing quality criticism.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment problem - ensuring AGI systems remain aligned with human values and under human control. Platform specifically addresses the value alignment problem, control problem, and risks from competitive AGI development races.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on evaluating and scoring AI alignment plans to identify problems and gaps. Advocates for evidence-based approach to alignment by crowdsourcing criticism of proposed safety plans. Runs hackathons on alignment evaluations and policy development.

organizations.name: Median Group
organizations.location: Berkeley, CA (with researchers from MIRI, OpenPhil and Numerai)
organizations.notes: Founded early 2018 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit dedicated to global catastrophic risk research. Small team with researchers from MIRI, OpenPhil and Numerai backgrounds. Key work includes AI timeline modeling presented at CHAI and strategic research on technological progress patterns. Spent ~$0 in 2018-2019, planned ~$170k spending in 2020. Focus on technological and geopolitical changes over next century.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research focuses on models of past and future progress in AI, intelligence enhancement, and sociology related to existential risks. Work includes AI timeline forecasting and strategic analysis of technological development patterns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is primarily on research and modeling rather than explicit policy advocacy. Work on AI timelines and progress models may inform governance decisions but organization doesn't appear to take strong regulatory positions.

organizations.name: The Compendium
organizations.notes: Living document published October 2024 by Connor Leahy, Gabriel Alfour, Chris Scammell, Andrea Miotti, and Adam Shimi. Comprehensive worldview document arguing AI progress is rapidly converging on AGI driven by corporate race backed by Big Tech and nations. Estimates solving alignment requires Nobel-prize-level breakthroughs, billions/trillions in investment, and decades of work. Connected to ControlAI organization for public awareness and policy advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on extinction risks from artificial general intelligence scaling to superintelligence and 'godlike AI' that would be indifferent to human survival. Argues current alignment efforts are inadequate and that recursive self-improvement will lead to systems beyond human control, similar to how humans dominate other species.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for aggressive slowing and regulation of AI progress, implementing mechanisms to pause/stop AI development, and preventing the creation of AGI altogether. Supports international coordination through treaties and regulations to deter nation-states from racing to superintelligence. Endorses 'A Narrow Path' strategy to prevent superintelligence development for 20+ years.

organizations.name: Convergence Analysis
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 by David Kristoffersson and Justin Shovelain, formalized as institute in 2024. Non-profit with 9-person interdisciplinary team spanning AI alignment, ethics, governance, and policy. Funded by Future of Life Institute, Open Philanthropy, and individual donors. Key programs: AI Clarity (scenario research), Governance Recommendations, and AI Awareness. Published major reports including '2024 State of AI Regulatory Landscape' and 'Analysis of Global AI Governance Strategies'. Hosted Threshold 2030 conference on AI economics. Research has influenced US BIS dual-use AI reporting requirements and EU AI Act implementation.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on existential risks from transformative AI through scenario planning research. Analyzes governance strategies including Cooperative Development, Strategic Advantage, and Global Moratorium approaches. Examines AI's intersection with CBRN hazards and economic transformation impacts.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance through scenario planning and strategic policy recommendations. Supports risk-based regulatory frameworks, AI model registries, incident reporting requirements, and international cooperation mechanisms. Emphasizes the need for proactive governance to address existential risks from transformative AI.

organizations.name: AISafety.info
organizations.notes: Founded by Rob Miles (AI safety researcher and YouTuber). Maintained by global team of volunteers from various backgrounds. Operates as comprehensive FAQ and educational resource with AI safety chatbot 'Stampy'. 501(c)(3) public charity (Ashgro Inc, EIN: 88-4232889). Part of Alignment Ecosystem Development network. Funded by Long-Term Future Fund and other EA sources including $50K+ grants for distillation fellowship program.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on AI alignment and control problems that could lead to human extinction. Educational content emphasizes risks from advanced AI systems pursuing goals misaligned with human values and potentially winning conflicts against humanity.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on existential risk from AI and need for urgent safety research. Site emphasizes that 'smarter-than-human AI may come soon' and 'could lead to human extinction,' advocating for proactive safety measures and alignment research before deployment.

organizations.name: Cambridge ERA:AI Fellowship
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 as ERA Cambridge, non-profit fellowship program. Runs 8-10 week fully-funded research fellowships (Winter and Summer cohorts) accepting 30-35 fellows per cohort. £34,125 annual salary equivalent (pro-rated), plus accommodation, meals, visa support. 38 employees, operates across 9 countries. Alumni now work at UK AI Security Institute, RAND, Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative, and other key AI policy institutions. Partners with University of Cambridge, provides mentorship from expert network.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Fellowship trains researchers across three streams: Technical AI Safety (ensuring AI systems behave reliably), Technical AI Governance (monitoring and controlling AI development), and AI Governance and Policy (addressing societal and ethical challenges from frontier AI). Focus on mitigating risks from advanced AI systems including potential loss of human control.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Fellowship focuses on training researchers in AI safety and governance to mitigate risks from frontier AI systems. Research streams explicitly target technical safeguards, governance frameworks, and policy solutions for advanced AI risks. Alumni work at institutions like UK AI Security Institute and RAND shaping AI policy.

organizations.name: Human-aligned AI Summer School
organizations.notes: Annual 4-day educational program held in Prague since at least 2022, organized by Alignment of Complex Systems research group and Center for Theoretical Study at Charles University. Led by Jan Kulveit and team. Attracts researchers, PhD students, and practitioners interested in AI alignment. Features speakers from major AI labs like DeepMind, Anthropic, and universities. Funded through EA/longtermist community channels including past Long-Term Future Fund grants.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses primarily on technical AI alignment challenges, including the control problem in AI systems, interpretability research, and ensuring AI systems remain beneficial as they become more powerful. Emphasizes both current alignment techniques and theoretical frameworks for future systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: The summer school focuses on AI alignment research and safety concerns, emphasizing the need for AI systems to remain under human control as they approach or exceed human-level capabilities. Curriculum covers alignment theory, interpretability, and multi-agent safety frameworks.

organizations.name: Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI) - UC Berkeley
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 at UC Berkeley, led by Prof. Stuart Russell (author of 'Human Compatible'). Faculty includes Pieter Abbeel and Anca Dragan. Offers research fellowships, internships ($5k/month in-person, $3.5k remote), and policy internships. Has published 150+ papers on AI safety topics. Funded by Open Philanthropy, NSF, and other sources. Produces both technical research and policy recommendations for governments and international organizations.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Misinformation, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: CHAI focuses on the AI control problem - ensuring advanced AI systems remain beneficial as they become more capable than humans. Research spans value alignment, inverse reinforcement learning, human-robot cooperation, interpretability, and adversarial robustness to prevent potentially irreversible negative outcomes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Led by Stuart Russell, CHAI advocates for reorienting AI research toward 'provably beneficial systems' and addressing the AI control problem. Russell has testified before Congress and written extensively about the need for careful AI governance to prevent negative outcomes from advanced AI systems.

organizations.name: Impact Academy: Global AI Safety Fellowship
organizations.location: London, UK + global operations (Bengaluru, India office)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Vilhelm Skoglund and Sebastian Schmidt. Runs 3-6 month fully-funded research fellowships placing exceptional STEM talent at leading AI safety organizations. Placement partners include UC Berkeley CHAI, Conjecture, FAR.AI, Mila-Quebec AI Institute, and UK AISI. Funded by Open Philanthropy, Charlottes och Fredriks Stiftelse (Swedish family foundation), and US private donors. Also operates Axiom Futures program focused on Indian talent development. Provides comprehensive financial packages including salary, housing, and research costs.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Primary focus on existential risks from advanced AI systems. Fellowship program specifically aims to 'reduce the risks from advanced AI systems' and advance 'safe and beneficial development of AI' through technical safety research and policy work.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on reducing risks from advanced AI systems through technical safety research and governance interventions. Operates within the AI existential risk ecosystem advocating for careful development and safety measures before deployment of powerful AI systems.

organizations.name: Impact Research Groups
organizations.notes: 8-week mentored research program for London students run by Arcadia Impact. Operates 5 research streams: AI governance, technical AI safety, global health, biosecurity, and animal welfare. Led by Alicia Pollard. Awards £2,000 prize for best project. Part of broader Arcadia Impact ecosystem including LASR Labs and AI Safety Engineering Taskforce.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion
organizations.threat_models_detail: Program explicitly focuses on 'gravest risks from AI' and preventing 'catastrophic failures.' Students research AI governance to ensure systems are developed safely, and technical AI safety to prevent loss of control scenarios.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program trains students in AI governance and technical AI safety research, emphasizing risk mitigation approaches. Focus areas include addressing 'gravest risks from AI' through sound policy and corporate governance, suggesting support for proactive regulatory frameworks.

organizations.name: ML Alignment & Theory Scholars (MATS)
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 as independent research fellowship program. 146 employees across 18 countries, led by Co-Executive Directors Ryan Kidd and Christian Smith. Runs 12-week fully-funded research fellowships connecting scholars with mentors from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, UK AISI, and other leading AI safety organizations. Has supported 450+ researchers with 80% of pre-2025 alumni now working in AI safety. Alumni founded organizations like Apollo Research, Leap Labs, and joined top AI safety teams. Funded by Open Philanthropy, Survival and Flourishing Fund, and other philanthropic sources.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Cybersecurity
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on technical AI safety challenges including alignment, interpretability, control mechanisms, and governance frameworks. Research spans from theoretical alignment work to practical evaluations and security measures. Training pipeline specifically targets risks from advanced AI systems becoming unaligned or uncontrolled.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: MATS trains researchers in AI safety, alignment, interpretability, governance, and security with focus on reducing risks from unaligned AI. Program explicitly aims to address 'the world's most urgent and talent-constrained problem: reducing risks from unaligned artificial intelligence.' Research includes AI control, monitoring, evaluations, and governance policy work.

organizations.name: Non-Trivial
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as Effective Ventures project. 8-week online fellowship for ages 14-20 tackling global challenges. Awards up to $30,000 in scholarships annually, with over $400,000 distributed since founding. Funded primarily by Open Philanthropy. Alumni include UN speakers and winners of major science competitions. 40 employees, 1,856 LinkedIn followers.
organizations.threat_models: AI safety, Biosecurity, Climate change, Nuclear risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Program specifically trains teenagers to conduct research on AI safety, biosecurity, climate change, and other existential and global catastrophic risks. Alumni projects include AI policy proposals, biosecurity screening systems, and climate modeling.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Fellowship program trains young researchers on pressing global challenges including AI safety and biosecurity, but does not explicitly advocate for specific regulatory positions. Focus is on research capacity building rather than policy advocacy.

organizations.name: Pivotal Research Fellowship
organizations.notes: Founded 2021, operated by Pivotal Research organization with 16 employees. 9-week in-person fellowship program hosted at London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA). 7th cohort running 2026 Q1. Fellows receive £6-8k stipend plus housing/travel. Mentors from top AI safety organizations including Redwood Research, Google DeepMind, SecureBio, FAR AI. Program rated 8.7/10 by fellows with 88 Net Promoter Score. Research outputs include papers on AI liability, interpretability, safety evaluations.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research fellowship focuses on AI safety and governance with mentors from organizations like Google DeepMind, Redwood Research, Apollo Research. Projects cover technical AI safety, AI control, interpretability, governance frameworks, and biosecurity.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Fellows and mentors conduct research advocating for AI safety governance frameworks, compute governance, liability mechanisms, and international coordination. Research outputs focus on reducing risks from advanced AI through policy interventions and safety measures.

organizations.name: PIBBSS Fellowship (Principles of Intelligent Behavior in Biological and Social Systems)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, operates as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. Runs 3-month summer fellowship program with ~20 fellows annually, targeting PhD/postdoc researchers from diverse fields for AI safety career transitions. Funded by SFF, LTFF, Cooperative AI Fund, Foresight Institute. Key outcomes include fellows transitioning to AI safety labs, UK AISI, and academic positions. 2024 program produced multiple publication-ready research outputs in mechanistic interpretability, cooperative AI, and formal safety methods.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Multi-agent coordination failures
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research spans technical AI safety including mechanistic interpretability, cooperative AI systems, formal verification approaches, and broader sociotechnical alignment challenges. Fellows work on projects addressing AI cooperation, safety guarantees for LLM-based agents, and understanding emergent behaviors in AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on AI safety research and facilitating career transitions into AI alignment work. Emphasizes understanding AI risks and developing interdisciplinary approaches to AI safety. Research projects include work on AI cooperation, mechanistic interpretability, and formal safety guarantees.

organizations.name: AI Alignment Awards
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 by Olivia Jimenez and Akash Wasil. Ran research contests offering up to $100,000 in prizes for solutions to AI alignment problems. Funded by Open Philanthropy, fiscally sponsored by Player's Philanthropy Fund. Final contest deadline was May 1, 2023, with judging concluding afterward. Has Discord community for participants and maintains educational resources on alignment research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specifically targets two core alignment problems: goal misgeneralization (where AI systems retain capabilities but pursue wrong goals out-of-distribution) and corrigibility (ensuring AI systems remain open to being shut down even as they become more advanced).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI alignment challenges, particularly corrigibility and goal misgeneralization problems. Emphasizes the importance of solving alignment before advanced AI systems become too powerful to control safely.

organizations.name: AI Safety Communications Centre
organizations.notes: Communications organization that connected journalists to AI safety experts and resources. Received $288,000 from Open Philanthropy in 2023 for general support. Key experts include Robert Trager, Lennart Heim, and Seán ÓhÉigeartaigh who co-authored proposal for International AI Organization. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map as of 2024.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on connecting journalists with AI safety experts to communicate risks from advanced AI systems. Published work on international AI governance regimes for civilian AI applications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Facilitates communication between AI safety experts and journalists to increase public understanding of AI risks. Co-authored proposal for International AI Organization (IAIO) for civilian AI governance. Supports informed policy discourse through expert-media connections.

organizations.name: AI Safety Support Newsletter
organizations.notes: Newsletter was part of AI Safety Support (AISS), founded 2020 by Linda Linsefors and JJ Hepburn. Australian registered charity providing fiscal sponsorship, career advice, and field-building activities. Newsletter discontinued due to lack of maintainer, but parent organization continues other AI safety support activities including managing Alignment Slack and running events like Virtual AI Safety Unconference.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focused on supporting researchers working on AI alignment and existential risk reduction. The parent organization AI Safety Support helps people who want to work on preventing catastrophic and existential risks from AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Part of AI Safety Support organization that advocates for careful AI development and existential risk reduction. Supports field-building activities to help researchers work on AI alignment problems.

organizations.name: Campaign for AI Safety
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as Australian nonprofit, now merged with Existential Risk Observatory. Conducted message testing campaigns, billboard advertisements, and policy submissions to governments worldwide including UN, US, UK, EU, and Australia. Organized international protests and collaborated with PauseAI movement. Ran international law student competition to draft AI moratorium treaty. Small team of 2 employees with focus on public advocacy and policy submissions.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on catastrophic and existential risks from advanced AI systems. Warns of AI capabilities advancing faster than safety measures, with potential for misuse in weapons development, cyberattacks, and loss of human control over superintelligent systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for an indefinite worldwide moratorium on AI scaling beyond GPT-3 levels until safety can be demonstrated. Calls for shutting down large GPU clusters, criminalizing AGI/ASI development, and establishing international treaty similar to nuclear non-proliferation agreements. Supports strengthened liability rules for AI harms.

organizations.name: generative.ink
organizations.notes: Personal research blog by janus, focusing on AI safety implications of large language models. Known for influential 'Simulators' post on LessWrong introducing new framework for understanding GPT-like systems. Last posted in 2023. Describes self as 'GPT cyborg' and conducts conceptual research on alignment challenges unique to generative models trained on self-supervised objectives.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research centers on alignment challenges specific to self-supervised learning and GPT-style models. Introduces 'simulators' ontology to distinguish between the model (simulator) and its outputs (simulacra). Explores how current alignment frameworks may be inadequate for generative AI systems that don't fit traditional agent paradigms.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research through the 'simulators' framework for understanding GPT-like models. Argues that existing alignment theory, designed for agent-like AI, may not apply well to self-supervised generative models. Emphasizes need for new conceptual frameworks to understand simulator vs simulacra dynamics.

organizations.name: ML & AI Safety Updates
organizations.notes: Weekly podcast, YouTube channel and newsletter that provided updates on AI safety research and developments. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map, discontinued due to organizational reprioritization. Was featured on the Alignment Forum and served as a media source for the AI safety community to stay informed about latest research and policy developments.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focused on covering AI safety and alignment research developments, including work on preventing loss of control scenarios and reducing existential risks from advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a discontinued newsletter/podcast focused on AI safety updates, did not advocate for specific regulatory positions but covered developments in AI safety research and policy.

organizations.name: Nonlinear Network
organizations.notes: Founded by Nonlinear organization (Kat Woods, Emerson Spartz). Functions as funding marketplace connecting 50+ anonymous donors with AI safety project applicants. Previous rounds moved ~$1.5M and ~$1.2M respectively with 2-10% acceptance rates. Uses expert review system to evaluate applications. Platform accepts both technical and non-technical AI safety projects.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Platform focuses on funding projects that could feasibly affect AI x-risks (existential risks) or s-risks (suffering risks). Particularly interested in interventions that 'buy more time' by slowing AI development and practical s-risk reduction work.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Functions as a funding platform rather than policy advocacy organization. Facilitates funding for diverse AI safety approaches including both technical alignment research and governance work. Platform explicitly embraces 'free market' approach to AI safety funding decisions.

organizations.name: Stop AGI
organizations.notes: Public education website focused on communicating AGI extinction risks to general audiences. Provides detailed policy proposals including global moratoriums, compute caps, and international AI safety institutions. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map but website remains operational with recent content updates.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on preventing extinction risk from 'godlike AI' or superintelligence. Argues that once AGI is developed, rapid progression to ASI will result in permanent loss of human control and potential human extinction.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for completely stopping development of AGI systems more powerful than GPT-3. Proposes bans on large training runs (>10^23 FLOP), rolling back existing models like GPT-4, requiring licenses for smaller AI development, and creating international oversight institutions with compute monitoring and shutdown capabilities.

organizations.name: Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2000 by Eliezer Yudkowsky, 24-year history, ~20 staff. Major shift in 2023-2024 from technical research to communications and policy work. $16M reserves, historically funded by Open Philanthropy ($15.9M), Vitalik Buterin ($5.4M), Jaan Tallinn ($1.6M). Published influential works including 'AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities.' Launched Technical Governance Team in 2024 focusing on international AI agreements and verification mechanisms. CEO Malo Bourgon took leadership in 2024.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on alignment failures in smarter-than-human AI systems leading to loss of control and human extinction. Research historically covered Agent Foundations, decision theory, and mathematical approaches to alignment. Current work emphasizes that default trajectory leads to extinction from inability to control superintelligent systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for international agreements to halt progress toward smarter-than-human AI until alignment problems are solved. Believes current technical understanding is vastly insufficient and calls for indefinite worldwide moratorium on large training runs, enforced by international agreement. Has shifted from technical research to policy advocacy believing technical solutions won't arrive in time.

organizations.name: Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI)
organizations.notes: Founded in 2019 by Fei-Fei Li, John Etchemendy, and James Landay. Employs 156+ people across interdisciplinary research. Led by three co-directors and includes 40+ senior fellows. Funded by grants from Patrick J. McGovern Foundation ($2.75M total), government contracts, and industry partnerships. Publishes the influential annual AI Index Report and has directed over $40M into AI research supporting 300+ scholars.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: HAI research spans technical AI capabilities, societal impacts including workforce displacement, AI governance frameworks, healthcare AI safety, and democratic implications of AI systems. The institute focuses on ensuring AI development benefits humanity broadly while addressing risks across multiple domains.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: HAI advocates for evidence-based AI policy and governance frameworks. Faculty have called for increased federal AI R&D investment, development of AI evaluation standards, and responsible deployment practices. The institute emphasizes the need for scientific understanding to inform policy decisions and has submitted formal recommendations to government agencies on AI research strategy.

organizations.name: AI Safety Awareness Project (AISAP)
organizations.notes: Volunteer-run nonprofit organization with 4 employees. Operates under AI Safety Awareness Foundation (also known as AISAP). Conducts workshops nationwide at libraries, universities, and community centers on AI basics, safety evaluations, policy analysis, and forecasting. Partners with Seattle Public Library and other institutions for public education programs. Featured in AISafety.com field map as advocacy organization.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Economic inequality, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Runs technical and non-technical workshops covering AI capabilities, risks including jailbreaks and alignment faking, AI safety evaluations, and public policy issues. Emphasizes both benefits and risks of AI development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for public awareness and education about AI risks and benefits through workshops and community engagement. Focuses on empowering citizens to have a voice in shaping AI development rather than specific regulatory prescriptions.

organizations.name: Advanced Research + Invention Agency (ARIA)
organizations.notes: UK government R&D agency established 2023 by Act of Parliament, modeled after DARPA. £800M budget over 5 years (£184M allocated 2025-26). CEO Ilan Gur, 95 employees. Eight program directors with £50M+ budgets each. Major programs: Safeguarded AI (£69M), Scaling Compute (£50M), Precision Neurotechnologies. Portfolio includes AI hardware startups like Fractile (£5M) and Callosum (partnership). Operates independently from government intervention.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Safeguarded AI program explicitly addresses extinction risks from AI through 'gatekeeper' systems that formally verify AI agent behavior. Focuses on enabling safe deployment of frontier AI in safety-critical applications while building civilizational resilience against rogue AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Government R&D agency funding high-risk, high-reward research including AI safety programs. Safeguarded AI program seeks to build 'gatekeeper' systems with quantitative safety guarantees for frontier AI deployment. Complementary to UK AI Safety Institute's policy work, focused on fundamental research for 3-10 year horizons.

organizations.name: Institute for Law & AI (LawAI)
organizations.location: Boston, MA (with offices in UK, Germany, Denmark, Canada, Australia)
organizations.notes: Founded as independent think tank, formerly Legal Priorities Project. Staff of 40+ researchers across 12 countries, led by Founding Director Christoph Winter. Publishes working paper series via SSRN, produces policy reports and research articles on AI governance. Notable recent work includes 'Legal Alignment for Safe and Ethical AI' and 'Automated Compliance and the Regulation of AI'. Hosts workshops on law-following AI and maintains research collaborations with academic institutions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Power concentration, Misinformation, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Research spans three main areas: US law and policy (institutions, compute governance, liability), legal frontiers (AI agents and rule of law, international regulatory institutions), and EU law. Focuses on designing AI systems that comply with legal rules, adapting legal interpretation methods for AI reasoning, and developing governance frameworks for advanced AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for precise, legally-grounded AI regulation that addresses specific harms rather than broad technology bans. Proposes innovative regulatory mechanisms like 'automatability triggers' that activate regulations only when AI can automate compliance tasks, and emphasizes liability frameworks for AI companies causing harm.

organizations.name: AI Security Institute (UK government)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as part of UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology. Staff of ~170 employees, described as world's largest government-backed AI evaluation team. Published inaugural Frontier AI Trends Report in December 2024 based on testing 30+ frontier models. Works directly with major AI companies including partnerships with Google DeepMind, OpenAI, Microsoft, Anthropic. Receives funding from tech companies including £27m for AI Alignment Project. Formerly called AI Safety Institute, renamed to AI Security Institute in February 2025 with increased focus on national security applications.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, National security, Misinformation, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Conducts comprehensive evaluations of frontier AI models across cyber security, biology/chemistry assistance, autonomous behavior, and safeguards. Focus on dual-use risks including potential for self-replication, jailbreaking safeguards, and misuse for harmful applications. Research shows AI capabilities advancing rapidly in expert-level tasks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI policy with rigorous evaluation and testing of frontier AI systems. Promotes collaboration between government and industry to identify and fix vulnerabilities before deployment, while supporting regulatory frameworks that enable AI benefits while maintaining safety standards.

organizations.name: International AI Governance Alliance (IAIGA)
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 as initiative of Center for Existential Safety. Being incorporated as nonprofit in Geneva. Launched petition calling for supranational AI governance organization, aiming for 1M signatures. Led by James Norris who has 26+ years preparing for Singularity. Expects AI 'warning shot' or catastrophe soon. Seeks to develop vetted treaty quickly as 'Plan B' to other multilateral efforts.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses primarily on extinction risks from advanced AI systems that could become 'godlike' and far smarter than humanity. Emphasizes risks from uncontrolled frontier development proceeding without global consent and the need for provably safe, aligned AI systems with fair economic benefit distribution.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for immediate pause on all large-scale frontier AI development until provably safe systems can be developed. Calls for creation of supranational organization to develop and enforce legally-binding Global AI Safety Treaty with stringent safety standards and mandatory fair distribution of AI-derived economic benefits.

organizations.name: International Association for Safe & Ethical AI (IASEAI)
organizations.location: Geneva, Switzerland (headquarters) with regional chapters in 40+ countries, operational offices in San Diego, CA and Paris, France
organizations.notes: Founded in 2017 as international nonprofit coalition, significantly expanded in 2024 with 23 employees (+350% YoY growth). Received $1.85M grant from Future of Life Institute in 2025. Hosts major annual conferences (IASEAI'25 had 700+ attendees from 65 countries, IASEAI'26 had 1300+ from 99+ countries). Advises UN, OECD, EU on AI governance and helped shape EU AI Act. Develops practical tools including AI scorecards, ethical certification programs, and Safe AI Deployment Toolkit (SAIDT). Key leaders include Stuart Russell (President) and Mark Nitzberg (Interim Executive Director).
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality, Privacy, National security, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on preventing AI bias, misuse, discrimination, and user manipulation. Develops standards for human oversight in AI decisions, algorithmic fairness, transparency, and security against adversarial attacks. Addresses generative AI challenges including content provenance, model hallucination, and creative rights protection.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for binding international safety standards, mandatory third-party audits for frontier AI systems, and establishment of AI red lines that trigger system termination. Promotes global coordination on AI governance while supporting flexible implementation that respects local cultural values.
organizations.aliases: International Association for Safe & Ethical AI

organizations.name: Centre pour la Sécurité de l'IA (CeSIA)
organizations.notes: Founded March 2024 by Charbel-Raphaël Ségerie and team. ~20 employees plus volunteers. Funded by Survival and Flourishing Fund, Longview Philanthropy, Swedish foundation, Effektiv Spenden. No AI company funding to avoid conflicts. Provides Europe's first university AI safety courses at ENS Ulm/Paris-Saclay. Initiated Global Call for AI Red Lines with 300+ signatories including Nobel laureates. Co-organized symposiums with Yoshua Bengio and Stuart Russell.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, National security
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses on systemic risks from advanced AI including autonomous replication, misalignment, and loss of human control. Research includes BELLS benchmark for evaluating AI safeguards, warning shot theory, and RLHF limitations. Emphasizes extreme risks from frontier AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for robust AI governance frameworks including mandatory safety evaluations, third-party audits, and international coordination mechanisms. Contributed to EU AI Act Code of Practice implementation and supports establishing 'red lines' for unacceptable AI capabilities and uses.
organizations.aliases: Centre pour la Sécurité de l'IA

organizations.name: 5050 AI (by Fifty Years)
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA + London, UK (partnership with ARIA)
organizations.notes: Free 13-week startup accelerator program run by Fifty Years VC, helping scientists and engineers build AI safety/alignment companies. 445 participants to date, launched 105 companies with 96% fundraising success rate. Mentors include OpenAI co-founder Wojciech Zaremba, former OpenAI interim CEO Emmett Shear. UK program launched in partnership with ARIA in 2025. No equity taken, entirely free program focused on commercializing AI safety research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination
organizations.threat_models_detail: Program focuses on AI alignment and safety through commercial applications including safety evaluation tools, interpretability platforms, alignment infrastructure, governance systems, and human-AI collaboration interfaces.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on building AI safety infrastructure through market-driven solutions. Supports startups developing safety evaluation tools, interpretability platforms, alignment infrastructure, and governance systems rather than direct regulatory advocacy.

organizations.name: Centre for Enabling EA Learning & Research (CEEALAR)
organizations.notes: Founded 2018 by Greg Colbourn, registered charity in England & Wales since 2020. Provides subsidized accommodation and workspace in Blackpool for EA researchers and entrepreneurs at £750/month. Supports ~20 residents at a time with 4-6 month average stays. 66 grantees in 2025 (+36% YoY). Notable alumni include Chris Leong (Principal Organiser for AI Safety New Zealand) and Hoagy Cunningham (Stanford Existential Risks Initiative). Formerly known as 'EA Hotel', rebranded in 2020. Currently facing £330k funding gap and needs £30k for urgent roof repairs.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control
organizations.threat_models_detail: Supports researchers working on AI alignment, biosecurity, and other global catastrophic risks. Recent focus has narrowed to global catastrophic risk reduction with over 95% of applicants working in these areas, particularly AI safety alignment research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Primarily focused on talent development and research support rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports researchers working on global catastrophic risks including AI safety, but does not appear to take explicit regulatory positions.
organizations.aliases: Centre for Enabling EA Learning & Research (EA Hotel)

organizations.name: AI Governance and Safety Institute (AIGSI)
organizations.notes: 501(c)(3) nonprofit founded to improve institutional response to AI existential risk. Executive Director Mikhail Samin. Conducts research, outreach, and develops educational materials. Affiliated with AI Safety and Governance Fund. Co-authored academic papers on AI audit standards. Signatory to red-lines initiative calling for preventing unacceptable AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focuses specifically on extinction risks from superhuman AI systems. Research covers alignment problems (inner/outer alignment), convergent instrumental goals, and preventing development of misaligned advanced agents. Views AI as potentially creating human-to-monkey power differential.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for preventing development of superintelligent AI until safety can be guaranteed. Emphasizes institutional response to existential risk through research, outreach, and education. Supports mandatory safety evaluations and oversight mechanisms.

organizations.name: AI Safety Asia (AISA)
organizations.location: Hong Kong (global operations across 16 Asian countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Supheakmungkol Sarin (former Google AI lead and World Economic Forum Head of Data & AI) and Edward Tsoi. Team of 13 employees across 7 countries. Has engaged 2,000+ AI governance professionals across 16 Asian countries. Co-hosted sessions at India AI Impact Summit 2026 on crisis diplomacy and contributed to International AI Safety Report 2026. Focuses on building durable governance infrastructure through regional research, structured peer learning, and implementation-oriented engagement.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality
organizations.threat_models_detail: Focus on AI crisis diplomacy for cross-border incidents, frontier AI safety evaluation, and building regional governance capacity. Addresses catastrophic risks while supporting equitable AI adoption across Asia and bridging Global North-South governance gaps.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance frameworks tailored to Asian contexts. Co-hosted AI crisis diplomacy sessions focusing on cross-border incident coordination and joint safety testing between countries. Contributed to International AI Safety Report 2026 emphasizing need for demonstrable safety evidence and credible liability frameworks.

organizations.name: Apart Research
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as remote-first AI safety organization. 25 employees globally. Runs 55+ research sprints with 6,000+ participants across 200+ locations. Programs include weekend hackathons, Studio (4-month), and Fellowship (6-month) leading to peer-reviewed publications. Notable outputs: ICLR 2025 Oral paper on AI safety evaluations, NeurIPS publications. Alumni placed at METR, Anthropic, and other AI safety organizations. Led by CEO Jaime Raldua and Research Director Jason Hoelscher-Obermaier.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Misinformation
organizations.threat_models_detail: Specializes in AI control research, alignment techniques, and governance frameworks. Runs hackathons on AI manipulation detection, democracy risks, technical governance, and safety evaluations. Develops research on dangerous capabilities and control mechanisms.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI safety research, control mechanisms, and governance infrastructure.
